title: Austrian State Treasury Note for Five Thousand Crowns
description: A Viennese bearer treasury note issued by the Austrian state in 1920 for 5,000 Kronen, this note is designated as tax-free (steuerfrei) and interest-bearing, with provisions for redemption by the state debt administration. The document features elaborate green and pink decorative borders with floral motifs, a double-headed Austrian imperial eagle at the top center, and ornamental medallions at each corner. The variegated, opulent, vibrant swirls of the bond's frame speak to the influence of the Art Nouveau movement: the note's design reveals how the visual language of Art Nouveau, having found a stronghold in Europe, had permeated the visual culture of the Viennese state. Issued in the immediate aftermath of World War I and the dissolution of the Habsburg Empire, this bond represents the new Austrian republic's efforts to raise capital during a period of severe economic instability and hyperinflation. This is further in conversation with the values of opulence, wealth, and extravagance espoused by the aesthetic values of Art Nouveau rippling throughout Europe at the time.
creator: Republic of Austria, Österreichische Staatsschuldenverwaltung
notes: Austrian State Treasury Note, Serie 1920b, No. 008,255, 5,000 Kronen, tax-free. Vienna, September 1, 1920. Signed by the Staatsschuldenverwaltung.
transcription:
# Austrian State Treasury Note - 5,000 Kronen

## Original (German):

**Header:**
Serie 1920b          5000 K          № 008255

Steuerfrei 6%iger, tilgbarer, lebenszeitlich kündbarer

**Title:**
Österreichischer Staatsschatzschein über Fünftausend Kronen

**Body Text:**
Die Staatsentleihasse in Wien zahlt an den Inhaber dieses auf Grund des Gesetzes vom 22. Juli 1920, St. G. Bl. Nr. 330, ausgegebenen Schatzscheines an dem durch die Kündigung bestimmten Tage, spätestens am 1. September 1926, den Betrag von 5000 Kronen. Der Inhaber ist berechtigt, diesen Schatzschein jederzeit für den Betrag des Tages der Kündigung nachfolgenden vierten Kalendermonates zur Rückzahlung zum Umtausch zu bringen. Die Kündigung erfolgt unter Vorbehalt des Zinsen nach Massgabe der Oster-äussrer Bankt Hauptnote Wien und in Filialen in Österreich sowie bei dem Postlesamtamt Wien und bei den Postämtern, im Auslande bei den bekanntgegebenen Poststeilen. Dieser nicht die einzige Kündigung auf dem Schatzscheine endgültig gemacht. Dieser Schatzschein wird mit sechs Prozent für jede Periode für die Dauer gefüllt. Die Zinsen werden ohne abug einer Renteusteuer jährlich im nachstem am 1. März und 1. September dieses Jahres an den Inhaber bei je Österr. Sparkasse gehörigt Schiene bei der Staatsentleihasse in Wien ausgezahlt. Die Verzinsung erfolgt mit Rückzahlungsgebühr des Kapitalbetrages. Der Schatzschein ist gegen Einlösung nach den zum fälligkeitstage noch nicht fällig geworbenen Annehmungen juristisch haften. Sollen die zum fälligkeitstage entfallenden Stilzinsen werden Einlösung des laufenden Anleihebetrages beglitet, der Vortrag der fälligen noch nicht fälligen Zinsschutsbeträge in Äußung gebracht. Die Rückzahlung des Kapitalcs erfolgt ohne Gebühren, Gebühren oder sonstige Wege wählt der Staatsentleihasse in Wien. Die Abmeldung dieser Schatzscheines erfolgt durch Verordnung in Äußung bei Sinnen je dem Daten, im Anlizung des Kapitals binnen 30 Tahren vom Publizitätsgege an. Dieser Schatzschein ist mit 12 Zinsfäldern, von denen der erste am 1. März 1921 fällig wird, versehen.

Wien, am 1. Dezember 1920.

Der Staatssekretär für Geldangner

[signature]

Dieser Schatzschein ist aufseglich begeben und im Handschrift der Staatssach eingetragen.

Der Präsident des Staatsrechnungshofes

[signature: "Ueeh"]

**Bottom section:**
Zur Rückzahlung für den 1. ..................... 192... gültig.

........................ am .......................... 192....

---

## English Translation:

**Header:**
Series 1920b          5000 K          № 008255

Tax-free 6% redeemable, cancellable for life

**Title:**
Austrian State Treasury Note for Five Thousand Crowns

**Body Text:**
The State Loan Office in Vienna pays to the bearer of this note, issued on the basis of the law of July 22, 1920, St. G. Bl. No. 330, on the day determined by cancellation, at the latest on September 1, 1926, the amount of 5,000 Crowns. The bearer is entitled to exchange this treasury note at any time for the amount of the day of cancellation following the fourth calendar month for redemption. Cancellation takes place with the reservation of interest according to the Austrian Bank main note Vienna and in branches in Austria as well as at the main post office in Vienna and at post offices; abroad at the publicly announced post offices. This is not the only cancellation on the treasury note made final. This treasury note is credited with six percent interest for each period for the duration. The interest is paid annually without deduction of income tax on March 1 and September 1 of this year to the bearer at each Austrian savings bank belonging to the State Loan Office in Vienna. Interest accrues with redemption fees for the capital amount. The treasury note is subject to redemption according to the conditions that are not yet due on the maturity date. Should the interest due on the maturity date be withheld from redemption, the outstanding portion of the due interest not yet paid remains due. Repayment of the capital is made without fees, charges or other costs to the State Loan Office in Vienna. The notification of this treasury note is made by decree in Vienna for 30 years from the date of publication. This treasury note has 12 interest coupons, the first of which is due on March 1, 1921.

Vienna, December 1, 1920.

The State Secretary for Finance

[signature]

This treasury note has been issued provisionally and registered in the register of the State debt.

The President of the State Audit Office

[signature: "Ueeh"]

**Bottom section:**
For repayment on the 1st. ..................... 192... valid.

........................ on .......................... 192....

---

## Notable Markings:

- **Serial Number:** № 008255
- **Series:** 1920b
- **Denomination:** 5000 K (Kronen)
- **Issue Date:** December 1, 1920 (Wien/Vienna)
- **Maturity Date:** September 1, 1926
- **Interest Rate:** 6% per annum
- **Status:** Tax-free (Steuerfrei)
- **Signatures:** Two official signatures present (State Secretary and President of State Audit Office)
- **Decorative Elements:** Double-headed Austrian imperial eagle at top center; elaborate green and pink ornamental borders with floral motifs and corner medallions
- **Interest Coupons:** 12 coupons attached (first due March 1, 1921)

title: Burmese Government Bond
description: A Burmese government bond written in native Burmese script, denominated Ten Kyat (တဆယ်ကျပ်; "၁၀" in the corner medallions). The note is adorned with green and purple decorative borders replete with floral and arabesque laurel motifs. The note's visual language communicates a story of self-determination and syncretic identity: the juxtaposition of indigenous Burmese script with European visual motifs — arabesque swirls and Roman laurel wreaths — speaks to the assertion of a uniquely post-colonial Burmese identity. Beneath the central vignette runs the national slogan တသွေး·တသံ·တမိန့် ("One Blood, One Voice, One Command"), the rallying motto of Ba Maw's wartime State of Burma. The footer carries a date in the Burmese calendar — year 1305 (1943/44 CE) — when Burma operated as a nominally independent state under Japanese occupation, following decades of British colonial rule. This is a rare surviving example of financial documentation from wartime Burma, notable for its exclusive use of the indigenous script at a politically turbulent moment in Burmese history.
creator: Government of Burma
notes: Approximate date ca. 1943-1945 based on historical context of Japanese-occupied Burma
transcription:
# State of Burma Independence Savings Bond (Ten Kyat) — Transcription

## Original:

**ဗမာနိုင်ငံတော်လွတ်လပ်ရေး·ငွေစုစာချုပ်**
(top, curved heading)

**တဆယ်ကျပ်**
(large central denomination)

**က** (red, left of centre)     **က** (red, right of centre)
(series letters)

008358 (red, lower left)     008358 (red, lower right)
(serial numbers)

**တသွေး·တသံ·တမိန့်**
(slogan, below denomination)

_[handwritten signature]_
**ဘဏ္ဍာရေးဝန်ကြီး၊**
(signature line, centre)

**၁၃၀၅ခု-ဝါခေါင်လဆန်း ၁ ရက်နေ့ ထုတ်သည်။**

## English Translation:

**State of Burma Independence Savings Bond** (*Bama Naingngandaw Lut-lat-yay Ngwe-su Sachouk*)

**Ten Kyat** (*ta-hsè kyat*)

Series letter: **ka** (က) — printed in red on both sides of the centre.

Serial number: **008358** (printed in red on both the lower left and lower right).

Slogan: **"One Blood, One Voice, One Command"** (*ta-thwei, ta-than, ta-meint*).

Signed: **Minister of Finance** (*Bandaya-yay Wun-gyi*) — above the handwritten signature.

Footer: **"Issued on the 1st waxing day of Wagaung, in the year 1305."** (Burmese Era 1305 = 1943/44 CE.)

Corner medallions: the numeral **"10"** (၁၀) appears in each of the four corners.

title: Internal State Loan for National Defense
description: A Bulgarian government bearer bond (облигация) for the 5% Internal State Loan of 1941 "for the National Defence" (за народната отбрана), with a face value of 5,000 leva at 5% interest, issued under the Main Directorate of State and State-Guaranteed Debts and signed by two senior officials. A cream-and-brown sheet framed by ornate engraved geometric medallions at each corner, with a central panel carrying the denomination and rate; "на приносителя" (to the bearer) marks it a negotiable bearer instrument. The loan belongs to the war finance of Tsar Boris III's Bulgaria: aligned with the Axis from 1941 yet anxious to fund mobilisation without ruinous new taxes, the government turned to domestic borrowing, marketing patriotic national-defence bonds to its own citizens. Such internal loans let a small combatant state raise capital at home while sparing its currency the strain of foreign debt.
creator: Kingdom of Bulgaria, Main Directorate of State Debts
notes: Approximate issue date; exact date not visible on document
transcription:
# Bulgarian 5% Internal State Loan of 1941 for the National Defence — Bearer Bond — Transcription

## Original (Bulgarian):

*(Ornate engraved border with fan rosettes at the corners; text is centred within the frame.)*

**ЦАРСТВО БЪЛГАРИЯ**
*(royal coat of arms — crowned lion shield — centred between the two words)*

**ГЛАВНА ДИРЕКЦИЯ НА ДЪРЖАВНИТѢ И НА ГАРАНТИРАНИТѢ
ОТЪ ДЪРЖАВАТА ДЛЪГОВЕ**

**5% ВЪТРЕШЕНЪ ДЪРЖАВЕНЪ ЗАЕМЪ отъ 1941 г.**
**ЗА НАРОДНАТА ОТБРАНА**

*(fine print, small type:)*

Издаденъ възъ основа на закона за 5% вътрешенъ държавенъ заемъ за нуждитѣ на народната отбрана, утвърденъ съ указъ № 3 отъ 24 май 1941 година, обнародванъ въ Държавенъ вестникъ, брой 116 отъ 30 май 1941 година и на законитѣ за измѣнението и допълнението му, утвърдени съ указъ № 4 отъ 15 юлий 1941 год. и № 1 отъ 26 януарий 1942 год., обнародвани въ Държавенъ вестникъ, брой 155 отъ 18 юлий 1941 год. и брой 20 отъ 29 януарий 1942 год.

*(central decorative panel:)*

**ОБЛИГАЦИЯ**
ОТЪ ПЕТЬ ХИЛЯДИ ЛЕВА
**5000    ЛЕВА    5000**
**5%**

Серия Б          № 022882
**НА ПРИНОСИТЕЛЯ**

*(main body text:)*

Заемътъ се представлява отъ облигации въ отрѣзки отъ 1.000, 5.000, 20.000, 50.000, 100.000, 500.000 и 1.000.000 лева.

Облигациитѣ носятъ 5% годишна лихва, платима въ края на всѣко шестмесечие, срещу купони съ падежъ 15 януарий и 15 юлий всѣка година.

Падежътъ на първия купонъ е 15 юлий 1942 година.

Погасяването на облигациитѣ ще се извършва шестмесечно въ срокъ отъ 15 години, на 15 януарий и 15 юлий всѣка година, чрезъ шестмесечни тегления по жребие.

Тегленията ще се извършватъ въ Главната дирекция на държавнитѣ и на гарантиранитѣ отъ държавата длъгове, единъ месецъ преди настъпване падежътъ на всѣки купонъ.

Първото теглене ще стане на 15 юлий 1942 година.

Купонитѣ съ изтекълъ падежъ и изтеглените въ погашение облигации се изплащатъ отъ Българската народна банка. Изплащането на изтеглените въ погашение облигации става едновременно съ купона, падежътъ на който настъпва следъ тегленето.

Представенитѣ за изплащане облигации трѣбва да бѫдатъ придружени съ всички купони, падежътъ на които не е настъпилъ на опредѣлената за плащане дата; стойностьта на непредставените купони се спада отъ капитала, който следва да се изплати на приносителя.

Непредставенитѣ за изплащане купони, за продължение на 5 години отъ деня на падежа имъ, се погасяватъ въ ползата на държавното съкровище. Този срокъ за погасенитѣ облигации е 15 години.

София, 15 декемврий 1941 година

*(signature block, three legends with handwritten signatures below:)*

ГЛАВЕНЪ ДИРЕКТОРЪ НА ДЪРЖАВНИТѢ ДЛЪГОВЕ:            МИНИСТЪРЪ НА ФИНАНСИТѢ:
*(handwritten signature)*                                              *(handwritten signature)*
ЗА КОНТРОЛЪ:
*(handwritten signature — [illegible])*

---

## English Translation:

*(Ornate engraved border with fan rosettes at the corners; text centred within the frame.)*

**KINGDOM OF BULGARIA** (Tsarstvo Bŭlgariya)
*(royal coat of arms — crowned lion shield — centred between the two words)*

**MAIN DIRECTORATE OF THE STATE AND STATE-GUARANTEED DEBTS**
(Glavna Direktsiya na Dŭrzhavnitě i na Garantiranitě otŭ Dŭrzhavata Dlŭgove)

**5% INTERNAL STATE LOAN of 1941**
**FOR THE NATIONAL DEFENCE** (Za Narodnata Otbrana)

*(fine print:)*

Issued on the basis of the law on the 5% Internal State Loan for the needs of the national defence, ratified by Decree No. 3 of 24 May 1941, promulgated in the State Gazette (Dŭrzhaven Vestnik), issue No. 116 of 30 May 1941, and of the laws for its amendment and supplement, ratified by Decree No. 4 of 15 July 1941 and No. 1 of 26 January 1942, promulgated in the State Gazette, issue No. 155 of 18 July 1941 and issue No. 20 of 29 January 1942.

*(central panel:)*

**BOND**
OF FIVE THOUSAND LEVA
**5000    LEVA    5000**
**5%**

Series Б (B)          No. 022882
**TO THE BEARER**

*(main body text:)*

The loan is represented by bonds in denominations of 1,000, 5,000, 20,000, 50,000, 100,000, 500,000 and 1,000,000 leva.

The bonds bear 5% annual interest, payable at the end of each half-year, against coupons falling due on 15 January and 15 July of each year.

The maturity of the first coupon is 15 July 1942.

The redemption of the bonds shall be carried out half-yearly over a term of 15 years, on 15 January and 15 July of each year, by means of half-yearly drawings by lot.

The drawings shall be carried out at the Main Directorate of the State and State-Guaranteed Debts, one month before the maturity of each coupon falls due.

The first drawing shall take place on 15 July 1942.

Coupons whose maturity has passed and bonds drawn for redemption are paid by the Bulgarian National Bank. Payment of bonds drawn for redemption is made simultaneously with the coupon whose maturity falls due after the drawing.

Bonds presented for payment must be accompanied by all coupons whose maturity has not fallen due on the date fixed for payment; the value of the coupons not presented is deducted from the principal to be paid to the bearer.

Coupons not presented for payment are extinguished in favour of the State Treasury after a further 5 years from the day of their maturity. This term for redeemed bonds is 15 years.

Sofia, 15 December 1941

*(signature block:)*

CHIEF DIRECTOR OF THE STATE DEBTS:            MINISTER OF FINANCE:
*(handwritten signature)*                                 *(handwritten signature)*
FOR CONTROL:
*(handwritten signature — [illegible])*

title: Insurance Company of Rotterdam Charter Documents
description: A seven-page Dutch pamphlet setting out, in some fifty consecutively numbered articles, a scheme for an insurance company at Rotterdam, headed by the promise "Hoe de Assurantie-Compagnie van Rotterdam kan werden gevordert, en dat niemant zou verliezen" (How the Insurance Company of Rotterdam can be advanced, and that no one would lose). Printed in plain roman type with an ornamental initial, it belongs to the flood of joint-stock prospectuses that circulated through the Dutch Republic during the speculative fever of 1720, and survives within "Het Groote Tafereel der Dwaasheid" (The Great Mirror of Folly). The articles detail how subscribers might trade shares and capital with the assurance, characteristic of the era's promoters, that participation carried no risk of loss.

pages.description: Title and opening text, headed "Hoe de Assurantie-Compagnie van Rotterdam kan werden gevordert, en dat niemant zou verliezen," with an ornamental initial capital. Numbered articles 1 through 7 begin the scheme. Marked page 1, signature A. Page 1 of 7.
A single column of continuous text carrying numbered articles 8 through 17, treating the transport of shares, the company's capital and the trading of "actien." Marked page 2. Page 2 of 7.
Continuation of the numbered articles, roughly 18 through 25, in one column of plain type addressing the buying and selling of shares and dealings with the regents. Marked page 3. Page 3 of 7.
One column of text continuing the articles, about 25 through 34, on subscription, share prices and the company's fund. Marked page 4. Page 4 of 7.
Continuous text bearing articles roughly 35 through 41, concerning regents, subscribers and the conduct of share dealings. Marked page 5, signature A 3. Page 5 of 7.
A column of numbered articles, about 42 through 48, elaborating obligations between the company, its directors and shareholders. Marked page 6. Page 6 of 7.
The closing page, with the final articles, roughly 49 through 51, ending the scheme; the lower half of the leaf is blank. Marked page 7. Page 7 of 7.

creator: Anonymous
notes: From Het Groote Tafereel der Dwaasheid (The Great Mirror of Folly), 1720
transcription:
## Page 1

**Original (Dutch):**
( 1 )

Hoe de Assurantie-Compagnie van Rotterdam kan werden gevordert, en dat niemant zou verliezen.

1. Zynde de Inschryving van deze Compagnie ter somma van twaalf millioenen geschiet op den 22 Juny 1720, is 't met de zelfde geweest een nooit gehoorde oploop.

2. Een ingeschreve Capitaal van vier stuivers per hondert guldens is in vier en twintig gesteigert, dat men 't heeft verkogt tot 100 guldens, en voor een uur vyf of zes optie gegeven 10 per Cent premie, om te mogen eyschen tot hondert en twee guldens.

3. Namentlyk zoo groote winsten als in Vrankryk en Engelant met Actien in Compagnie-schappen nu onlangs zyn gedaan, hebben by vele de Impressie gemaekt, al was 't al winst, dat maar Actie heete, zoo dat zy alle raisonnementen verwerpen, en zeggen, dat men in Actien maar dom moet toeloopen.

4. Maar ten minste daar is iets, waar na de Actien moeten werden gewaardeert. En waar na zal die waardering geschieden, als na 't Fonds, Inkomen, of eenig voorrecht, of apparente hope van dien, welke aan de Actien vast is.

5. Beschouw nu deze Compagnie, 't Fonds, of Capitaal, is zoo te zeggen, niets alleen 4 stuivers per 100 guldens. Eenig byzonder, of profitabel voorrecht boven particuliere, of hope, daar toe heeft ze niet 't minste, dat bekent is.

6. Haar Inkomen zal zyn 't profyt van de Assurantie, daar zyn hier niet boven 6 a 7 Assuradeure, die naam mogen hebben, staat hun winst zoo opgeschept, 't is te denken, 'er meer zouden zyn. Laaten deze met den andere ('t geen al een raisonable winst schynt) ordinaris dertig duyzent guldens 's jaars winnen, hoe groot dan hier te rekenen de winst van deze Compagnie?

7. De Compagnie zal ook Assureren huyzen &c. voor brant a 4 stuivers per hondert guldens, laat hier van Geassureert werden, voor vyftig millioenen (dat hachlyk is, of 't zal zyn) is 's jaars hondert duyzent guldens premie.

**Notable markings:** Manuscript annotations in the margin of the original leaf (partly cut off). Signature mark "A" and catchword "8. Dat" at foot of page.

## Page 2

**Original (Dutch):**
( 2 )

8. Dat de Compagnie zal trekken van de transporten een per Mille, is voor de Actionisten een koekje van hun eyge deeg.

9. Hier moeten af de onkosten van de Compagnie, en twee boekhouders is alleen drie duyzent guldens 's jaars.

10. Zy zullen meest Assureren, die meest 't gevaar zyn onderworpen. 't Zou dan Redeloos zyn te stellen, dat de Assurantie van deze Compagnie altyd winst zou zyn, en mogelyk dat 'er voor de Assuradeurs wel jaaren zyn geweest van meer verloren als gewonnen.

11. Hier by blyvende de Particuliere Assuradeurs, en dat nog meer steden gelyke Compagnien op te regten, zal de Calandise verdeelt, of mogelyk om die te trekken, 'er wel tegens den andere werden geklat, immers deze Compagnie heeft reeds parten minder geteykent, als particulier wilden doen.

12. Zoo indien deze Compagnie, af de onkosten en schade op een jaar zal halen, hondert en twintig duysent guldens winst, 't schynt een goet jaar te zullen moeten werden gerekent, en in opsigt van den Inleg al 't ook waarlyk zeer groot zyn, want dus de winst van een jaar zal ophalen vyfmaal 't Ingeleide Capitaal.

13. By voorspoedige jaren dan een, Actie, die men zegt van hondert guldens schynt niet te konnen werden gerekent, dat ze meer zal geven, als eene gulde.

14. En werdende die uytgedeelt wat by tegenspoet, als, dewyl de Compagnie is naakt, gelt te moeten bybrengen?

15. Zoo die 50, 60, ja tot 100 guldens voor deze 4 stuivers Inleggen hebben gegeven, waar ergens zag men zoo dol Actie kopen?

16. 't Is geen twyfel, of de Regering met het Consenteren van deze Compagnie heeft voor gehad de gemeene welvaart van hun burgers.

17. Nogtans zynde daar op gevolgt 't zo dol koopen in deze Compagnie, dat vele, indien zy zouden betalen, zouden werden gekrenkt, en bedorven gelyk 't inzicht van de gemeene welvaart, de Regeerders tot dit Consent heeft gemoveert, zoo ook 't zelfde inzicht schynt af te eyschen, om tegens 't kwaat, 't welk daar uyt ontstaan is, te voorzien.

**Notable markings:** Catchword "18. En kan" at foot of page.

## Page 3

**Original (Dutch):**
( 3 )

18. [...continuation:] een vreemt vaarwater; en onbedreven, en onkundig zyn ze door een breet opgeven, en velerley Praktyken, en zommige door snoode streeken in 't net geraakt en verdronken eer zy 't water kenden, misleit door de naam van Actien, die hier zeer abusief schynt gebruykt, en gantsch tegens oude Hollandse styl; want waar hier ooit voor voor heen, dat een Inleg van 4 stuivers was gebaptiseert een Actie van hondert guldens.

19. Ook dat deze Compagnie was aangelegt onder 't gezag van de Regeerders, schynt die te meer onbedagt te hebben te doen toeloopen, op een vertrouwen, dat haar Ed: wel zouden zorgen 't gelt in deze wel te zyn besteet.

20. Zoo als de luyden zyn bekogt, 't is niet te denken, dat daar op Executie zal werden verleent.

21. Maar dewyl vele, na 't schynt, by een dol koopen zullen hebben een malle schaamte om zich niet te bedienen van de billykheyd des Rechts, zoo deze schaamte vereyscht een Reglement van de Regeerders, en by ontkentenisse van dien ook de wezentlyke schult-eyschers zullen by dit Actie koopen komen te lyden.

22. Vorders dragende deze Compagnie de naam van zoo veel duyzende, en zynde in der daad niet, en zonder Fonds, schynt ze een opstel gelyk de Compagnie-schappen, welke nu vers in Engeland zyn verboden, als Praktyken om onkundige de beurs te ligten.

23. Maar met een goed Reglement de geheele weereldt zou moeten overtuygt zyn van de opregte intentie van de Regeerders in 't con007senteren van deze Compagnie; en ten zy alvorens dit Reglement was 't goet, dat de zaak in staat wiert gehouden, en geen Actien uytgegeven.

24. 't Hier volgende voorstel (en 't welk apparent nog veel zou konnen werden verbetert) schynt te behelzen, al wat de goede intentie van de Regeerders zou hebben konnen beoogen. De Compagnie zou bevordert en alle schade en bederf werden verhoet, en indien niet een eenige zoo over groote zommen, ten minste 't zou een minder geven meer algemeen, en zonder opspraak.

25. Zynde reeds Confiderabel in deze Compagnie verkogt, moesten die verkogt of Premien hebben getrokken om te leveren, daar van genieten een tiende, en de overige rede werden gebragt in de kas van de Compagnie.

**Notable markings:** This page opens mid-sentence, continuing point 18 from the previous page. Catchword "26. De" at foot of page.

## Page 4

**Original (Dutch):**
( 4 )

26. De hoogste koopers moesten volstaan met 50 guldens, of zoo als de coers zal zyn op 't Reglement van de Regeerders.

27. De verkooping van Actien moest alleen zyn aan de Compagnie, indien niet tot 't volle Capitaal, ten minste tot dat 't wat geleek na de Inschryving.

En dus na proportie van een Actie, die heeft van vyftig duyzent guldens, en nu maar in Capitaal is hondert guldens, eer ze tot tien duyzent was vermindert, zou al effectif 't Capitaal in zich hebben, waar na ze werd genoemt.

33. Voor die reeds van particuliere hebben gekogt, de vermindering kon werden geschikt na billykheyd, maar die van de Compagnie zullen koopen, deze zou niet mogen werden vermindert, voor dat hun Actien wezentlyk de waarde hadden van de koop.

34. Die zyn Actien wilde vol houden, zou dat konnen doen, mits Supplerende in gelt, 't geen zy moesten werden vermindert.

35. Op deze wys de Compagnie zou aanstonds hebben een goed Fonds, en niet ongelooflyk, dat zonder eenige bylagen (zoo als de Actien [...]

## Page 5

**Original (Dutch):**
( 5 )

35. [...continuation:] dus niet alleen dat de Assurantie zou konnen zyn een stuyver winst, maar boven dien 'et nog merkelyk voordeel bykomen.

36. Wat al dan de Regeerders in dit voorstel mishagen, zouden haar Ed: denken de verkoopers te verongelyken?

37. Maar zynde de Inschryving geschiet zonder eenige stipulatie, konnen de Regeerder, als welke in 't oprechten van publique Compagnie-schappen komt 't Opperste gezag wetten voorschryven, die 't nu te beliest, en vervolgens ook deze, dat men niet zal mogen zonder hun belieft Confent zyn Inschryving verkoopen, of over doen. Die zoo niet en wil, zie daar zyn 4 stuivers weder.

38. En rust deze wet hier op groote reden. 't Oogmerk is geweest van deze Assurantie Compagnie te bevorderen: de Inschryving was daar toe een verbintenis, en den Inleg, zynde maar dubbelt arme-gelt, is te rekenen als een penning op de hand, welke hy zou verbeuren, die zyn verbintenis niet zou nakomen.

39. Maar dat nu veele voor deze hun Inschryvingen met groote zommen, eenige mogelyk meer als hondert duyzent guldens zoumen, den heen gaan, en dat de Compagnie in 't hemt gelaten en daar by nog vele goede luyden en familien zouden werden gekrenkt en bedorven, is dit niet alleen ver af van de Compagnie te bevorderen, maar 't stryd ook tegens de gemeene welstant zulke zaken in te voeren, waar door, terwyl d'eene zich verrykt, andere noodzakelyk verarmen.

40. Daar dan deze verkoopers niets min denken, als volgens hun verbintenis de Compagnie te helpen bevorderen, ja die de Compagnie als zy den buyt weg hebben, zullen bespotten, wat zal de Regeerders bewegen, dat hebbende 't regt, en de magt om de beschadigde te zalveren, en t'evens de Compagnie te bevorderen, de zelve aan die Verkoopers zouden toestaan, tot rampling en bederf van andere burgers, en tot nadeel en opspraak van de Compagnie, te verryken met een meer inhalend gewin, als ooit de aldergrootste woeker, want waar ooit gehoort, dat men voor een uytgeschoten 4 stuivers begeerde tot hondert guldens of meer.

41. Met de tiende, zoo als hier voorgestelt, zouden zommige nog al tot drie a vier duyzent guldens konnen trekken. 't Is reden, dat de Verkoopers zich genoegen niet alleen om dat 't al winst is, maar ook om dat zy, als van hun verbintenis afgegaan, niets schynen te verdienen.

42. Echter indien dit voor de Verkoopers mogt te hart schynen, men kan 't dan in de keur geven, van de helft van 't verkogte te mogen behouden onverkogt, mits d'andere helft geheel aan de Compagnie.

## English translation

**Title:** How the Insurance Company of Rotterdam can be advanced, and that no one would lose by it.

**1.** The subscription of this Company, to the sum of twelve millions, having been made on the 22nd of June 1720, it was at the same time an unheard-of rush.

**2.** A subscribed capital of four stuivers per hundred guilders has risen twenty-four-fold, so that it has been sold at 100 guilders, and within one hour, for five or six, an option was given of 10 per cent premium, with the right to call it at one hundred and two guilders.

**3.** Namely, such great profits as have lately been made in France and England with shares in trading companies have made an impression on many, as though it were all profit, so long as it was merely called "a share," so that they reject all reasoning and say that one must just rush blindly into shares.

**4.** But at the very least there is something against which shares must be valued. And against what shall that valuation be made, if not against the fund, the income, or some privilege, or the apparent hope thereof, that is attached to the shares?

**5.** Now consider this Company: the fund, or capital, is, so to speak, nothing but 4 stuivers per 100 guilders. Of any special or profitable privilege above private parties, or of any hope, it has not the least that is known.

**6.** Its income will be the profit from the insurance. There are here not above 6 or 7 underwriters who may bear the name; if their profit were so abundant, one would think there would be more. Let these together (which already seems a reasonable profit) ordinarily earn thirty thousand guilders a year; how great then is the profit of this Company to be reckoned here?

**7.** The Company will also insure houses etc. against fire at 4 stuivers per hundred guilders. Suppose fifty millions were insured here (which is doubtful whether it will happen), that is one hundred thousand guilders premium a year.

**8.** That the Company shall take one per mille on transfers is, for the shareholders, a cake of their own dough [i.e., they pay it themselves].

**9.** From this must come the Company's expenses, and two bookkeepers alone are three thousand guilders a year.

**10.** They will chiefly insure those who are most exposed to danger. It would then be unreasonable to suppose that the insurance of this Company would always be a profit; and possibly there have been years for the underwriters of more lost than won.

**11.** With the private underwriters remaining in business, and yet more towns setting up similar companies, the custom will be divided, or perhaps, to draw it away, there will be undercutting against one another; indeed this Company has already subscribed shares at a lower rate than private parties were willing to.

**12.** So if this Company, after expenses and losses, should make in one year one hundred and twenty thousand guilders profit, that would seem to have to be counted a good year, and in respect of the stake it would indeed be very large, for thus the profit of one year would amount to five times the paid-in capital.

**13.** In a prosperous year, then, a share said to be of a hundred guilders does not seem able to be reckoned to yield more than one guilder.

**14.** And if that be distributed, what is to be brought in case of misfortune, since the Company is naked [has no reserves]?

**15.** When people have given 50, 60, even up to 100 guilders for these 4-stuiver stakes, where anywhere was such mad share-buying ever seen?

**16.** There is no doubt that the Government, in consenting to this Company, had in view the common prosperity of its citizens.

**17.** Yet, there having followed such mad buying in this Company that many, if they had to pay, would be injured and ruined, just as the regard for the common prosperity moved the Regents to this consent, so the same regard seems to demand that provision be made against the harm that has arisen from it.

**18.** [...they are, as it were, in] a strange fairway; and being inexperienced and ignorant, they are, by exaggerated representations and manifold tricks, and some by wicked devices, drawn into the net and drowned before they knew the water, misled by the name "shares," which here seems very abusively used, and quite against old Dutch usage; for when was it ever heard before that a stake of 4 stuivers was baptized a share of a hundred guilders?

**19.** Also, that this Company was established under the authority of the Regents seems all the more to have made people rush in heedlessly, trusting that Their Honours would surely see to it that the money was well employed in this.

**20.** As the people have been duped, it is not to be thought that execution [forced payment] will be granted upon it.

**21.** But since many, it seems, after such mad buying, will have a foolish shame to make use of the equity of the law, this shame requires a regulation from the Regents; and by the denial of it, the genuine creditors too will come to suffer from this share-buying.

**22.** Further, this Company bearing the name of so many thousands, while being in fact nothing and without a fund, seems a contrivance like the trading companies lately forbidden in England as practices to pick the pockets of the ignorant on the exchange.

**23.** But with a good regulation the whole world would have to be convinced of the upright intention of the Regents in consenting to this Company; and unless this regulation were first made good, the matter should be held in suspense and no shares issued.

**24.** The following proposal (which apparently could still be much improved) seems to embrace all that the good intention of the Regents could have aimed at. The Company would be advanced and all damage and ruin prevented; and if not a single one [would gain] such very great sums, at least it would give a smaller [gain] more generally, and without scandal.

**25.** Those who have already sold a considerable amount in this Company, or who have drawn premiums to deliver, should enjoy a tenth of it, and the remainder be brought into the Company's treasury.

**26.** The highest buyers should be content with 50 guilders, or as the rate shall be set by the Regents' regulation.

**27.** The sale of shares should be only to the Company, if not up to the full capital, at least until it somewhat resembled the subscription.

**28.** For those who have bought their shares, the Company should allow them to be sold, or take the shares back for the money for which they were bought.

**29.** If the Company sets its shares at 50 guilders, buyers having been found for a fund of [only] 4 stuivers per 100 guilders, it is to be thought there will also be buyers when there is a real fund.

**30.** The South Sea Company in England, though having so mighty a fund, has even so, for the improvement thereof, used this means of selling shares.

**31.** There the shares to be sold were increased; here the shares should remain at the sum of twelve millions, as they now are, and each person's shares be reduced pro rata as much as was required to make up the shares sold.

**32.** It is credible that the shares, in such manner, would soon be brought to a real value of 30 to 40 per cent. That being so, the Company could set them at 60, and so on. And thus, in proportion, a share of fifty thousand guilders that is now only a hundred guilders in capital, before it was reduced to ten thousand, would actually contain the capital after which it is named.

**33.** For those who have already bought from private parties, the reduction could be arranged according to equity; but those who shall buy from the Company, these should not be reduced until their shares were really worth the purchase price.

**34.** Whoever wished to keep his shares at full value could do so, provided he supplied in money the amount by which they were to be reduced.

**35.** In this way the Company would at once have a good fund; and it is not unbelievable that, without any additions (as the shares [...]) thus not only that the insurance might be a stuiver's profit, but besides that a considerable advantage would accrue.

**36.** What then in this proposal might displease the Regents; would Their Honours think it does the sellers an injustice?

**37.** But, the subscription having been made without any stipulation, the Regents — to whom, in erecting public companies, belongs the supreme authority to prescribe laws as they now please — may also [decree] this, that one shall not be allowed without their good pleasure and consent to sell or transfer one's subscription. Whoever will not so do, there are his 4 stuivers back again.

**38.** And this law rests here upon great reason. The aim has been to advance this Insurance Company: the subscription was an obligation toward that, and the stake, being but a paltry pittance, is to be reckoned as earnest-money [a deposit], which he would forfeit who did not fulfil his obligation.

**39.** But that now many, for these their subscriptions, should go off with great sums, some perhaps more than a hundred thousand guilders, and that the Company should be left in the lurch, and besides many good people and families be injured and ruined — this is not only far from advancing the Company, but it also runs counter to the common welfare to introduce such things, whereby, while one enriches himself, others must necessarily be impoverished.

**40.** Since then these sellers think nothing less than, according to their obligation, to help advance the Company — nay, who will mock the Company once they have carried off the spoils — what should move the Regents, who have the right and the power to relieve the injured and at the same time to advance the Company, to grant to those sellers, to the ruin and harm of other citizens and to the detriment and scandal of the Company, enrichment by a more grasping gain than ever the greatest usury? For where was it ever heard that for an advance of 4 stuivers one demanded up to a hundred guilders or more?

**41.** With the tenth, as here proposed, some could still draw up to three or four thousand guilders. It is reasonable that the sellers be content, not only because it is all profit, but also because, having withdrawn from their obligation, they seem to deserve nothing.

**42.** Yet if this should seem too hard for the sellers, one may then leave it to their choice to retain unsold one half of what they sold, provided the other half go wholly to the Company.

**43.** It is true, one goes further here than merely to relieve those who have already bought; but it is right that provision be made also for those who are still buying.

**44.** The rate is now said to be 40 per cent; but the paid-in capital being only 4 per cent per 100 guilders, what [happens] at the least misfortune, when money has to be brought in? Or shall the capital of 4 stuivers be increased without bringing anything in? What then yearly dividend can be expected on a so-called share of a hundred guilders, when it is at best 10 stuivers? This would be outstanding in respect of the paid-in capital; but those who give 40 guilders, how can they be reckoned otherwise than to have bought three times above the value?

**45.** And what then, when one shall see oneself thus left for one's great sums? The Company will be decried, as though it had been set up to catch the ignorant in the net, and the city itself will thereby come into ill repute, and that even abroad, it being said that already foreigners, and persons of rank, have bought into this Company — no doubt also on the good trust which they have hitherto had in the public companies of this country.

**46.** And were there ever before companies here that, with a stake of 4 guilders, flew the flag of millions? So, if the good name in regard of the public companies is to be preserved: it seems one cannot allow that to the buyers, for their great sums, merely an empty shadow should be delivered.

**47.** To say, those who buy must look to it [buyer beware]. So it was also with the premiums in [time-]buying, yet it has been ordained not to recognize them as purchases, namely because it was seen as harmful to many.

**48.** But here there is not only the loss, but the Regents too would be implicated in it, by reason of having approved this Company.

**49.** [...one sees] people quite carried away by the share-craze, so that, like a pack of madmen, they value pieces of glass as diamonds.

**50.** For why do they spend such great sums as for a [stake of] 4 stuivers, and for an adventure which they can have for nothing? Let 24 persons, in order to insure, contribute together 24,000 guilders: each has really 1,000 guilders, and a share in the profit of a twenty-fourth. But those who have bought into this Company at 100 guilders, what have they for their 1,000 guilders? A capital of ten times 4 stuivers, and in the profit a twelve-hundredth part. If ever there was madness in the spending of one's money, where [was it] so grievous, and that for so many thousands?

**51.** And this seems enough to show that what is here proposed would not only advance the Company, but would also be fair and lawful; for who (these being no bonds, on which money was advanced and promises made) could complain, when he still receives a great deal for nothing?

title: Conditions of the Maritime Insurance Company within Middelburg
description: A single-page printed broadsheet presenting the conditions of a maritime insurance company established within the city of Middelburg in the province of Zeeland, the Netherlands, set in two columns with numbered articles covering membership, share subscriptions, claims procedures, and governance. Middelburg was a major Dutch trading port and the capital of Zeeland. Its prominence as a center for maritime insurance ventures during the speculative climate of 1720 dates the document to around this era. Hence, this document is likely associated with the speculative companies promoted during the Dutch financial bubble of 1720 and is preserved in the "Groote Tafereel" collection. The two-column layout and Roman typeface are typical of Dutch commercial printing of the early eighteenth century, and the numbered articles present the company's terms in a formal legalistic manner intended to convey legitimacy. The broadsheet format and dense text suggest it was intended for public posting or distribution to prospective investors.
creator: Maatschappy tot het Asseureeren van Scheepen en Goederen, Middelburg
notes: Likely from Het Groote Tafereel der Dwaasheid (1720); exact page count uncertain
transcription:
# ORIGINAL (Dutch):

**CONDITIEN VAN DE MAATSCHAPPY Tot het ASSEUREEREN Van Scheepen en Goederen binnen, de Stad MIDDELBURG.**

Nademaal het Assureren van Schepen en Koopmanschappen, zoo voor de gevaren van de Zee, als die van Oorlogen en Zeerovery, noodzakelyk is voor de Negotie en Zeevaart, en dat men ziet in Engeland een Maatschappy daar toe opgeregt, om door zoo grooten zamengevoegde Kapitaal, die geene die zig willen doen Assureren, volstrektelyk gerust te stellen; zo moet men vast stellen, dat indien zodanig een Maatschappy of zaamenvoeging van Kapitaal in deze Stad van Middelburg wierd gemaakt, van de uyterste Vrugt zoude zyn voor 't Gemeen, en werd ten dien eynde geproponeerd op wat Voet dit Concept best Werkstellig te maken is.

**I.** Dat het geheele Kapitaal zal bestaan in 200000 pond en niet meer; dat ieder Kapitaal Actie zal zyn 1000 pond Vlaams, en by gevolg zal bestaan in 200 Actien, dat [men] niet minder als 500, en niet meer als 80000 pond Vlaams zal mogen Intekenen.

**II.** Dat 'er geen zullen gekend werden in zaaken daar de gehele Societeyt by malkander ontbooden werd, als die voor 1000 pond Kapitaal op de Boeken bekend staan.

**III.** Niemand zal ooit verder aansprekelyk wezen, als voor zyne getekende Zom.

**IV.** Dat ieder Intekenaar in handen van Commissarissen (Provisioneel door de Regering aan te stellen) zullen betalen 50 per cent van ieder zyn ingeteekend Kapitaal, veertien daagen na de affectie van Biljetten dat het vereischte Kapitaal voltekend is: Dat door de Actionisten zullen aangesteld werden vier of meer Commissarissen, by de meerderheid te verkiezen.

**V.** Dat de Commissarissen aan te stellen zullen zyn voor haar leven lang, en hier binnen Middelburg moeten woonagtig zyn.

**VI.** Dat 'er geen Polissen zullen mogen getekend werden, of ook van waarde zyn, als door alle de Commissarissen of wel by de helft van haar gerekend.

**VII.** Dat alle de Penningen zo van Ontfang als Uytgeef, zullen moeten betaald en ontfangen werden, zoo per Banco als per Cassa, op de Namen van alle de Commissarissen, en dat alle Quitantien zullen moeten getekend werden, ten minsten door de helft van de voorsz. Commissarissen.

**VIII.** Dat de Penningen van den Inleg zullen aangelegt moeten werden op vaste Effecten, zoo in diervoegen als omtrent Weze Penningen werd gedaan.

**IX.** Dat geen Effecten zoude mogen Beleend of Verkogt werden, als met eenpaarig Consent van de Commissarissen.

**X.** Dat de ontfange Premieen en Interesten zullen blyven in Kas of Bank tot betalinge der Schaden.

**XI.** Dat de Commissarissen zullen mogen Assureren op alle Scheepen en Koopmanschappen, zoo van Vriend als Vyand, als mede van en naar alle Plaatsen, gepermitteert of ongepermitteert, en dat de Commissarissen zullen gedragen naar de Artikelen van de Kamer van Assurantie deezer Stad Middelburg; dat ze ook zullen moogen verzekeren op Huyzen, Pakhuyzen, Koopmanschappen, leggende in Pakhuyzen of Kelders, als mede op Plantagien, zo binnen als buyten 's Lands.

**XII.** Dat alle kwestien ter eerster instantien zullen moeten dienen voor de Kamer van Assurantie dezer Stad, en by Appel voor Burgemeesters en Schepenen dezer Stad, en geen verder Appel zullen subject wezen.

**XIII.** Dat door Commissarissen zoo veel en zoo weinig op de voorgaande Conditien en Polissen zal mogen getekend werden, als zy goed zullen vinden.

**XIV.** Dat 'er door Commissarissen zullen aangesteld werden by provisie een bekwaam Boekhouder, en Onder-Boekhouder, zullende de Opper-Boekhouder ook voor Kassier dienen, en stellen daar voor suffisante Cautie na genoegen van Commissarissen.

**XV.** Dat Directeurs en die Bediende zullen hebben zeeker Tractement 's Jaars, nader te reguleren by de meerderheid van d'Actionisten.

**XVI.** Dat de Commissarissen by eenig voorval en nodig zynde, zullen alle de Kapitalisten dagvaarden ter plaatze daar de Vergadering zal gehouden werden, by Biljet en overnagte Wete, behalven die uyt de Provintie wonen.

**XVII.** Dat de Commissarissen zullen doen alle 2 Jaren Rekeninge in prezentie van alle Actionisten die zig daer by zullen willen laten vinden en 14 dagen te vooren by affectie van Biljetten zullen werden gedagvaard.

**XVIII.** Dat men na het sluyten der Boeken zal resolveren wat Uytdeeling dat 'er zal gedaan werden, en zal zulks moeten geschieden met meerderheyd van Stemmen, namentlyk een Commissaris tegens twee Actionisten, op die tyd met meerderheyd van Stemmen daar toe te committeren; en zal de Repartitie moeten geschieden in kontante Penningen, maar zal geen Repartitie mogen geschieden ten zy het ingelegde Kapitaal van 100000 pond Vlaams in wezen is.

**XIX.** En dat buiten deezen Comp. alle en een yder zoude mogen Assureren in zyn privé zo als voor deze. Ook zullen de Commissarissen daer toe te nomineren moeten den Eed doen van getrouwigheyd, als mede dat zy wezentlyk een Kapitaal Actie van 1000 pond Vlaams daar in [houden].

**XX.** En alzo dit concept maar provisioneel is, zo staat zo dra de Voltekening zal geschied zyn een nader Reglement gemaakt te werden, ten overstaan der Actionisten.

De Actien van deze Assurantiekamer doen reets 40 percent opgeld.

# ENGLISH TRANSLATION:

**CONDITIONS OF THE COMPANY FOR THE INSURANCE OF SHIPS AND GOODS WITHIN THE CITY OF MIDDELBURG.**

Inasmuch as the insurance of ships and merchandise — against the perils of the sea as well as those of war and piracy — is necessary for commerce and navigation, and since one sees that in England a company has been established for this purpose, so that by such a great pooled capital those who wish to insure themselves may be entirely set at ease; it must be held that if such a company, or pooling of capital, were formed in this City of Middelburg, it would be of the utmost benefit to the public. To that end it is proposed on what footing this plan may best be put into effect.

**I.** That the whole capital shall consist of 200,000 pounds and no more; that each capital share shall be 1,000 Flemish pounds, and shall accordingly consist of 200 shares; and that no one may subscribe less than 500 nor more than 80,000 Flemish pounds.

**II.** That none shall be recognized in matters where the whole society is convened together, save those who stand entered on the books for 1,000 pounds of capital.

**III.** No one shall ever be liable beyond the sum he has subscribed.

**IV.** That each subscriber shall pay into the hands of the Commissioners (to be appointed provisionally by the government) 50 percent of his subscribed capital, fourteen days after notice by bills that the required capital has been fully subscribed; and that four or more Commissioners shall be appointed by the shareholders, chosen by majority.

**V.** That the Commissioners to be appointed shall serve for life, and must reside here within Middelburg.

**VI.** That no policies may be signed, or be of any value, unless reckoned by all the Commissioners or at least by half of them.

**VII.** That all monies, both received and paid out, must be paid and received whether by bank or by cash, in the names of all the Commissioners; and that all receipts must be signed by at least half of the said Commissioners.

**VIII.** That the monies of the deposit shall be invested in sound securities, in the same manner as is done with orphans' monies.

**IX.** That no securities may be pledged or sold without the unanimous consent of the Commissioners.

**X.** That the premiums and interest received shall remain in the cash or bank for the payment of losses.

**XI.** That the Commissioners may insure all ships and merchandise, whether of friend or foe, and to and from all places, permitted or unpermitted, and that the Commissioners shall conduct themselves according to the Articles of the Chamber of Assurance of this City of Middelburg; that they may also insure houses, warehouses, and merchandise lying in warehouses or cellars, as well as plantations, both within and outside the country.

**XII.** That all disputes shall in the first instance be brought before the Chamber of Assurance of this City, and on appeal before the Burgomasters and Aldermen of this City, and shall be subject to no further appeal.

**XIII.** That the Commissioners may underwrite as much or as little upon the foregoing conditions and policies as they shall see fit.

**XIV.** That the Commissioners shall provisionally appoint a competent Bookkeeper and an Under-Bookkeeper, the Head-Bookkeeper also serving as Cashier, and they shall furnish sufficient surety to the satisfaction of the Commissioners.

**XV.** That the Directors and the staff shall have a certain salary per year, to be further regulated by the majority of the shareholders.

**XVI.** That the Commissioners, on any occasion and when necessary, shall summon all the capital-holders to the place where the meeting is to be held, by bill and overnight notice, except those who live outside the province.

**XVII.** That the Commissioners shall render accounts every 2 years in the presence of all shareholders who wish to attend, who shall be summoned 14 days in advance by notice of bills.

**XVIII.** That after the closing of the books it shall be resolved what distribution is to be made, and this must be done by majority of votes — namely one Commissioner against two shareholders, deputed thereto at that time by majority of votes; and the distribution must be made in ready cash, but no distribution may be made unless the paid-in capital of 100,000 Flemish pounds is intact.

**XIX.** And that outside this Company anyone may insure privately as before. The Commissioners to be nominated thereto must also take the oath of fidelity, as well as that they actually hold a capital share of 1,000 Flemish pounds therein.

**XX.** And since this plan is only provisional, a further regulation shall be drawn up as soon as the subscription is complete, in the presence of the shareholders.

The shares of this Assurance Chamber are already at 40 percent premium.

---

# Notable markings:

- Two-column letterpress, Roman type; page numbered "18" at the upper right.
- No signatures, seals, or stamps.
- The closing line, noting the shares already stood at a 40 percent premium, reflects the speculative share fever of 1720, the year of the Bubble.

title: Regulations for the Exchange Bank within Utrecht
description: Printed ordinance establishing a public exchange bank, the Wisselbank, within the city of Utrecht. Adopted by the Burgemeesteren en Vroedschap — the city's magistrates and council — on 7 October 1720 and published 11 October by the civic printer Jacob van Poolsum, it runs to seven pages of articles governing the bank's operation: the heavy silver coin accepted on deposit (ducatoons, three-guilder pieces, rijksdaalders, daalders, guilder pieces), the opening and crediting of accounts, transfers and assignations between account-holders made in specie, the fees levied, the commissioners' attendance days and hours, the twice-yearly balancing of the books, and penalties for overdrawing. Lodged in the town hall under the city's guarantee and modelled on the Amsterdam bank, it furnished reliable bank money for trade as municipal authority built the financial machinery of the early eighteenth century.

pages.description: Title page: "Reglement op de Wisselbank binnen Utrecht," adopted 7 October 1720 and published the 11th. The city arms — a shield flanked by two lions — appear beneath the title. Imprint: Utrecht, printed by Jacob van Poolsum, civic printer, 1720. Page 1 of 7.

Opening text under the running head "Reglement op de Wisselbank," with an ornamented initial B. The Burgemeesteren and Vroedschap of Utrecht establish a public exchange bank in the town hall under commissioners, to open 1 November 1720, and pledge the city's full security. Page 2 of 7.

Numbered articles on accounts: each holder receives a folio in the bank's ledger; deposits must be the heavy, coarse coin named — ducatoons, three-guilder pieces, rijksdaalders, daalders and guilder pieces — with fees stated, and no sum under three hundred guilders accepted. Page 3 of 7.

Articles on payments and transfers. Account-holders settle debts by assignation in specie, ordering sums moved from their folio to another's; the assignation must be entered, the fee two stuivers per thousand guilders. Morning hours of eight to nine are set; three stuivers per hundred guilders. Page 4 of 7.

Articles on the commissioners' attendance: deposits and withdrawals handled Mondays, Wednesdays and Fridays from nine to eleven in the morning and Saturday afternoons, never on feast or Sundays; afternoon hours three to four, fee six stuivers. Twice yearly, in January and July, the books are to be balanced. Page 5 of 7.

Balancing the books: closed folios are carried to new ledger pages, with fees and ten-guilder charges noted. Signed assignations and assignments are covered; powers of attorney lodged by procuration remain valid twelve months, against a twenty-stuiver fee. Page 6 of 7.

Final articles: one disposing of more than the folio's balance pays a penalty of two per cento; silver must be brought in of the named weight and fineness for examination. Closing clause records unanimous adoption by the Vroedschap on 7 October and publication 11 October 1720, signed by the secretary E. V. Harscamp. Page 7 of 7.

creator: Vroeschap der Stadt Utrecht
notes: Printed by Jacob van Poolsum, Stads Drukker, Utrecht; established 7 October, published 11 October 1720

title: Inventory of Assets Belonging to the Colony of Berbice
description: A Dutch inventory document listing the assets of the Colony of Berbice (present-day Guyana), including 895 enslaved people described as large and small, cacao plantations, Fort Nassau, military equipment including sixty cannon pieces, ships, weapons, livestock, medical supplies, and a church. The document is formatted as a numbered list of asset categories with quantities and estimated values, a characteristically Dutch colonial bookkeeping convention. The page number 574 visible at the bottom indicates this is an extract from a larger bound volume, likely related to speculative colonial investment promoted during the 1720 Dutch financial bubble. Berbice was a Dutch colony on the northern coast of South America administered under a chartered company structure, and this inventory reflects the entanglement of colonial enterprise, enslaved labor, and early modern financial speculation. The document is a stark record of the commodification of enslaved people alongside material assets in Dutch colonial accounting.
creator: Colony of Berbice / Dutch West India Company
notes: Extract from larger bound volume (page 574); likely from Het Groote Tafereel der Dwaasheid (1720) or related colonial prospectus

title: Project for the Ruin of the Turkish Pirates of Algiers, Tunis, Tripoli and Salé
description: A printed Dutch petition and project, addressed to the States General ("Uw Hoog Mogende"), proposing the chartering of a privately funded joint-stock shipping venture ("Rederye") to destroy the Barbary corsairs ("Turkze Rovers") of Algiers, Tunis, Tripoli, and Salé, to free enslaved captives, and to secure Dutch navigation and commerce. Merchants who have suffered some thirty years of losses request an octroi to fit out eight frigates to cruise the Strait of Gibraltar and Mediterranean. The scheme is capitalised at one million guilders in ten thousand transferable shares of five hundred guilders each, governed by directors and principal shareholders, with prize money, pensions, and Exchange Bank payments. The document cites a placard of 1705, after which it must have been printed; it was later bound into a Bubble-era compilation around 1720.

pages.description: Title page, set within ruled borders. The heading "PROJECT tot het ruïneeren der TURKZE ROVERS van Algiers, Tunis, Tripoly en Zalée" announces a plan to suppress the corsairs, advance navigation and commerce, free enslaved "ongelukkige en onmachtige Menschen," and recoup damages to the Republic. Page 1 of 8.

Opening of the petition, marked "(2)," a single justified text block. Merchants trading to these lands address the States General, recalling thirty consecutive years of injury from the Barbary corsairs and seeking an octroi to establish a Rederye and equip ships. Page 2 of 8.

Continuous text, page "(3)." Details the fitting-out of armed ships through the Admiralty, their cannon, ballast, and crews, the division of prizes taken from corsairs, the ransoming and freeing of slaves, pay for officers and sailors, and convoy protection. Page 3 of 8.

Continuous text, page "(4)." Describes benefits to the State and to poor seamen: pensions for the aged and disabled, provision for sailors dying intestate at sea, and contributions from ships sailing past Cape Finisterre into the Bay of Biscay, Spain, Portugal, and the Mediterranean, against a fixed premium of five thousand guilders. Page 4 of 8.

Continuous text, page "(5)." Sets the premium for protected ships, terms for officers and crew, and provisions should war with a Christian prince void the cited placard of 1705. States that the venture will form a first fund and raise a capital of one million. Page 5 of 8.

Continuous text, page "(6)." Specifies the capital: ten thousand portions of five hundred guilders each, called in twenty-percent instalments, transferable like Zeeland obligations without transport. Eight equipped frigates are to cruise the Strait and Mediterranean toward Port Mahon, freeing ships and guarding the Portugal and France routes. Page 6 of 8.

Continuous text, page "(7)." Outlines governance: the octroi without monopoly, election of directors by principal shareholders each holding thirty portions, payment of shares through the Exchange Bank (Wisselbank), and the requirement of two directors' signatures. Page 7 of 8.

Closing text, page "(8)," with administration and dividend provisions and an appeal to the States General to grant the project. Signed: "Ondertekent ... Was geteekent, C. Schryver, A. v. Waeyen, Isaac Clifford & Carel C. vande Putten, Jean Fizeaux." Page 8 of 8.

Without the help of [place names] and the sound of the Land at that time, good cannons will be made available, and the remaining money of the projected fleet shall have [funds], so as not to lie idle, and in order to advance the affairs of the Reederij in some way, that one likewise therewith against the permitted Negroes, without through further calculation to name the useful Reederij, and her Odium thereupon... [text continues with discussion of maritime operations, directors, cannon ownership, and financial participation in what appears to be a privateering or naval venture]

The Supplicants do not doubt that the Reederij in good order will believe, and that some regulated Hotchpodge Participants, arrangements are counted, which those which thirty portions in ownership belt, which ownership in truth no longer enlightens will become, than when the Reederij therewith against the permitted Negroes...

[The text continues discussing directors, capital ventures, and annual meetings]

These shall be done once a year, on a valley wide thereto [designated for this purpose].

---

**Notable markings:**

- Page number "(7.)" at top
- Signature or notation area visible at bottom of text block
- Document appears to be page 7 of 8 from a bound collection

---

# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

ren, reckelijcklijck doen van haere administratic aan de Hoofdparticipanten, dewelcke daar toe in lysts sullen worden beschreeven, en na fulcke sal door de Directeurs uyt de gedaante wilste de bijdragende worden gereglementeerd.

De Supplanten vermeenen te moogen verwagten, dat Uw Hoog-Mogende, het geprojecteerde vindende sullen Bracken cordoijelt van de Republick, gelijck iedere Supplanten verloekeeren, dat voor de uytvindingte en gezeedheydt omallechet genie in het Eyck daer mee te voeren, van groote importe sal weesen, ende dat dan ook billijck bewogen te laten een cidulent portion (mits aan de Rederije betaalende de hier vooren getelde tarisije per cent) tot het nodoumagertecn van veerkig-dorient guidens aghtertellige, Soedert neegen jaaren voor den dienst van het Land, noch ie pretenderren hebbende, en daar beneffens het summa, dat voor het onderhollende der gemeents gelden en kosten van het Stadhuis der Rederije aanbiedt, en daarboven dat aan hem, boneevens zijn ondergeschicende Mecklislanders werd aanbevoolen de uytvoering van het gerojecteerde.

Ondertscht:
'tWaerachtigh Memoria, &c.
Was getekent,
C. Schreyser.
A. v. Wergen.
Hans Glijsford & Caroll C. sander Putters
Jean Eisslanus.

---

**English translation:**

ren, faithfully do [the administration] of their administration to the Chief Participants, by which they shall be described in lists, and according to such shall be regulated by the Directors from the aforementioned will of those contributing.

The Petitioners hope to be able to expect that Your High Mightiness, finding the projected [plan] the Bracket cordial of the Republic, just as every Petitioner curses, that for the invention and piety of all genius in the Eye [the matter] with which to proceed, of great importance shall be, and that then also rightfully moved to grant a silent portion (provided to the Redery paying the here counted tariffs per cent) to the necessary [means] of reasonable-present guilders for the service of the Land, yet claiming [nothing], and thereupon the sum, that for the maintenance of the common funds and costs of the Town Hall of the Redery offers, and moreover that to him, as his subordinate Mecklislanders was offered the execution of the projected [plan].

Undersigned:
'tWaerachtigh Memoria, &c.
Was signed,
C. Schreyser.
A. v. Wergen.
Hans Glijsford & Caroll C. sander Putters
Jean Eisslanus.

Four signatures at bottom of page:
