**POMONA.**

POMONA, the Goddess, now comes to display
Her fruits, on an apple-stall spread, this Twelfth Day:

The crops of her orchard most surely must fail,
When thus she's reduc'd to be trading retail:
Small profits from dealing are slender supplies,
To those which next Valentine's Day may arise
From Fortune's State Lott'ry; for then she intends
With Two Hundred Thousands to welcome her friends.

# Transcription

**HERCULES.**

HERE's HERCULES, fam'd for his wonderful feats,
Reduc'd to a pavior, now mending the streets;
He rams till he's faint; so, his spirits to cheer,
Because it's Twelfth Night, has a pot of good beer.
Poor dog! was he prudent to husband his mite,
How soon might he be in a much better plight!
The Lott'ry, which draws on next Valentine's Day,
Maymake the rogue happy without more delay.

**PANDORA.**

**MISCHIEVOUS PANDORA** will find her mistake,
If she comes expecting a piece of Twelfth Cake,
I like not the baggage; she may, if she pleases,
Withdraw from our treat with her box and diseases.
'Tis open'd, alas! and the plagues fly about,
While Hope lies behind, and is loth to come out.
Hope! sure I mistake, 'tis a Ticket I see,
A Prize in the Lottery—I hope it's for me.

"Who'll play To-Night at Blind-Man's buff?"
"I will," says JANE,—"but don't play rough:"

"So stand now clear,—mind how you tread;

To her friends all alike, and no difference knows;

**ANGER.**
*The Rubber lost by a Revoke.*

[Engraving of Lady Pennypoint standing beside a table with two lit candles]

**LADY PENNYPOINT.**

With a *knave* I was caught, and put quite to the blush,
All *honours* were *play'd*, yet of hearts I'd a *flush*,
But my partner, poor deal, was as cross as Old Nick,
And 'twas by his *revoking* he lost the odd trick.
If my lead he'd return'd, and left shuffling alone,
I'd have *won* the first point, and the *game* was our own.
My only resource to some Office to *pack*,
Take a *rubber* with Fortune, on *loo* turn my back.

Lottery begins 21st This Month (Jan.)—2 of 20,000 Guineas, and
40 other Capitals—all Sterling Money (no Stock Prizes.)

# GREATNESS.

KING OF THE ROUND TABLE.

I'm King! and, as such, like to see
My subjects jolly, plump, and free:
I love to see their cheeks disclose
The lily mingling with the rose.
I'll strike the hag Oppression dumb--
I'll shew her my tremendous bomb!--
Thus gratified my warmest wishes,
The poor shall have the loaves and fishes--
Each Lottery Office will supply
Fresh droits from Fortune's Admiralty.

Lottery begins 21st This Month (Jan.)--2 of 20,000 Guineas, & 40 other Capitals--all Sterling Money (no Stock Prizes.)

Hear him! hear him! Order! Order!

The *Ayes* have got it. (*Chair him, chair him!*)

New Year's Lotteries begin JANUARY 23, containing
NINE PRIZES OF £21,000 EACH.
All Other Prizes Doubled.

MOGGY CLEISHBOTHAM.

The Season's best Compliments wait on us all,
And a rich New Year's Present from fam'd Coopers' Hall.
In truth where such good entertainment invites
What spirits are proof 'gainst such cheering delights?
On SWIFT I will wait, as I like to be doing,
For things good and plenty make excellent brewing.
Twenty-one Thousands! I'll pledge them in wine,
Should Fortune give orders that one shall be mine,
And we'll toast the Queen we All Love.

**Figure 1 (Left):**
Come buy my young lambs for a trifle apiece,
And believe me, they're glad with a fine golden fleece;
Here are Two, and to keep all your spirits in tune,
There are Three Thousand more on the 14th of June.

**Figure 2:**
Tho' a dab, I'm not scaly—I like a good plaice,
And I hope that good luck will soon smile in my face;
On the 14th of June, when Prizes in *shoals*,
Will cheer up the *cockles* of all sorts of souls.

Which the wealth of the Lottery Wheel may give me;
If fortune I catch, and I finger the cole,
By the powers! I'll then bid adieu to the pole.

For a Prize in the Lottery Wheel I've bespoke;
And I who knows how high in the world I may rise,
If Fortune should butter my bread with a Prize?

For the gay fruits of nature what wish you can feel,
When compar'd with the *fruits* of the Lottery Wheel
My basket of fruit I'd exchange with great glee,
If one golden *pippin* they'd only give me.

# Transcription

**POLICEMAN.—Hollah! What are you two boys talking about, eh? I think you are entering into a very wicked conspiracy to overturn the Government. I have just overheard ye talking about the Bank. Now I con't want you to say anything that will prejudice yourselves on your trial; at the same time I am willing to hear anything you have got to say in explanation of your conduct concerning your talk about the Bank. I have no doubt you meant the Bank of England.**

**FIRST BOY.— Bank of England! What be that? Whoy, it was that mud bank we were a talking on.**

**SECOND BOY.—To be sure it was. Don't you see, Mr. Policeman, it's a country bank,— and we were a-going to make a run upon it, by troving to jump through it!**

title: French Royal Tontine Certificate
description: A subscription certificate to the tenth royal tontine of France, created by the Edict of December 1759 and here placed in the eighth class. Combining engraved formulae with secretarial hand, it records that Jean Marie Joseph François Gueneau de Vauzette, a Paris burgher, has paid two hundred livres for a share whose annuity is contingent on the life of Victoire Sophie de Noailles, aged sixty. Issued under Joseph Micault d'Harvelay, Garde du Trésor royal, payment falls due twice yearly. Such tontines were a favoured instrument of crown borrowing under Louis XV: subscribers pooled in age-classes received annuities that swelled as fellow members died, the last survivor inheriting the whole, financing the monarchy through wagers on longevity.

pages.description: The certificate proper, headed "10.me Tontine créée par Édit du mois de Décembre 1759," eighth class. Engraved formulae filled in cursive name the subscriber Gueneau de Vauzette, the two hundred livres paid, and the nominated life, Victoire Sophie de Noailles. Dated at Paris, March 1760, with the receipt signature of the Garde du Trésor royal below. Page 1 of 2.

The verso, bearing the engraved registration clause certifying entry at the Contrôle général des Finances by its Garde des Registres, completed at Paris in September 1760 and signed by the clerk. The reverse otherwise shows only mirror-image ink bleed-through from the certificate on the recto. Page 2 of 2.

creator: Joseph Micault d'Harvelay (Garde du Trésor royal)
notes: French Royal Tontine certificate, 10th Tontine (created by Royal Edict, December 1759). Subscriber: Jean Marie Joseph François Gueneau de Vauzette. Nominee: Victoire Sophie de Noailles. Registered at the Contrôle général des Finances, Paris, 22 September 1760.
transcription:
# French Royal Tontine Certificate - 10th Tontine, 1759

## Original (French):

N.° 02
8e CLASSE.
II. Subdivisions.

10me TONTINE criée par Edit du mois de Décembre 1759.

Je Joseph Micault d'Harvelay, Conseiller du Roi en ses Conseils, Garde de son Trésor royal, confesse avoir reçu comptant en cette ville de Paris, de

**Paul Marie Joseph François Gueneau de Paucelle** Bourgeois, à titre payié les Bacheliers ayant causé de la rente d'après l'Edit de Mad Marie Victoire Sophie De Noailles âgée de soixante trois ans aux laisser D'or d'annière de M.r Alexandre & Bourbon Comte de Coulange

**Douze cent Livres**
en louis d'or, d'argent & monnoie, pour le principal de [unclear] mille livres de rentes viagères, qui [unclear] seroient constituées au Profit des Marchands & Echevins de la ville de Paris, sur les trois millions de Rentes viagères dites Tontines, vendues & aliénées auxdits sieurs Prévôt des Marchands & Échevins, en conséquence de l'Edit du mois de Décembre 1759, reglées ou befoin à cet, lesdites rentes alliaguées tant sur la Ferme générale des Postes que sur les droits d'Aides & Gabelles, pour jouir par [unclear] Gueneau de Paucelle à raison d'une action dans la Tontaine

[The document continues with legal language regarding the division of the 8th Class of the 10th Tontine, with references to financial obligations and conditions.]

Quittance du Garde du Trésor royal. Exercice mil sept cent cinquante-neuf.

[Signature] Micault D'Harvelay 1760

M.r D'Hilly Notaire.

---

## English Translation:

**No. 02**
**8th CLASS**
**II. Subdivisions**

**10th Tontine issued by Edict of the month of December 1759.**

I, Joseph Micault d'Harvelay, Counselor to the King in his Councils, Guardian of his Royal Treasury, confess to have received in cash in this city of Paris, from

**Paul Marie Joseph François Gueneau de Paucelle**, Bourgeois, as payment for the annuities caused by the Edict of Madame Marie Victoire Sophie De Noailles, aged sixty-three years, paid in gold louis and currency, the sum of

**Twelve hundred Livres**
in gold louis, silver, and currency, for the principal of [unclear] thousand livres of life annuities, which would be constituted for the benefit of the Merchants and Aldermen of the city of Paris, on the three million livres of annuities called Tontines, sold and transferred to the aforementioned Messieurs Provost of the Merchants and Aldermen, in consequence of the Edict of the month of December 1759, registered or if necessary to date, the said annuities charged both on the General Farm of Posts and on the rights of Aides and Gabelles, to be enjoyed by [unclear] Gueneau de Paucelle by reason of one share in the Tontine...

[Continues with legal conditions and terms]

Receipt from the Guardian of the Royal Treasury. Year seventeen hundred fifty-nine.

[Signature] Micault D'Harvelay 1760

Mr. D'Hilly, Notary.

---

## Notable Markings:

- **Serial Number:** N.° 02
- **Class:** 8e CLASSE
- **Signatures:** "Micault D'Harvelay 1760" (official signature); "D'Hilly" (Notary signature)
- **Date:** December 1759 (issued); 1760 (signed)
- **Visible wear and aging marks** on the document
- **Official stamps and seal impressions** (partially visible)

title: Continental Lottery Subscriber List
description: A manuscript subscriber list and accompanying letter for the United States (Continental) Lottery, kept by William Holliday, the lottery's agent at Winchester, Virginia, and forwarded to James Searle of Congress's lottery managers at Philadelphia. In a close column the sheets record each purchaser by name, the number of tickets taken, and their ticket numbers (all under the prefix "No 52"), running to one hundred tickets at ten dollars each, totalling £375. A covering letter dated 24 July 1777 remits one thousand dollars by the hand of Samuel Gilkeson. Congress organised the lottery to help finance the Revolutionary War, and the densely filled return shows how briskly tickets sold in the Virginia backcountry, where buying a ticket joined a popular flutter to the work of the cause.

pages.description: A single dense column of subscribers, each with tickets taken and ticket numbers under the prefix "No 52": John Sentz, John Abbott Esq., William Holliday, Albert Russell, Gabriel Thunker, and dozens more, holdings ranging from one to ten tickets. A running tally, "80 Carried over," is struck at the foot. Page 1 of 3.

The list concludes (Thomas Edmondson, William McClanachan, Robert Russell, and others) and is totalled: "100 Tickets @ 10 dollars each is £375." Below, a letter dated "Winchester July 24th 1777" to Mr James Searle remits 1000 dollars by Mr Samuel Gilkeson for the hundred tickets sold, signed Wm Holliday. Page 2 of 3.

The folded address leaf, directed "Mr James Searle, Philadelphia," noted "favor of Mr Gilkeson," with the remnant of a red wax seal at center and a docket at the edge reading "July 24th 1777 / William Holliday / Winchester." Page 3 of 3.

creator: William Holliday (lottery agent, Winchester, VA)

title: Continental Lottery Ticket Sales List
description: A handwritten account drawn up by Robert Traill, a local agent at Easton, Pennsylvania, recording his sales for the United States Lottery, "Class the Second." Authorized by Congress in 1776 to raise funds for the Revolutionary War, the lottery was distributed in successive classes through agents who carried tickets into country towns and remitted the proceeds to the commissioners in Philadelphia. The sheet lists the numbers of twenty-five tickets sold, each at twenty dollars, and tallies the return at five hundred dollars. Dated December 27, 1778, and subscribed by Traill, it documents the mechanics of revolutionary war finance at its smallest scale, where the new nation's credit was raised one ticket at a time through a chain of named local intermediaries.

pages.description: Recto, in a single hand: "United States Lottery, Class the Second, List of Tickets sold by Robert Traill of Easton." A column of twenty-five ticket numbers prefixed "gom" follows (one row obscured by losses at the fold), then the tally "25 Tickets, 20 Dollars each, 500 Dollars," signed "Rob. Traill, Easton, Decemr. 27, 1778." Page 1 of 2.

Verso, otherwise blank, with the recto's writing showing faintly in reverse through the paper. A short arithmetical calculation is jotted at the top, and an archival reference number is inked at the foot. Holes from the central fold are visible. Page 2 of 2.

creator: Robert Traill (lottery agent, Easton, PA)
notes: United States Continental Lottery, Class the Second, ticket sales list by Robert Traill, Easton, PA, December 27, 1778. 25 tickets, $500 total. Page 1 of 2.

title: United States Continental Congress Lottery Ticket, Philadelphia 1776
description: A printed ticket for the United States Lottery, the first national lottery, authorized by the Continental Congress at Philadelphia on November 18, 1776 to raise money for the war of independence. Drawn in successive classes, the scheme offered the bearer whatever prize fell against the ticket's number. This example belongs to the third class; the engraved heading and italic body type, framed by a decorative border and an ornamental engraved panel at left, lend the slip the formality of public paper. A manager's manuscript signature validates it. The verso carries later collector notes identifying it as a Continental Lottery ticket and remarking on its rarity. Surviving tickets are exceedingly scarce, making the document a tangible relic of how the new union improvised its earliest finances.

Ink signature "Jo: Budden" lower right; "S." lower left; ornamental border and engraved panel at left. Page 1 of 2.

title: Anglo-American Tontine Plan Prospectus
description: A printed prospectus, dispatched as a banker's circular letter, proposing "A Plan of a Tontine to consist of 1000 Shares of £100 each" divided into seven age classes whose dividends grow by survivorship. Shares are priced by the nominee's age, from £6 2s per £100 on lives under ten to £8 3s on lives above sixty, raising £6,679. Subscribers pay half to Messrs. Lockhart, bankers of Pall-Mall, by November 1789 and the balance by January 1790, naming each nominee. The annuities are secured on $600,000 of United States government funds (about £135,000 sterling at six per cent), vested in trustees Francis Baring, Edmund Boehm, and Thomas Henchman, plus £50,000 in British Three per Cent Consolidated Annuities. The scheme illustrates the close transatlantic credit ties binding Britain to the young United States soon after independence.

pages.description: The opening printed leaf sets out the tontine table: seven age classes with share counts, per-£100 prices, and class totals summing to £6,679. Following clauses fix the subscription deadlines, the half-yearly dividend dates, and the pledge of $600,000 in United States funds vested in the named trustees. Page 1 of 3.

The printed text continues on the left with further conditions—sale of the Three per Cent stock to cover early payments, return of subscriptions with four per cent interest if classes fail, forfeiture of unclaimed dividends, and the Dutch loan security at Amsterdam. The right bears a manuscript cover letter, "London 31 Aug.t 1789," to Messrs. Cobb & Son, Margate, soliciting their subscription, signed by Lockhart. Page 2 of 3.

The outer address leaf of the folded letter, directed in a large hand to "Mess.rs Cobb & Son, Margate," with a circular postal mark and traces of a wax seal. A docket reads "Po[st] Pa[id] Lockart, London Aug.t 31st / 1st Sept 1789." Printed lines show faintly through the paper. Page 3 of 3.

creator: Messrs. Lockhart (bankers); trustees Francis Baring, Edmund Boehm, Thomas Henchman
notes: Anglo-American Tontine prospectus and solicitation letter, 1789. Backed by $600,000 in US federal funds. Trustees: Francis Baring, Edmund Boehm, Thomas Henchman. Bankers: Messrs. Lockhart, Pall-Mall. Solicitor: James Fonnereau.

# Transcription

A PLAN OF A TONTINE to consist of 1000 Shares of £100 each, to be divided into SEVEN CLASSES, as follow; the Dividend of each Class to increase by Survivorship.

| | | £. s. | | £. |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| First | Class, on Lives under 10 Years of Age | 170 Shares | at 6 2 | for each 100 | 1037 |
| Second | Class, on Lives above 10 and under 20 Years | 170 Shares | at 6 4 | for each 100 | 1054 |
| Third | Class, on Lives above 20 and under 30 | 170 Shares | at 6 6 | for each 100 | 1071 |
| Fourth | Class, on Lives above 30 and under 40 | 140 Shares | at 6 10 | for each 100 | 910 |
| Fifth | Class, on Lives above 40 and under 50 | 150 Shares | at 7 0 | for each 100 | 910 |
| Sixth | Class, on Lives above 50 and under 60 | 120 Shares | at 7 7 | for each 100 | 882 |
| Seventh | Class, on Lives above 60 Years | 100 Shares | at 8 3 | for each 100 | 815 |
| | | 1000 Shares | | | £.6679 |

Every Subscriber to mention the Class in which he will subscribe, and to pay £.50 on every £.100 subscribed, into the Hands of the Treafers, Messrs. LOCKHART, Bankers, in Pall-Mall, on or before the First of November 1789; and the remaining 50 per Cent. to be paid on or before the First Day of January 1790, when the Name, Age, and Description of every Nominee shall be given, and the Deed of Trust executed.

The Annuities to be paid half yearly, at Messrs. LOCKHART's, the Treafers, to commence from the Time of Subscribing; and the First half-yearly Payment to be made on the Second Wednesday in February 1790, and the Second half-yearly Payment on the Second Wednesday in August following; and on the same Days in each ensuing Year.

To secure the regular Payment of the Annuities, Six HUNDRED THOUSAND DOLLARS, in the Funds of the United States of America, which, at the Par of Exchange, are equal to ONE HUNDRED AND THIRTY-FIVE THOUSAND POUNDS STERLING, bearing an Interest of Six per Cent. per Annum, will be vested in the Names of FRANCIS BARING, EDMUND BOEHM, and THOMAS HENCHMAN, Esquires, of London, in Trust, to receive the Interest on the fame, and apply it in Payment of the Annuities as aforesaid.

The aforesaid Funds to be legally vested in the said Trustees, and by them declared fo to be; and the Deed securing the Application of the Interest to the Payment of the Annuities, to be enrolled in one of his Majesty's Courts of Record at Westminster.

The Proprietors of the 600,000 Dollars will alfo place FIFTY THOUSAND POUNDS Capital Stock in the Three per Cent. Consolidated Annuities of Great-Britain, in the Names of the said Trustees,

**Left Page (Printed Text):**

Trustees, in Discharge of the First Fourteen half-yearly Payments; and so much of the said £50,000 "Three per Cent." shall be held by them, half-yearly, and paid to the Treasurers, as will pay the Sum of £3339 10s. the Sum that will be then due to the Annuitants.

In case all or either of the several Classes should not be completed by the First Day of *January* 1790, each Subscription shall be returned, with Interest after the Rate of Four *per Cent. per annum,* from the Time of Payment paid, unless such Class or Classes can be modified to the Satisfaction of the Parties concerned.

In case any Dividend shall remain unclaimed for the Space of Three Years, Notice shall be three Times inserted in the *London Gazette,* that such Dividend remains unclaimed; and unless the Parties entitled to receive the said Dividend shall claim the same within Three Months after such Notice, the said Dividend shall become forfeited, together with all further Claim to, or Interest in, the said Annuity; and the same shall be divided among, and continue to, the Survivors belonging to the Class wherein such Life was, after deducting the Expenses attending such Notice.

The 600,000 Dollars are on the same Security as the Loans made by the United States in *Holland,* which bear an Interest of Five *per Cent.* only; and the Capital Stock of those Loans now falls, on the Exchange at *Amsterdam,* at 94 *per Cent.*

If the Capital Stock of the United States should be paid off, the Sum being placed at Interest at Five *per Cent.* will produce nearly the annual Sum of £7000 Sterling.

Subscribers not completing their Second Payments on or before the First Day of *January* 1790, will forfeit their first Subscription.

title: The Decline and Fall of the English System of Finance
description: Thomas Paine's pamphlet "The Decline and Fall of the English System of Finance," written in Paris in 1796 and reprinted in Philadelphia the same year by John Page for Benjamin Franklin Bache. Paine argues that Britain's funded national debt and inconvertible paper bank notes are advancing toward collapse along a calculable, geometric path: each war heaps a new layer of debt and paper onto the last, accelerating depreciation until specie payment becomes impossible and the funding system ends in bankruptcy. Drawing on Adam Smith and figures for successive war expenditures, he predicts the failure of the Bank of England and contrasts the French and English systems. An appended convention speech on the constitution and Declaration of Rights follows. The prediction was partly borne out when the Bank suspended cash payments in 1797.

pages.description: Worn, water-stained paper front wrapper bearing only the title in plain type between ornamental rules; library call numbers inked at upper left. Page 1 of 23.

Title page: title, author ("By Thomas Paine, author of Common Sense, Rights of Man, Age of Reason"), Philadelphia imprint and 1796 date, with a parliamentary epigraph; facing the opening of the argument. Page 2 of 23.
Continuation: the funding system as a path to ruin; comparison of the English and French paper systems and how quantity of paper outruns gold and silver. Page 3 of 23.
Continuation: the national debt's growth across successive wars, listing the war years from 1697 onward, and introducing the debt's geometrical progression. Page 4 of 23.
Continuation: tabulating and computing the mounting expense of each war by a fixed ratio to illustrate the debt's accelerating increase. Page 5 of 23.
Continuation: projecting the cost of a future war and the impossibility of sustaining the funding system, citing Newton on ratios. Page 6 of 23.
Continuation: a table contrasting the first and second six wars (444 vs. 5042 millions); argument on funding, labour, and the price of paper. Page 7 of 23.
Continuation: how paper substituted for gold and silver, and the necessary acceleration of depreciation as government issues ever more notes. Page 8 of 23.
Continuation: depreciation as a slow "death" of the funding system; whether taxes paid in paper can be funded; on the right to demand cash. Page 9 of 23.
Continuation: the limited stock of gold and silver in England measured against bank notes; impossibility of paying all note-holders in cash. Page 10 of 23.
Continuation: paper and the impossibility of paying the funded debt; failures in France and parallels to the Bank of England's exposure. Page 11 of 23.
Continuation: bankruptcy as the system's natural end; Mr. Pitt and government appearances; nature as perpetually renewing while paper systems exhaust. Page 12 of 23.
Continuation: bankruptcy of a government compared to that of an individual; long footnote on the delusion of paper credit and bank notes. Page 13 of 23.
Continuation: the several ways bank money is forced into circulation, beginning with discounting; the bank's difficulty in redeeming notes. Page 14 of 23.
Continuation: bank notes versus the annual quantity of land and malt taxes; footnote on Adam Smith and notes payable to bearer. Page 15 of 23.
Continuation: how disposition of governments to issue paper differs in England and France; the funding system as a self-operating machine. Page 16 of 23.
Continuation: a second generation of creditors; quotes Adam Smith's "Wealth of Nations" on the scheme of Law; bank notes before 1755. Page 17 of 23.
End of the main pamphlet: summing the dependence of bank notes on credit; closing address to the British nation; signed Thomas Paine, Paris, April 8, 1796. Page 18 of 23.
Opening of the appended "Speech of Thomas Paine, as delivered in the Convention, July 7, 1795," on the constitution and Declaration of Rights. Page 19 of 23.
Speech continuation: vindication of the French Revolution; pledge to comment on the Declaration of Rights, beginning with property and labour. Page 20 of 23.
Speech continuation: articles on the Rights of Man and liberty; public revenue and the proportioning of taxes among citizens. Page 21 of 23.
Speech continuation: government and mercantile property; remarks on a citizen's duty, military service, and the family of the soldier. Page 22 of 23.
Final page (p.40): close of the speech, urging the constitution be revised to match the Declaration of Rights; Beinecke Library "American Tracts 1796" note. Page 23 of 23.

NOTHING, they say, is more certain than death, and nothing more uncertain than the time of dying; yet we can always fix a period beyond which man cannot live, and within some moment of which he will die. We are enabled to do this, not by any spirit of prophecy, or foresight into the event, but by observation of what has happened in all cases of human or animal existence. If then any other subject, such for instance as a system of finance, exhibits in

A

# Transcription

**Left Page (Page 2):**

its progress a series of symptoms indicating decay, its final dissolution is certain, and the period of it can be calculated from the symptoms it exhibits.

Those who have hitherto written on the English system of finance (the funding system) have been uniformly impressed with the idea of its downfall happening *some time or other.* They took, however, no data for that opinion, but expressed it predicatively, or merely as opinion, from a conviction that the perpetual duration of such a system was a natural impossibility. It is in this manner that Doctor Price has spoken of it; and Smith in his Wealth of Nations has spoken in the same manner; that is, merely as opinion without data. "The progress, says Smith, of the enormous debts, which at present oppresses and will in the long run *may probably ruin,* all the great nations of Europe, (though it might make some suppositions carried with it no conviction.

It is not my intention to predict any thing but I will throw from data already known, from hypotheses and facts, which the English funding system has already exhibited to, that it will not continue to the end of Mr. Pitt's life, supposing him to live the the age of a man. How much sooner it may fall, I leave to others to predict.

Let financiers diversify systems of credit as they will, it is nevertheless true, that every token of credit is a system of paper money. Two experiments have already been had upon paper money's the only one in both these cafes the America the other in France. In both cases the whole capital was emitted, and that whole capital, which in America was called continental money, and in France assignats, appeared in circulation; the consequence of which was, that the quantity became

**Right Page (Page 3):**

enormous, and to disproportioned to the quantity of population, and to the quantity of objects upon which it could be employed, that the market, if I may so express it, was glutted with it, and the value of it fell. Between *five and fix* years determined the fate of those experiments. The fame fate would have happened to gold and silver; could gold and silver could only have been iffued in the fame abundant manner as paper had been, and confined within the country as paper always is; by having no depreciation out of it; or, to speak on a larger scale, the fame thing would happen in the world, could the world be glutted with gold and silver, as America and France have been with paper.

The English system differs from that of America and France in this one particular, that its capital is kept out of light; that is, it does not appear in circulation. Were the whole capital of the national debt, which at the time I write this is about four hundred million pounds sterling, to be emitted in assignats or bills, and that whole quantity put into circulation, as was done in America and France, those English assignats, or bills, would sink in value as those of America and France have done with said bills in a greater degree, because the quantity of their would be more disproportioned to the quantity of population in England, than was the cafe in America or the other two countries. A nominal pound sterling in such bills would not be worth one penny.

But though the English system, by thus keeping the capital out of light, is preferred from [an] early destruction, as in the cafe of America and France, it nevertheless approaches the fame fate, and will arrive at it with the fame certainty, though by a flower progress. The difference is altogether in the degree of speed by which the two systems approach their

**Notable markings:** 

Page numbers visible at top: (2) and (3). Library stamp visible on right page. Binding margin visible on left side of left page.

# Transcription

**Left Page (4)**

fate, which, to speak in round numbers, is as twenty is to one; that is, the English system, at the time of giving the capital instead of idling it, contained within itself a capacity of enduring twenty times longer than the systems adopted by America and France; and at the end of that time it would arrive at the time common grave, the Potter's field, of paper-money.

The datum I take for this proportion of twenty to one, is the difference between a capital and the interest at five per cent. Twenty times the interest at five per cent. is equal to the capital. The accumulation of paper money in England is in proportion to the accumulation of the interest upon every new loan, and therefore the prospect to dissolution is twenty times closer than if the capital were to be emitted and put into circulation immediately. Every twenty years in the English system is equal to one year in the French and American systems.

Having thus stated the duration of the two systems, that of funding upon interest, and that of emitting the whole capital without funding to be as twenty to one, I come to examine the symptoms of decay approaching to dissolution, that the English system has already exhibited, and to compare them with similar symptoms in the French and American systems.

The English funding system began one hundred years ago; in which time there has been six wars including the war that ended in 1697.

1. The war that ended, as I have just said, in 1697.
2. The war that began in 1702.
3. The war that began in 1739.
4. The war that began in 1756.
5. The American war that began in 1775.
6. The present war that began in 1793.

**Right Page (5)**

The national debt, at the conclusion of the war, which ended in 1697, was twenty one millions and an half. (See Smith's Wealth of Nations, chapter on Public Debts.) We now see it approaching fast to four hundred millions. If between these two extremes of twenty one millions and four hundred millions, embracing the several expences of all the wars including wars, there exists some common ratio that will ascertain arithmetically the amount of the debt at the end of each war, as certainly as the fact is now known to be, that ratio will in like manner determine what the amount of the debt will be in all future wars, and will ascertain the period within which the funding system will expire in a bankruptcy of the government; for the ratio I allude to is the ratio which the nature of the thing has citabilitied itself.

Hitherto no idea has been entertained that any such ratio existed, or could exist, mine a problem of this kind, that is, that could ascertain, without having any knowledge of the fact, what the expence of any former war had been, what the expence of any future war would be; but it is nevertheless true that such a ratio does exist, as I shall shew, and also the mode of applying it.

The ratio I allude to is not in arithmetical progression, like the numbers

2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9,

nor yet in geometrical progression, like the numbers
2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256,

but is in the series of one half upon each preceding number; like the numbers

8, 12, 18, 27, 40, 60, 90, 135.

Any person can perceive that the second number

12, is produced by the preceding number 8, and half 8; and that the third number 18 is the number produced by the preceding number 12 and half 12, and fo on for the reft. The difference between the two firft numbers is but 4, but the difference between the two laft is 45; and from thence they may fee with what immense rapidity the national debt has encreafed, and will continue to encreafe, till it exceeds the ordinary powers of calculation, and lofes itfelf in cyphers.

I come now to apply the ratio as a rule to determine all the cafes.

I begin with the war that ended in 1697, which was the war in which the funding fyftem began. The expence of that war was twenty-one millions and an half. In order to afcertain the expence of the next war, I add to twenty-one millions and an half, the half thereof (ten millions and three quarters) which makes thirty one millions and three quarters for the expence of that war. This thirty one millions and three quarters, added to the former debt of twenty-one millions and an half, carries the national debt to 52 millions and three quarters. Smith, in his chapter on Public Debts, fays, the national debt was at this time 53 millions.

I proceed to afcertain the expence of the next war, that of 1739, by adding, as in the former cafe, one half to the expence of the preceding war. The expence of the preceding war was thirty-one millions and three quarters; for the laft numbers, fay 32 millions, the half of which (16) makes forty eight millions for the expence of that war.

I proceed to afcertain the expence of the war of 1756, by adding, according to the ratio, one half to

I proceed to afcertain the expence of the American war of 1775, by adding, as in the former cafes, one half to the expence of the preceding war. The expence of the preceding war was 72 millions, the half of which (36) makes 108 millions for the expence of that war. In the laft edition of Smith, (chapter on Public Debts) he fays, the expence of the American war was *more than an hundred millions*.

I come now to afcertain the expence of the prefent war, fuppofing it to continue as long as former wars have done, and the funding fyftem not to break up before that period. The expence of the preceding war was 108 millions, the half of which (54) makes one hundred and fifty-two millions for the expence of the prefent war. It gives fymptoms of going beyond this fum, fuppofing the funding fyftem not to break up; for the loffes of the laft year and of the prefent year, are twenty-two millions each; which exceeds the ratio compared with the loans of the preceding war. It will not be from the inability of procuring loans that the fyftem will break up. On the contrary, it is the facility with which loans can be procured, that haftens that event. The loans are altogether paper tranfactions, and it is the exceefs of them that brings on, with accelerating speed, that progreffive depreciation of funded paper money that will diffolve the funding fyftem.

Perhaps it may be asked, that as governments or ministers proceeded by no ratio in making loans or incurring debts, as nobody intended any ratio, or thought of any, how does it happen that there is one? I answer that the ratio is founded in necessity, and I now go to explain what that necessity is.

It will always happen that the price of labour or of the produce of labour, be that produce what it may, will be in proportion to the quantity of mo­ney in a country, admitting things to take their na­tural course. It is said in England that the value of paper keeps equal with the value of gold and silver. But the case is not rightly stated; for the fact is, that the paper has *pulled down* the value of gold and silver to a level with itself. How long this hanging together of money and paper will continue, makes a new query because it daily exposes the system to sudden death, independent of the natural death it would otherwise suffer. However disposed governments may be to wring money by taxes from the people, there is a limit to the practice established in the nature of things. That limit is the proportion between the quantity of money in a nation, be that quantity what it may, and the greatest quantity of taxes that can be raised upon it. People have other rules for money besides paying taxes; and it is only a proportional part of that money they can spare for taxes, as it is only a proportional part they can spare for house-rent, for clothings, or for any other particular use. proportions find out and establish themselves; and that with such exactness that if any one part exceeds its proportion, all the other parts feel it.

Before the invention of paper money (bank notes) there was no other money in the nation than gold and silver, and the greatest quantity of money that ever was raised in taxes, during that period, never exceeded a fourth part of the quantity of money in the nation. It was high taxing when it came to this point. The taxes in the time of William the third, never reached to four millions before the invention of paper; and the quantity of money in the nation at that time was estimated to be about fifteen millions. The same proportions established themselves in France. There was no paper-money in France before the present revolution, and the taxes were collected in gold and silver money. The highest quantity of taxes never exceeded twenty-five millions sterling; and the quantity of gold and silver money in the nation, at the same time, as stated by Mr. Necker from returns of coinage at the mints, in his treatise on the administration of the finances, was about ninety millions sterling. To go beyond this limit, of a fourth part, in England, they were obliged to introduce paper-money; and the attempt

The quantity of gold and silver stated by Lord Hawkesbury's secretary (George Chalmers) as I have before, therein, is twenty millions; and therefore the total amount of bank notes in circulation, all made payable on demand, is sixty millions. This enormous sum will astonish the most stupid stock-jobber, and overpower the credulity of the most thoughtless Englishman; but were it only the most thoughtless Englishman; but were it only a third part of it, that a fund the bank cannot pay half in the crown in the pound.

There is something curious in the movements of this modern complicated machine, the funding system to unfold it; and it is only now that it is beginning to unfold itself in the first part of its movements. In the first part of its movements it gives great powers into the hands of government, and in the last part it takes them completely away.

The funding system let out with raising revenues under the name of loans, by means of which government became both prodigal and powerful. It was soon discovered that loaning was going on at a prodigious rate, and though it was soon discovered that loaning was going on at a prodigious rate, jobbing, where pretended loaners, or the persons who purchased into the funds afterwards, conceived themselves not only to be creditors, but to be the only creditors.

duced, unperceived, a second generation of creditors, more numerous and far more formidable, and more real than the first generation; for every holder of a bank note is a creditor, and a real creditor, and the debt due to him is made payable on demand. The debt therefore which the government owes to individuals is composed of two parts; the one about four hundred millions bearing interest, the other about fifty millions payable on demand. The one is called the funded debt, the other is the debt due in bank notes.

This second debt (that contained in the bank notes) has, in a great measure, been incurred to pay the interest of the first debt; to that in fact little or no real interest has been paid by government. The whole has been delusion and fraud. Government first contracted a debt in the form of loans with one class of people, and then ran clandestinely into debt with another class, by means of bank notes, to pay the interest. Government acted of itself in contracting the first debt, and made a machine of the bank to contract the second.

It is this second debt that changes the feat of power and the order of things; for it puts it in the power of even a small part of the holders of bank notes (had they no other motive than difficult at Pitt and Grenville's edition bills) to control any measure of government they found to be injurious to their interest; and that not by popular meetings, or popular societies, but by the simple and ealy operation of with-holding their credit from that government; that is, by individually demanding payment at the bank for every bank note that comes into their hands. # SPEECH OF THOMAS PAINE.

As delivered in the Convention, July 7, 1795.
Wherein he alludes to the preceding Work.

ON the motion of Lanthenas, "That permission be granted to Thomas Paine, to deliver his sentiments on the Declaration of Rights and the Constitution, Thomas Paine attended; an opposition being made to the motion, one of the Secretaries, who stood by Mr. Paine, read his speech, of which the following is a literal translation:

CITIZENS,

The effects of a malignant fever, with which I was afflicted during a rigorous confinement in the Luxembourg, have thus long prevented me from attending at my post in the bosom of the Convention, and the magnitude of the subject under discussion, and no other consideration on earth, could induce me now to repair to my station.

A recurrence to the vicissitudes I have experienced, and the critical situations in which I have been placed in consequence of the French Revolution, will throw upon what I now propose to submit to the Convention, the most unequivocal proofs of my integrity, and the rectitude of those principles which have uniformly influenced my conduct.

In England I was proscribed for having vindicated the French Revolution, and I have suffered a rigorous imprisonment in France for having pursued a similar moit

C

mode of conduct. During the reign of terrorism, I was a close prisoner for eight-long months, and remained so above three months after the era of the 10th Thermidor. I ought, however, to state, that I was not persecuted by the people either of England or France. The proceedings in both countries were the effects of the despotism existing in their respective governments. But, even if my perfection had originated in the people at large, my principles and conduct would still have remained the same. Principles which are influenced and judged to the control of tyranny have not their foundation in the heart.

A few days ago I transmitted to you, by the ordinary mode of distribution, a short Treatise, entitled "Dissertation on the First Principles of Government." This little work I did intend to have dedicated to the people of Holland, who, about the time I began to write it, were determined to accomplish a Revolution in their Government,—rather than to the people of France, who had long before effected that glorious object. But there are, in the Constitution which is about to be ratified by the Convention, certain articles, and in the report which preceded it, certain points, to repugnant to reason, and incompatible with the true principles of liberty, as to render this Treatise, drawn up for another purpose, applicable to the present occasion, and under this impression I presumed to submit it to your consideration.

If there be faults in the Constitution, it were better to expose them now, than to abide the event of their mischievous tendency; for certain it is, that the plan of the Constitution which has been presented to you is not confilient with the grand object of the Revolution; nor congenial to the sentiments of the individuals who accomplished it.

To deprive half the people in a nation of their rights as citizens, is as easy matter in theory or on paper; but it is a moist dangerous experiment, and rarely practicable in the execution.

But

But the article of the Constitution to which I have just advertised proposes as the object of the tax, the public good, or in other words, the good of all, but a partial good, or the good only of a few; and the Constitution provides solely for the rights of this few, to the exclusion of the many.

The second article of the Declaration of Rights says:—

"The Rights of Man in society are Liberty, Equality, Security of his person and property."

But the article alluded to in the Constitution has a direct tendency to establish the converse of this position, inasmuch as the persons excluded by this provision can neither be laid to possess liberty, nor security against oppression. They are condemned justly to the caprice and tyranny of the rest.

The third article of the Declaration of Rights says:—

"Liberty consists in such acts of volition, as are not injurious to others."

But the article of the Constitution, on which I have observed, breaks down this barrier. In enabling the liberty of one part of society to destroy the freedom of the other.

Having thus pointed out the inconsistency of this article to the Declaration of Rights, I shall proceed to comment on that part of the same article which makes a direct contribution a necessary qualification to the right of citizenship.

A modern document on the object of public revenue has divided the taxes or contributions into two duties, the direct and the indirect, without being able to define precisely the distinction, or difference between them, because the effect of both is the same.

Those are designated indirect taxes which fall upon the consumers of certain articles, on which the tax is imposed, because the tax being included in the price, the consumer pays it without taking notice of it.

The same observation is applicable to the territorial tax. The land proprietors, in order to reimburse them-

**Right Page (37)**

selves, will rack-rent their tenants: the farmer, of course, will transfer the obligation to the miller, by enhancing the price of grain; the miller to the baker, by increasing the price of flour; and the baker to the consumer, by raising the price of bread. The territorial tax, therefore, though called *direct*, is in its consequence *indirect*.

To this tax the land proprietor contributes only in proportion to the quantity of bread and other provisions that are consumed in his own family. The defecit is furnished by the great mass of the community, which comprehends every individual of the nation.

From the logical distinction between the direct and indirect taxation, some emolument may result. I allow, to auditors of public accounts, &c. but to the people a large capital in their respective pursuits, can ever, according to this system, attain the pre-eminence of a citizen. On the other hand, any upstart, who has by speculation or management, got possession of a few acres of land, or a miserable tenement, may exultingly exercise the function of a citizen, although perhaps he neither possesses a hundredth part of the worth of property of a simple mechanic, nor contributes in any proportion to the exigencies of the state.

The contempt in which the old government held mercantile pursuits, and the obloquy that attached on merchants and manufacturers, contributed not a little to its embarrassments, and its eventual destruction; and, strange to tell, though the mischiefs arising from this mode of conduct are so obvious, yet an article is proposed for your adoption, which has a manifest tendency to restore a defect inherent in the monarchy.

I shall now proceed to the second article of the fame title, with which I shall conclude my remarks.

The second article says, "Every French soldier, who shall have served one or more campaigns in the cause of liberty, is deemed a citizen of the Republic, without any respect or reference to other qualifications."

It would seem, that in this Article, the Committee were desirous of extricating themselves from a dilemma into which they had been plunged by the preceding article. When men depart from an established principle, they are compelled to resort to trick and subterfuge, always shifting their means to preserve the unity of their objects; and as it rarely happens that the first expedient makes amends for the profusion of principle, they must call in aid a second of a more flagrant nature to supply the deficiency of the former.

In this manner legislators go on accumulating error upon error, and article upon article, until the mast becomes to bulky and incongruous, and their embarrassment to desperate, that they are compelled, as their last expedient, to resort to the very principle they had adopted.—The Committee were precisely in this predicament, when they framed this article; and to now I confess, their conduct appears specious rather than efficacious.

It was not for himself alone, but for his family, that the French citizen, at the dawn of the revolution (for then indeed every man was considered a citizen) marched soldier-like to the frontiers, and repelled a foreign invasion. title: Proposed Lottery Scheme, London
description: This printed broadside by Jacob Henriques of London, dated April 1753, proposes a new lottery scheme of 100,000 tickets at four pounds each, to be drawn by quadruple numbers so that 25,000 numbers will determine the lottery. The prize scheme offers 4 prizes of £10,000, 8 of £5,000, 40 of £1,000, 80 of £500, 340 of £100, 800 of £50, 2,000 of £20, and 12,000 of £10 — totalling 15,272 prizes worth £394,000 against 84,728 blanks, a ratio of only five and a half blanks per prize. A further £6,000 goes to the first and last drawn tickets. Twelve per cent is to be deducted from the prizes, paid in money or in 3-per-cent annuities, the proceeds going toward reducing the national debt — the document's stated public purpose, and the pitch by which Henriques, one of many private projectors of the age, sought Parliament's favour.
creator: Jacob Henriques
notes: Jacob Henriques, proposed lottery scheme broadside, London, April 1753. 100,000 tickets at £4 each.
transcription:
# Transcription

THO' I have already taken the Trouble and Pains to fettle the following Lottery Scheme of four Pound each Ticket, that they may have a Choice either for the prefent or the future yet I conceive the fame will be a Detriment to many People ; whereas my publifhed Guinea Lottery Scheme delivered laft Week, can hurt none, but will pleafe every body ; and though there are nine Blanks to a Prize, thofe who will take four Tickets will make it hard only to one and half Blanks a Prize ; befides, that to have one Ticket of four Pound, or four at one Guinea each, is four Chances to one ; and with four Guineas they have the Chance to 4000l. and be a prudent Pattern to fhew the Probability of my publifhed great Propofal for this Nations Welfare and Happinefs, which I have much at heart, &c.

SCHEME

100,000 Tickets (either more or lefs in equal Proportion) at four Pound each Ticket to depofit one Pound, and one Pound every Month afterwards, is 400,000l.

Thofe 100,000 Tickets to be drawn by quadruple Numbers, with quadruple Prizes, fo that 25000 Numbers will determine the whole Lottery, and fave Time, Trouble and Expence.

4 Prizes of     10000 each, is     40000
8               5000              40000
40              1000              40000
80              500               40000
340             100               34000
800             50                40000
2000            20                40000
12000           10                120000

15272 Prizes                amounts to £ 394000
84728 Blanks (is only five and half Blanks to a Prize)

100000 Tickets

For the firft Drawn at 500l. 2000
For the laft Drawn at 1000l. 4000

Amounts to £ 400000

Note, From the Prizes are to be deducted 12 per Cent. for the intended Purpofe, and to be paid either in Money, or in Annuities of 3 per Cent.

My Propofal for leffening the national Debt is real Good, and my Intention for it is ftill better, and if the Parliament and Government are pleafed for the Sake of their Community to encourage me with their prudent public Approbation, I will find out other great Matters for the Glory, Honour, and Welfare of his excellent Majefty and this Kingdom, and which is my chief Intention, moft earneft Defire, and humbly pray for it, &c.

London, April 1753.                JACOB HENRIQUES.

title: Ancient Papyrus Greek or Coptic Financial Document
description: A fragment of ancient papyrus, torn and holed, bearing several lines of cursive script in Greek or Coptic — the everyday administrative hand of Egypt under Ptolemaic, Roman, or Byzantine rule (roughly the third century BCE to the seventh century CE). The content cannot be read with confidence from the fragment, but documents of this form are typically financial or administrative: a tax receipt, grain account, loan agreement, or land lease. Papyri like this are the oldest surviving form of financial record-keeping in the Western tradition, among the earliest physical evidence of organised credit, taxation, and contractual obligation. It is in all likelihood the oldest item in the collection.
creator: Unknown (Egyptian administrative scribal office)
notes: Ancient papyrus fragment with cursive Greek or Coptic script. Likely a financial or administrative document (tax receipt, grain account, or land lease). Ptolemaic, Roman, or Byzantine Egypt, ca. 1st–7th century CE. Oldest item in the collection.
transcription:
# Fragmentary Egyptian Papyrus Document (Greek/Coptic Cursive) — Transcription

## Original:

Script: a rapid documentary cursive of the Greco-Roman/Byzantine Egyptian type. Several letterforms resemble Coptic rather than pure Greek majuscule, so the hand is best described as Greek or Coptic cursive; it cannot be assigned with confidence from this fragment.

Layout: the sheet is torn along the top and holed throughout, laid out in TWO COLUMNS of text. Each column preserves roughly 20–25 lines; the ink is faded and abraded over much of the surface. A modern cataloguer has drawn ink circles/underlines around a few individual words (an editorial annotation, not part of the ancient text).

Legibility: continuous reading is NOT possible. Only disconnected clusters of letters can be made out, and even these are uncertain. No complete word or phrase can be transcribed with confidence.

Tentative isolated letter-sequences (uncertain, offered only as scattered traces — not a running text):
- One line in the upper right column shows a cluster resembling: ο υ μ ν τ … ρ ε υ … (?) [uncertain]
- A circled word in the left column opens with what may be κ ε φ … / ⲕⲉⲫ … (?) [uncertain]

Everything else: [illegible]

Right column: [illegible — ~20–25 lines of cursive, mostly abraded]
Left column: [illegible — ~20–25 lines of cursive, mostly abraded]

---

## English Translation:

The text is not confidently legible. Beyond identifying it as a two-column documentary papyrus in a Greek/Coptic cursive hand, no reliable words or phrases can be read, and therefore no honest translation can be given. The catalogue classification (a Roman-period Egyptian financial/administrative document) is plausible for a papyrus of this form but cannot be confirmed from the surviving, abraded text itself.

title: New York Central Rail Road Company Bond
description: An engraved $1,000 bearer bond of the New York Central Rail Road Company, paying 6% annual interest, issued in August 1858 and later cancelled (struck with a red CANCELLED overprint). The sheet is worked in the fine steel-engraved manner of mid-century American bond production: an allegorical vignette of seated female figures with the national shield and eagle across the top, and a locomotive down the right margin — the railroad advertising its own modernity in the document's very ornament. The New York Central had been formed in 1853 by consolidating the lines linking Albany to Buffalo, and became a flagship of the Vanderbilt railroad empire. Its 6% coupon reflects the high returns American railroads were paying to draw capital in the 1850s; the cancellation marks redemption before the 1869 merger into the New York Central and Hudson River Railroad.
creator: New York Central Rail Road Company
notes: New York Central Rail Road Company. $1,000 bond, 6%. No. 1890. August 1858. CANCELLED.

**UNITED STATES OF AMERICA.**

**The New-York Central Rail Road Company.**

*Hereby certify, that the person to whom this Certificate is issued, being a Stockholder in one or more of the several Railroad Companies authorized to be consolidated into one great Organization, under the Act forming the main road, is entitled for the consideration herein stipulated, to the sum of* **ONE THOUSAND DOLLARS**, *to be paid out of the revenues, arising from the said Road, and the maintaining their Head Office at the city of Thirty years in the... [text continues with bond terms], with interest from that day at the rate of **SIX per cent per annum**, payable semi-annually on the last day of May, and the first day of November in each year, in the manner of the mystive Interest Warrants hereby apportioned. Upon equal and similar being payable in the City of New York. This Certificate is only transferable on the books of the company upon surrender of this Certificate, and in written assignment, under the Act forming the consolidation of certain Rail Road Companies pursuant to the resolution, and in accordance with such proviso. The whole thereof to the...*

*In testimony whereof, this Certificate, in pursuance of the order of the Board of Directors of the said New-York Central Rail Road Company, and in behalf of the officers even signed by the President and Treasurer of the said Company, the first day of August, in the year eighteen hundred and fifty-eight.*

title: Spanish Colonial Government Payment Order
description: A handwritten libramiento (treasury payment order) of the Spanish colonial Real Hacienda at Lima, dated July 1816, promising payment one year hence of 500 silver pesos (pesos fuertes) drawn against the Empréstito Patriótico — an emergency forced loan levied on the population of Lima to finance the royalist war effort during the South American wars of independence. It carries an extraordinary 25% annual interest, a measure of how dear money had become to a regime fighting for survival. The crowned Spanish royal arms and ornate penwork border at the head assert colonial authority on what is otherwise a manuscript instrument, and a later 1817 notation records further processing. Issued from one of the last strongholds of Spanish rule in South America — Peru would not gain independence until 1821 — it is a rare financial trace of the colonial order's final phase.
creator: Spanish Colonial Treasury, Lima (Tesorería de Lima)
notes: Spanish colonial libramento No. 444. Lima, Peru, October 1816. Empréstito Patriótico (forced loan). 500 pesos at 25% annual interest. Additional notation from 1817.
transcription:

title: Imperial Russian Internal Prize Loan
description: A printed bearer bond of the Imperial Russian 5% Internal Prize Loan of 1864, with a capital value of 100 rubles yielding an annual 5 rubles, set bilingually in Russian (Билетъ внутренняго 5% съ выигрышами займа) and German (Obligation der Russischen 5% Inneren Anleihe mit Prämien-Verlösungen). The Romanov double-headed eagle sits at the centre within finely engraved pink-and-green guilloche — imperial authority joined to the era's anti-counterfeiting ornament. A prize loan (заём съ выигрышами) married a fixed coupon to periodic lottery draws for large cash prizes (выигрыши), a device European governments used to make state debt appealing to small and middling savers. The parallel German text shows the loan was meant to circulate in German financial markets as well as at home.
creator: Imperial Russian Government
notes: Imperial Russian Government Internal 5% Prize Loan 1864. 100 rubles. Series 15871, No. 45. Bilingual Russian/German. Lottery prize-bond format.

title: Thomas C. Jenkins, Wholesale Dealer in Flour & Groceries Invoice to Offutt & Son (Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania)
description: A printed billhead invoice of Thomas C. Jenkins — styling itself "The Largest Flour and Grocery House in the World" — issued at Pittsburgh on 19 December 1902 to Offutt & Son. The engraved letterhead is a piece of corporate boosterism in its own right: a commanding perspective view of the firm's multi-storey "Checkered Front" warehouse on Penn and Liberty Streets, wreathed in advertising for flour, teas, roast coffees, and tobaccos, with a discount table and the insistence "No Goods Sold Families… No Retail Store." The handwritten lines below bill two lots of plug tobacco. As a strictly wholesale house disclaiming any retail trade, Jenkins exemplifies the specialised distribution networks that organised American food commerce in the Gilded Age, when the printed billhead doubled as a tool of corporate self-promotion.
creator: Thomas C. Jenkins (Wholesale Dealer in Flour & Groceries)

title: Holladay Overland Mail and Express Company Blank Check, Virginia City, Montana Territory
description: An unused bank check of the Holladay Overland Mail and Express Company, printed for issue at Virginia City, Montana, and payable on order in dollars. The form is blank: the payee, sum, and date all remain open, the printed date reading only "186_". It bears the imprint of John E. Russell, Esq., 35 William St., New York, as Treasurer and Agent of the company, and was engraved by Slote & Janes of Fulton Street, New York. Ben Holladay's enterprise was the foremost stagecoach and express carrier of the trans-Mississippi West in the 1860s, drawing its revenue from federal mail contracts; Holladay sold it to Wells, Fargo & Co. in 1866. The two vignettes advertise that business and its frontier setting: a galloping mail coach at right, and at left a trapper with a seated companion and dog amid the wilderness the line professed to bridge.
creator: Holladay Overland Mail and Express Company

title: States of Gelderland (Quarter of Zutphen) Redeemable Bond, Arnhem, c. 1647-1650
description: This printed parchment bond, with its day and year completed by hand, is a redeemable obligation of the Quarter of Zutphen within the province of Gelderland. It opens with the formula of Willem, Prince of Orange (Willem II), as Stadholder, together with the Raden acting in the name of the Heeren Staten, and recites a resolution taken at the Landdag held at Zutphen in April 1644. It invokes the late Frederick Henry, Prince of Orange, described as of laudable memory and "in leven mede Stadtholder, Heer Vader van ons Stadtholder" (in life also Stadholder, father of our Stadholder), which places the actual issue after his death in March 1647. The body promises to pay interest on the principal at six per hundred (6%) yearly, from year to year, until redemption (aflossinge), securing both capital and interest, the sum reckoned in guilders of twenty stivers each. Given at Arnhem and sealed with the great seal countersigned by the Court's clerk, the instrument shows a provincial States borrowing against its tax revenues: the credit of the assembled Estates, not the prince personally, stands behind the loan. The text is unadorned, dominated by an ornate woodcut initial; no art-historical movement is named.
creator: Prince of Orange-Nassau (Willem / Frederick Henry)
notes: Archaic Dutch princely bond/obligation. "Y VVILHELM BY DER GRATIE GODES, Prince van [?]..." References FREDERICUS HENRICX (Frederick Henry), Prince of Orange. Tresorier General and Rentmeester noted. Mid-17th century Dutch Golden Age fiscal document.

Y VVILHELM; BY DER GRATIE GODES, Printze van Oraingnen, &c. Stadtholder, mitgaders die Raeden uyt den naem van de Heeren Staten des Vorltendoms Gelre ende Graeffschapszutphen, Doen condt in crachtc deses, Alfoo 't den wel gedachten Heeren Staeten besieft heelt te revolueeren op den Landstach in April. 1644. binnen de Stadt Zutphen gehouden, bij dadius ernde wiltoude notice Horde Representaten ende Hooghgebooreri Vorst ende Heere FREDERIJCK HENRIJCK, Printze van Oraingnen, &c. Hooghilingdeste memoiris, in leven mede Stadtholder, Heer Vader van ons Stadtholder, gedaert den 5. vande voornoemste Maert Aprils, datmen tot meesten wellantende reddingihe van tien voorze: Vorstendtom ende Graelschap bij jegenwooordige gelegenheyt, doeh lichten ende op Intereele negotieteren is decstecke sommen van penningen, om bekesede geène plooyel te woorden, soo begaunce vau der Landdschap noodirijche [schulden, die laoten, nacnder verhaclt by de voovle: uytspraccke, ende tic voornm le Resolutie daer op gevolght vande wel gemelde Heeren Staten. Soo beken wij in krachte defs dat [unclear] op huyden dato onderschieven in goeden gauckbaren gelde [unclear] Summe van [unclear] sijnde uyt dier voorn [unclear] twintig luyfers Stuck. Ende willemde mits dien de voorn: [unclear] erven, ofte Actie van hebbende tot

[*Second paragraph continues with similar archaic Dutch text regarding financial obligations, interest rates, and conditions of repayment*]

Willende dat delen onsen brief, Vidimus ofte Copie authentijcq, mitgaders alle gnatie op eenigh Comptoir des voornoemden Vorltendoms ende Graeffschap van ons, voor deselve reyle met quítantie, ende dat na quitantie allenlijck, soo dícslls als 't van noodis is [unclear] gedienden sal, welcke beleenigden, Lieuetennlter generale [unclear] Vorltendom ende Graeffschaps, ofte dien fulcx anders te iallen geleyst sal mogén weelen, in uytgaue sonder [swarigheyt] al worden geleelen; Alles sonder argh of lijst; In oorkundé hebben wij 't groote Segel gedachtes Vorltendoms en de Graeffschaps onder jegenwoardt ze verschryvinse laere hangen, ende deelve door des Hoves Guiffiet doen beteckenacn. Gegeven Arnhem den [date] [unclear]

title: Kingdom of Sweden Royal Loan Bond (Amsterdam, 1785)
description: A printed sovereign loan bond of King Gustavus III of Sweden, opening with the royal style "Wy GUSTAVUS, door Gods Genade Koning van Zweeden, der Gothen en Wenden." The Crown raises Twee Millioenen Guldens, or acht maal honderd Duizend Rijksdaalders Hollandsch Courant Geld (two million guilders, equal to 800,000 Rijksdaalders Holland current money), divided into four obligations each of 200,000 Rijksdaalders, bearing interest at five per cent yearly payable the first of December, redeemable after the first year on six months' notice. The loan is secured on the effects and revenues of the Crown and ranks after an earlier loan of 800,000 Rijksdaalders. It is contracted at Amsterdam through the houses Hope en Compagnie and Grand en Compagnie, granted under the king's hand at Gripsholm Castle on 20 October 1784 and countersigned by J. Liljencrants. This Dutch sheet is a sworn translation, made at Amsterdam on 9 February 1785 by the sworn translator Johannes Vergeel, of a French original. The instrument shows how Gustavus III drew on the Amsterdam capital market, then the chief lender to northern courts, to fund a treasury strained by his policies. The text is unornamented, its authority resting on the engrossed royal style and the seal rather than on pictorial device.
creator: King Gustav III of Sweden
notes: Swedish royal loan bond, King Gustav III. 400,000 Rijksdaalders Hollandsch Courant. Amsterdam, February 9, 1782. Issued through Amsterdam money market. Johannes Verge, agent/translator. Gustav III borrowed extensively in Amsterdam to finance Swedish policy.
transcription:
# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Wy GUSTAVUS, door Gods Genade Koning van Zweeden, der Gothen en Wenden &c. &c. &c., Erfheer van Noorwegen, Hertog van Schleswyck, Hollstein, Stornarn en Ditmarschen, Grave van Oldenburg, Delmenhorst &c. &c.

Doen te weeten: Dat wy, goedsgevonden liebbende ten welzyn van onze Staten een Leening te doen van Twee Millioennen Gouldens, of achter maal honderd Duizend Ryksdalders Hollandsch Courant Geld, geeevende tot zeekerhood alle de Effecten en Revenuen van de Kroon, welke niet reeds zyn verpyptheked en Speciaal en Speciaal Hypotheken van onze Douanen en Tolien, aan de Heeren Hope en Compagnie en Fizzaux, Grand en Compagnie, te Amsterdam, laet gegeven hebben en dezelve uit krachte van deze tegenwoordige, Volkomen gegeven om voor ons en ons Konigryk dezelve Leening te protesteren.

En vermits wy van bovengemeelde Heeren tot ons volkomen genoegen en ten behoeve van ons Koninge, onvermijdelijk hebben eene Somma van Acht maal Honderd Duizend Ryksdalders Hollandsch Courant geld, om aan one te worden geleend, tegen Een Interest van vier op een half ten Honderd Jaarlykx, en voor den tyd gelijk bepaald in de vier Obligatien van outs Staats Comptoir, mitsgader groot Tweemal Honderd Duizend Ryksdalders Hollandsch Courant geld, aestalpsed by de voorzeiide Heeren als Speciale Hypotheek van diezse Leening, welke Somma van Achtmaal Honderd Duizend Ryksdalders Hollandsch Courant geld, aan ons ons Konigryk deduijdig is geleourneerd geworden.

Zoo beschlieten wy ons, voor ons en onze Erfgenaamen en Opvolgers in het Koningryk Zweeden, om niet alleen aan opgemeide Heeren te Amsterdam, allie zes Maanden te doen betaalen de Interesten van Honderdachtig Speciaal en Speciaal Hypotheken van onze Achtmaal Honderd Duizend Ryksdalders Hollandsch Courant geld, tegen vier en een half ten Honderd Jaarlykx, maar ook het geheele Capitaal op de vervaldy der deselve binnen Twaalf Jaren zyn en bekomen en der Eersten December aanstaande, te doen reimbourseeren, ten zy niet gezamentlyk overeesteentiming mogte worden goedsgevonden, den Termyn van het Zwelf Reimbourseement te prolongeeren, in welke laast geval zulks zes Maanden na vorren zal worden geclareerd; ons echter als Faculteits voorschuldende op deze Leening naar verloop van de Eerste Jil-jaarlyksch geslachts de doen reimbourseeren.

Tellens peranderen wy mits deze aan opgemeide Heeren, om de voorgemeide Hooftsomma in Oost-indien met inbcoe Commercie te verdeelen, en dezelve onder hunne Handelsbreng aan wien zy zullen goedsviniden te vergoedereeu, mits deselve Obligation door eene bepecijd Openbaar Notaris geheel geclareerd is, zondige dezelve in zolanig geval van dezelve kraft en van arde zon dese tegenwoorrige Hoofd Obligatie, zodatnde, dat aan de Eigenars of Honders van dezelve Obligatien van zes les Maanden en de Comptounie wij bovengemeilde Heeren zal worden betaald de Interest tegen vier en een half en Honderd Jaarlykx, en by expiratie van der vervaldy der Leening, het Capital zelve, ten waare der nog verguns zal Literaire Prolongatie moghe zyn overeeustcoming. Alles zonder eenige korting.

De zekerheid van het Reimboursfement dezer Geldleening, zo wel ten opzichte van het Capitaal als de Interest, verhinden en verlyphothequeeren wy alle de Effecten en Revenuen van ons Kroon, vry en ontlangen van alle verhypothekeerde, van wat naam en Hoofdligheid deselve ook mogen zyn en Speciale Inkonsten van onze Douanen en Tolien, en dart ook de Effecten van Honders van deselve Obligatien van ons Staats Comptoir, heden groot Tweemal Honderd Duizend Ryksdalders, Hollandsch Courant geld, Authoriteride die meestgeslede Heeren, hunne Eigenamen en Opvolgers in hun Interest op Plaats en Termyn voorschreeven, als dan direct de iemaidzul besluiting van het Capitaal der Interest op Plaats en Termyn vastgestesl en vergoedgeslesd tot de Cozurende van het gunt hen verzehijdinge zal zyn.

Na dat het Capitaal en de Interest van ons tegenwoorige Hooftsobligation door een goedheel zullen zyn Geremboursseert, zo zal deselve Obligation van ons Staats Comptoir voor dezid Leening Speciaal en Speciaal verpythothequeert, aan ons en onze Kroon, vry en ontlangen van alle verbintenisse nog gemijdt, moeten word te rug gegeven, en alle geente meestgeladhe Heeren zullen nodig vinàen by deze der Leening Negotiatie, het beste in voordsche vond Koningryk, overeeenkomltig onze hier voren uitgeveelde Intentie, te reglulieren.

Ten Ofkondes heaervan, hebben wij dezen aan opgemeide Heeren Authorities der Obligation gemaijljk heeten geteckend, en oorkonde gesteeld, zegul dan doen Appoloren.

Gedain op het Konigkl Griphold, dezen Twintighden Oktober des Jaars Een Duizend Zeven Honderd twee en Tachtig.

[Signature mark] GUSTAF. (L.S.)

(Lager was gctekend)
I. LILL. IENCRANTS.

Accordeerd met het Fransch Origineel geteekend No. 2155, de varietur.

Amsterdam den 9 February 1785.

JOHANNES VERGEEL Lic. Zk.
Beleidge Translateur.

---

## English Translation:

We GUSTAVUS, by the Grace of God King of Sweden, of the Goths and Wends, &c. &c. &c., Hereditary Lord of Norway, Duke of Schleswig, Holstein, Stormarn and Dithmarschen, Count of Oldenburg, Delmenhorst, &c. &c.

Know Ye: That we, having deemed it good for the welfare of our States to contract a Loan of Two Million Guilders, or in other terms Four Hundred Thousand Rijksdaalders in Dutch Currency, granting as security all the Effects and Revenues of the Crown, which are not already pledged and specially hypothecated, and specifically the Hypothecation of our Customs and Tolls, to the Messrs. Hope and Company and Fizzaux, Grand and Company, in Amsterdam, have given and do hereby give in the force of this present [instrument], fully given for us and our Kingdom to guarantee the same Loan.

And whereas we have received from the aforesaid Messrs. to our full satisfaction and for the benefit of our Kingdom, unavoidably a Sum of Eight Hundred Thousand Rijk

title: Potosian Land Grant (Reverse)
description: This is the reverse of a Potosian Land Grant certificate conveying 400 acres, issued in London circa 1825 by a British company promoting mineral and land ventures in the Potosí region following Spanish America's wars of independence. The certificate denominated landholding in acres rather than in pounds, packaging speculative title to distant ground as a transferable instrument; the engraved face here serves chiefly to carry handwritten endorsements and initialled signatures by which the grant passed from hand to hand. The printed heraldic device dominates: a crowned shield charged with a saltire, flanked by two rampant horses and a pair of flags, set above the Latin mottoes NESCIUS VINCI ('not knowing how to be conquered') and PRO LIBERTATE DEFENDENDA SOCIORUM ('for defending the liberty of the partners'). The borrowed chivalric arms lend a fictive antiquity and respectability to a venture floated amid the London mania for South American mining shares.
creator: Potosian Land Grant Company (London)
notes: Potosian Land Grant, reverse/back side of 400-acre certificate. Shows printed header with coat of arms and handwritten endorsements. Complements front face (goetzmann0506). London, ca. 1825. British speculative investment in Bolivian Potosí region.

title: Bourbon Princes in Exile Loan Certificate, Amsterdam, 1793
description: This is a bond certificate raising a loan in the name of the exiled French Bourbon princes. Its authorization, given at Hamm in Westphalia on 25 September 1793 and dated to the first year of the King's reign, bears the names of Louis Stanislas Xavier (the future Louis XVIII) and Charles Philippe, Comte d'Artois, countersigned by the Maréchal Duc de Broglie as Garde du Trésor Royal. The Amsterdam house of J. Bourcourd and the widow F. Croese & Comp. acknowledges, on behalf of the Princes of France, receipt of een duizend guldens Hollands Courant (f. 1,000 Dutch current guilders) for a share in the obligation, with coupons attached. Drawn at Amsterdam on 28 September 1793, the instrument shows the royalist cause, deprived of French revenues after the execution of Louis XVI in January 1793, seeking credit in the deep capital market of the Dutch Republic. The plain engraved text, free of heraldry or vignette, lends the émigré loan the sober appearance of an ordinary Amsterdam obligation.
creator: Louis Stanislas Xavier (Louis XVIII); Charles Philippe (Comte d'Artois); Maréchal Duc de Broglie
notes: French émigré princes' bond No. 34, f. 1,000 Dutch Guilders. Authorized by Louis Stanislas Xavier (future Louis XVIII) and Charles Philippe (Comte d'Artois). Duc de Broglie, Hamm en Westphalie, September 25, 1793. Amsterdam, J. Bourcourd & Wedowe F. Croese & Comp., September 28, 1793. French Royalist exile finance.

title: Ricardo & De Lara Forward Contract for British Consolidated Stock
description: This printed-and-completed forward sale contract, in Dutch, is issued on a form supplied by the brokers Ricardo en De Lara, on the Weesperveld over het Werkhuis, Amsterdam. The undersigned acknowledges having sold £4,000 sterling capital of the Geconsolideerde 3 pct. Annuities of England (British Consols) at a price of 59½, these being remainders of the dividend (Uitdeling) issued the previous January, for delivery in London at the coming August Rescontre (settlement) at the named price. Intervening dividends accrue to the buyer and are deducted from the price; all terms follow the Reglement of December 1803. Done at Amsterdam, 13 May 1805, with the seller's signature. The form documents Amsterdam's role as a forward market in British government debt during the Napoleonic wars, when Consols traded near 59 and Dutch capital priced and dealt in London stock across the North Sea. The sheet is plainly utilitarian: an engraved italic heading and ruled lines, no allegory, the commercial instrument as its own ornament.
creator: Ricardo & De Lara (Amsterdam)
notes: Amsterdam forward contract for British Consolidated Stock (Consols). Ricardo en De Lara, No. 70 Amsterdam. Governed by December 1803 Reglement. Amsterdam, May 13, 1805. Ricardo family Amsterdam firm; Anglo-Dutch financial intermediation.
