We undersigned J. BOURCOURD and Widow E: CROESE & COMP., as thereto, just as in the aforementioned Obligation further stated, authenticated hereby, acknowledge for the account of the Royal Highnesses the Princes of France, to have received from the hands of

This countersigned and noted in the Protocol, [illegible] belonging through me the signed Shares not exceeding the number of Twelve Thousand Numbers. And the Letters Patent dated 7 September 1793, together with the-Original Obligation of date 25 September 1793 have been deposited and [illegible] remaining in my custody.

By order of their Royal Highnesses.

title: Russian Imperial Treasury Note
description: This is a short-term interest-bearing Treasury note of the Imperial Russian State Treasury ("Билетъ Государственнаго Казначейства") for fifty rubles, dated 1915, the year repeated in roundels flanking the central text. Printed in green and black, it carries a 4% annual rate stated in the lower corners and declares itself valid until 1 August 1929 ("Билетъ дѣйствителенъ по 1 Августа 1929 г."). A column of four detachable coupons at left, numbered five through eight and each worth one ruble, provides for periodic interest payments falling due in 1919. The note bears the engraved double-headed imperial eagle and the signatures of the Manager of the State Treasury, the Director, and a bookkeeper. Such казначейские билеты were issued to fund the Imperial government as the strains of the war of 1914 deepened its fiscal deficits; the dense engraved acanthus and heraldic framing project the authority of a regime that would fall before the note matured.
creator: Imperial Russian State Treasury
notes: Russian Imperial Treasury Note, 50 rubles, 4%, No. 352814, 1915. Valid until August 1, 1929. With four interest coupons. Never redeemed due to collapse of Imperial government in 1917.

title: Kingdom of Greece 2½% Guaranteed Gold Loan Bond, Athens, 1898
description: A bearer bond of the Kingdom of Greece (Βασίλειον της Ελλάδος), issued for the gold-guaranteed loan of 1898: Δάνειον εις Χρυσόν Ηγγυημένον 2½% 1898, headed in French along the border as Emprunt Hellénique Garanti 2½% Or de 1898. The certificate carries a face value of 2,500 drachmas in gold and a coupon of 2½ per cent. Printed in Athens and authenticated in London, it bears the signature of the Greek Minister of Finance and a counter-signature for the Governor and Company of the Bank of England, the loan's paying agent. The 2½ per cent gold loan followed Greece's defeat in the war with the Ottoman Empire of 1897 and the sovereign suspension of 1893; it was floated under the International Financial Commission of foreign powers, whose guarantee—reflected in the modest coupon—is the document's true subject. The engraved frame, with Greek-key borders, the royal arms beneath a crown, and draped classical figures, asserts Hellenic continuity even as the terms record a treasury placed under outside supervision.
creator: Kingdom of Greece (Ministry of Finance); guaranteed by Bank of England
notes: Kingdom of Greece Gold-Guaranteed Loan bond, 2½% 1898, 2,500 drachmas, No. 30,731. International guarantee; authenticated by Bank of England. Issued under International Financial Commission supervision following Greek default of 1893.

**(French terms):**
Le capital et les intérêts de cet emprunt sont à jamais affranchis de tout impôt hellénique.
Le porteur a droit à un capital de 2,500 fr. produisant un intérêt annuel de 2 1/2 p. o/o, payable par semestre, les 1ᵉʳ avril et 1ᵉʳ octobre de chaque année. Ces intérêts se prescriront par cinq ans.
L'amortissement de l'emprunt s'effectuera au pair, par voie de tirages opérés les 1ᵉʳ janvier et 1ᵉʳ juillet de chaque année, ou au moyen de rachats sur le marché.
Le premier tirage aura lieu le 1ᵉʳ janvier 1903. Le capital des titres amortis se prescrira par 30 ans. Jusqu'au 1ᵉʳ avril 1918 il ne pourra être procédé ni à des remboursements anticipés, ni à la conversion de l'emprunt.
Si le service de l'emprunt ne peut être assuré par les ressources prévues à l'article 21 du décret royal du 22 avril / 4 mai 1898, la garantie collective et solidaire de la France, de la Grande-Bretagne et de la Russie s'exercera suivant les clauses et conditions de la convention conclue à Paris les 17/29 mars 1898, entre ces trois Puissances et la Grèce.
La garantie est autorisée en France par la loi du 8 avril 1898 et le décret du 28 mai suivant, en Angleterre par la loi du 1ᵉʳ avril 1898 et en Russie par la ratification impériale de la convention en date du 28 mars / 9 avril 1898.

**(English terms):**
The capital and interest of this loan are exempt for ever from every Greek tax.
The Bearer is entitled to a capital sum of £100 with interest at the rate of £2:10s. per cent. per annum payable half-yearly on the 1ˢᵗ April and 1ˢᵗ October in each year. Coupons not presented within five years of their due dates will be forfeited.
The redemption of this loan will be effected at par, by means of drawings which will take place on the 1ˢᵗ January and 1ˢᵗ July in each year, or by means of purchases in the market. The first drawing will take place on the 1ˢᵗ January 1903. Drawn bonds not presented within thirty years will be forfeited. Until the 1ˢᵗ April 1918, no repayment in advance may be made nor can the loan be converted until that date.
If the resources prescribed by article 21 of the Royal Decree of the 22 April/4ᵗʰ May 1898 are insufficient for the service of the loan, the joint and several guarantees of France, Great Britain and Russia will become operative under the clauses and the conditions of the Convention concluded in Paris, the 17ᵗʰ/29ᵗʰ March 1898, between the three Powers and Greece.
The guarantee is authorized in France by the law of the 8ᵗʰ April 1898 and the decree of the 28ᵗʰ May following; in England by the law of the 1ˢᵗ April 1898, and in Russia by the Imperial Ratification of the Convention of March 28ᵗʰ/April 9ᵗʰ 1898.

**(Russian terms):**
Капиталъ и проценты сего займа освобождаются навсегда отъ всякихъ греческихъ налоговъ. Владѣлецъ сей облигаціи имѣетъ право на сумму въ девятьсотъ тридцать семь рублей пятьдесятъ копѣекъ, приносящую 2 1/2 процента годоваго дохода, выплачиваемаго по полугодно, 1 Апрѣля и 1 Октября н. ст. каждаго года. Симъ процентамъ присвоивается пятилѣтняя платежная давность. Погашеніе сего займа будетъ совершаться по нарицательной цѣнѣ путемъ тиражей, производимыхъ 1 Января и 1 Іюля н. ст. каждаго года, или путемъ выкупа по биржевой цѣнѣ. Первый тиражъ будетъ произведенъ 1 Января н. ст. 1903 г. Погашеннымъ облигаціямъ присвоивается тридцатилѣтняя платежная давность. До 1 Апрѣля н. ст. 1918 г. не можетъ быть приступлено ни къ досрочному выкупу, ни къ конверсіи сего займа.
Въ случаѣ, если бы расходы по уплатѣ процентовъ и погашенія по займу не могли быть покрыты изъ источниковъ, предусмотрѣнныхъ § 21 Королевскаго Декрета 22 Апрѣля (4 Мая) 1898 г., совмѣстная и солидарная гарантія Россіи, Франціи и Великобританіи вступитъ въ силу согласно условіямъ конвенціи, заключенной въ Парижѣ 17 (29) Марта 1898 г. между сими тремя Державами и Греціей. Гарантія сего займа установлена: въ Россіи Высочайшей ратификаціей вышеозначенной конвенціи, послѣдовавшей 28 Марта (9 Апрѣля) 1898 г., во Франціи закономъ 8 Апрѣля н. ст. 1898 г. и декретомъ 28 Мая н. ст. 1898 г. и въ Великобританіи закономъ 1 Апрѣля н. ст. 1898 г.

Bearer Bond of 2,500 francs / a bond for £100 (interest £2 10s. per cent) / a bond of 937 roubles 50 kopecks to the bearer.

**Terms of the loan** (identical in all four languages):
The capital and interest of this loan are exempt for ever from every Greek tax. The bearer is entitled to a capital of 2,500 francs (£100 / 937.50 roubles) yielding annual interest of 2½ per cent, payable half-yearly on 1 April and 1 October each year (New Style); interest not claimed within five years is forfeited. The loan is redeemed at par by drawings held on 1 January and 1 July each year, or by purchases on the market. The first drawing will take place on 1 January 1903. The capital of drawn bonds is forfeited after thirty years. Until 1 April 1918 there may be no early repayment nor any conversion of the loan.

Should the service of the loan not be covered by the revenues provided under article 21 of the Royal Decree of 22 April / 4 May 1898, the joint and several guarantee of France, Great Britain and Russia will take effect according to the clauses and conditions of the Convention concluded at Paris on 17/29 March 1898 between the three Powers and Greece. title: Romania National Defense Fund 4% Army Equipment Bond (Bon pentru Înzestrarea Armatei), 500 Lei, Bucharest, 1940
description: This is a 4% interest-bearing bond (bon) of 500 lei issued by Romania's Casa Autonomă a Fondului Apărării Naţionale — the Autonomous Administration of the National Defense Fund — under the title "Bon pentru Înzestrarea Armatei" (Bond for the Equipping of the Army), Series A. The text records that it was issued through the Autonomous Administration for Financing and Amortization on the basis of the Council of Ministers' Journal No. 296 of 6 February 1940 and was to be redeemed at par on 1 March 1945, with principal and interest exempted from all present and future taxes, dues, and state or communal levies. Issued at Bucharest in 1940, the bond financed an urgent rearmament drive as the kingdom braced against the territorial pressures that would soon cost it Bessarabia and northern Transylvania. The ornament is emphatically national: dense Romanian folk-revival interlace and grapevine borders, the royal arms beneath a crown, and three vignettes — a machine-gun crew, a tank, and a military aircraft — that picture the modernization of all three services the loan was meant to fund.
creator: Casa Autonomă a Monopolului Apărării Naţionale (Romania)
notes: Romanian Army Equipment Bond, Series A, No. 361,551, 500 lei, 1940. Issued to finance military rearmament. Due March 1, 1945.

title: American & British Securities Company Common Stock Certificate, Delaware, 1923
description: This engraved certificate records one hundred shares of common stock in the American & British Securities Company, a corporation organized under the laws of Delaware. The shares are issued "without nominal or par value," a then-novel form that the printed text takes pains to explain, and the document is overprinted with the word COMMON in bold red letters set diagonally across its face to distinguish it from the company's preferred issue. The text affirms that each preferred and each common share carries a single vote at stockholders' meetings, and that the holders of preferred stock enjoy priority in dividends. Dated 1923 and signed by the secretary and president, the certificate belongs to the wave of Anglo-American investment holding companies chartered in the years after the First World War, when Delaware's permissive corporate law and the closer financial ties between London and New York encouraged such vehicles. The restrained steel-engraved border, in the conventional banknote manner, signals security and probity rather than any particular artistic program.
creator: American & British Securities Company
notes: American & British Securities Company common stock certificate, 100 shares, No. 18, issued to Hattie J. Barnhill, September 1919. Incorporated in Delaware.
transcription:
# Transcription

**No. 18                                                                                                           100 Shares**

## AMERICAN & BRITISH SECURITIES COMPANY
INCORPORATED UNDER THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF DELAWARE.

This Certifies that **Hattie J. Barnhill**

is the owner of **ONE HUNDRED (100)** shares of COMMON STOCK of AMERICAN & BRITISH SECURITIES COMPANY, each share without nominal or par value, fully paid and non-assessable, transferable only on the books of the corporation, in person or by attorney, upon the surrender of this certificate properly endorsed.

The holders of preferred stock are entitled to receive on or before the first day of each annum, payable semi-annually in each year, at such times as the board of Directors shall determine, and which said dividends shall be in preference to and shall be payable before any dividends shall be apart on paid upon the common stock. The dividends due upon the preferred stock semi-annually from the date of issue to and including September 15th, 1919, shall not be cumulative, but thereafter, to-wit, after September 15th, 1919, dividends at the rate of seven per cent. (7%) per annum on any deficiency in any of the dividends to be paid on the preferred stock on or before October 15th, 1919, shall be made good out of the earnings of subsequent years before dividends shall be paid upon the common stock.

In the event of a dissolution or liquidation of the corporation or a voluntary or involuntary, or upon any distribution of its capital, there shall be paid to the holders of the preferred stock the par value thereof, to wit, one hundred dollars ($100) per share, and also any assets distributed among the holders of the common stock, after payment to the holders of the preferred stock of its accrued and unpaid, accrued and accumulated dividends as above provided, the remaining funds of the corporation shall be divided among and paid to the holders of the common stock, according to the shares held by each respectively.

The corporation shall have the option of redeeming the preferred stock, in whole or in part, at any time after issue, on any semi-annual dividend period, by payment to the holders thereof of the sum of one hundred dollars ($100) per share for each share of such preferred stock so redeemed, together with all accrued and accumulated dividends thereon, upon thirty days actual or published notice to the holders thereof.

Each share of preferred stock and each share of common stock outstanding shall be entitled to one vote each at all meetings of stockholders for any purpose. The common stock shall have no nominal or par value, and each share of the same shall be entitled in the event of the sale or other disposition of all or substantially all of the assets of the corporation to vote thereon, subject to the payment of all sums due and payable upon the preferred stock, cumulative or non-cumulative according to their nature.

IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the corporation has caused this certificate to be signed by its duly authorized officers, and its corporate seal to be hereunto affixed this _____ day of _____________, 19___.

**Edward P. Johnson**                                                                                              [illegible signature]
Secretary                                                                                                                    President

title: American Sugar Company 5.44% Cumulative Preferred Stock Certificate, Delaware, 1967
description: This engraved certificate represents twenty fully-paid and non-assessable shares of the 5.44% Cumulative Preferred Stock of the American Sugar Company, a Delaware corporation, each share carrying a par value of $12.50. Printed in brown with a steel-engraved central vignette and dated November 6, 1967, it is registered to the brokerage house Shearson Hammill & Co. Incorporated and countersigned by First National City Bank as registrar. The fixed 5.44% cumulative dividend marks it as a senior, income-oriented instrument, its modest par value and "less than 100 shares" notation reflecting the postwar diffusion of equity among many small holders. The vignette's three idealized classical nudes — an aged figure with globe and instruments, flanked by youths bearing a trident-like fork, cogwheel, and sheaf — render commerce, industry, and agriculture in a Beaux-Arts academic idiom, lending the refining concern the dignity of allegory.

title: American Trust Company Capital Stock Certificate (Boston)
description: This engraved capital-stock certificate of the American Trust Company, incorporated under the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts in 1881, certifies that Harriette S. Foster holds nine shares of the company's capital stock, transferable on the books of the company in person or by attorney upon surrender of the certificate. Issued at Boston and dated the twenty-fourth of January, the instrument is printed in green on white with a tinted security ground. A red diagonal overprint, "NOT OVER NINE SHARES," guards against fraudulent alteration of a small holding. The certificate embodies the fiduciary banking that flourished in late nineteenth- and early twentieth-century Boston, where trust companies pooled deposits and administered estates. Its dominant motif, a bald eagle with outspread wings perched upon a rocky crag, asserts national solidity and commercial confidence in the manner of period American financial engraving.

title: Anglo-Argentine Tramways Company Debenture Stock Certificate (reverse), London, 1910
description: This is the reverse of a £20 5% Debenture Stock Certificate of the Anglo-Argentine Tramways Company Limited, a British-registered firm operating the tramway network of Buenos Aires. Printed in red, it reproduces a dense "Extract from the Trust Deed, dated 15th June, 1910," setting out the conditions of the stock, the trustees' powers, and stockholders' rights, followed by a "Certificate of the Registration of a Mortgage or Charge" under the Companies (Consolidation) Act, 1908. That certificate records a charge securing the whole £1,500,000 debenture issue, registered at London on 20 June 1910 and signed by F. Atterbury, Registrar of Joint Stock Companies. The sheet documents the legal machinery behind Edwardian Britain's export of capital to South American infrastructure: a London-floated, mortgage-secured loan against distant tramway assets. The engraved scrollwork frame and Bradbury, Wilkinson & Co. imprint signal the period's standard high-security financial printing.
creator: Anglo-Argentine Tramways Company Limited

title: Act of Parliament, 8 George I (1722): paying off Exchequer Bills and ease to the South-Sea Company
description: A printed Act of the Parliament of Great Britain passed in the eighth year of the reign of King George I, headed "Anno Octavo Georgii Regis" and printed at London by John Baskett in 1722. Framed as an act for paying off and cancelling one million pounds of Exchequer Bills and for giving ease to the South-Sea Company, it ranks among the principal financial measures following the collapse of the South-Sea scheme of 1720. Its many clauses provide for circulating and exchanging Exchequer Bills against the Sinking Fund, regulate dealings of the South-Sea and East-India Companies, charge the South-Sea Company with annuities, prescribe penalties for counterfeiting bills, appropriate supplies for the army, navy, garrisons and Chelsea Hospital, grant debentures to planters of Nevis and St. Christopher, and lay new duties on imported apples and pictures.

pages.description: Title page: "Anno Regni GEORGII REGIS Magnae Britanniae, Franciae, & Hiberniae OCTAVO," with the royal arms and "G R" cypher. London, printed by John Baskett, 1722. Page 1 of 37.
Page 299: opening of the Act, headed "Anno Octavo Georgii Regis," with title and recital of paying off one million in Exchequer Bills and giving ease to the South-Sea Company. Page 2 of 37.
Pages 300-301: continuation of the Act, on circulating and exchanging Exchequer Bills over a seven-year term at fixed daily interest rates. Page 3 of 37.
Pages 300-301 (facing): further clauses on exchanging bills for ready money in the Bank's lawful coin and supporting their currency. Page 4 of 37.
Pages 302-303: continuation, on interest payable to the South-Sea Company and security charged on the surplus and Sinking Fund. Page 5 of 37.
Page 303 (facing): further provisions on the annuity to the South-Sea Company and repayment of principal from the Sinking Fund. Page 6 of 37.
Page 306: continuation, citing the act enabling the South-Sea Company and the East-India Company stock and fund. Page 7 of 37.
Pages 306-307: further clauses on discharging Exchequer Bills, the one million sum and interest charged on supplies. Page 8 of 37.
Page 308: continuation, on application of monies for repaying and cancelling Exchequer Bills out of the supplies. Page 9 of 37.
Page 309: further provisions for cancelling bills, with public notice at the Royal Exchange and in the London Gazette. Page 10 of 37.
Page 310: continuation, on burning or defacing cancelled bills and the joint currency period of the South-Sea Company. Page 11 of 37.
Page 308 (facing): clauses on circulating Exchequer Bills, money charged on the surplus, and authority of officers. Page 12 of 37.
Page 311: continuation, on the principal sum of one million, interest at five per cent, and quarterly payments dated from June 1722. Page 13 of 37.
Pages 312-313: further provisions for raising one million sterling on the surplus and Sinking Fund to make good the bills. Page 14 of 37.
Page 313: continuation, on issuing new Exchequer Bills, their currency and discharge against the Sinking Fund. Page 15 of 37.
Page 314: clauses requiring receivers and collectors to accept Exchequer Bills in payment of revenue, taxes and duties; remedy for refusal. Page 16 of 37.
Page 315: continuation, on interest on bills, exchanges, and dates from which interest is reckoned. Page 17 of 37.
Page 316: further clauses on bills lost or defaced, accounts kept by receivers general, and inspection of books. Page 18 of 37.
Page 317: continuation, on filling up new bills to replace defaced ones; penalties for counterfeiting Exchequer Bills. Page 19 of 37.
Page 318: provisions on counterfeit bills and endorsements, felony without benefit of clergy, and discharging the principal. Page 20 of 37.
Page 319: continuation, on cancelling discharged bills and interest reckoned to the time of repayment. Page 21 of 37.
Page 320: further clauses on bills defaced or destroyed, security given to the King, and contracts for circulating bills. Page 22 of 37.
Page 321: continuation, on the contractor's allowance, rate per cent for circulating bills, and the comptroller's office. Page 23 of 37.
Pages 322-323: provisions on the contractor's allowance not exceeding a set rate, and money lent to the South-Sea Company on the Sinking Fund. Page 24 of 37.
Page 324: continuation, citing revenues settled on the Civil List and lottery tickets, and loans by the East-India Company and Bank of England. Page 25 of 37.
Page 325: further clauses on payments, allowances and discharges by tallies and orders, charged on the Sinking Fund. Page 26 of 37.
Page 326: continuation, on tallies and orders to be cancelled, and annuities purchased and transferred at the Exchequer. Page 27 of 37.
Page 327: provisions for raising money for the Sinking Fund, purchasing annuities, and a clause touching the Land-Tax act of this session. Page 28 of 37.
Page 328: continuation, on Land-Tax interest, charges allowable, monies by way of lottery, and naval services. Page 29 of 37.
Page 329: appropriations for the Navy, victualling, wages, the Ordnance, Chelsea Hospital, land forces, and garrisons at Minorca and Gibraltar. Page 30 of 37.
Page 330: continuation, on garrisons at Annapolis Royal, Placentia and Gibraltar, half-pay to officers, and regulation of payments. Page 31 of 37.
Page 331: further provisions on half-pay to reduced officers of dragoons and foot, and duties distributed among proprietors of forfeited estates. Page 32 of 37.
Page 332: clauses granting debentures to planters of Nevis and St. Christopher for losses by the French invasion, at four per cent interest. Page 33 of 37.
Page 333: continuation, on redemption of these debentures from the Sinking Fund, with principal and interest payable from June 1721. Page 34 of 37.
Page 334: further provisions on assigning the debentures, registering assignments at the Exchequer, and a new additional duty on imported apples. Page 35 of 37.
Page 335: continuation, on the additional duty on imported apples per bushel, and new duties on imported pictures graded by size and dimension. Page 36 of 37.
Page 336: conclusion, on duties on imported pictures payable in ready money, drawback on re-export, with the duties ceasing with the act; ends "FINIS." Page 37 of 37.

creator: Great Britain. Parliament
notes: Printed by John Baskett, Printer to the Kings most Excellent Majesty, and by the Assigns of Thomas Newcomb, and Henry Hills, deceas'd. London, 1722. Acts of the Eighth Session of Parliament begun 17 March 1714, continued by prorogation to 19 October 1721.

GEORGII
REGIS
*Magna Britanniæ, Francia, & Hibernia,*
OCTAVO.

At the Parliament Begun and Holden at *Westminster,*
the Seventeenth Day of *March, Anno Dom.* 1714.
In the First Year of the Reign of our Sovereign
Lord GEORGE, by the Grace of God, of *Great
Britain, France,* and *Ireland,* King, Defender of
the Faith, *&c.*

And from thence Continued by several Prorogations to the Nineteenth Day of

# Transcription

**Anno Octavo**

**Georgii Regis.**

An Act for Paying off and Cancelling One Million of Exchequer Bills, and to give Ease to the South-Sea Company, in respect of its present Obligation, to circulate or contribute towards Circulating Exchequer Bills; and to give further Time to that Company for Repayment of One Million, which was lent to them; and for issuing a further Sum in New Exchequer Bills, towards His Majesties Supply, to be Discharged and Cancelled, when the said Company shall repay the Million owing by them; and that the Exchequer Bills, which are to Continue, may be Circulated at sale and moderate Rates; and for Appropriating the Supplies granted to His Majesty in this Session of Parliament; and for Relief of the Sufferers at Nevis and Saint Christophers, by an Invasion of the French in the late War; and for laying a further Duty on Apples Imported; and for Ascertaining the Duties on Pictures Imported.

**WHEREAS** amongst divers Matters and Things contained in an Act of Parliament, made and passed in the Sixth Year of Your Majesties Reign; Intituled, An Act for Enabling the South-Sea Company to increase their present Capital Stock and Fund, by Redeeming such Publick Debts and Incumbrances as are therein mentioned; and for raising Money to be applied for Lessening several of the Publick Debts and Incumbrances; and for Calling in the present Exchequer Bills remaining Uncancelled; and for making forth New Bills in lieu thereof, to be Circulated and Exchanged upon Demand at or near the Exchequer: It was recited or mentioned, That after the Application of such Monies as are therein specified, the Principal Monies

That no Person shall have of receive any Part of the same, who was a Pinor under the Age of Sixteen Years, at the time when the Regiment, Troop, or Company in which he served, was reduced.

That no Person shall have of receive any Part of the same, except such Persons who did actual Service in some Regiment, Troop, or Company.

That no Person having any other Place of Employment of Profit, Civil or Military, under His Majesty, shall have or receive any Part of the half-pay.

That no Chaplain of any Regiment or Troop, or Company, who has any Ecclesiastical Benefice or other Preferment in Great Britain or Ireland, shall have or receive any Part of the said half-pay.

**336          Anno Regni octavo Georgii Regis.**

**Which Duties shall be paid Down in Ready Money by the respective Importers of such Plates, from time to time, before Landing of the same respectively.**

And be it Enacted by the Authority aforesaid, That the said Duties upon Plates Imported, shall be raised, levied, recovered, and paid, and be brought into the Exchequer, by such Rules, Maps, Means, and Methods, and under such Penalties and Forfeitures, and in such Manner and Form, as the Duties upon Plates hereby taken away, or any of them, might have been raised, levied, recovered, and paid, if the same or any of them had continued.

**How appropriated.**

Provided always, and it is hereby Enacted by the Authority aforesaid, That the Duties to arise upon the Importation of Plates, pursuant to this Act (the necessary Charges of Management excepted) shall be appropriated and applied, as near as may be, to the same Uses and Purposes, to which the former Duties upon Plates by this Act taken away, were applicable or ought to have been applied if this Act had not been made.

**How redeemable.**

Provided also, and it is hereby Enacted, That the Duties by this Act charged upon Plates Imported, or any part thereof, shall be redeemable by Parliament, in the time and Manner as the said former Duties on Plates hereby taken away, or any of them, were redeemable; and in all Cases where any of the said former Duties on Plates were to cease or determine, a proportional Part of the Duties on Plates hereby charged, shall likewise cease and determine.

title: Acts of Parliament, 8th year of George I, London, 1722
description: This leaf is the blank verso of the title page of a printed volume of the Acts of Parliament of Great Britain, gathered for the eighth year of the reign of King George I ("Anno Regni Georgii Regis... Octavo"). Nothing is printed on this side; what one sees is the recto's letterpress showing through in mirror image, a familiar artefact of impression on hand-laid rag paper. The reversed text records that the Parliament was begun at Westminster on the seventeenth day of March 1714, in the first year of the reign, and continued by prorogations to the nineteenth of October 1721, the eighth session. The imprint names John Baskett, printer to the King's most excellent Majesty, with the assigns of Thomas Newcomb and Henry Hills, London, 1722, the date the volume was printed. The royal arms, bearing the motto "Dieu et mon droit," appear faintly through the sheet. Such sessional volumes made the realm's statutes public, the legal scaffolding within which Crown finance, chartered trade, and the post-South-Sea credit settlement operated.
creator: Great Britain. Parliament
notes: Printed by John Baskett, Printer to the Kings most Excellent Majesty, and by the Assigns of Thomas Newcomb, and Henry Hills, deceas'd. London, 1722. Acts of the Eighth Session of Parliament begun 17 March 1714, continued by prorogation to 19 October 1721.

The faintly visible text includes:

**Anno Regni
GEORGII REGIS
Octavo.**

[Additional body text is present but too faint and reversed to read with confidence]

Below this is a coat of arms or royal seal impression.

title: Anno Regni Georgii II Regis Vicesimo Quarto: An Act for raising money by Exchequer Bills to pay off the South Sea Annuities
description: A printed Act of the Parliament of Great Britain from the twenty-fourth year of King George II (1750), set in roman and black-letter type by Thomas Baskett, the King's Printer, in London. Running from page 79 to the closing "FINIS" on page 95, the Act empowers the King to raise specified sums by Exchequer Bills charged on the Sinking Fund, in order to pay off the Treasury's Old and New South Sea Annuities and to admit subscribers to the Bank of England and South Sea Company. It fixes interest at four pounds per centum per annum, regulates the form, registration, and circulation of the Exchequer Bills, and directs the application of the Sinking Fund to their discharge. The text marks a late stage in the long unwinding of the South Sea Company debt, roughly three decades after the Bubble of 1720.

pages.description: Title page. "Anno Regni Georgii II. Regis... Vicesimo Quarto," with the royal arms and G R cypher; Parliament begun at Westminster 10 November 1747, continued to 17 January 1750. London, Thomas Baskett, 1750. Page 1 of 18.

Page 79. Heading "Anno vicesimo quarto Georgii II. Regis" opens the Act for raising sums by Exchequer Bills charged on the Sinking Fund to pay off Old and New South Sea Annuities and admit Bank of England subscribers. Page 2 of 18.
Page 80. Continuation of the Act, reciting the National Debt annuities at four pounds per centum, redeemable amounts, and powers to charge further sums on the Sinking Fund. Page 3 of 18.
Page 81. Continuation reciting unsubscribed annuities and principal sums payable from the Sinking Fund and duties, with detailed figures for the lottery and South Sea annuities. Page 4 of 18.

Page 82. Continuation: Exchequer Bills to be issued for the sums to be advanced, at three pounds per centum interest, with directions to the Commissioners of the Treasury and the Bank of England. Page 5 of 18.
Page 83. Continuation specifying the reduced principal sums, interest at three pounds per centum, and directions for payment out of the Receipt of Exchequer. Page 6 of 18.
Page 84. Continuation on the form, denominations (not under five pounds), printing, and numbering of the Exchequer Bills, signed by appointed officers. Page 7 of 18.
Page 85. Continuation: counterfeiting and forgery of the bills, their circulation as cash, and authority to the Treasury Commissioners to settle their form and currency. Page 8 of 18.
Page 86. Continuation governing exchange, renewal, and payment of the Exchequer Bills, premiums, and charges on the Paymaster appointed by the Treasury. Page 9 of 18.
Page 87. Continuation directing charges of making and circulating the bills to be met from the Sinking Fund, with provisions for the Paymaster and incidental expenses. Page 10 of 18.
Page 88. Continuation enabling a further sum of 200,000 pounds at three pounds per centum on Old and New South Sea Annuities, advanced by the Treasury and charged on the Sinking Fund. Page 11 of 18.
Page 89. Continuation authorising the further three pounds per centum interest, registration of the bills numbered arithmetically, and directions for their issue. Page 12 of 18.
Page 90. Continuation directing repayment of principal and interest from the Sinking Fund; recital concerning the South Sea Annuities and powers to admit subscribers. Page 13 of 18.

Page 91. Continuation at four pounds per centum, empowering the South Sea Company to subscribe up to roughly 2,272,000 pounds of Old and New South Sea Annuities, with dividends among proprietors. Page 14 of 18.
Page 92. Continuation on transfer of the capital stock of annuities, dividends, purchase at market price, and registration of transfers under hand and seal before witnesses. Page 15 of 18.
Page 93. Continuation with provisos on oaths, the declaration and subscription required by the Bank of England charter, and conduct of General Courts and Courts of Directors. Page 16 of 18.

Page 94. Continuation reciting named persons who subscribed Old and New South Sea Annuities under a prior Act (28 February 1749), listing sums such as Charles Apthorp of Boston and others. Page 17 of 18.
Page 95. Conclusion listing remaining subscribers and sums, closing the recital of those who accepted the terms by 30 May 1750. Ends "FINIS." Page 18 of 18.

creator: Great Britain. Parliament
notes: Printed by Thomas Baskett, Printer to the King's most Excellent Majesty, and by the Assigns of Robert Baskett. London, 1750. Acts of the Fourth Session of Parliament begun 10 November 1747, continued by prorogation to 17 January 1750.

title: Baltimore and Ohio Railroad Company Common Stock
description: This engraved certificate conveys one hundred shares of common stock, par value one hundred dollars each, in the Baltimore and Ohio Railroad Company, a corporation incorporated by the State of Maryland on February 28, 1827. The certificate was issued at Baltimore to Evans, Stillman & Co. and dated June 19, 1934. The B&O ranks among the earliest American railroads, and by the 1930s it stood as one of the great eastern trunk lines; that this share changed hands in 1934 places it squarely in the depths of the Depression, when railroad securities traded under heavy strain and railroads struggled with collapsing freight traffic and fixed debt. The vignette across the top depicts an early scene of the line: a primitive steam locomotive and open passenger cars running beside horse-drawn carriages along the rails, a wide bay and town behind, recalling the railroad's pioneering origins and lending the modern instrument a heritage of priority.
creator: Baltimore and Ohio Railroad Company
notes: Baltimore and Ohio Railroad Company, Common Stock, 100 shares, No. C243475, Evans Stillman & Co., June 19, 1934
transcription:

is the owner of

*One Hundred Shares of the Perpetual Preferred Letters and half paid and assessable of the Common Capital Stock in and Ohio Railroad Company transferred to and standing on the books of the Company in the name of [the holder hereof upon and subject to all the conditions upon which the same were issued and to all assessments and calls made thereon. The holder of Preferred Capital Stock is the owner of Forty Million Dollars ($40,000,000) now issued and such additional amounts as may be lawfully issued from time to time by the Board and Directors of said Railroad Company the property and franchise and rights granted to receive in each year out of the surplus net profits of the Company for the current year such yearly dividend (commutative as the Board of Directors of said Railroad Company may declare) to be not exceeding [for per centum] upon all dividends then declared. No transfer of said stock shall be valid until endorsed by or under the direction of the Transfer Agent. The holder of Preferred Capital Stock is the owner of Forty Million Dollars ($40,000,000) now issued and such additional amounts as may be lawfully issued from time to time by the Board and Directors of said Railroad Company.*

Dated: **JUN 19 1934**

title: Baltimore and Ohio Rail Road Company Preferred Stock
description: This engraved certificate conveys sixty shares of the Six Per Cent. Preferred Stock of the Baltimore and Ohio Rail Road Company, each share carrying a par value of $100, and belonging to the second series of that stock. Issued at Baltimore and dated September 7th, 1875, it rests on an act of the General Assembly of Maryland passed at the January session of 1868, the same authority under which the shares are marked "Guaranteed, May 25th, 1868"—a guarantee meant to reassure holders of a fixed six-per-cent return. The text speaks to a railroad financing its post-war expansion through preferred capital that ranked ahead of common stock in dividends. The central vignette of a steam locomotive hauling cars across the landscape advertises the line itself, the oldest common-carrier railroad in the country, as the engine securing the investor's claim.
creator: Baltimore and Ohio Rail Road Company
notes: Baltimore and Ohio Rail Road Company, 6% Preferred Stock, No. 466, ca. September 1863
transcription:
# Baltimore and Ohio Rail Road Company Stock Certificate - Transcription

**TITLE AND HEADER:**
The Baltimore and Ohio Rail Road Company.
Six Per Cent. Preferred Stock.
Shares

**MAIN TEXT:**

This is to Certify, That A. Gregg & Co. [handwritten names]
are entitled to [handwritten number] Sixty [unclear] Shares
in the Joint Purse of the Preferred Stock of the BALTIMORE AND OHIO RAILROAD COMPANY, being a part of the Twenty Thousand Shares of the said Preferred Stock, which were authorized by a general meeting of the Stockholders of the said Company, held on the eleventh day of May, in the year Eighteen Hundred and Sixty Eight, under and by virtue of the provisions of an Act of the General Assembly of Maryland, passed at the January Session, Eighteen Hundred and Sixty Eight, enacting a substitute for Article Twenty-Six, of the Public General Laws of the said State, relating to Corporations; upon which the said [handwritten] A. Gregg & Co.
and [unclear] assigns are entitled, under all the terms of the creation of the said second series of said Preferred Stock, and of the agreement made by the subscribers thereto, and of the guarantee of the said Company, dated on the 25th day of May, in the year Eighteen Hundred and Sixty Eight, to a perpetual annuity of six per centum per annum, and so long as the same shall be payable out of the Net Income of the Stock and Profit in each and every year, before any dividend is declared to any of the Stockholders of the said Company. When the holders of the Preferred Stocks thereof, or their assigns, in the order in which the said Preferred Stocks were issued, it being hereby expressly understood to be the condition of the issue of this Certificate, and of the guarantee aforesaid, that the holder of this Certificate, and their assigns shall not be entitled to any dividend, of any nature or description, declared upon the Stock of the said Company, other than the said dividend of six per centum per annum, and no more, guaranteed as aforesaid, to be paid upon said Preferred Stock; and that the Stock evidenced by this Certificate is held subject to the conditions of its original issue, and of the subscription thereto, and of the guarantee thereof. The said Stock is transferable on the Books of the Company on or before [unclear] with an assignment endorsed thereon.

**WITNESS,** the Seal of the Company, attested by the Signature of the Treasurer thereof.

Baltimore, Sept. 7th, 1863 [handwritten date]

**SIGNATURES:**
[Illegible] Treasurer [handwritten signature]
[Illegible] President [handwritten signature]

Kell & Fitt, Print, Baltimore.

No. 466

title: Banca Românească Share Certificate, Bucharest, 1938
description: This engraved bearer share certificate (titlu la purtător) represents one share (una acțiune) of 500 lei nominal value in Banca Românească, a société anonyme whose subscribed capital is stated at 350,000,000 lei. Dated Bucharest 1938 and issued under the bank's eighth emission, it belongs to one of Romania's principal commercial banks, founded in 1911, which channelled credit into the country's agriculture, trade, and industry through the interwar years. The allegorical vignettes carry the document's argument: a robust standing peasant and a pastoral group with grazing flocks frame the text, while a steamship and a busy quay anchor the lower border—imagery that binds the bank's promise of return to Romania's soil and its export trade. The dense engraved guilloche border serves both ornament and anti-counterfeiting, the visual idiom of early twentieth-century security printing.
creator: Banca Românească
notes: Banca Românească, 500 lei share, Emisiunea VIII-A, Capital 350,000,000 lei, Bucharest 1938

title: Banca Românească Share Certificate
description: A bearer share (titlu la purtător) for one share of 500 lei nominal value in Banca Românească, a joint-stock company (societate anonimă) with a share capital of 160,000,000 lei, issued at Bucharest in 1920. The certificate dates from the immediate aftermath of the First World War, when the kingdom, enlarged by the union of Transylvania, Bessarabia, Bukovina, and the Banat, recapitalised its leading domestic bank to finance commerce and production across the newly enlarged realm. The engraved frame, in an academic allegorical manner, sets a standing labourer with his tool beside a working harbour against a seated figure amid the fruits of the harvest, while a steamer crosses the lower border—a programme that binds agriculture, labour, and seaborne trade to the bank's promise of national prosperity.
creator: Banca Românească

title: Banco Territorial de Cuba (Crédit Foncier Cubain), Acción Beneficiaria
description: This is a bearer beneficiary share (Acción Beneficiaria al Portador) of the Banco Territorial de Cuba, styled in French as the Crédit Foncier Cubain, a sociedad anónima domiciled in Havana with a paid-up capital of $5,000,000 in gold dollars of the United States of America. Issued at Havana on the 1st of March 1911 under Cuban laws of 20 July 1910 and 21 February 1911, the certificate carries text in Spanish, French, and German, signalling the European capital behind this mortgage bank. As a beneficiary share, it conferred a claim on profits rather than a fixed par value, the founders' instrument by which promoters retained an interest in a land-credit institution serving the young republic. The vignette pairs the Cuban arms and flags with a view of Havana and a yoked ox-cart, binding sovereign standing to the agrarian wealth the bank was meant to mobilise.
creator: Banco Territorial de Cuba
notes: Banco Territorial de Cuba (Crédit Foncier Cubain), Acción Beneficiaria No. 22752, Havana, March 1, 1911

CREADO POR LAS LEYES DE LA REPÚBLICA DE CUBA | CRÉÉ PAR LES LOIS DE LA REPUBLIQUE DE CUBA

**Capital Stock Information:**
CAPITAL STOCK FULLY PAID UP: $5,000,000 | EINGEZAHLTES STAMMKAPITAL:
IN GOLD OF THE UNITED STATES CURRENCY. | $5,000,000 IN DER GOLDWÄHRUNG DER
INCORPORATED UNDER THE LAWS OF JULY 20TH 1910 | VEREINIGTEN STAATEN VON AMERIKA.
AND FEBRUARY 21ST 1911. | GEGRÜNDET NACH DEN GESETZEN DER REPUBLIK KUBA
DIVIDEND SHARE OF STOCK ISSUED TO BEARER. | VOM 20.JULI (BEZW.AM 21.FEBRUAR 1911.
| TRÄGER-GEWINNANTEILSCHEIN.

**Main Body Text (Spanish - Left Column):**
"Este título representa una división de los 5,000 Beneficiarios de la Sociedad Anónima por acción Beneficiaria al Portador inscrita en el Registro Mercantil de la Isla de Cuba.

El tenedor de preferencia de este Título recibirá anualmente de entre los beneficios netos de la Sociedad, después de haber distribuido los dividendos ordinarios a las acciones ordinarias de la Sociedad, la parte que le corresponda en la cantidad que anualmente distribuya la Sociedad entre sus accionistas Beneficiarios.

Lo tenedor gozará de las mismas derechos y privilegios que confiere este Título a cualquiera otro tenedor, salvo que la transferencia de este Título se hará de conformidad con los términos que expresamente se requieren en los estatutos de la Sociedad."

**English Translation (Center Column):**
"This certificate represents one of the 5,000 Beneficiary Dividends of the Société Anonyme per Beneficiary Share to bearer registered in the Commercial Register of the Island of Cuba.

The holder of preference of this Title shall receive annually from among the net profits of the Company, after having distributed ordinary dividends to the ordinary shares of the Company, the part that corresponds to it in the amount that annually distributes the Company among its Beneficiary shareholders.

The holder shall enjoy the same rights and privileges that this Title grants to any other holder, except that the transfer of this Title shall be made in accordance with the terms that are expressly required in the statutes of the Company."

**French Translation (Center-Right Column):**
"A représente une fraction des 5,000 parts bénéficiaires de la Société Anonyme par action bénéficiaire au porteur inscrite au Registre de Commerce de l'Île de Cuba.

Le porteur de préférence de ce Titre recevra annuellement sur les bénéfices nets de la Compagnie, après avoir distribué les dividendes ordinaires sur les actions ordinaires de la Compagnie, la part qui lui correspond dans la somme que la Compagnie distribue annuellement entre ses actionnaires bénéficiaires.

Le porteur jouira des mêmes droits et privilèges que confère ce Titre à tout autre porteur, sauf que le transfert de ce Titre se fera conformément aux conditions qui sont expressément requises aux statuts de la Compagnie."

**German Translation (Right Column):**
"Diese Urkunde stellt einen Anteilschein dar von den 5,000 Gewinnanteilscheinen der Aktiengesellschaft pro Gewinnanteilschein auf den Inhaber, eingetragen im Handelsregister der Insel Kuba.

Der Inhaber dieses Anteilscheins wird jährlich aus dem Reingewinn der Gesellschaft, nachdem die ordentlichen Dividenden auf die ordentlichen Aktien der Gesellschaft verteilt worden sind, seinen Anteil an der Summe erhalten, die die Gesellschaft jährlich unter ihre Gewinnanteilschein-Inhaber verteilt.

Der Inhaber genießt die gleichen Rechte und Privilegien, welche dieser Anteilschein jedem anderen Inhaber gewährt, ausgenommen daß die Abtretung dieses Anteilscheins nach Maßgabe der ausdrücklich in den Satzungen der Gesellschaft festgelegten Bedingungen erfolgt."

**Date:**
Habana, 1º de Marzo de 1911 (Havana, March 1, 1911)

**Signatures:**
[Signature - left side] Francisco B. Reyes
[Signature - center/right side] [illegible but present]

**Coupon Information:**
COUPON TRANSLATION | COUPON ÜBERSETZUNG

"COUPON FOR DIVIDEND NUMBER_____
This coupon entitles the bearer to receive from the Banco Territorial de Cuba, or its agents or correspondents in Havana, Paris, Berlin, London, New York and other cities, the dividend corresponding to this share for the year [blank], with the presentation of this coupon duly endorsed at the offices of the Company."

DIVIDENDENANTIE NO._____
[German text describing dividend coupon procedures]

**Certificate Number:**
TITRE Nº22752
BON POUR RÉCLAMER LA FEUILLE DE COUPONS

title: Bank of Roumania Limited Share Warrant
description: A bearer share warrant of the Bank of Roumania Limited, certifying the holder as proprietor of five fully paid shares of £6 each in the company, given under its common seal in May 1903. Issued from 7, Great Winchester Street, London, the bank was incorporated under the Companies Acts, 1862 to 1900, with a capital of £300,000 divided into 50,000 shares of £6 each. The certificate is bilingual, its clauses set in parallel English and French ("Share Warrant to Bearer" / "Titre au Porteur"), a convention easing circulation of the instrument across Continental exchanges. As a British-registered house channelling London capital into Romanian commerce, the warrant exemplifies the westward flow of investment that financed south-eastern Europe at the turn of the century. The crowned arms and engraved scrollwork at the head lend the document the dignified, conservative aspect favoured by City finance.

title: Bank Małopolski Share Certificate, Kraków, 1920
description: This is a joint-stock share certificate of Bank Małopolski Spółka Akcyjna (the Bank of Little Poland) of Kraków, representing twenty-five shares ("Dwadzieścia pięć akcji") at a nominal value of 400 Koron, or 280 Polish Marks, each — together 10,000 Koron, that is 7,000 Polish Marks. It is dated Kraków, 15 December 1920. Bank Małopolski served the formerly Austrian Galicia, newly absorbed into the reconstituted Polish Republic. The doubled denomination, Austrian Korony rendered as Polish Marki, registers the unfinished currency unification of a state stitched together from three partitioning empires, with the older crown still the unit of account. Later revenue stamps and overprints dated 1922 and 1923, including a "Bezugsrecht" (subscription-right) endorsement, attest to the certificate's continued life amid the inflation of those years. The restrained engine-turned border and central monogram favor sober, ledger-like respectability over ornament.
creator: Bank Małopolski
notes: Bank Małopolski, Kraków, 25 shares, 10,000 Koron / 7,000 Polish Marks, December 15, 1920

title: Siberian Commercial Bank, Dividend Coupon Sheet
description: This is a sheet of five attached dividend coupons (the sixth through tenth) issued by the Сибирскій Торговый Банкъ — the Siberian Commercial Bank — each coupon entitling the bearer of two shares to collect the dividend for one year, running consecutively from 1917 through 1921. Every coupon repeats the bank's name in heavy slab-serif Cyrillic, the line "на полученіе дивиденда за [year] г." (for receipt of the dividend for the year), and the printed designation "Предсѣдатель Правленія" (Chairman of the Board) above an engraved facsimile signature. At the left of each stub sits a circular medallion bearing the interlaced monogram "СТБ". One of the late Imperial era's leading joint-stock banks, with a St. Petersburg seat and a wide Siberian branch network, the institution was swept into the post-1917 nationalisations; that these later coupons survive uncut and unredeemed makes the sheet a quiet record of dividends the revolution rendered uncollectible.
creator: Siberian Commercial Bank
notes: Siberian Commercial Bank (Banque Commerciale de Sibérie), dividend coupons 6-10 for shares 17747-17748, years 1917-1921

title: St. Petersburg Private Commercial Bank Share, St. Petersburg, 1911
description: This is a bearer share (Акція / Action au porteur) for two hundred rubles in the St. Petersburg Private Commercial Bank (С.-Петербургскій Частный Коммерческій Банкъ / Banque de Commerce Privée de St.-Pétersbourg), issued at St. Petersburg in 1911. The certificate records a joint-stock capital of 20,000,000 rubles divided into 100,000 shares of 200 rubles, and cites the imperial confirmation of the bank's charter on 28 July 1864. A coupon sheet (Талонъ) for collecting dividends survives at the left. Bilingual Russian and French text addresses both domestic holders and the Western European capital markets on which Russian banks drew. Issued at the height of Russia's pre-war boom, the share illustrates how the empire's leading commercial banks raised funds to finance railways and industry. The restrained typographic frame, large numeral, and engraved guilloche borders project sober institutional credit rather than ornamental display.
creator: St. Petersburg Private Commercial Bank
notes: St. Petersburg Private Commercial Bank (Banque de Commerce Privée de St.-Pétersbourg), 200 Ruble share No. 82719, Emission 1911

title: Sofiyska Banka (Banque de Sofia) Share Certificate
description: This is a single bearer share (акция / action) of one hundred gold leva (сто лева златни / cent leva en or) in the Софийска Банка / Banque de Sofia, a Bulgarian joint-stock company (българско анонимно дружество / société anonyme bulgare) headquartered in the capital. Printed in parallel Bulgarian and French and dated at Sofia in 1917, the certificate entitles its holder to a proportional interest in the bank's capital and to all rights and obligations fixed by its statutes. Issuing the share in gold leva rather than ordinary paper currency shielded subscribers from the steep wartime depreciation Bulgaria suffered as a member of the Central Powers, where domestic notes were losing value against metal. The engraved vignette, a seated figure of Mercury with caduceus and emblems of trade beside a shield bearing the bank's monogram, casts the institution as a patron of commerce. The certificate documents private banking capital mobilized at the height of the war.
creator: Sofiyska Banka
notes: Sofiyska Banka (Banque de Sofia), 100 gold leva share No. 035685, Bulgaria, 1917

title: Banque Industrielle de Chine (中法實業銀行) Share, Paris, 1913
description: An ordinary bearer share (action ordinaire au porteur), fully paid, of 500 francs in the Banque Industrielle de Chine — 中法實業銀行, the Sino-French Industrial Bank — a société anonyme seated in Paris under statutes dated 15 March 1913. The certificate records a capital of 45,000,000 francs divided into 90,000 shares of 500 francs each, of which 87,000 are ordinary and 3,000 founders' shares; a marginal register notes a later raising of capital from 75 to 150 million francs. The bank was floated to channel French savings into Chinese commerce and industry, and the engraving advertises that ambition: dragons, a temple in the manner of Beijing's Hall of Prayer, and guardian lions frame a panorama, binding Parisian capital to an imagined China. The institution collapsed in 1921, ruining small French shareholders.
creator: Banque Industrielle de Chine

title: Banque Territoriale d'Espagne Share
description: This is a provisional bearer share (titre provisoire au porteur) of 500 francs in the Banque Territoriale d'Espagne, issued at Madrid on 20 May 1872 and here paid up to 150 francs, bearing annual interest of 6 per cent payable semi-annually. The certificate proclaims a capital of one hundred million francs, expressed in parallel as thirty million écus and four million pounds sterling, divided into two hundred thousand shares of 500 francs, with equivalents stated in reales and pounds. Constituted by acts of 10 March and 28 October 1869, the bank was a French-financed land-mortgage institution modeled on France's Crédit Foncier, channeling foreign capital toward Spanish real estate and agriculture. The triple-currency denomination and the bilingual French text on a Madrid instrument reveal a company straddling two markets, raising money in Paris and London to lend against Spanish land.
creator: Banque Territoriale d'Espagne
notes: Banque Territoriale d'Espagne, 500 Franc action No. 011,824, Madrid, May 30, 1871

title: Beate Uhse Aktiengesellschaft, 1 Euro Share
description: This is a registered share (Aktie) of one Euro par value in Beate Uhse Aktiengesellschaft, issued at Flensburg in May 1999 and identified by the German securities number WKN 755140. The certificate's text declares the holder a shareholder of the company according to its statutes. Beate Uhse AG, founded by the aviator Beate Uhse, grew into Germany's leading retailer of erotic goods; this share marks its flotation on the Frankfurt exchange in 1999, an early instance of a firm in that trade entering the public capital markets and a moment widely noted in the postwar liberalisation of German manners. The design makes no attempt at concealment: a reclining nude is printed in pale relief across the body of the note, and two figures in red bathing costumes stand at the right, so that the visual register openly advertises the issuer's business.
creator: Beate Uhse Aktiengesellschaft
notes: Beate Uhse Aktiengesellschaft, 1 Euro share No. 000069560, Flensburg, May 1999; first adult entertainment company to go public on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange

title: Black-Sea-Kuban Railway Company Bond
description: A bearer bond of £20 sterling = 189 roubles, bearing 4½% interest, drawn on the 4½% Bond Loan of 1911 of the Black-Sea-Kuban Railway Company. The whole loan amounts to £1,913,760 sterling = 18,085,032 roubles nominal, and is issued under the absolute guarantee of the Imperial Russian Government for the payment of interest and principal, with coupons payable half-yearly. Dated at Ekaterinodar (now Krasnodar) in 1911, the obligation served a line opened across the fertile Kuban region of southern Russia toward the Black Sea. Its dual denomination in pounds and roubles, the parallel Russian and English texts, and above all the sovereign guarantee speak to a railway floated chiefly on London's market, where the imperial pledge was the surety that drew foreign capital to a provincial Russian enterprise.
creator: Black-Sea-Kuban Railway Company
notes: Black-Sea-Kuban Railway Company, 4½% Bond £20 = 189 Rubles, No. A13852, Yekaterinador, 1911

title: Boston and Providence Rail Road Corporation Stock Certificate
description: This engraved certificate attests proprietorship of ten shares, at one hundred dollars par each, in the capital stock of the Boston and Providence Rail Road Corporation, made out to Haliburton Fales and dated the fifteenth day of August 1839, signed by the treasurer Jno. J. Loring. Chartered in 1831 and opened in 1835, the line was among the earliest steam railroads in the United States, and by 1839 its shares already changed hands as seasoned securities, transferable only on the corporation's books upon surrender of the certificate. The engraved vignette crowning the sheet sets a locomotive and train against a busy harbor of sailing vessels, while the left margin frames a romantic cataract plunging through wooded cliffs — a pairing that yokes the new machine to picturesque nature and advertises the enterprise as both progress and prospect.
creator: Boston and Providence Rail Road Corporation
notes: Boston and Providence Rail Road Corporation, 10 shares, No. 122, Haliburton Fales, August 15, 1839

title: British Honduras Company Limited Share Certificate
description: This is a certificate for one fully paid share of £5 in the British Honduras Company, Limited, issued to John Campbell, Esq., of 26 Old Broad Street, London, under the company's common seal on 1 June 1863. The certificate records a nominal capital of £100,000 divided into shares of £5 each and notes that the firm was incorporated pursuant to the Joint Stock Companies' Acts of 1856–7. British Honduras (present-day Belize) was a British settlement whose economy rested on mahogany and logwood extraction, and the company was floated to work its timber and to promote commercial settlement. The instrument exemplifies how the newly codified limited-liability share form was harnessed to finance colonial ventures, spreading the risk of a distant enterprise across many small subscriptions. The engraving is plain and typographic, its Old English titling and copperplate clerical script signalling probity rather than ornament.

title: Calvert, Waco & Brazos Valley Railroad Company Stock Certificate
description: This is a blank, unissued stock certificate of the Calvert, Waco & Brazos Valley Railroad Company, a corporation existing under the laws of the State of Texas, with capital divided into shares of one hundred dollars each. Every field — owner's name, number of shares, and date — stands empty, and the engraved date line carries only the printed "18__", marking this as a printer's specimen or archive copy rather than an instrument actually placed in commerce. The certificate is made transferable only on the company's books at its office in the City of Calvert. Its central engraved vignette joins a town with a stone-arch bridge and grazing cattle beside a stream, an image binding rail to the surrounding farm country. The document speaks to the wave of short-line promotions that, in the closing decades of the century, sought to link Texas agricultural districts to the trunk railroads.

title: Compagnie Universelle du Canal Interocéanique de Panama Share
description: A bearer share (action au porteur) of 500 francs in the Compagnie Universelle du Canal Interocéanique de Panama, the société anonyme whose statutes were deposited before the Paris notary Champetier de Ribes on 20 October and 29 Novembre 1880. The face records a capital social of 300,000,000 francs divided into 600,000 shares of 500 francs, this one libérée de deux cent cinquante francs—paid up only by half, the balance called in instalments. It is signed for the Président-Directeur Ferdinand de Lesseps, the engineer of Suez who summoned vast subscriptions from small French savers to cut a sea-level canal through the Isthmus of Panama. Engraved by Stern of Paris, the sheet personifies the continents as reclining figures around a gridded globe, framed by columns naming the nations from which capital was sought—an iconography of universal enterprise that the venture's 1889 collapse and ensuing scandal would mock. Attached versement receipts record later calls, the third paid at Paris on 4 février 1886.

title: Compagnie Universelle du Canal de Panama Provisional Share
description: This printed provisional bearer certificate (titre provisoire au porteur négociable) represents one lottery bond (obligation à lots) of the Compagnie Universelle du Canal Interocéanique de Panama, a société anonyme capitalised at 300 million francs in 600,000 shares of 500 francs. It belongs to the 720-million-franc loan of two million lottery obligations authorised by the law of 8 June 1888 and offered to the public without any guarantee of the State ("sans aucune garantie ou responsabilité de l'État"). Each obligation yields 15 francs yearly, payable half-yearly on 1 December and 1 June, and is redeemable either by lot or at 400 francs within a maximum of ninety-nine years; this title is paid up to only 60 francs of the 360 subscribed, the remaining 300 francs falling due in later instalments. Issued at Paris, 26 June 1888, it bears the engraved signatures of Ferdinand de Lesseps as président-directeur, the administrator H. Cottu, and Ch. A. de Lesseps for the société civile d'amortissement. A marginal notice requires exchange after 16 December 1889 for a definitive title, the only form admitted to the drawings. The company, launched in 1881 under de Lesseps, builder of Suez, collapsed in 1889, ruining hundreds of thousands of small French savers in one of the century's gravest financial scandals; this lottery loan was among its final desperate attempts to raise capital. Stamps reading "REMBOURSÉ PAR LE SÉQUESTRE" record the later partial settlement of bondholders by the court-appointed receiver.
creator: Compagnie Universelle du Canal Interocéanique de Panama
notes: Compagnie Universelle du Canal Interocéanique de Panama. Titre provisoire 500 francs. No. 0,269,304. Paris, June 20, 1888. Signed by H. Cottu and J.F. de Lesseps. Stamped REMBOURSE PAR LE SEQUESTRE. Panama Canal Company scandal.

title: Compagnie Universelle du Canal Maritime de Suez Obligation
description: A bearer obligation of 500 francs bearing 3% interest, issued by the Compagnie Universelle du Canal Maritime de Suez as part of its 100-million-franc loan authorised by the shareholders' general assembly of 4 June 1885 (Deuxième Série). Interest of 15 francs fell due half-yearly, on 1 March and 1 September, the coupons here running into the 1950s and beyond as the long amortisation worked itself out. Signed for the board by its president, Prince Auguste d'Arenberg, the certificate dates to his tenure and the years in which this series was placed. Issued at Paris, the bond financed the deepening, widening, and upkeep of the canal opened in 1869, the company's revenue resting on tolls levied at the Egyptian isthmus. The engraved frame stages that geography directly: globes and an Egyptian winged solar disc flank the title, while a panoramic bird's-eye view of the canal threading the desert from sea to sea runs across the lower field, binding the security to the waterway whose traffic secured it.

title: Caramanian Iron Corporation Limited Share Warrant
description: A share warrant to bearer of the Caramanian Iron Corporation Limited, certifying title to ten ordinary shares (Action Ordinaire / Dix Actions) of one pound each in a company capitalised at £80,000, or Frs 2,000,000. Printed in parallel English and French and dated at Paris on 4 February 1907, the bilingual text and dual-currency capital register a venture floated simultaneously on the London and Paris markets. The company held mining concessions in Karamania, the coastal Cilicia of southern Anatolia, and belongs to the influx of British and French capital into Ottoman mineral concessions in the empire's final decades, when concessions were granted liberally to draw foreign money. A later overprint converts the instrument into a deferred share by resolution of an extraordinary general meeting of 7 June 1907, recording a subsequent reordering of the company's share classes. The dense engraved border, in the eng-leaf manner of fin-de-siècle French bond work, frames the company monogram and lends the speculative paper an air of solidity.

title: Central Trust Company Stock Certificate, Cambridge, Massachusetts, 1929
description: This certificate records ownership of common stock in the Central Trust Company of Cambridge, Massachusetts, a state-chartered trust company with a capital stock of $1,500,000, the shares carrying a par value of $100 each. It conveys one thousand and thirty (1,030) shares to Edgar R. Champlin and is dated in July 1929, on the eve of the October crash. Incorporated under the laws of Massachusetts, such trust companies blended deposit banking with fiduciary and investment functions, a hybrid that would prove acutely fragile once asset values collapsed and depositors withdrew. The engraved vignette—an eagle astride a globe gripping a banner inscribed "Progress," flanked by a steamship and an electric streetcar—translates the era's faith in commerce and technological advance into heraldic imagery, an optimism the document's timing renders poignant.
creator: Central Trust Company
notes: Central Trust Company, Cambridge MA, 1,030 shares, No. 1636, Edgar R. Champlin, July 21, 1929

title: Amsterdam Depositary Certificate for Bank of the United States Shares
description: This printed Dutch certificaat is an Amsterdam administration certificate representing one share ("eene Actie") of one hundred dollars in the Bank der Vereenigde Staten van America at Philadelphia—the Bank of the United States—deposited under the joint administration of the banking houses Hope & Co., Ketwich & Voombergh, and the Widow Willem Borski, and executed before the notaries Commelin and Weyland. Issued at Amsterdam and dated 31 January 1844, it entitles the bearer to dividends payable on coupons each 5 January and 5 July, here furnished through 5 January 1845, all conforming to the administration deed ("Berigt") of 30 June 1824. The instrument shows how Amsterdam's leading houses repackaged American bank stock into bearer certificates for Dutch investors. By 1844 the bank, stripped of its federal charter, was foundering under a Pennsylvania charter, and such certificates trace the lingering foreign stake in its residual claims. The sober Gothic title and engine-turned numeral panel project bankerly probity rather than ornament.
creator: Hope & Co.; Ketwich & Voombergh; Widow Willem Borski
notes: Amsterdam depositary certificate No. 36,013 for Bank of the United States shares, Hope & Co./Ketwich & Voombergh/Widow Willem Borski, January 31, 1844
transcription:
# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

CERTIFICAAT.

N°. 36012

Houder dezes is geregtigd tot eene Actie, groot HONDERD DOLLARS, in de Bank, genaamd de Bank der Vereenigde Staten van Amerika, te PHILADELPHIA, onder meerderen ingeschreven, op Naam van het Kantoor van Administratie, opgerigt te Amsterdam, onder directie van

HOPE & COMP.,
KETWICH & VOOMBERGH.
EN
WEDUWE WILLEM BORSKI,

en gedeponeerd bij de Notarissen Commelin en Weyland.

De UITTEELINGEN op de gedeponeerde Actien, verschijnende d Januari en d Juli van elk jaar, zullen voor rekening en risico van den Houder, in AMERICA ontvangen, overgemaakt en alhier op COUPONS uitbetaald worden, volgens Art. 6, van het uitgegeven Bericht, in dato 30 Junij 1824.

De HOUDER zal ten allen tijde geregtigd zijn, om tegens intrekking van dit Certificaat en de als dan nog Onverschenen Coupons, met het Bewijs tot bekoming van Afentie Couponj, en tegen betaling van de KOSTEN, terug te vorderen, eene Actie van HONDERD DOLLARS, in gemeelde BANK, alles overeenkomstig Art. 10, van het voormelde Bericht.

Amsterdam den 31 Januari 1844

Gezien bij mij
S. Commelin
Notaris.

[Signatures - illegible but present]
H. Voombergh
[unclear name]

Hierbij afgegeven HALFJAARIGE COUPONS, tot d Januari 1845, en Bewijs voor volgende Coupons.

---

**English translation:**

CERTIFICATE.

No. 36012

The holder hereof is entitled to one Share, of the amount of ONE HUNDRED DOLLARS, in the Bank, named the Bank of the United States of America, at PHILADELPHIA, registered among others, in the Name of the Administration Office, established at Amsterdam, under the direction of

HOPE & COMP.,
KETWICH & VOOMBERGH.
AND
WIDOW WILLEM BORSKI,

and deposited with the Notaries Commelin and Weyland.

The DISTRIBUTIONS on the deposited Shares, falling due in January and July of each year, shall be received for the account and risk of the Holder in AMERICA, remitted and paid out here on COUPONS, according to Art. 6, of the issued Notice, dated 30 June 1824.

The HOLDER shall at all times be entitled, upon surrender of this Certificate and the then outstanding Coupons, with the Proof of receipt of Agency Coupons, and upon payment of the COSTS, to recover one Share of ONE HUNDRED DOLLARS, in the said BANK, all in accordance with Art. 10, of the aforesaid Notice.

Amsterdam the 31st January 1844

Seen by me
S. Commelin
Notary.

[Signatures]
H. Voombergh
[unclear name]

Hereby issued SEMI-ANNUAL COUPONS, until January 1845, and Proof for following Coupons.

---

**Notable markings:**

- Serial number: N°. 36012 (handwritten at top)
- Date: Amsterdam den 31 Januari 1844 (31 January 1844)
- Signatures of notary Commelin and banking house representatives (H. Voombergh and others)
- Handwritten notations and signature marks
- Ornate decorative border frame typical of 19th-century financial documents
- Reference to coupons valid until January 1845

title: Hamburg Office of Administration Certificate for 5% Russian Funds
description: A printed certificate (Certificaat) in Dutch, issued by a Hamburg "Kantoor van Administratie" (Office of Administration) under the direction of the banking houses Sillem, Benecke & Comp. and H. J. Stresow. It is good for an inscription (Inscriptie) of five hundred roubles in the 5 percent Russian funds, payable in metallic specie and entered in the great book of Russian Inscriptions in the administration's name; the holder is entitled to collect interest by coupons and, on first demand, to reclaim the original inscription. The certificate is dated Hamburg, 23 March 1821, and is countersigned for deposit by the notary M. Langhans. Issued amid the wave of Russian state borrowing that followed the Napoleonic wars, the document shows how Hamburg houses interposed themselves as custodians, holding sovereign Russian stock and issuing transferable depositary receipts that let distant investors hold foreign debt without crossing borders. The plain, type-set frame is purely functional, befitting an instrument of trust rather than display.
creator: Sillem Benecke & Co.; H.J. Stresow
notes: Hamburg depositary certificate No. 14,463 for 500 Rubles 5% Russian bonds, Sillem Benecke & Co./H.J. Stresow, April 23, 1851
transcription:
# Document Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

CERTIFICAAT.

No. 14464.

Goed voor eene Inscriptie van Vyfhonderd Roebels 5 pct. Russische fondsen, betaalbaar in Metalick, ingeschreeven in het grootboek der Russische Inschrijvingen ten Naame van de administratie opgericht te HAMBURG onder directie van de Heeren SILLEM, BENECKE & Comp. en de Heer H. J. STRESOW,

waarvan de Intressen voor Rekening van den Houder ontfangen & uitbetaald zullen worden op Coupons, in gevolge Art 5. van het uitgegeven bericht.

De houder van dit Certificaat zal ten allen tyde en op deszelfs eerste aanvrage, gerechtigd zyn, de voorschreven Som van Vyfhonderd Roebels terug te vorderen, in Originecle Inscriptiën, tegen terug gave van dit Certificaat, met de overscheene Coupons, en het daarbij afgegeven bewys voor de afgift van volgende Coupons, alles in gevolge Art. 2. van het bericht.

Hamburg 23 Maart 1821.

[Signatures: Willem Benecke, H.J. Stresow]

Voor depot ingevolge Art. 4. van het bericht

M. Langhans
Notaris.

---

**English translation:**

CERTIFICATE.

No. 14464.

Valid for one Registration of Five Hundred Rubles at 5 percent Russian funds, payable in Metallic currency, registered in the ledger of Russian Registrations in the Name of the administration established in HAMBURG under the direction of the Gentlemen SILLEM, BENECKE & Company and Mr. H. J. STRESOW,

whereby the Interest for the Account of the Holder shall be received and paid out on Coupons, in accordance with Art. 5 of the issued notice.

The holder of this Certificate shall at all times and upon his first request be entitled to demand return of the aforementioned Sum of Five Hundred Rubles in Original Registrations, against return of this Certificate, with the remaining Coupons, and the accompanying proof for the delivery of following Coupons, all in accordance with Art. 2 of the notice.

Hamburg 23 March 1821.

[Signatures: Willem Benecke, H.J. Stresow]

For deposit in accordance with Art. 4 of the notice

M. Langhans
Notary.

- Two handwritten signatures: Willem Benecke and H.J. Stresow
- Signature of M. Langhans (Notary) at bottom
- Ornamental border on left side with "RUIT VAN ADMINIS." text
- Circular stamp/seal in upper right corner (partially legible)

title: Charles Laffitte & Company Limited Share Certificate
description: A bilingual share certificate (Certificat d'Action / Share Certificate) of Charles Laffitte & Company, Limited, certifying ownership of one share of £20, of which ten pounds — the French text's "Dix Livres Sterling" — has been paid. It is dated the fifteenth day of January 1866 and issued under the common seal of the company. Charles Laffitte, kin to the celebrated Paris banking house of Jacques Laffitte, ran this Anglo-French finance and trading concern from London, and the document's parallel French and English columns advertise its cross-Channel character at a moment when British and Continental capital flowed freely toward joint-stock ventures. The engraving is sober and typographic rather than allegorical: a plain ruled register for name, address and number frames a vignette of the company's name, the restraint itself signalling a respectable banking house rather than a speculative promotion.
creator: Charles Laffitte & Company Limited
notes: Charles Laffitte & Company Limited, One Share £20 (half paid), No. 67906, January 5, 1866

title: Chicago, Rock Island & Pacific Rail Road Company Mortgage Bond, Chicago
description: This engraved certificate is a $5,000 mortgage bond of the Chicago, Rock Island & Pacific Rail Road Company, bearing interest at six per cent per annum payable semi-annually each first of January and July, the principal payable on the first day of July 1917 in lawful money. It is one of an issue limited to $12,500,000, secured by a deed of trust dated 1 May 1877, with the United States Trust Company of New York as trustee. The header names the "States of Illinois & Iowa," the territory the line crossed from Chicago toward Council Bluffs. Issued to fund a western trunk road in the years after the 1873 panic, the bond's forty-year term and modest fixed rate speak to the long horizons of railway finance. The steel-engraved vignette pairs a speeding locomotive with a harbor of ships, joining rail and water commerce in a single emblem of through traffic.

title: Chicago, Rock Island and Pacific Railroad Company Gold Bond
description: A registered $1,000 four per cent gold bond of the Chicago, Rock Island and Pacific Railroad Company, styled the "Gold Bond of 2002" and due November 1, 2002, with interest payable in gold semi-annually on May 1 and November 1 and principal and interest payable in the City of New York. The Central Trust Company of New York certifies, as trustee, that the bond is one of those secured under the within-mentioned trust agreement; the engraving is the work of the American Bank Note Company. The remarkable hundred-year term, the gold clause, and the great trust-company guaranty advertise the road's ambition to fund itself on the longest horizon the market would bear, binding capital to a single mortgage for a full century. The dense steel-engraved border and the formal lettered cartouche, framing the denomination in a heavy ornamental medallion, lend the instrument the gravity of a permanent obligation rather than a passing loan.
