title: Republic of China Construction Gold Bonds, First Series US Dollar Bond, 1940
description: This certificate is a five-dollar (伍圓) bond of the Republic of China's Construction Gold Bonds of the twenty-ninth year of the Republic (民國二十九年建設金公債), First Series, payable in United States currency, as the recurring "US" monograms in the lower corners announce. The body of the text is dated the first day of the fifth month of Republic Year 29 (1 May 1940) and bears the seal of the Ministry of Finance (財政部) over the names of its minister and vice-minister, with redemption to proceed by periodic drawing of lots (抽籤). Issued from the wartime capital while Japanese forces held the coast, the gold-dollar denomination courted overseas Chinese and foreign subscribers whose remittances the Nationalist treasury could not raise at home. The engraved vignettes—viaducts, a dam, railways threading mountains, mines and harbours—frame the loan as a pledge of construction rather than mere war finance, binding the lender's coin to the promise of a rebuilt country.
creator: Republic of China, Ministry of Finance
notes: Republic of China Year 29 Construction Gold Bonds (建設公債), $5 USD bond, 1940

A large red square seal (seal script) is overprinted across the centre, obscuring parts of several columns. Engraved vignettes of viaducts, dams, railways, mines and harbours frame the sheet; "US" monogram devices appear in the lower corners.)*

## English Translation:

**Title:** Republic of China Twenty-Ninth-Year (1940) Construction Gold Loan (Minguo Ershijiu Nian Jianshe Jin Gongzhai) / First Series US-Dollar Bond (Diyiqi Meijin Zhaipiao)

**Denomination cachet (upper left):** FIVE YUAN (Wu Yuan), encircled by a stylised inscription (partly legible: "…Republic… First Series… Construction Gold… Loan… bond…").

**Summary of Regulations (heading):** Summary of the Regulations of the Republic of China Twenty-Ninth-Year Construction Gold Loan, First Series US-Dollar Bond.

- **Amount fixed:** US$25,000,000, issued at 98 percent of face value.
- **Method of purchase:** (1) For those paying in US dollars or [foreign exchange] … ; (2) for those paying in other foreign currencies or exchange, converted at the current rate of the day …; (3) for those paying in legal-tender (fabi) currency, converted at the commercial exchange rate fixed by the Ministry of Finance … *(remainder obscured by the seal)*.
- **Interest:** Five percent (5 li) per annum, from 1 May of the 29th Year of the Republic; payable each year on 30 April and 30 [31] October, [each an interest payment] *(columns partly covered by the seal)*.
- **Redemption of principal:** Beginning in the [33rd] Year of the Republic … redemption by drawing of lots executed [three] months before each [due date] … repayment commencing on [the stated date] *(largely obscured by the seal)*.
- **Fund:** The [principal-and-interest] fund shall be transferred periodically to the Central Bank of China from the receipts of the Ministry of Finance National Treasury *(remainder obscured)*.
- **Managing organ:** The Central Bank of China is designated as the managing [agent] … for repayment of principal and [interest] *(remainder obscured)*.
- **Face value:** Issued in denominations of 100 yuan, 50 yuan, 10 yuan, and 5 yuan [and others].
- **Use:** This bond is a bearer instrument; it may be bought, sold and pledged; where a guarantee/security deposit must be paid for official purposes [it may serve as a substitute], and it may serve as a bank's security reserve.

**Date:** 1 May, 29th Year of the Republic of China (1 May 1940).

**Signatures:** Ministry of Finance (Caizhengbu) — Minister: Kong Xiangxi (H.H. Kung); Vice-Minister: Zou Lin. (Each name is followed by a red square name-seal in seal script, illegible.)

**Central seal:** A large red square seal in seal script overprinted across the body (Ministry of Finance seal; the seal-script characters are stylised and not clearly legible).

*(Note: figures such as US$25,000,000 and the denomination "Five Yuan" are as printed on the certificate; the catalogue's "$5 bond" refers to this Five-Yuan/US-dollar face value. Portions of the interest, redemption, fund, and managing-organ clauses are physically obscured by the central seal and are marked [illegible] rather than reconstructed.)*

title: Gouvernement Général de l'Indo-Chine Obligation, Paris, 1902
description: A 500-franc bearer obligation (obligation au porteur) of the Gouvernement Général de l'Indo-Chine, issued under the République Française at Paris on 5 August 1902. It forms part of the second issue (deuxième émission) of 155,000 bonds bearing 3½ percent interest, drawn from the 200-million-franc loan authorized by the law of 25 December 1898 and dedicated expressly to building railways in Indochina, with service charged on the colony's annual budget. The note pays 15 francs yearly in two coupons (1 May and 1 November) and is amortized over seventy-five years. Issued as France pressed its mise en valeur of the Union indochinoise, the bond converts metropolitan savings into colonial infrastructure secured on local revenues. Its engraved frame answers that ambition decoratively: paired dragons, fretwork borders, and palms frame a riverine scene of laborers, an elephant, and harbor works—an Orientalist vocabulary that renders the colony picturesque and tractable to the investor.
creator: Gouvernement Général de l'Indo-Chine
notes: Gouvernement Général de l'Indo-Chine, 500 Franc 3.5% obligation No. 153,376, August 5, 1902
transcription:
# Document Transcription

**Original (French):**

RÉPUBLIQUE FRANÇAISE

GOUVERNEMENT GÉNÉRAL DE L'INDO-CHINE

Emprunt de 200 Millions de Francs
dont la réalisation est autorisée par la Loi du 15 décembre 1898, affectée spécialement à la construction de chemins de fer en Indo-Chine. L'intérêt versé pour assurer le service des intérêts et de l'amortissement sera inscrit obligatoirement au budget annexé de l'Indo-chine.

DEUXIÈME ÉMISSION DE 155,000 OBLIGATIONS 3⁰/₀ DE 500 FRANCS
Effectuée en vertu du décret en date du 25 Juillet 1902 et suivant arrêté du Gouverneur général en date du 28 Juillet 1902.
Remboursables par tirage semestriel, qui aura lieu à Paris les 15 avril et 15 octobre de chaque année, à partir du 1er Avril 1903.

INTÉRÊT ANNUEL: 15 FRANCS PAYABLES PAR SEMESTRES LES 1er MAI ET 1er NOVEMBRE DE CHAQUE ANNÉE

Ces obligations sont affectées au service de la Banque de l'Indo-Chine. Elles peuvent être libellées en or ou en titres mobiliers à l'Indochine. Elles ne sont mobiliers à l'Indochine. Elles en sont admises aux droits de transmission sur les Valeurs mobilisés à l'Indochine le revenu, tell qu'il serait actuellement durable en France.

Le paiement des coupons semestriels sera tiré à Paris, à la Banque de France et à la Banque de l'Indo-Chine; à Saïgon, à la Banque de l'Indo-Chine et à Hanoï, à la même Banque. Les obligations seront également payables à Paris.

Le Gouvernement général de l'Indo-Chine reconnait à la faculté d'amortir l'emprunt par anticipation au cours du pair.

Obligation de 500 Francs au Porteur N°153,376

LE DÉLÉGUÉ DES SOCIÉTÉS CONTRACTANTES,     LE DÉLÉGUÉ DU GOUVERNEMENT GÉNÉRAL DE L'INDO-CHINE,

Paris, le 5 Août 1902.

---

**English Translation:**

FRENCH REPUBLIC

GENERAL GOVERNMENT OF INDO-CHINA

Loan of 200 Million Francs
the implementation of which is authorized by the Law of 15 December 1898, specifically allocated to the construction of railways in Indochina. The interest paid to ensure the service of interests and amortization shall be inscribed obligatorily in the annex budget of Indochina.

SECOND ISSUE OF 155,000 OBLIGATIONS 3% OF 500 FRANCS
Effected by virtue of the decree dated 25 July 1902 and pursuant to the order of the Governor General dated 28 July 1902.
Redeemable by semi-annual drawing, which shall take place in Paris on 15 April and 15 October of each year, starting from 1 April 1903.

ANNUAL INTEREST: 15 FRANCS PAYABLE BY SEMESTERS ON THE 1ST OF MAY AND 1ST OF NOVEMBER OF EACH YEAR

These obligations are allocated to the service of the Bank of Indochina. They may be denominated in gold or in securities in Indochina... [text continues regarding transmission rights and payment arrangements]

Bearer Obligation of 500 Francs No. 153,376

THE DELEGATE OF THE CONTRACTING SOCIETIES,     THE DELEGATE OF THE GENERAL GOVERNMENT OF INDOCHINA,

Paris, 5 August 1902.

title: Chinese Government Loan Bond
description: This is a bearer bond of The Chinese Government Loan of £6,866,046 10/10, a single ten-pound denomination drawn from a sterling issue bearing interest at the rate of £8 per cent per annum. The bond states that it was issued in substitution for and extension of the unpaid portion of a series of earlier authorised Chinese Government loans dated between January 1912 and June 1916, and that authentication had been communicated by the Wai Chiao Pu (the foreign ministry) to the Ministers in Peking for France and Italy. It was sealed by the Board of Finance at Peking on the 30th day of September 1925, under an agreement of 14 November 1922 and a supplementary agreement of 30 September 1925 with the bank formerly known as the Sino-Italian Bank, and signed by Li Shih-hao (李思浩), Minister of Finance. The instrument exemplifies the Republic's recurrent practice of consolidating and rolling over defaulted foreign-currency obligations: rather than repaying the lapsed advances, the government bundled them into a fresh sterling bond at a higher coupon, trading immediate relief for a heavier servicing burden. The engraved frame is restrained and classical, foregrounding the printed terms and the vermilion seal rather than allegorical ornament.
creator: Republic of China, Ministry of Finance
notes: Chinese Government Loan £6,866,046 10/10, £10 Bond at 4½%, signed by Li Sihao, Minister of Finance
transcription:
# Transcription of Chinese Government Loan Bond

## Main Text Transcription:

**The Chinese Government Loan**

of £ 6,866,046.10/10

bearing interest at the rate of £ 8.-/- per cent per annum,

being in substitution for and a consolidation of the unpaid portion of the following duly authorized Chinese Government Loans: Loan for £ 300,000.- dated the 1st day of March 1912, Loan for £ 1,200,000.- dated the 16th day of January 1912, Loan for £ 2,000,000.- dated the 27th day of April 1912, Loan for £ 500,000.- dated the 27th May 1912 Loan for £ 500,000.- dated the 8th day of June 1912.

Authorization of this Loan has been officially communicated by the Wai Chiao Pu to the Ministers in Peking for France and Italy.

No. 0005102 **BOND** No. 0005102

**TEN POUNDS STERLING.**

1. This Bond is one of a series of Bonds issued under the above Loan Agreement dated the [date]. The whole amount of the Loan is divided into two classes of Bonds, numbered 1—600 (of £ 500 and £ 1000)—all of which bear interest at 4½ per cent per annum from the 1st day of December 1912.

2. In each year, in accordance with the Loan Agreement, a certain number of the Bonds of this series shall be drawn by lot and redeemed at par on the 1st day of December in the manner following:—

On the 1st day of December [followed by detailed redemption schedule with bond numbers and dates from 1913-1932]

3. The Chinese Government has covenanted that in accordance with the terms referred to in Clause 2 of the Loan Agreement...

[continuing with detailed terms regarding interest payments, bond numbers, and redemption schedules]

4. The Chinese Government has agreed to accept this Bond in lieu of the same amount of taels...

5. The interest on this bond will be payable on the surrender of the proper coupons hereto attached and the principal on the surrender thereof on the due date at the offices of Messrs. MESSRS. ITALIANS, LONDON, or at such other place or places and in respect of the whole sum as payable at any one of the following banks in London:

6. [Terms regarding substitution and extension of unpaid portions of prior loans...]

7. [Detailed provisions regarding security, charges, mortgages, and Chinese Government obligations...]

8. [Final provisions regarding government agreement dated 18th November 1912 between Chinese Government and the Italian Bank for China...]

---

**Notable Markings:**

- **Serial Numbers:** No. 0005102 (appears twice, top and bottom of bond number section)
- **Signature/Seal:** Chinese characters "李思浩" (Li Sihao) as Minister of Finance, with red official seal (approximately 1.5" x 1.5")
- **Decorative Border:** Ornate purple and green Art Nouveau-style border with geometric patterns

title: Rent Receipt (收租票) of the Zhou Household, Guangxu 22 (1896)
description: A woodblock-printed rent receipt (收租票) on thin fibrous paper, issued by the Zhou household (周宅) under the estate name Bao Shan Tang (寶善堂) and dated the tenth month of the twenty-second year of the Guangxu reign (1896). The form acknowledges receipt of rent in full from a named tenant farmer (佃戶) of Wei village against a holding of land, the sum entered by hand in copper cash (足錢 … 文) and authenticated with red seals. Its printed clauses set the terms of tenancy: the rent must be paid clear by the appointed day of the tenth month, with no arrears tolerated, failing which the land is to be withdrawn and let to another. Such a printed-and-filled receipt shows how landlords in late Qing rural China standardised tenancy collection, fixing payment dates and the threat of eviction in advance. The plain ruled columns and the trefoil-arched cartouche framing the title are functional rather than decorative, the marks of an estate's routine bookkeeping.
creator: Bao Shan Hall
notes: Chinese rent receipt (收租票), Guangxu period (Qing Dynasty), ca. 1875-1908
transcription:
# Rent Receipt of the Zhou Household, Guangxu 22 (1896) — Transcription

## Original (Chinese):

(Woodblock-printed form on thin fibrous paper. A trefoil-arched cartouche at the top frames the title, read right-to-left. Below, the body is set in vertical columns read right-to-left; printed clauses with handwritten fill-ins entered in a cursive hand and authenticated with red seals.)

**收租票** (title, in the arched cartouche; read right-to-left: 收 → 租 → 票)

(Body columns, right to left:)

1. 光緒廿二年拾月〔[illegible handwritten day]〕日
2. 周宅為給與憑收執照事全收到
3. 起尾村佃戶〔[handwritten tenant name, only partly legible; appears to be 松文…]〕名下　年
4. 現租　足錢〔[handwritten numerals, largely illegible over a red seal; apparent components 仟 … 十]〕文
5. 嗣後該佃戶准於十月〔　〕日將租錢照
6. 數交清不准稍有帶欠如是日租錢不到
7. 將地撤出另租此照
8. 寶善堂賢字第〔[illegible handwritten serial]〕號

(Seals: a red diamond-shaped seal struck over the sum in column 4, its text illegible. A red rectangular seal with a magenta/red annotation at the lower left of column 8, only partly legible — appears to include 堂 … 號.)

(Reconstructed reading of the connected clause running through columns 5–7: 嗣後該佃戶准於十月〔　〕日，將租錢照數交清，不准稍有帶欠。如是日租錢不到，將地撤出另租，此照。)

---

## English Translation:

**Rent Receipt**

Tenth month of the twenty-second year of the Guangxu reign [1896], the 〔___〕 day.

The Zhou household (Zhōu zhái), for the purpose of issuing a voucher to be held as proof, has received in full [the following].

From the tenant farmer of Qiwei village (Qǐwěi cūn), 〔name — handwritten, only partly legible〕, under whose name, for the year: the present rent, being copper cash (zúqián) 〔___〕 wén.

Hereafter the said tenant is to have the rent-money paid clear in full by the appointed day of the tenth month, and no arrears whatsoever will be tolerated. Should the rent-money not arrive by that day, the land will be withdrawn and let to another. This receipt [is issued as proof].

Bao Shan Tang (Bǎoshàn Táng) — "Xián" character series, No. 〔___〕.

(pinyin of key names: 周宅 Zhōu zhái "the Zhou household"; 寶善堂 Bǎoshàn Táng, the estate/hall name; 起尾村 Qǐwěi cūn, the village; 光緒 Guāngxù, reign title; 足錢 zúqián, full-weight copper cash.)

title: Sui Yik Loan & Godown Co. Share Certificate
description: A share certificate (股份憑票) of the 遂億按揭貸倉有限公司 — the Sui Yik Loan & Godown Co., Ltd., as its red oval seal renders the name in English ("godown" being the East Asian term for a warehouse). Issued in the 28th day of the intercalary fifth month of Guangxu 29 (1903), it records a firm "established expressly for mortgage lending and warehousing" (專為按揭貸倉而設), with a fully subscribed capital of 300,000 silver yuan divided into shares of 100 yuan each, of which 50 yuan was paid on the first call and the balance summoned as resolved. The enterprise fused two credit traditions: the pawnshop's practice of lending against pledged goods and the joint-stock company's transferable share, the reverse text setting out terms for assigning shares only after notice to the firm. Its plain woodblock frame and seal-script title reflect the late-Qing reform years, when Chinese merchants recast Western corporate forms in indigenous financial practice.
creator: 遂億換揭貸倉有限公司
notes: 遂億換揭貸倉有限公司股份憑票 (Sui Yi Mortgage Warehouse Co.), Guangxu Year 29 (1903)
transcription:
# Sui Yik Loan & Godown Co., Ltd. — Share Certificate — Transcription

## Original (Chinese):

*(Layout: a vertical woodblock certificate. Large seal/bold banner title across the top in two rows, each read right-to-left. To the right of the printed frame is a handwritten vertical counterfoil serial. The main body is six vertical columns of printed text read right-to-left (columns 1–5 large type; column 6, the transfer clause, in smaller type). To the left are two filled-in columns naming the subscriber and amount, the managers' names and a brush signature, and the date. Red oval company seal, an embossed blind seal, and a "registered" stamp overlap the lower-left.)*

**遂億按揭貸倉**
**有限公司股份憑票**
*(banner title, top row then bottom row, each read right→left = 遂億按揭貸倉有限公司股份憑票)*

*(right-margin counterfoil, handwritten, vertical):* 遂億第玖佰伍拾壹號

*(main body — six columns, right → left):*

本公司專爲按揭貨倉而設所有章程
例辦理實備資本銀叁拾萬員分作叁
銀壹伯員先科銀伍拾員合共先科本
其餘每股本銀伍拾員按股自存俟有
時集議繳足今特立此股票交執是實
倘有願將股份轉讓與人者必先投知本公司如本公司無人承受方許轉換股票

*(continuous reading: 本公司專爲按揭貨倉而設，所有章程例辦理，實備資本銀叁拾萬員，分作叁〔仟〕銀壹伯員，先科銀伍拾員，合共先科本，其餘每股本銀伍拾員，按股自存，俟有時集議繳足，今特立此股票交執是實。倘有願將股份轉讓與人者，必先投知本公司，如本公司無人承受，方許轉換股票。)*

*(left, filled-in subscriber clause — two columns, right → left):*

余彤山翁遵照本公司章程條欵附入
本銀伍拾員共貳仟伍伯員第玖伯伍拾壹號

*(managers / signatures):*
總理人　李右泉　謝賡唐
林喬生 *(handwritten brush signature)*

*(date column, far left):*
光緒貳拾玖年又五月念八日司理人 *(followed by a handwritten signature over the seal — [illegible])*

*(seals / stamps):*
(seal, red oval, English: **SUI YIK LOAN & GODOWN CO. LTD.**; Chinese within: 遂億按揭貸倉有限公司)
(embossed blind seal, oval: "SUI YI[K] …", Chinese 按揭貸倉 legible; remainder [illegible])
(printed/stamped at lower-left: 經已註冊)

---

## English Translation:

**Sui Yik Loan & Godown Co., Ltd. — Share Certificate**
(*Sui Yik An-kit Toi-chong Yau-han Kung-si*; the red oval seal renders the name "Sui Yik Loan & Godown Co., Ltd.," "godown" being the East-Asian term for a warehouse.)

*(Counterfoil, right margin, handwritten):* Sui Yik, No. 951.

*(Body):* "This Company is established expressly for mortgage lending and warehousing (godown); all its regulations are conducted according to the rules. It has duly provided capital of 300,000 silver yuan, divided into three thousand [shares], each share being 100 silver yuan, of which 50 yuan is paid on the first call — the first-call principal being thus collected in full; the remaining 50 yuan of principal per share is retained by each shareholder in proportion to his shares, to be paid up in full when, in due course, it is resolved in assembly. This share certificate is now specially issued to be held as proof. Should anyone wish to transfer his shares to another, he must first notify the Company; if no one within the Company will take them up, only then may the share certificate be transferred."

*(Subscriber clause):* "Mr. Yu Tongshan (*Yu Tongshan*), in accordance with the Company's regulations, has paid in capital at 50 yuan [per share], totaling 2,500 yuan — Certificate No. 951."

*(Managers / signatures):* General Managers (*zongli ren*): Li Youquan (*Li Youquan*), Xie Gengtang (*Xie Gengtang*). Signature: Lin Qiaosheng (*Lin Qiaosheng*).

*(Date):* Guangxu 29, intercalary (*you*) 5th month, 28th day [= 1903]. Managing agent (*sili ren*): [signature illegible].

*(Seals):* Red oval company seal — "SUI YIK LOAN & GODOWN CO. LTD." (Chinese: 遂億按揭貸倉有限公司); an embossed blind seal of the same company; and a stamp reading 經已註冊 ("already registered").

title: 簡碧鐵路公司 (Jianbi Railway Company) Share Certificate
description: A railway share certificate (股票) of the 簡碧鐵路公司 (Jianbi Railway Company), conveying one full share (大股壹股) and dated the tenth month of the second year of the Chinese Republic — late 1913. The engraved body records that the line was subscribed under three heads — 簡碧, 舊錫 (the old tin works) and 沙炭 — and binds the holder to terms typical of the moment: shares might be transferred only among registered Chinese persons, never sold to foreigners. At the right a finely drawn route map traces the projected line through a riverine, hill-set landscape, serving at once as corporate emblem and as proof of intended works. Issued two years after the 1911 revolution, the certificate belongs to a wave of provincially financed, deliberately Chinese-owned railways — here tied to Yunnan's tin trade — raising native capital to carry ore and reclaim the right of railway-building from foreign concessionaires.
creator: 簡碧鐵路公司
notes: 簡碧鐵路公司股票 (Jianbi Railway Company), Republic of China Year 2 (1913), with map
transcription:
# Jianbi Railway Company Share Certificate — Transcription

## Original (Chinese):

(Layout: a single engraved certificate. RIGHT panel = ornamental title cartouche over a printed route map; MIDDLE panel = the engraved body text in vertical columns read right-to-left; LEFT panel = a stub/counterfoil with a header box, receipt line, date and the company seal. Text transcribed in reading order.)

**RIGHT panel — title cartouche (read right-to-left):** 簡碧鐵路公司

(Two ornamental roundels flanking the top of the map, read right-to-left: **股** ／ **票** ; below them a pictorial route map showing a river-and-hill landscape crossed by a bridge/embankment, with small place labels — legible ones include 簡碧, 元海, 雜街, 黑龍潭, and [illegible] — engraved as a corporate emblem.)

**LEFT counterfoil — header box:** 票

**MIDDLE panel — body text (vertical columns, right-to-left):**

發給股票事，本公司議定，由簡、舊錫、沙炭三項認股，修築簡碧鐵路。照章以伍拾元為一大股，伍元為一中股，壹元為一小股。茲收到天雲祥由錫項繳納大股壹股，合大洋伍拾元，如數訖，合行填給股票收執為據。本公司認票不認人，如欲將轉售，不得售與非中國人，如達本公司決不承認。須至股票者。

**LEFT counterfoil — receipt line:** 計實收到天雲祥號大股壹股大洋伍拾元

**LEFT counterfoil — date:** 中華民國二年十月　　日 （日 obscured by seal, [illegible]）

**Seals:**
- (seal, red, small square intaglio near the counterfoil header — text [illegible])
- (seal, red, small oval control stamp over the characters 伍拾元 — text [illegible])
- (seal, red, large vertical company seal at lower-left, overlapping the counterfoil; partly legible characters include 東 … 會 … 錫 … 股 … 圖 …，remainder [illegible])

---

## English Translation:

**Title:** Jianbi Railway Company (Jiǎnbì Tiělù Gōngsī) — Share Certificate (股票, gǔpiào)

(Right panel roundels: "Share" / "Certificate"; with an engraved map of the projected line.)

**Body:**
"In the matter of issuing shares: This Company has resolved that, through the three heads of Jian [碧], the Old Tin works (舊錫, Jiù Xī), and Sha-tan (沙炭), shares are subscribed to build the Jianbi Railway. By regulation, fifty yuan constitutes one large share, five yuan one medium share, and one yuan one small share. We have now received from Tian-Yun-Xiang (天雲祥, Tiān Yún Xiáng), paid in under the tin head, one large share, amounting to fifty silver dollars, the full sum being settled; accordingly this share certificate is issued to be received and held as proof. This Company recognizes the certificate, not the person. Should [the holder] wish to resell, it may not be sold to any non-Chinese person; if this is contravened, the Company will absolutely not acknowledge it. This being the share certificate [so issued]."

**Counterfoil receipt:** "Total actually received from Tian-Yun-Xiang firm (天雲祥號): one large share, fifty silver dollars."

**Date:** Tenth month, second year of the Republic of China (October 1913); day obscured by seal.

**Note:** The company name is 簡碧鐵路公司 (Jiǎnbì Railway Company); the first character is consistently printed as 簡 (Jiǎn), matching the body text "修築簡碧鐵路."

title: 日本勧業銀行 (Hypothec Bank of Japan) Discount Debenture, 11th Issue — Sales Poster, July 1936
description: A sales placard (売出し) for the 第十一回割引勧業債券, the eleventh issue of premium-bearing discount debentures of the 日本勧業銀行 (Nippon Kangyō Ginkō, the Hypothec Bank of Japan). The terms are stated plainly: purchase at ten yen, redemption always at twenty (買ふ時は十円・還へる時はいつも二十円), entirely free of tax, with subscriptions open from the fifteenth to the eighteenth of July. As a discount bond it carried no coupon; instead, semi-annual drawings (first in October, thereafter each April and October) distributed prize money (割増金), the first prize reaching three thousand yen, with thirty such prizes, three hundred of one hundred yen, a thousand lesser awards, and more in later rounds. Issued by this government-chartered special bank to gather small savings for agricultural and land credit, the poster sets a luminous Mount Fuji against deep blue, the unadorned modern lettering and flat color recalling contemporary commercial design, lending an instrument of public credit the appeal of a popular lottery.
creator: 日本勤業銀行 (Japan Industrial Bank)
notes: 日本勤業銀行 第十面割引勤業債券 (Japan Industrial Bank 10th Discount Bond), 3,000 yen

title: Republic of China Third Lottery Bond (第叁次有獎公債)
description: This is a lottery bond (有獎公債) of five yuan face value issued by the Ministry of Finance of the National Government of the Republic of China (國民政府財政部), dated the first day of the eighth month of the sixteenth year of the Republic—1 August 1927. In lieu of ordinary fixed interest, such instruments rewarded holders through periodic prize drawings, blending the appeal of a lottery with the security of sovereign debt to coax savings from a cautious public. The timing is telling: issued at Nanjing in the months after the Northern Expedition delivered the city to the Nationalists, it served the fledgling Kuomintang regime's urgent need for revenue. The design carries this political claim openly, framing a portrait of Sun Yat-sen between crossed party and national flags above an ornate engraved border, the founder's image lending the new government's paper the authority of its revolutionary origin.
creator: 國民政府財政部 (Republic of China Ministry of Finance)
notes: Republic of China Third Lottery Bond (第叁次有獎公債), 5 yuan No. 0675928, August 1, 1927, with Sun Yat-sen portrait
transcription:
# Third Lottery Bond of the Ministry of Finance, National Government of the Republic of China (Five Yuan) — Transcription

## Original (Chinese):

(Layout: horizontal note. Top center — oval portrait of Sun Yat-sen flanked by two crossed Nationalist "Blue Sky, White Sun" flags. Below the portrait a horizontal heading band, then a single-line date band, then a central ornate denomination panel. A block of fine print in vertical right-to-left columns fills the right side (headed 條例摘要) and continues down the left side, ending in the signature. Serial number in red along the lower edge. Text is traditional characters; vertical columns read top-to-bottom, right-to-left.)

**國民政府財政部第叁次有獎公債** (heading band, right-to-left)

**中華民國十六年八月一日發行** (date band, right-to-left)

**伍圓** (central denomination panel: 伍 above, 圓 below; the numeral 伍 also appears in the corner medallions)

**條例摘要** (heading of the fine-print block, right-hand side)

一　國民政府為津貼罷工委員會及維持工友生活起見先籌借現款五百萬元發給茲特由財政部發行第三次有獎公債五百萬元為償還本項借款之用

二　此項有獎公債以廣東人口附加二五稅為還本給獎之基金並由中央銀行擔負償還責任

三　此項有獎公債之還本辦法分三期每十個月為一期第一期每一個月還本百分之二第二期每一個月還本百分之三第三期每一個月還本百分之五第二十個月全數還清每屆還本由財政部抽籤定之

四　此項有獎公債之利息定為每月五萬元卽以此項利息全數撥充獎金不再另行給息

五　此項有獎公債之獎金計一等獎一張給獎金貳萬元二等獎二張各給獎金壹萬元三等獎四張各給獎金伍百元四等獎十張各給獎金壹百元五等獎二十張各給獎金伍拾元六等獎五十張各給獎金叁拾元七等獎壹百張各給獎金拾伍元八等獎一百五十張各給獎金拾元九等獎三百張各給獎金五元十等獎除前列九等獎外其餘每張中獎債票各給獎金五毫

六　此項有獎公債於中籤給獎時卽連信本一併發還自定期給獎還本之日起如逾期六個月仍不領欵者該債票卽作無效

七　此項有獎公債得爲銀行之保證準備金

八　此項有獎公債之還本給獎由財政部委託中央銀行及其他殷實商號或各征收機關按期支付

九　此項有獎公債爲無記名式得由買賣抵押並得作爲公務上繳納保證金之擔保品及中央銀行抵押欵之抵押品

十　經理此項有獎公債之官吏或其他商民對於此項公債如有損毀信用之行爲者照刑律治罪

## English Translation:

**Third Lottery Bond of the Ministry of Finance, National Government** (Guómín Zhèngfǔ Cáizhèngbù Dì-sān-cì Yǒujiǎng Gōngzhài)

**Issued on the 1st day of the 8th month of the 16th year of the Republic of China** (1 August 1927)

**Five Yuan** (伍圓)

**Summary of Regulations**

1. In order to subsidize the Strike Committee (罷工委員會) and to maintain the livelihood of the workers, the National Government (Guómín Zhèngfǔ) first raised a cash loan of 5,000,000 yuan for disbursement; the Ministry of Finance therefore specially issues this Third Lottery Bond of 5,000,000 yuan to repay the said borrowing.

2. This lottery bond takes the 2.5% surcharge tax (附加二五稅) on the Guangdong (Guǎngdōng) population as its fund for repayment of principal and payment of prizes, and the Central Bank (Zhōngyāng Yínháng) bears responsibility for repayment.

3. Repayment of principal is divided into three periods, each of ten months being one period: in the first period 2% of principal is repaid each month, in the second period 3% each month, in the third period 5% each month, with the whole amount redeemed by the twentieth month; each repayment is determined by drawing lots conducted by the Ministry of Finance.

4. The interest on this bond is fixed at 50,000 yuan per month; this entire interest is allocated to prize money, and no separate interest is otherwise paid.

5. The prizes of this bond are: 1st prize — 1 ticket, prize of 20,000 yuan; 2nd prize — 2 tickets, 10,000 yuan each; 3rd prize — 4 tickets, 500 yuan each; 4th prize — 10 tickets, 100 yuan each; 5th prize — 20 tickets, 50 yuan each; 6th prize — 50 tickets, 30 yuan each; 7th prize — 100 tickets, 15 yuan each; 8th prize — 150 tickets, 10 yuan each; 9th prize — 300 tickets, 5 yuan each; 10th prize — apart from the nine classes listed above, every other winning bond ticket receives a prize of 5 jiao (0.5 yuan).

6. When this bond wins in the draw, the principal is returned together in full at that time; counting from the fixed date of prize payment and principal repayment, if it is overdue by six months and still unclaimed, the bond ticket becomes void.

7. This lottery bond may serve as a bank's guarantee reserve fund.

8. The repayment of principal and payment of prizes for this bond is entrusted by the Ministry of Finance to the Central Bank and to other substantial merchant houses or to the various revenue-collection offices, to be paid on schedule.

9. This bond is in bearer form; it may be bought, sold, and pledged, and may be used as collateral for guarantee deposits in public/official matters and as collateral for Central Bank loans.

10. Any official or other merchant or civilian managing this bond who commits an act damaging its credit shall be punished according to the criminal law.

Minister of Finance of the National Government, **Gu Yingfen** (古應芬) (signature)

(Seal: red square seal beneath the signature — illegible.)

title: 真裕地產股份有限公司 (Zhenyu Real Estate Co., Ltd.) Share Certificate, China, 1944
description: This is a share certificate of 真裕地產股份有限公司 (Zhenyu Real Estate Company, Limited), a joint-stock land and property concern of Republican China. The certificate represents 500 shares at a par value of ten yuan each — 國幣伍仟圓整, five thousand yuan in national currency (fabi) — drawn against an authorized capital of fifty million yuan divided into five million shares, and is registered to the shareholder 宏業堂 (Hongyetang). It is dated the twelfth month of the thirty-third year of the Republic, that is December 1944, and was issued under the signatures of the chairman and managing directors. The staggering nominal capitalization speaks less to the firm's real worth than to the runaway wartime inflation of the national currency in the final year of the Sino-Japanese War, when prices in unoccupied China rose at a ruinous pace. The engraved vignette sets a modern stepped tower beside a sweep of traditional palatial roofs, joining old and new to advertise a company trading in urban land.
creator: 裕真地產有限股份公司
notes: 裕真地產有限股份公司 (Keh-Yul Land Co.), No. 012430, 500 shares

title: Carlist Five-Percent Perpetual Rente (Ouvrard Loan), Oñate
description: This is a certificate of the Cinco Por Ciento Español, a five-percent perpetual rente (renta perpetua) issued not by the reigning government in Madrid but in the name of Don Carlos, the legitimist pretender styled "S.M.C. el Señor Don Carlos V," under his Real Decreto of 6 February 1836. Signed at Oñate—the seat of the Carlist court—on 6 February 1836, it was contracted by the French financier G. J. Ouvrard, whose signature appears as "El Contratante." The certificate represents an annual rente of forty-seven pesos fuertes (with parallel valuations of 940 dollars, 2,400 florins, 200 pounds sterling, and 5,076 francs), payable in Oro ó Plata at the Tesorería Real de Madrid or, at the holder's option, at Amsterdam, London, Paris, Brussels, and Turin. Tellingly, the rente is to begin running only after Don Carlos enters Madrid: an instrument whose value was wagered entirely on the outcome of the First Carlist War, financing a contested claimant against the security of his eventual victory.
creator: Government of Spain
notes: Cinco Por Ciento Español (5% Spanish Perpetual Rente), Primera Serie, Oñate, February 6, 1860, payable in Madrid/London/Paris/Brussels/Turin, with coupon strips
transcription:
# Transcription: Cinco Por Ciento Español (Spanish 5% Perpetual Rente)

## Original Text:

**HEADER:**

Cinco Por Ciento Español
RENTAS PERPETUAS,
PAGADERAS

EN MADRID, á la eleccion del Portador,

EN AMSTERDAM, LONDRES, PARIS, BRUSELAS Ó TURIN.

PRIMERA SERIE.

No. 2460

**CAPITAL AND RENTA SECTIONS:**

Capital:
- Nros Flor. 2,400
- Nro. 200
- Nro. 5,076
- Total: 8,940

Renta Anual:
- Nro. Flor. 120 en Amst.
- Nro. £ 10 en Londres
- Nro. [amount] en París
- Nro. 55.50 en Florines

**MAIN TEXT:**

"El presente Certificado, desde que los pagos que se especifican á continuacion, que han sido estipulados por el Real Decreto dado este día en Oñate á S. M. C. el Señor Don CARLOS V. representará una Renta anual y perpetua de CIENTO Y SIETE PESOS FUERTES de ocho Reales de Vellon cada uno, pagaderos en la Tesorería Real de Madrid, en especies de Oro ó Plata, y se recibirá en las inscripciones activa, que se establecerá, con preferencia á todas las demás obligaciones en el erario de la Deuda Pública de España, en virtud del mencionado Decreto de este día.

Esta renta anual y perpetua imprescriptible á correr desde primero de 1856, y después de la entrada de S. M. C. el Señor Don CARLOS V. en Madrid, á 4 de eleccion del Portador, en Amsterdam, Londres, París, Bruselas ó Turin, las cuales Bolsas y en los casos indicados al pie del presente Certificado, en virtud de las entregas de los adjuntos pagos.

El Contratante;
[Signatures present but largely illegible]

Oñate, 6 de Febrero de 1856."

**COUPON SECTION (Right side):**

Multiple coupon strips listing:
- "CINCO POR CIENTO ESPAÑOL"
- "Un Ceros en VEINTE Y TRES PESOS FUERTES Y MEDIO, pagadero en Madrid ó en Amsterdam, Londres, Paris, Bruselas ó Turin,"
- Various dates and serial numbers (Nº. 2460, 2459, 2458, etc.)
- Recurring text about payment locations: "en Amsterdam, Londres, París, Bruselas ó Turin, en las mismas Bolsas y en los casos indicados al pie del adjunto Certificado."

**FOOTER NOTES:**

Payment instructions for various European cities:
- En Amsterdam, a razon de 120 florines por $ 7
- En Londres, en cada de los Banqueros de S.M.C., a razon de 10 libras esterlinas por $ 7
- En París,... á razon de...francos per $ 7
- En Bruselas,... á razon de... francos, per $ 47
- En Turin,... á razon de... liras, igual de valor per $ 7

---

## English Translation:

**HEADER:**

Spanish 5% [Perpetual Rente]
PERPETUAL INCOMES,
PAYABLE

IN MADRID, at the election of the Bearer,

IN AMSTERDAM, LONDON, PARIS, BRUSSELS OR TURIN.

FIRST SERIES.

No. 2460

**MAIN TEXT:**

"The present Certificate, from the time that the payments specified in continuation have been stipulated by the Royal Decree issued this day in Oñate to H.M.C. the Lord Don CARLOS V., shall represent an annual and perpetual income of ONE HUNDRED AND SEVEN STRONG PESOS of eight Reales of Vellon each, payable in the Royal Treasury of Madrid, in species of Gold or Silver, and shall be received in the active inscriptions that shall be established, with preference to all other obligations in the Treasury of the Public Debt of Spain, by virtue of the aforementioned Decree of this day.

This annual and perpetual, non-prescriptible income to run from the first [of the year] 1856, and after the entry of H.M.C. the Lord Don CARLOS V. in Madrid, on the 4th, at the election of the Bearer, in Amsterdam, London, Paris, Brussels or Turin, which Stock Exchanges and in the cases indicated at the foot of the present Certificate, by virtue of the delivery of the attached payments.

The Contractor;
[Signatures]

Oñate, 6th of February, 1856."

**COUPON SECTION:**

SPANISH 5% [RENTE]
"Twenty-three Pesos and One-Half Strong, payable in Madrid or in Amsterdam, London, Paris, Brussels or Turin, in the same Stock Exchanges and in the cases indicated at the foot of the attached Certificate."

---

## Notable Markings:

- **Serial Number:** No. 2460 (main certificate)
- **Date:** Oñate, 6 de Febrero de 1856 (February 6, 1856)
- **Signatures:** Present but largely illegible due to image quality
- **Royal Seal:** Visible in upper right ornamental border
- **Coupon Numbers:** Sequential (2460, 2459, 2458, etc.)
- **Ornamental Borders:** Decorative spiral patterns in left and right margins
- **Print Quality:** Period engraving with fine detail work typical of 19th-century bond certificates

title: Citrus Belt Land Company Stock Certificate, California, 1911
description: This is a stock certificate for fifty shares, par value one hundred dollars each, in the Citrus Belt Land Company, a corporation organized under the laws of California with a capital stock of $250,000. Issued on the 26th day of January 1911 and signed by the company's secretary and by its president, Geo. R. Murdock, it conveys an ownership stake transferable on the books of the corporation upon surrender of the certificate. The company belonged to the wave of land enterprises that subdivided and marketed the irrigated citrus districts of San Bernardino and Riverside counties, where railroad access and water projects turned semi-arid range into orange groves and townsites. The engraved vignette of a helmeted female figure crowned "E PLURIBUS UNUM," bearing a sword and the national shield, lends a martial, patriotic dignity to a speculative agricultural venture, dressing real-estate promotion in the iconography of the Republic.
creator: Citrus Belt Land Company
notes: Citrus Belt Land Company, California, 50 shares, No. 426, S.J. White, January 26, 1911

title: Clădirea Românească (Romanian Building Society) Share Certificate
description: A nominative share certificate of Clădirea Românească, Societate Anonimă, a Romanian building and real-estate company seated in Bucharest. The document conveys 50 registered shares (acțiuni nominative) of 500 lei each, totalling 25.000 lei, drawn from the sixth emission (Emisiunea VI-a) against a paid-up capital of 600.000.000 lei. It is dated Bucharest, January 1946 (București, Ianuarie 1946) and bears manuscript signatures of the administrators and the council president. Issued in the unsettled first months after the war, as Romania moved under Soviet pressure toward communist control, the certificate documents a private capital-raising on the eve of the nationalisations of 1947–1948 that would extinguish such ventures. The engraved face is dominated by a crowned woman in medieval dress cradling a building, an allegory of the society's name and purpose set within a dense Gothic-revival frame of pointed arches and interlace.
creator: Clădirea Românească S.A.
notes: Clădirea Românească S.A., 50 nominative shares of 500 lei each (Nos. 1095201-1095250), Emission VI-a, Capital 600,000,000 lei, Bucharest, January 1946

title: Colombian India-Rubber Exploration Company Limited Share Warrant
description: A bearer share warrant (titre au porteur) of The Colombian India-Rubber Exploration Company, Limited — the Société d'Exploration pour le Caoutchouc en Colombie — incorporated under the Companies' Acts, 1862 to 1895, with a nominal capital of £300,000 divided into 300,000 fully paid ordinary shares of £1 each. This instrument entitles its bearer to twenty-five such shares, the warrant given under the common seal of the company on the 4th day of March 1907 and impressed with a duty stamp of seventy shillings and sixpence. Printed in parallel English and French, it speaks to an Edwardian enterprise floated to work wild-rubber concessions in Colombia, when South American latex still fetched premium prices before East Asian plantation rubber overtook the trade. The dense foliate border, rendered in blue upon a yellow ground, frames the share as a thing of cultivated value while gesturing, fittingly, toward the tropical vegetation from which the venture hoped to profit.
creator: Colombian India-Rubber Exploration Company Limited
notes: Colombian India-Rubber Exploration Company Limited, Share Warrant 25 shares (188,476-188,500), March 4, 1907

title: Compagnie d'Inguaran (Mexique), Part de Bénéfices
description: This is a Part de Bénéfices au Porteur (bearer profit-sharing share) of the Compagnie d'Inguaran, a French société anonyme constituted to work mining concessions in Mexico. The certificate carries no fixed par value; instead it entitles the bearer to 1/14,000 of 40% of the company's net profits. The capital stands at three million five hundred thousand francs, divided into 7,000 shares of 500 francs each, with the registered office in Paris. It was issued at Paris and dated 15 January 1898, following statutes drawn before the notaries Marc and Portefin in December 1897 and assemblies of 1 and 15 January 1898. The Inguaran district of Michoacán was long noted for copper and silver, and the company belongs to the wave of French capital drawn to Mexican mining under Porfirio Díaz, when the government courted foreign investment. The bénéfice (profit) share, separate from the voting action, let promoters distribute a slice of expected returns without diluting control. The dense engraved border and classicising frame project the solidity such speculative ventures sought to advertise.
creator: Compagnie d'Inguaran

title: Compagnie Occidentale de Madagascar Share, Paris, 1910
description: An engraved bearer share (action de cent francs au porteur, entièrement libérée) in the Compagnie Occidentale de Madagascar, a société anonyme with its registered office in Paris. The certificate records a capital of three million two hundred thousand francs, divided into 32,000 shares of 100 francs, while an overprinted stamp at the head raises that capital to 7,000,000 francs. The statutes, dated 4 May and 17 June 1895 and revised by general assemblies through 30 June 1910, situate the company within France's commercial expansion into Madagascar, annexed as a colony in 1896. Issued in Paris and dated 16 September 1910, the share funded enterprise on the island's western coast, whose grasslands favoured stock-raising and the export of hides. A finely engraved border of scrollwork, shells and foliate ornament, in the ornate Belle Époque manner of Parisian securities, frames the text; the embossed fiscal seal of the Republic, bearing scales, attests the stamp duty.
creator: Compagnie Occidentale de Madagascar
notes: Compagnie Occidentale de Madagascar, Action de Cent Francs No. 16,102, Paris, ca. 1910s

title: Compañía Salitrera de Tarapacá y Antofagasta share certificate, Santiago, 1937
description: This bearer share certificate represents ten acciones, each of fifty pesos nominal value and fully paid ("totalmente pagadas"), in the Compañía Salitrera de Tarapacá y Antofagasta, a Chilean nitrate concern. The text records an authorized capital of 209,247,800 pesos divided into 4,184,956 shares and a corporate life of fifty years, the company having been constituted by public deeds of 6, 7, and 10 July 1934 and authorized by Supreme Decree No. 2094 of 11 July 1934. Issued at Santiago on 30 April 1937, it dates from the consolidation that followed the collapse of Chile's natural-nitrate trade, after synthetic nitrogen fixation eroded the salitre fields of the northern deserts. The engraved vignette of a sower broadcasting seed across a furrowed field invokes the fertilizer to which Chilean nitrate owed its value, casting the enterprise as a servant of agricultural abundance.
creator: Compañía Salitrera de Tarapacá y Antofagasta
notes: Compañía Salitrera de Tarapacá y Antofagasta, 10 shares at 50 pesos, No. C30095, Santiago, April 30, 1937, with coupon sheet

title: Companhia de Mossamedes Share Warrant to Bearer for Five Shares
description: This is a bearer share warrant (Título ao Portador / Titre au Porteur) of the Companhia de Mossamedes, a Portuguese société anonyme à responsabilité limitée, certifying that the holder owns five fully paid shares of Escudos 4.50 each. It forms part of a capital of 13,995,000 escudos divided into 3,110,000 shares, authorised by extraordinary general assemblies cited on the face as 23 January and 12 July 1901 and 21 March 1927. Printed trilingually in Portuguese, French, and English, the warrant courted international subscribers for a venture in Moçâmedes (now Namibe), a port in southern Angola, then Portuguese West Africa. Its three columns and engraved guilloche borders, struck by the Paris house Charles Skipper & East, present the security in the restrained late-Victorian commercial idiom favoured for bearer instruments meant to circulate across European bourses.
creator: Companhia de Mossamedes
notes: Companhia de Mossamedes, Share Warrant for 5 shares (Nos. 2272126-2272130), Esc. 4.50 each, Capital 13,995,000 Esc.

title: Compañía Anónima Minas de Guanandi Share
description: A bearer share (título de una acción al portador) in the Compañía Anónima Minas de Guanandi, with a face value of 500 pesos nacionales oro, drawn against a capital of 2,000,000 pesos oro divided into 4,000 shares. It was issued at Montevideo, dated 5 May, in the late 1880s. The printed text records that the company was authorized by Uruguayan governmental decree of 19 October 1887 to work fifty mineral concessions (datas minerales) in the municipality of Poconé, province of Mato Grosso, Brazil, granted under Brazilian Imperial Decree No. 9239 of 28 June 1884. The certificate documents the cross-border speculative mining ventures of the period, a Montevideo-chartered company holding Brazilian gold-bearing concessions and raising capital from anonymous bearers. The engraved frame, with its medallion of crossed mining tools and classical scrollwork, lends the prospect of frontier extraction the reassuring dignity of an established enterprise.
creator: Compañía Anónima Minas de Guanandi
notes: Compañía Anónima Minas de Guanandi, 1 share 500 Pesos ¾ Oro, No. 2980, Capital 2,000,000 Pesos, Montevideo 1887; concessions in Mato Grosso, Brazil

title: Madrid-Zaragoza-Alicante Railway Company Share
description: This engraved bearer share certifies one acción of 1,900 reales vellón, equivalent to 500 francs, in the Compañía de los Ferro-Carriles de Madrid a Zaragoza y a Alicante (the MZA), printed bilingually in Spanish and French and dated at Madrid, 1 July 1859. The text records a company capital of 456,000,000 reales vellón (120,000,000 francs) divided into 240,000 shares, and notes that the title replaces an earlier one of the same number and date. The dual denomination and language announce the road's reliance on French capital, the engine behind much of Spain's mid-century railway boom under the 1855 general railway law. The engraving is dense and panoramic: topographic city vistas of the line's termini crowned by the Spanish royal arms, framed by allegories of commerce and industry, presenting the railway as an instrument of national integration linking Madrid to the Mediterranean.
creator: Compañía de los Ferro-Carriles de Madrid a Zaragoza y a Alicante

title: Compañía La Petrolífera Nacional Bearer Share, San Sebastián, 1928
description: This engraved bearer share (título al portador) represents a single Series A share of 500 pesetas in the Compañía La Petrolífera Nacional, a sociedad anónima constituted by public deed before the notary Emilio Fernández Sánchez on 29 September 1924. Issued at San Sebastián on 15 May 1928, with its registered office at Plaza del Centenario 5, the company carried an authorised capital of 100,000,000 pesetas, divided into 140,000 Series A shares of 500 pesetas and 600,000 Series B shares of 50 pesetas. The vignette of an oil derrick spouting a dark plume above a coastal works advertises the enterprise's purpose, capturing Spanish ambitions in petroleum exploration during the 1920s, when the country sought a domestic foothold in an industry the great powers had come to dominate after the Great War. The decorative green border and ornamental lettering reflect the conventional engraver's idiom of contemporary share certificates.

title: Compañía Petrolera Mexicana "Faros de Aztlán" Share Certificate
description: A bearer share certificate of the Compañía Petrolera Mexicana "Faros de Aztlán", S.A., representing fifty shares of one peso oro nacional each, fully paid, drawn on a capital of 250,000 pesos oro nacional divided into 250,000 shares. The company, domiciled in Ciudad de México and constituted for a term of fifty years, was incorporated before Notario Público No. 14, Lic. Francisco Merino Ortiz, on 24 June 1916; this title was issued in Mexico City on 1 October 1916. The certificate dates from the Carranza presidency, when the Constitutionalist regime was tightening its claim over subsoil petroleum even as new oil ventures multiplied; such modestly capitalized companies sought to enlist domestic savings in the booming Gulf-coast oil trade. Its nationalist name and imagery — a beam-casting lighthouse rising over oil derricks, flanked by a plumed Aztec figure and borders of stepped Mesoamerican fretwork — bind the enterprise to an indigenist mythology of Aztlán, the legendary homeland.
creator: Compañía Petrolera Mexicana "Faros de Aztlán"

title: Compañía de las Hulleras de Ujo-Mieres Share Certificate
description: This bilingual bearer share certificate (resguardo al portador / titre au porteur), printed in parallel Spanish and French, conveys five fully paid-up shares of 25 Pesetas Oro each in the Compañía de las Hulleras de Ujo-Mieres, Concesiones Reunidas de Ujo-Mieres y Riosa, a Spanish joint-stock company with a capital of 15,000,000 Pesetas Oro divided into 600,000 shares. The text records that the company was constituted by public deed on 3 December 1904 before Don José Menéndez y de Parra, notary at Madrid, with its registered seat at Ujo in Asturias. The instrument belongs to the financing of the Asturian coalfields of northern Spain, the country's foremost coal-mining district, whose output supplied the era's railways and iron industry. The engraved vignettes—muscular miners hewing at the seam above, a smoking pithead works below—frame the enterprise heroically, casting subterranean labour as the source of the shareholder's gold-denominated claim.
creator: Compañía de las Hulleras de Ujo-Mieres
notes: Compañía de las Hulleras de Ujo-Mieres, 5 shares 25 Pesetas Oro each (Nos. 370241-370245), No. 72049, Capital 15,000,000 Pesetas Oro, Madrid

title: Confederate States of America Cotton Loan Bond
description: This is a $1,000 coupon bond of the Confederate States of America, issued under the loan authorized by the Act of Congress approved 30 April 1863 — the so-called Cotton Loan. It carries interest at six per cent per annum and promises payment of the principal on the first day of June 1883, but with the telling provision that the Secretary of the Treasury may instead discharge it in cotton of the quality of New Orleans Middling. The engraved certificate is dated at Richmond the first day of June 1863 and bears the signature of the Register of the Treasury. Attached coupons, headed "C.S. Cotton Coupon Loan, April 30, 1863," each pay sixty dollars and name New Orleans, Mobile, Savannah, Charleston, Wilmington, Richmond or Norfolk as places of payment. The instrument reveals the Confederacy's strategy of pledging its staple crop to secure specie and foreign credit while the Union blockade strangled its ordinary commerce. A central portrait vignette and finely scrolled border lend it the conventional dignity of a sovereign obligation, an authority the issuing government would not outlive.
creator: Confederate States of America
notes: Confederate States of America, $1,000 Cotton Loan Bond, Act of Congress April 30, 1863, C.G. Memminger, Register signed
transcription:
# Transcription of Confederate States of America $1,000 Cotton Loan Bond (1863)

**CONFEDERATE STATES OF AMERICA**

**1000**

**LOAN AUTHORIZED BY ACT OF CONGRESS APPROVED APRIL 30, 1863.**

On the first day of June 1865, the Confederate States of America will pay to the Bearer of this BOND, at the Seat of Government, or at such place of deposit, as may be appointed by the Secretary of the Treasury, the Sum of ONE THOUSAND DOLLARS with interest thereon from the date hereof at seven per centum per annum, to be paid at the pleasure of the Government, at Richmond, or at the office of the Financial Agent of the Government in London at other places as the Secretary of the Treasury may direct. Coupons will be paid semi-annually at the places named above.

Interest Coupons attached hereto dated April 30, 1863, and numbered 1-4, or such supplementary [text unclear] to any Act of Congress approved April 30, 1863, entitled an "Act supplementary to an Act to provide for the funding and further issue of Treasury Notes."

In Witness whereof, the Registers of the Treasury in pursuance of the said Act of Congress hath hereunto set his hand and affixed the Seal of the Treasury at Richmond, this First day of June 1863.

[Signature] C.G. Memminger
Entered [illegible] N.M.
Recorded [illegible]

**REGISTER OF THE TREASURY**

---

**Bottom coupons (repeated four times):**

C.S. COTTON COUPON LOAN, APRIL 30, 1863.

The Confederate States of America will pay to bearer at New Orleans, Mobile, Savannah, Charleston, Wilmington, N.C. or Norfolk, SIXTY DOLLARS, for this year's interest due June 1, 1863, on a Bond for One Thousand Dollars of the Cotton Loan authorized by Act of Congress approved April 30, 1863.

---

**Notable markings:**
- Ornamental borders with wave patterns
- Portrait engraving (center top, likely a Confederate official)
- "1000" printed in four corners
- "ONE THOUSAND DOLLARS" on left panel
- Serial numbering areas (partially visible/illegible)
- Signature of C.G. Memminger, Secretary of the Treasury
- Four cotton coupon strips attached at bottom
- Official seals and decorative scrollwork throughout

title: Confederate States of America Bond
description: A $500 coupon bond of the Confederate States of America, issued under the loan authorized by Act of Congress of August 19, 1861. Dated at Richmond on 31 July 1862 and signed for the Register of the Treasury, it promises payment to bearer of five hundred dollars on 1 July 1869, with interest at eight per cent per annum payable semi-annually on surrender of the annexed coupons; the surviving strip of twenty-dollar warrants confirms the half-yearly schedule. The body pledges a "War Tax" for the debt's redemption. Issued in the second year of the war, when battlefield reverses had begun to outrun the early hope of swift European recognition, the bond shows the Treasury borrowing against future taxation rather than specie. The engraved vignette—an official's portrait flanked by allegorical figures, sailing ships, cotton bales, and a steam locomotive—binds together the staple, maritime commerce, and the railroad on which Confederate finance ultimately leaned.
creator: Confederate States of America
notes: Confederate States of America, $500 Bond, 8% per annum, Act of August 19, 1861, C.G. Memminger, with coupon strips
transcription:
# Confederate States of America $500 Bond - Transcription

**FIVE HUNDRED**

**CONFEDERATE STATES OF AMERICA**
**LOAN.**

**No. [blank]                                                                                                    No. [blank]**

AUTHORIZED BY THE ACT OF CONGRESS, C.S.A., AUGUST 19, 1861

On the first day of July, 1869, the Confederate States of America will pay to the bearer or registered owner thereof, or his legal representatives, the sum of **FIVE HUNDRED DOLLARS** with Interest thereon from this date, payable at the same place, semi-annually, at the rate of Eight per Cent, per annum, on surrender of the annexed Warrants or Coupons. This debt is authorized by an Act of Congress approved August 19, 1861, entitled "An Act to authorize the issue of Treasury Notes and to provide a War Tax for their redemption."

In Witness whereof His Majesty of the Treasury, in pursuance of the said Act of Congress hath hereunto set his hand and official seal of the Treasury at **RICHMOND**, this 31 day of July, 1862.

Entered A.T.L.                                                                    C.G. Memminger
Recorded, C.S.T.                                                                  Reg'r Register of the Treasury

[At bottom: Five coupon strips for "C.S. LOAN OF THE CONFEDERATE STATES OF AMERICA" dated "AUG 19 1862" and "Will pay to Bearer"]

title: Confederate States of America Bond
description: This is a $500 bond of the Confederate States of America, bearing interest at seven per cent per annum payable semi-annually upon surrender of the annexed coupons. Issued at Richmond and dated the second day of March 1863, it was authorized by the Act of Congress of February 20, 1863, entitled "An Act to authorize the issue of Bonds for funding Treasury Notes." The Confederate States promise to pay the bearer Five Hundred Dollars on the first day of July 1868, with the unusual proviso that payment may be deferred, period by period, up to thirty years at the same rate upon surrender of the bond. The instrument belongs to the Confederacy's effort to absorb its swelling stock of treasury notes and curb wartime inflation by converting paper currency into funded debt. The central vignette of a seated figure and a small locomotive scene below evoke the agrarian economy and rail lines on which the Southern war effort depended.
creator: Confederate States of America
notes: Confederate States of America, $500 Bond, 7% per annum, Act of February 20, 1863, dated Richmond March 2, 1863
transcription:
# Transcription

**Seven Per Cent. February 20th, 1863.**

**Confederate States of America Bond**

**Authorized by Act of Congress**                    **C.S.A. of February 20th, 1863.**

No. _______________                    No. _______________

On the first day of July, 1868, the Confederate States of America will pay to the Owner of his bond at the seat of Government, or at such place as may be appointed by the Secretary of the Treasury, the Sum of **Five Hundred Dollars** with interest thereon from date at the rate of Seven per Cent. per annum, payable semi-annually on the maturities of the accrued Coupons. This Contract is authorized by An Act of Congress approved February 20th, 1863, Entitled "An Act to authorize the issue of Bonds for funding Treasury Notes, and to open the Express Condition that said Confederate States may from time to time extend the time of payment for any period not exceeding thirty years from this date at the same rate of interest upon the surrender of the Bond."

**In Witness Whereof** the Register of the Treasury in pursuance of said Act of Congress hath here under set his hand and affixed the Seal of the Treasury at Richmond this second day of March 1863.

Entered                                                     Register of the Treasury
Manifold

title: Société des Comptoirs d'Importation & d'Exportation "Congo-Cameroun" Share
description: This engraved bearer share (action au porteur, entièrement libérée) represents one fully paid-up share of 100 francs in the Société des Comptoirs d'Importation & d'Exportation "Congo-Cameroun", a société anonyme capitalised at 12,000,000 francs divided into 120,000 shares of 100 francs each. Its statutes were deposited at the étude of Maître Laeuffer, notary in Paris, where the company kept its siège social. The instrument bound metropolitan capital to commerce in French Equatorial Africa and the Cameroon mandate, financing trading posts (comptoirs) that exchanged European goods for tropical produce. The finely lithographed blue border, classical columns and the anchor device of the company seal advertise maritime trade and the solidity sought by a public subscription of small denominations. A notice records that the stamp duty was discharged by subscription under an authorisation published in the Journal Officiel of 3 April 1928.

title: Conseil de la Dette Publique Répartie de l'Ancien Empire Ottoman, Provisional Receipt for the Ottoman 5% Loan of 1914, Paris, 1933
description: This bearer provisional receipt (Récépissé Provisoire au Porteur) was issued in Paris by the Conseil de la Dette Publique Répartie de l'Ancien Empire Ottoman on 1 October 1933. It was delivered against the surrender of one unit obligation, nominal capital Frs. 500 (or £19,17, or Ltqs. 22), of the Ottoman 5% loan of 1914, and carries coupons numbered 1 to 12. A table of débiteur states apportions the loan's unamortised capital among the Empire's successors under Article 49 of the 1923 Treaty of Lausanne, listing the shares of Greece, Bulgaria, Transjordan (including the Caza of Maan), the Hedjaz, the Yemen, and Nedjd against a total of some 19,834,205 francs still outstanding on 804,675 unit titles. The instrument records, in sober administrative form, the dissolution of an empire's obligations into the apportioned debts of the states that replaced it.
creator: Conseil de la Dette Publique Répartie de l'Ancien Empire Ottoman
notes: Conseil de la Dette Publique de l'Ancien Empire Ottoman, Provisional Receipt No. 0,288,837, Ottoman 5% Emprunt of 1914, Paris October 9, 1933, with coupons 1-10
transcription:
# Transcription

**Original (French):**

CONSEIL DE LA DETTE PUBLIQUE RÉPARTIE DE L'ANCIEN EMPIRE OTTOMAN
RÉCÉPISSÉ PROVISOIRE AU PORTEUR N° 0,288,837
délivré contre retrait d'une obligation unitaire d'un capital nominal de Frs. 500 ou £ 19,17 ou Liqs. 22
DE L'EMPRUNT OTTOMAN 5% 1914.

Conformément à la décision en date du 9 Juin -1933 de la Commission pour la répartition du capital nominal de la Dette Publique Ottomane, instituée par l'Article 49 du Traité de Lausanne, le présent récépissé a été délivré contre retrait de l'obligation susvisée, pour constater :

a) que la quote-part de la Turquie a fait l'objet d'une émission spéciale ; que les quotes-parts de l'Italie (Dodécanèse), de la Palestine, de l'Irak et des États du Levant sous Mandat français ont été entièrement amorties ;

b) et que, pour celle ci cent régiments, à la date d'extinction du présent récépissé, le capital nominal reste seul engagé auprès de l'emprunteur ; que les montants indiqués dans le tableau ci-dessous, incarnant comptés États qui en sont les débiteurs à raison des montants conces en regard de chacun d'eux.

[TABLE showing debtor states and capital amounts for: GRÈCE, BULGARIE, TRANSJORDANIE (à compter de la Caisse de Masque), HONGRIE, ITALIE, NEDJED, with proportions and capital amounts in Frs., en £, en Liqs.]

TOTAL: 100,- | 19,833,296 | 797,418 | 872,705 | 3.93 | 0.98 | 1.09

Le présent récépissé provisoire est exclusivement seuls pour constater les droits des porteurs des titres retirés de la circulation sur les États débiteurs. Ottomans, indiqués ci-après. Ce titre provisoire ne pourra, par le moyen de cession de droits effectifs au moyen du présent récépissé provisoire, qui pourrait être effectuées au moyen du présent récépissé provisoire, tous aussi qualifié pour procéder aux placements ou autres opérations quotés-parts dans la Dette Ottomane. Les comptes conclus entre les États pour le règlement de leurs parts respectives des opérations, dont les modalités seront portées, en temps utile, à la connaissance des porteurs, par les publications du Conseil de la Dette.

POUR CONTRÔLE:
Par Déclaration du Conseil:              POUR LE CONSEIL DE LA DETTE PUBLIQUE RÉPARTIE
                                         DE L'ANCIEN EMPIRE OTTOMAN.

[Signatures present]                      Le Président:

Paris, le 16 Octobre 1933.

[Document continues with 10 numbered coupons below, each labeled "COUPON" with identical header text and reference number 0,288,837]

---

**English Translation:**

COUNCIL OF THE PUBLIC DEBT APPORTIONED FROM THE FORMER OTTOMAN EMPIRE
PROVISIONAL RECEIPT TO BEARER NO. 0,288,837
issued in exchange for the withdrawal of a unit obligation with nominal capital of Frs. 500 or £ 19.17 or Liqs. 22
FROM THE 5% 1914 OTTOMAN LOAN.

In accordance with the decision dated June 9, 1933 of the Commission for the apportionment of the nominal capital of the Ottoman Public Debt, established by Article 49 of the Treaty of Lausanne, the present receipt has been issued in exchange for the withdrawal of the aforementioned obligation, to establish:

a) that Turkey's share was the subject of a special issue; that the shares of Italy (Dodecanese), Palestine, Iraq and States under French Mandate were entirely amortized;

b) and that, as far as the others are concerned, as of the date of extinction of the present receipt, the nominal capital remains solely engaged with the borrower; that the amounts indicated in the table below, representing the States which are the debtors, in proportion to the amounts shown opposite each of them.

[TABLE of debtor states and amounts]

The present provisional receipt is exclusively for the purpose of establishing the rights of holders of securities withdrawn from circulation on the debtor States. Ottoman, indicated below. This provisional title may not, by means of assignment of rights, be effective by means of the present provisional receipt, which could be used as a means for the placement or other operations of shares in the Ottoman Debt. The accounts concluded between the States for the settlement of their respective shares of operations, the modalities of which will be made known, in due course, to the holders, by publications of the Debt Council.

FOR CONTROL:
By Declaration of the Council:           FOR THE COUNCIL OF THE PUBLIC DEBT APPORTIONED
                                         FROM THE FORMER OTTOMAN EMPIRE.

The President:

Paris, October 16, 1933.

---

**Notable Markings:**

- Official red circular stamp of the "Conseil de la Dette Publique" (appears multiple times)
- Serial number: **0,288,837** (consistent across all coupons)
- Date: **16 Octobre 1933** (October 16, 1933)
- Handwritten signatures present (appears to be initials/approval marks)
- Ten numbered coupons (1-10) attached below the main certificate, each numbered identically
- Ornamental border framing the entire document
- Text printed in elegant serif typeface typical of 1930s financial documents

title: State of South Carolina Consolidation Bond
description: This is a $500 six per cent Consolidation Bond of the State of South Carolina, secured by an annual tax, receivable for taxes, with interest payable semi-annually in January and July and principal falling due in July 1893. Issued in the early 1870s by South Carolina's Reconstruction government, the bond formed part of a sweeping program to consolidate and refund the debt amassed under the Republican administration, exchanging older obligations for new ones at a fixed rate. Its promise that the paper itself would be receivable for taxes reveals an issuer straining to shore up confidence in its credit. The engraved face, framed in dense foliate scrollwork and floral medallions with the oversized denomination set in ornamental cartouches, follows the conventional security-printing idiom of the period, where intricate ornament served as a guard against counterfeiting. After the Democratic "Redeemer" government took power in 1876, the state repudiated much of this Reconstruction debt, making such bonds a flashpoint in the financial reckoning of the post-war South.
creator: State of South Carolina
notes: State of South Carolina, Six Per Cent Consolidation Bond, $500, No. DP 1602, secured by annual tax, principal due July 1876

title: Continental Investment Co. Stock Certificate
description: This is a certificate for one hundred shares of Common Stock in Continental Investment Co., a holding company incorporated under the laws of Delaware, with each corner bearing the figure "100." Issued to John V. Lorimer and dated January 19, 1929, it was registered two days later, on January 21, 1929, by Wells Fargo Bank & Union Trust Co. of San Francisco, which served as transfer agent and registrar. Engraved at the bottom of a turbulent decade, the certificate belongs to the speculative climate that drove the New York market upward until the October 1929 crash; its dense legal text reserving the issuance of "successive series of Prior Preferred" stock reflects the layered capital structures of late-1920s holding and investment companies. The spread-winged American eagle perched at the center crown speaks the conventional iconographic language of national strength and probity that engravers lent to financial paper of the period.
creator: Continental Investment Co.
notes: Continental Investment Co., Delaware, 100 shares, Certificate c 53, John V. Lorimer, January 13, 1925

title: Dutch Coupons for American Land Shares, C.J.M. De Wolf, Antwerp
description: Two engraved interest coupons, each in the sum of fifty Guldens "wissel-geld" (exchange-money), drawn against a share certificate (Acte van Aendeel) worth f 1000 in "Landen in America" — lands in America. The upper coupon falls due on the first of June 1795, the lower on the first of June 1796; both are payable, pursuant to Articles 2 and 3 of the underlying deed, at the counting-house (Comptoire) of C.J.M. De Wolf in Antwerp. The instrument records the Dutch and Flemish appetite for American land speculation in the 1790s, when capital from the Low Countries flowed into vast tracts of the young republic's frontier through houses dealing in such shares. The sober typography, unadorned save for the swelling drop-capital "C," befits a fiscal coupon meant to be clipped and presented rather than displayed.
creator: C.J.M. De Wolf
notes: Two Dutch coupons No. 827, 50 Gulden each for f.1000 American land shares, payable at C.J.M. De Wolf, Antwerp, June 1795 and June 1796

title: Crédit Foncier Agricole du Maroc Share
description: This engraved bearer share (action au porteur) of 100 francs, fully paid (entièrement libérée), certifies a single participation in the Crédit Foncier Agricole du Maroc, a société anonyme with capital of 1,000,000 francs divided into ten thousand shares of one hundred francs. Its statutes were deposited in the study of Maître Lecuyer, notaire at Étampes, on 17 March 1920, with the registered office (siège social) at 92, Rue de Richelieu, Paris. Issued in 1920, the share belongs to the wave of Paris-based finance houses formed to channel metropolitan capital into the French Protectorate of Morocco, established in 1912, extending mortgage and agricultural credit to settler estates and land improvement. The orientalist vignette, with robed figures, camels and palms framed beneath a horseshoe arch and interlaced arabesque borders, advertises the colonial venture while its title and currency anchor the enterprise firmly in Paris.
creator: Crédit Foncier Agricole du Maroc

title: Connecticut Comptroller's Office Interest Certificate
description: This printed interest certificate, issued by the Comptroller's Office of the State of Connecticut and dated 23 February 1792, certifies that Solomon Ensign Junior is entitled to receive thirteen shillings and six pence (£0.13.6) in Lawful Money out of funds appropriated for the payment of interest on the liquidated debt of the state. Signed by the comptroller, A. Kingsbury, and printed at Hartford by Hudson and Goodwin, the slip is a routine fiscal instrument of the early republic: a small interest coupon disbursed to a holder of Connecticut's Revolutionary war debt. Its denomination in pounds, shillings, and pence underscores that lawful money was still reckoned in the colonial sterling account even after federal coinage had been authorized. The plain typographic border, lettered "INTEREST" and "ISSUED," eschews allegory for clerical economy, the visual register of a state methodically retiring its wartime obligations.
creator: State of Connecticut, Comptroller's Office
notes: Connecticut Comptroller's Office, Interest Certificate No. 13,729, £0.13.6 for Solomon Ensign Jr., February 23, 1792; printed by Hudson and Goodwin, Hartford

title: The Cuba Company Common Stock Certificate
description: A specimen common stock certificate of The Cuba Company, incorporated under the laws of the State of New Jersey, with the shares denominated in dollars and the value left blank for engraving. Voided across the lower field with "SPECIMEN" in red, it bears the imprint of two New York fiduciaries: Central Union Trust Company of New York as registrar and The National City Bank of New York as transfer agent, the date and signature lines standing empty at Jersey City. The Cuba Company, formed at the turn of the century by William Cornelius Van Horne to build the Cuba Railroad eastward from Santiago, drew North American capital into the island's sugar frontier, and the certificate's reliance on New York banks marks the channel through which that money flowed. The engraved vignette confirms the venture's purpose: yoked oxen haul a cart heaped with cut cane past palms while a laborer loads the harvest, an idealized scene of the plantation economy the road was meant to serve.
creator: The Cuba Company
notes: The Cuba Company, common stock specimen certificate, State of New Jersey; company built the Cuba Railroad ca. 1900

This certificate is not valid until countersigned [unclear] the transfer agent and registered by the registered agent.

title: Dale Land & Cattle Co. Stock Certificate
description: This engraved stock certificate conveys fifty shares of the capital stock of the Dale Land & Cattle Co., a concern incorporated under the laws of the State of Texas with a capital stock of $300,000 divided into 3,000 shares of $100 each. The certificate is made out to J. B. Dale Sr. and dated April 1903, signed by B. A. McKinney as secretary and J. B. Dale as president, beneath the gold embossed seal of the company. The printed places of business are Bonham and Henrietta, Texas, with headquarters of ranches in Clay County. Issued as Texas ranching consolidated under the laws of incorporation, the share funded land and livestock operations across the Red River prairies and Cross Timbers in the years after the open range had closed. The central vignette of a mounted herder driving longhorn cattle past a homestead advertises the enterprise's assets and the pastoral economy underwriting the shares.
creator: Dale Land & Cattle Co.
notes: Dale Land & Cattle Co., 50 shares $100 each, No. 8, J.B. Dale Sr., Bonham & Henrietta/Clay County Texas, March 20, 1895

title: Danforth, Scudder & Co., Business Receipt
description: This printed commercial receipt acknowledges payment of two hundred and seven and 84/100 dollars ($207.84) received from D. T. Saunders, paid on account per the hands of Bradford & Anthony. Issued at Boston and dated July 8th, 1872, it bears the imprint of Danforth, Scudder & Co. and is signed for the firm by J. R. Mathews. The lithographed form, produced by Leonard B. Wilder & Co., Printers & Stationers of 66 Congress Street, Boston, doubles as a trade advertisement: a finely engraved stag dominates the left margin while a small flock of sheep grazes at lower centre, the pastoral menagerie hinting at a house dealing in hides, wool, or related goods. Such ornamented forms reflect a mercantile culture in which even a routine acknowledgment of payment served to display a firm's standing and craftsmanship.
creator: Danforth, Scudder & Co.
notes: Danforth, Scudder & Co. (Boston), receipt $207.84, D.T. Saunders, July 8, 1872; printed by Leonard, Millder & Co.

title: Ostend Company (Generale Keijserlijcke Indische Compagnie) Share Subscription Receipt
description: A manuscript subscription receipt issued by the directors of the Generale Keijserlijcke Indische Compagnie — the Imperial, or Ostend, Company — instructing their cashier Jan Baptist Cogels junior to receive in cash from Anna Rosalia de Bruijn van Holst the sum stated as "twee hondert [acht-en?]vijftig Guldens wisselgelt," being the first payment of twenty-five per cent ("vijfentwintigh ten hondert") upon one share of one thousand gulden in the Company's capital, on the conditions set out at length in its charter ("Octroy"). Dated at Antwerp the sixteenth of August 1723. The document belongs to the brief, contested life of the company chartered by Emperor Charles VI in 1722 to pursue the East India and China trade from the Austrian Netherlands, a venture the maritime powers soon forced into suspension. The upper-left engraved arms, surmounted by the imperial double-headed eagle, assert that sovereign authority and lend the private subscription the dignity of a state enterprise.
creator: Generale Keijserlijcke Indische Compagnie (Ostend Company)
notes: Ostend Company (Generale Keijserlijcke Indische Compagnie) share subscription receipt, 250 Gulden first installment (25% of 1,000 Gulden share), Anna Rosalia de Bruijn van Holst, Antwerp August 16, 1723

title: Grand Society of Russian Railways (Главное Общество Российских Железных Дорог) Silver Bond
description: This is a 3% bearer bond (облигація) of 125 silver metallic rubles issued by the Grand Society of Russian Railways (Главное Общество Россійскихъ Желѣзныхъ Дорогъ), seated in St. Petersburg, from its Third Issue of 105,176 bonds totalling 13,147,000 silver rubles. The face is rendered in five currencies — 500 francs, 20 pounds sterling, 402 German marks, 236 Dutch guilders — yielding 3 rubles 75 kopecks a year, payable each 1/13 June and 1/13 December. Dated St. Petersburg, 23 December 1880 / 4 January 1881, it draws on the Society's 1857 imperial charter to fund Russia's trunk-line network. The multilingual denomination and listed paying-agents in Paris, Berlin, London and Brussels show a deliberate appeal to Western European capital. The dense engraved vignette of reclining allegorical figures and classical ornament lends the security a reassuring air of permanence.
creator: Grand Society of Russian Railways (Главное Общество Российских Железных Дорог)

in shares: 600,000 shares of 125 silver rubles = 75,000,000 silver rubles (metallic);
