title: N.V. De Woning Maatschappij (Housing Company), Dividend Talon
description: A renewal talon ("TALON ter verkrijging van nieuwe dividendbewijzen") together with its attached sheet of dividend coupons, Nos. 46 through 56, belonging to share No. 1499 of the Naamlooze Vennootschap "De Woning Maatschappij", a limited-liability housing company established at Djakarta in the Netherlands East Indies. Each coupon repeats the issuer's name and seat; the talon at the top is the stub a holder surrendered to claim a fresh coupon sheet once the previous one was spent. The high serial range of the coupons points to a company already long in the habit of distributing dividends, the talon marking yet another cycle of disbursement. The engraving is plain and utilitarian, an ornamental guilloché border framing letterpress text, the restrained style typical of Dutch colonial company paper, where the document's purpose was clerical rather than commemorative.

title: Italian Public Debt Rendita Certificate
description: This certificate inscribes a holding in the Gran Libro del Debito Pubblico del Regno d'Italia, the consolidated ledger of the Kingdom of Italy's public debt. It registers a 5 per cent Rendita — a perpetual government annuity — yielding 20 lire a year, payable in two semi-annual instalments on 1 January and 1 July. The body invokes the founding Legge 10 luglio 1861, the law that constituted the Grand Book for the newly unified kingdom, and the certificate is issued from Florence ("Firenze"), dated 5 August 1864. A portrait vignette of King Victor Emmanuel II crowns the sheet, flanked by an engraved cityscape; the impersonal, ledger-like typography and the sovereign's image bind creditor to crown. Multiple cancellation and transfer stamps, including "ANNULLATO," record its later retirement. The instrument exemplifies how the young kingdom funded itself on perpetual consolidated debt, following the French and British rente model.
creator: Kingdom of Italy, Ministry of Finance
notes: Debito Pubblico del Regno d'Italia, 5% Rendita, annual income 20 lire, issued from Florence; portrait of Victor Emmanuel II

title: Deli Railway Company Share
description: This is a bewijs van aandeel (share certificate) for one share of one thousand Gulden in the Naamlooze Vennootschap Deli-Spoorweg-Maatschappij (Deli Railway Company), a limited company capitalised at ten million Gulden. Issued at Amsterdam on 5 May 1914 and signed by the President-Commissaris and a Director, it traces the firm to its founding deed of 28 June 1883 and a chain of statutory amendments confirmed by Royal Decree through 5 September 1913. The certificate funded a railway across the Deli district of East Sumatra in the Dutch East Indies, the artery serving the celebrated tobacco and rubber estates whose thin-leaf wrapper tobacco fetched premium prices abroad. The engraved Dutch royal arms and restrained typographic border, printed by Joh. Enschedé en Zonen of Haarlem, lend the instrument the sober authority of metropolitan finance underwriting a colonial enterprise.
creator: Deli-Spoorweg-Maatschappij
notes: Deli-Spoorweg-Maatschappij (Deli Railway Company, Sumatra), 1,000 Gulden share No. 7390, Capital 10,000,000 Gulden, Amsterdam, May 5, 1914

THE HOLDER hereof is entitled to One Share, amount ONE THOUSAND GULDEN, in the Private Company Deli Railway Company, established by deed, dated 28 June 1888, approved in draft by Royal Decree of 29 May 1888 No. 69, amended by deed, dated 17 December 1888, approved in draft by Royal Decree of 19 December 1888 No. 31, amended by deed, dated 29 June 1897, approved in draft by Royal Decree of 7 June 1897 No. 13, amended by deed, dated 5 August 1905, approved in draft by Royal Decree of 21 July 1905 No. 50, amended by deed, dated 2 September 1912, approved in draft by Royal Decree of 20 August 1912 No. 43, lastly amended by deed, dated 15 September 1913, approved in draft by Royal Decree of 5 September 1913 No. 16.

title: Société Belge d'Exportation (Jules Van der Laat & Cie) Action de Fondation, Antwerp
description: This is a founder's share (action de fondation) of the Société Belge d'Exportation, a commandite par actions—a limited partnership by shares—trading under the raison sociale Jules Van der Laat & Cie, with its siège social at Anvers (Antwerp). The capital social of 5,000,000 francs is divided into 10,000 shares of 500 francs each. Issued sans détermination de valeur, this founder's share carries no fixed par; the present coupure represents one-tenth of a full action de fondation. Constituted by authentic act of 29 July 1881 for a term of thirty years, the partnership unites the gérant Jules Van der Laat with two commissaires whose signatures, beside the red company seal, authenticate the title. Founder's shares of this kind rewarded the organizers and contributors of non-monetary assets with a claim on surplus profits—a characteristic device of Belgian and French company law that channelled Antwerp's export trade into share form.
creator: Société Belge d'Exportation, Jules Van der Laat & Cie
notes: Société Belge d'Exportation (Jules Van der Laat & Cie), Action de Fondation No. 021, Coupure 08, représentant 1/10 de l'Action de fondation, Antwerp, July 29, 1901

title: Banque Royale (John Law) Tournois Banknote
description: A printed bearer banknote for ten livres tournois, payable to the holder on sight in silver coin ("en Especes d'Argent"). The text reads "LA BANQUE promet payer au Porteur à vüe Dix livres Tournois en Especes d'Argent, valeur reçeüe," dated at Paris the first of July 1720 ("le premier Juillet mil sept cens vingt") and marked "Division." It bears the manuscript-style countersignatures of the bank's officers: examined by Fenellon/Giraudeau, signed by Bourgeois/Delanauze, and controlled by Durevest/Granet. Issued by John Law's Banque (the Banque Royale, successor to the Banque Générale of 1716), the note belongs to the great paper-money experiment that financed and inflated the Mississippi venture. Its July 1720 date places it at the height of the collapse, after the bank had begun suspending silver redemption in May; the very promise of payment in silver "on sight" had by then become unenforceable. The leaf is austere and purely typographic, its authority resting on text and signatures rather than ornament.
creator: Banque Royale
notes: Banque Royale (John Law), 10 livres Tournois "Division" banknote No. 495,360, Paris July 1, 1720; issued during collapse of Mississippi bubble

title: Compagnie Française du Haut & du Bas Congo Ordinary Share
description: This is a bearer share certificate (action ordinaire au porteur, entièrement libérée) for one ordinary share of 5,000 francs in the Compagnie Française du Haut & du Bas Congo, a société anonyme agricole, industrielle et commerciale de l'Afrique équatoriale with its siège social in Paris. The engraved text records a capital social of 850,000,000 francs, divided into 170,000 shares of 5,000 francs each — 34,000 priority shares and 136,000 ordinary shares — with statutes deposited at the étude of Maître Aubrion, notary in Paris, and the sheet signed by J. Cousin as président-directeur général and a delegate of the board. Printed in blue by Morax of Paris, the certificate belongs to the concessionary enterprises that exploited French Equatorial Africa: the colossal nominal capital signals an ambitious extractive venture in the Congo basin. The frame makes the colonial premise explicit — two elephant heads flank the title, and vignettes of riverine landscapes, plantation labour, and a steam launch render the territory as a field of resources to be worked.
creator: Compagnie Française du Haut & du Bas Congo

title: Banco giro di Venezia 100-ducat cedula, Venice, 1798
description: This is a printed cedula, or credit note, of the Banco giro di Venezia for one hundred ducats (Ducati Cento), valued at eight lire per ducat. Its text declares that the note shall be received as effective money (dinaro effettivo) by all royal and public treasuries in payment of customs, land, and any other contributions, and likewise by the bank's own cashier for exchange in account (cambio in partita). It is issued in correlation with the proclamation of the first of October 1798. Founded in 1619, the Banco del Giro was among Venice's earliest public transfer banks; this note belongs to its twilight, issued under the Habsburg administration that followed Bonaparte's extinction of the Republic in 1797. The double eagle crowning the sheet announces Austrian sovereignty, while the winged lion of Saint Mark below clings to the city's older identity, the engraved emblems registering a transfer of dominion as much as of credit.
creator: Banco Giro di Venezia
notes: Banco Giro di Venezia, 100 Ducat cedula No. 1175, issued per proclamation of October 1, 1798 under Austrian administration; signed by Giovannelli and Schiantavello

title: Overend, Gurney and Company Limited Share Certificate
description: This is a share certificate of Overend, Gurney and Company, Limited, recording that John Metcalfe Smith, Esq., of Leeds is the registered proprietor of one share of £50, of which fifteen pounds had been paid up. Issued under the Common Seal at London and dated the fifteenth of November 1865, it followed the firm's registration and incorporation on the twelfth of July 1865 under the Companies Act 1862. Overend, Gurney & Company, the foremost discount house in the City and styled "the bankers' bank," adopted limited liability to raise fresh capital and offload mounting bad debts onto new shareholders. The partly paid structure left holders liable for the unpaid balance, a liability called in when the house suspended payments on 10 May 1866, precipitating the panic remembered as Black Friday. The certificate's restrained engraved script projects the probity its proprietors no longer commanded.
creator: Overend, Gurney and Company Limited
notes: Overend, Gurney and Company Limited, £50 share No. 72249 (£15 paid), John Metcalfe Smith, Leeds, November 15, 1865; company failed May 1866 causing "Black Friday"

title: Eagle Bank of New-Haven Stock Transfer Receipt
description: This printed receipt records a transfer of stock in the Eagle Bank of New-Haven, a Connecticut state-chartered commercial bank. The form acknowledges that the named holder has, at the date inscribed, one share of the Capital Stock—of one hundred dollars—standing to his credit on the books of the bank, the share "this day transferred" and the transaction completed over the Cashier's signature. The printed note below insists that "Transfers of Stock must be made at the Bank in Person or by Attorney," a procedural safeguard typical of the period, when ownership existed only as an entry in the bank's ledger rather than as a freely circulating instrument. Dated 10 August 1824 at New Haven, the document belongs to the wave of small state banks that multiplied across New England after the War of 1812, and it captures the everyday mechanics—par value, in-person registration, manuscript authentication—by which early republican equity changed hands.
creator: Eagle Bank of New-Haven
notes: Eagle Bank of New-Haven, 1 share $100, W.A. Brockett, August 10, 1824; Connecticut state-chartered bank

title: St. Petersburg Electric Lighting Preference Share
description: This engraved certificate represents one fully paid-up preference share (Action Privilégiée, entièrement libérée) of 250 francs in the Société Anonyme de l'Éclairage Électrique de St. Pétersbourg, a Brussels-domiciled company (Siège Social: Bruxelles). It was constituted by notarial deed before the Brussels notaries Dedoncker and Van Halteren, published in the special supplement to the Moniteur Belge of 11 June 1891. The capital of ten million francs was divided into 40,000 preference shares of 250 francs and 20,000 ordinary shares. The instrument is a vehicle of Belgian capital export: investors in Brussels financed electric street and urban lighting in the Russian imperial capital, a pattern of foreign-controlled utility concessions then common across the Russian Empire. A round French foreign-securities tax stamp dated 1920 records its later circulation in Paris. The vignette frames a panoramic view of the St. Petersburg waterfront between two ornate lamp-standards crowned by lit globes, the lighting motif and flowing botanical ornament drawing on the Art Nouveau idiom of 1890s Brussels.
creator: Société Anonyme de l'Éclairage Électrique de St. Pétersbourg
notes: Éclairage Électrique de St. Pétersbourg, 250 Franc preference share No. 38859, Belgian company providing electric lighting in St. Petersburg, constituted Brussels 1891

title: Hungarian Mortgage Credit Bank Prize Bond
description: This is a bearer prize bond (Nyeremény-Kötvény / Obligation à Primes / Prämien-Obligation) of one hundred florins, bearing interest at four and one-half per cent, issued by the Magyar Jelzálog-Hitelbank — the Royal Hungarian Mortgage Credit Bank (Société de Crédit Hypothécaire du Royaume de Hongrie / Ungarische Hypotheken-Bank) — at Budapest, dated 1880. The text is printed in parallel Hungarian, French and German, payable to the bearer (bemutatóra / au porteur / an Überbringer). Founded in 1870 as the kingdom's principal vehicle for long-term mortgage lending, the bank funded itself through such instruments, which married a fixed coupon to periodic prize drawings — a lottery-loan device favoured across the Habsburg lands to broaden a security's appeal among small savers. The engraved frame, with the Hungarian crowned arms above and restrained classical scrollwork, projects the sober institutional authority the offering required.
creator: Magyar Jelzálog-Hitelbank
notes: Magyar Jelzálog-Hitelbank, 4½% 100 Florin Prize Bond, Series 2884, No. 061, Budapest July 1, 1880; trilingual Hungarian/French/German
transcription:
# Magyar Jelzálog-Hitelbank Prize Bond Transcription

## Original Text:

**Header Section:**
SÉRIE 2884 SOROZAT | NUMMER 061 SZÁM

SOCIÉTÉ DE CRÉDIT FONCIER DU ROYAUME D'HONGRIE | MAGYAR JELZÁLOG-HITELBANK | UNGARISCHE HYPOTHEKEN BANK

**Main Title (Trilingual):**
Obligation à Primes | Nyeremény-Kötvény | Prämien-Obligation
au porteur de | bemutatóra | auf Ueberbringer über

CENT FLORINS | 100 | EGYSZÁZ FORINTRÓL | 100 | HUNDERT GULDEN

**Left Column (French):**
Cette obligation forme une part de l'émission de Obligations à 4½ % à primes, effectuée par la Société de Crédit Foncier du Royaume de Hongrie pour faire face aux prêts consentis par elle, conformément à ses statuts, aux Municipalités, Villes, Communes et Personnes légales en tant qu'elles sont autorisées par la loi à contracter des emprunts.

Cette obligation rapporte 4½ % normes val. annuels. L'intérêt est payable deux fois par an au remboursement de l'intérêt. Ces intérêts sont payables chaque semestre le 1. Juin et le 1. Décembre à charge d'une raison à Buda-Pesth et à Vienne conformément à la teneur des conditions énoncées d'autre part.

Le Royaume de Hongrie à Budapest et aux placements qui seront désignés à cet effet.

La Société de Crédit Foncier du Royaume de Hongrie s'engage à rembourser en cinquante années au plus tard le capital de cette obligation que les primes envisagées à la concordance au tableau.

Le remboursement des obligations sorties au tirage aura lieu le paiement des taux se fait dans les conditions énoncées d'autre part.
Budapest, le 1. Juillet 1884.

Le président. | Le directeur.
[Signatures]
Pour le contrôle.
Le président du conseil de surveillance.
[Signature]

**Center Column (Hungarian):**
Ezen kötvény részét képezi azon osztr. ért. 40 millió forinyi összegben kibocsátandó 4½%-os nyeremény-kötvényeknek, melyek a Magyar Jelzálog-Hitelbank által felvételezett, az alapszabályokban előírbozottságának, Országoknak és más jogi személyeknek — a mennyiben ezek hitoktatók hitoktatásnak nyeremény számtani vonatkozásában engedélyeztetik — közösolok alapján alatnak ki.

Ezen kötvény a negyede évente történő kamatszakaszok és kamatlevékvénti minden év junius 1-én és december 1-én ósszr. ért. 2—2 fitat a Magyar Jelzalog-Hitelbank pénztárán allal Budapeśten vagy más kijelölt helyen fizétetik ki.

A Magyar Jelzalog-Hitelbank kötelezi magát, hogy őzen részletkötvény törlesztendo és az inom nev azt nyeremenyek 50 év alatt a magz. kir. pénzigminisztérium által javáhagyott, a hatlapou foglalt sorolast térület fogju hitelni.

A kihuzott kötvények bevallása és a nyeremények kifizetése a följófátok leru iratazandelésenel eltartoitt.

Budapest, 1884. július 1-én.

A Jelen kötvény alapszábayzean föde-
fozottságának biztosítására.

[Signatures]
Igàszán.

**Right Column (German):**
Diese Schuldverschreibung bildet einen Theil der Emissionen von Främlien-Obligationen, welche von der Ungarischen Hypotheken-Bank im Budapest gemäß ihrer Statuten gezogeneu Darlehnen an Muntzipien, Städte, Gemeinden und andere juristische Personen — soweit diese selbigen zu deren Anlahen befugniset werden — entgegent zu werden.

Diese Schuldverschreibung wird bis zur Fälligkeit bezinst und zwar jährlich vier v.H. Die Zinsen halbigjahn am 1. Juni und am 1. Dezember gezahlt werden sollen zu dem Schaltrage der Ungarischen Hypotheken-Bank in Budapest und bei sonstigen hiertigen Lottung fallenderweise zahlbar.

Die Einlösung der gesprochen Schuldverschreibungen, sowie die Auszahlung der Gewinste erfolgt in Gemässheit nachstehender Bedingungen.

Budapest, am 1. Juni 1884.

Der Präsident. | Der Director.
[Signatures]

Für die Controle.
Der Präsident des Ausschusses.
[Signature]

title: El Callao Consolidated Gold Mining Company Limited Share Warrant for Five Shares
description: A bearer share warrant for five fully-paid ordinary shares of £1 sterling each in the El Callao Consolidated Gold Mining Company Limited, registered under the Companies Act, 1862, with a nominal capital of £800,000 divided into 800,000 shares of £1. Issued under the company's common seal in London on 22 August 1903 and printed bilingually in English and French, the warrant signals a security floated for an international, cross-Channel investing public. El Callao, in the Bolívar region of Venezuela, ranked among the richest gold districts of the nineteenth century, and this consolidated undertaking gathered its claims under London capital. The transferable-by-delivery bearer form, the prominent denomination, and the radiating sunburst that fills the central panel — framed by dense purple scrollwork — present the certificate as a liquid, tradable instrument of speculative mining finance.

title: Société Anonyme d'Exploitation de Phu-Quoc Share
description: A bearer share (action au porteur) of 100 francs, fully paid (entièrement libérée), in the Société Anonyme d'Exploitation de Phu-Quoc, a Paris-domiciled company formed to work concessions on the island of Phu Quoc in Cochinchina (now southern Vietnam). The certificate states a capital of 6,000,000 francs divided into 60,000 such shares, with statutes filed before Maître Robert Aubron, notary in Paris, and a registered office in Paris (55, rue Lafayette). The face documents French colonial enterprise channelled through the Paris bourse: small-denomination paper invited metropolitan savers to fund the exploitation of a tropical possession prized for pepper and fish-sauce production. The engraved frame reinforces that promise, ringing a central silhouette map of the island with lush coastal panoramas of palms and sea, an idealized vision of the colony as a productive garden. Printed by Marcel Charles Verneau, Paris.

title: Imperial Russian Government Perpetual Bond
description: A perpetual bearer bond (Билетъ … Безсрочный) of the Imperial Russian Commission for the Repayment of State Debts, recorded in the State Debt Book in the name of its Administration. Its face capital is 5,000 rubles, stated expressly in assignats (Ассигнаціями Пять тысячъ Рублей), yielding a perpetual income of six per hundred per year — 300 rubles annually, as the side margins confirm — payable in semi-annual portions of 150 rubles each January and July. Issued in 1839 and registered that January, it falls at the moment of the Kankrin currency reform, when the depreciated paper assignat was being pegged to the silver ruble; that the instrument still reckons in assignats marks it as a creature of the old paper standard about to pass. The crowned double-headed eagle and the restrained engraved wave-and-line border project sovereign credit without ornamental excess, the document's authority residing in its lettering rather than allegory.
creator: Imperial Russian Government, State Commission for Debt Repayment
notes: Imperial Russian Government 6% Perpetual Bond. 5,000 rubles. Issued by the State Commission for Debt Repayment. 1859.

title: Ferro-Carril y Almacenes de Depósito de Santiago de Cuba Share
description: This engraved share certificate (cédula de propiedad) conveys title to one share—una acción—of one hundred pesos in the Ferro-Carril y Almacenes de Depósito de Santiago de Cuba, the railroad-and-warehouse company of Santiago de Cuba. Issued at Santiago de Cuba on 20 April 1861 in favour of Gabriel de Llamas, it carries the signatures of the president, J. M. de Llaguno, the contador, and the secretary. The undertaking yoked rail transport to bonded storage depots (almacenes de depósito), an arrangement suited to moving and holding the sugar and coffee that flowed through the island's second port. At the certificate's heart a wood-vignette of a steam train crossing a mountainous coast announces the modernity the venture promised, framing the locomotive as the engine of Cuba's export commerce in the last decades of Spanish colonial rule.

title: Commonwealth of Pennsylvania Five Per Cent Stock
description: This engraved certificate of public stock, headed "Five per cent. stock of 1825," records a hundred-dollar loan owed by the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania to James P. Parker, in trust, of Philadelphia. It bears interest at five per centum per annum from the first day of August 1829, payable half-yearly at the Bank of Pennsylvania on the first of February and the first of August, the principal reimbursable fifteen years after the first day of January 1825. Issued by the Treasury Department at Harrisburg and dated November 18, 1829, the stock was created under an act of Assembly of April 11, 1825 entitled "An act authorizing a loan." Such funded debt underwrote the Commonwealth's ambitious internal-improvement program of canals and roads then taking shape as the Main Line of Public Works. At the upper left a seated allegorical figure of Liberty with the state arms lends republican dignity to an instrument of borrowing, presenting state credit as a public virtue rather than a mere fiscal expedient.
creator: Commonwealth of Pennsylvania
notes: Commonwealth of Pennsylvania, Five Per Cent Stock of 1825, $100, No. 71, James P. Parker in trust, November 18, 1829; printed by Mowry & Amnon, Harrisburg

title: Fosses à Charbon de M. le Marquis de Traisnel, fractional share (France, 1781)
description: This engraved and manuscript share certificate is an "action d'un denier dans vingt-cinq sols d'intérêt" issued by the Fosses à Charbon de Monsieur le Marquis de Traisnel, a coal-mining enterprise in the Hainaut–Valenciennes coalfield of northern France. Drawn up at the assembly of the said mines and dated 13 March 1781, it certifies the bearer's proprietorship of a single denier out of twenty-five sols of interest — one three-hundredth of the venture — entitling him to share proportionally in its profits and losses, subject to assessments for loans (emprunts) raised by the company; transfer requires the directors' consent. Signed by the directors De Wavrechin and Dehault, the document predates France's general mining legislation and shows how seigneurial coal concessions on the eve of the Revolution were financed by dividing the works into transferable fractional interests. The engraved crowned escutcheon of fleurs-de-lis at the head asserts the noble standing under which the concession was held.
creator: Compagnie des Fosses à Charbon de M. le Marquis de Traisnel
notes: Fosses à Charbon de M. le Marquis de Traisnel, Interest Share No. 263, signed by La Marsche and Dehault, June 13, 1781; pre-revolutionary French coal mining share
transcription:
# Document Transcription

## Original (French):

**Fosses à Charbon de M. le Marquis de Craisnel; Action d'un Denier dans vingt cinq sols d'intérêt. N°. 263**

Nous Soussignés Directeur, associés de la Compagnie des Fosses à Charbon de M. Le Marquis de Traisnel, reconnaissons que Monsieur D'. Montmoreuil Pelletier D'en Suanne D. est propriétaire d'in Denier d'intérêt dans l'entreprise de dites Fosses et, qu'en consequence, il participera dans la proportion audit Denier aux benéfices et aux pertes de la D. Compagnie, a condition qu'il sera soumis aux emprunts et aux mises faites à faire, et, qui ont de ou seront cy après délibérés, à charge, en outre de ne pouvoir vendre allener, ni céder la présente action dans l'agrément de la Compagnie, laquelle en conformité de son acte de societé, sera libre de retraire ladite action à un profit à en cas de vente ou de trasport. Le nouveau propriétaire devra se presenter dans l'année pour obtenir une nouvelle reconnoissance sous son nom, après avoir Donné, ou hypotheque, ou caution à l'appaisement des Directeurs associés, pour la surete des emprunts faits et à faire, fait à l'assemblée deD. Fosses, à Anniéd ___ Le 13 Juin 1781

[Signatures: La Marsche and Dehault]

---

## English Translation:

**Coal Mines (Fosses) of the Marquis de Craisnel; Share of One Denier in Twenty-five Sols of Interest. No. 263**

We, the undersigned Directors and associates of the Company of Coal Mines of the Marquis de Traisnel, acknowledge that Monsieur D'. Montmoreuil Pelletier D'en Suanne D. is the proprietor of one Denier of interest in the enterprise of said Coal Mines and, consequently, he shall participate in the proportion of said Denier in the profits and losses of the said Company, on the condition that he shall be subject to the loans and contributions made or to be made, and which have been or shall hereafter be deliberated, charged furthermore that he shall be unable to sell, transfer, or cede the present share without the approval of the Company, which in conformity with its deed of association, shall be free to redeem said share at a profit in case of sale or transfer. The new proprietor shall present himself within the year to obtain a new acknowledgment under his name, after having provided, mortgaged, or pledged security to the satisfaction of the Associated Directors, for the security of the loans made and to be made, executed at the assembly of the said Fosses, at Anniéd ___ June 13, 1781

[Signatures: La Marsche and Dehault]

---

- **Serial number:** N°. 263
- **Coat of arms:** Ornate heraldic shield at top center (fleur-de-lis design, indicating French nobility)
- **Signatures:** Two signatures at bottom - "La Marsche" and "Dehault" (Directors)
- **Date:** Le 13 Juin 1781 (June 13, 1781)
- **Reference mark:** "FB" in top left corner
- **Archival mark:** Small notation in top right corner

title: Fukuyama-Han (Bingo Province) Silver Note, 1 Mon-me
description: This is a hansatsu, a domain paper note issued in the name of the Fukuyama fief of Bingo Province (後備, present-day eastern Hiroshima Prefecture, Japan), redeemable in silver to the value of one monme (銀壹匁), the monme being a weight measure of silver rather than a coin. The tall, narrow strip is woodblock-printed in the standard vertical hansatsu format: a seated deity of fortune crowns the sheet, the great denomination characters fill the center, and the issuing exchange house, 濱口屋源左衛門, is named at the foot beside its seals. The date 享保十五 (Kyōhō 15, 1730) appears at left, with a later 寶曆 (Hōreki-era) notation marking a re-authorization of the issue. Such notes, valid only within their own domain, let a daimyo stretch scarce silver and finance local obligations; the guardian deity above the sum quietly underwrites trust in paper backed by a lord's word.
creator: Fukuyama-Han (Fukuyama Domain)
notes: Fukuyama-Han (Bingo Province) silver note (銀札), 1 Mon-me denomination, 1730; Japanese Edo-period feudal domain currency (hansatsu)

title: General Investment Corporation Stock Purchase Warrant
description: This stock purchase warrant of the General Investment Corporation, a company incorporated under the laws of Delaware with its principal office in Boston, Massachusetts, entitles the bearer to purchase four shares of the corporation's common stock at a fixed price of $30 per share, exercisable at any time on or after September 16, 1929, without limit of duration. Dated June 13, 1938, the instrument is a detachable warrant of the kind investment trusts of the era issued—often as a "sweetener" attached to securities—granting the right, but not the obligation, to subscribe to shares at a predetermined strike. By 1938 the $30 exercise price stood well above the depressed market valuations that followed the 1929 crash, so the warrant likely traded as a speculative claim on future recovery. The engraved border and bank-note lettering, in the restrained steel-engraved style of the issuing security printer, signal trustworthiness and resistance to forgery.
creator: General Investment Corporation
notes: General Investment Corporation (Delaware), Stock Purchase Warrant No. NCW5418 to purchase common stock, Boston, June 13, 1938
transcription:
# Stock Purchase Warrant Transcription

**No NCW5418**

---

**STOCK PURCHASE WARRANT**

**TO PURCHASE COMMON STOCK OF**

# GENERAL INVESTMENT CORPORATION

*Incorporated under the Laws of the State of Delaware*

**WARRANT TO PURCHASE ___/___ SHARES**

---

THIS CERTIFIES that the bearer is entitled to purchase at any time on or after September 14, 1929 (without limit) all or any part of

_____ SHARES

full-paid and non-assessable shares of COMMON STOCK of GENERAL INVESTMENT CORPORATION, a corporation of the State of Delaware (herein called the "corporation"), as such stock may be constituted at the time of purchase, at the price of $8 per share, upon surrender of this warrant accompanied by full payment of the purchase price in cash at the principal office of the corporation in Boston, Massachusetts. This warrant does not entitle the holder to any rights whatsoever as a stockholder of the corporation. It is expressly agreed that the shares of common stock of the corporation agreed to be sold hereunder are shares of the common stock of the corporation as such stock may be constituted at the time of purchase and that delivery of the number of shares of common stock called for herein, in good delivery hereunder whether or not the claim is made, shall constitute full performance of all the obligations of the corporation hereunder with respect to the exercise of the rights or interests represented by the shares of common stock of the corporation authorized and outstanding at the date hereof or at any other time prior to such purchase and irrespective of any dividends, sales, exchanges or other disposition of stock of the corporation of any class, any change of authorized capital or reclassification thereof, the sale or lease by the corporation of all or any part of its property, or any change whatsoever; provided, however, that if while this warrant is in effect the corporation shall have recapitalized by subdivision or combination of its outstanding shares of common stock into a greater or lesser number of shares, then, in each such case, the number of shares of common stock purchasable upon exercise of this warrant shall be increased or decreased (as the case may be) in proportion to the change through such subdivision or combination in the number of outstanding shares of common stock of the corporation, and provided further, that if while this warrant is in effect the corporation shall consolidate or merge with or into any other corporation, then, as a condition of such consolidation or merger, adequate provision shall be made whereby the holder of this warrant shall thereafter be entitled to purchase, in lieu of the shares of common stock of the corporation theretofore purchasable upon exercise of this warrant, such shares of stock, securities or assets as would have been purchasable upon the exercise of this warrant had such consolidation or merger not taken place. The existence of this warrant shall not prevent the lawful dissolution of the corporation. Whenever in this warrant the corporation is referred to, it shall be deemed to include any corporation into or to which the corporation may have been merged or consolidated. This warrant is exchangeable at the option of the bearer at the principal office of the corporation in Boston, Massachusetts for other similar warrants of different denominations entitling the bearer thereof to purchase an equal aggregate number of shares of common stock of the corporation as specified in this warrant. Every holder of this warrant, by accepting the same, consents and agrees with the corporation and with every subsequent holder of this warrant as follows: Title to this warrant shall be registered by the absolute owner of this warrant and is hereby granted power to transfer absolute title thereto by delivery thereof to a purchaser thereof for value (present or accrued); without notice of prior defenses or equities or claims enforceable against his transferor; every bearer of this warrant shall be presumed to be such a purchaser thereunder unless the contrary be established; every bearer of this warrant waives and renounces all his equities and rights herein in favor of every such purchaser and every such purchaser shall acquire title hereto and to all rights represented hereby; the corporation and any warrant agent shall treat any bearer who is a purchaser hereof of the character above described, as the absolute owner thereof for all purposes without being affected by any notice to the contrary; the corporation and any warrant agent shall be entitled to accept the surrender of this warrant from the bearer hereof and such surrender shall constitute full and complete discharge of all liability of the corporation and any warrant agent hereunder; the corporation does not make any contract with any bearer hereof except as contained in this warrant and any performance by the corporation with respect to the bearer hereof of any agreement contained with respect to this warrant, shall be due performance of such agreement. On the back of this warrant is a form of subscription which must be used upon exercise thereof.

IN WITNESS WHEREOF the corporation has caused this warrant to be signed in its behalf by the undersigned warrant agents.

**GENERAL INVESTMENT CORPORATION**
*(Boston, Mass.)*

Dated **JUN 14 1928** [handwritten]

By _____________________________ [signature]

Warrant Agent _____________________________ [signature]

title: Imperial Japanese Government 4% Loan Bond, Paris, 1910
description: This green-printed certificate is a 500-franc bearer bond (obligation) of the 4% Imperial Japanese Government Loan of 1910 (Emprunt de Quatre Cent Cinquante Millions de Francs), issued under Law No. 6 of 1906 and the Finance Minister's Ordinance No. 19, promulgated 30 April 1910. The text records its issue at the head office, dated 15 May 1910, with coupons payable in Paris, as the red overprint "Coupons délivrés, Paris" attests. Such franc-denominated bonds carried Japanese sovereign debt deep into the Paris market, where France stood among the largest holders, the proceeds servicing the costs of the wars against China and Russia and the public works that followed them. A finely engraved vignette of Mount Fuji crowns the sheet, lending a familiar emblem of the lender's distant sovereign to reassure the European subscriber.
creator: Gouvernement Impérial du Japon

title: Gouvernement de la Nouvelle-Calédonie et Dépendances 4% Bond, Paris, 1931
description: A bearer bond (obligation au porteur) of 1,000 francs bearing 4% interest, free of present and future taxes ("Net d'Impôts Présents et Futurs"), issued by the Gouvernement de la Nouvelle-Calédonie et Dépendances under its "Emprunt 4% 1931." The certificate records that this is one of 34.020 obligations of 1,000 francs each, amortizable over fifty years, forming part of a larger loan of 95 million francs authorized by the law of 22 February 1931 and approved by decree of 30 July 1931. It is dated Paris, 31 August 1931, and carries an attached coupon sheet. The issue speaks to the fiscal reach of a French Pacific colony turning to the Paris market to fund its administration and public works at the onset of the Depression, as world prices for nickel, the territory's principal export, were already falling. The restrained typographic frame and the interlaced NCG monogram convey official sobriety rather than ornamental display.
creator: Gouvernement de la Nouvelle-Calédonie et Dépendances

title: Republic of Texas Government Bond
description: A government bond of the Republic of Texas, headed "Government Bond, payable to holder. Receivable for all Government Dues," promising the bearer One Hundred Dollars with interest at eight per cent per annum from the first day of January 1840, payable semi-annually at the Treasury Department in gold or silver upon presentation. Dated at the Treasury Department, Houston, and signed by John G. Chalmers as Secretary of the Treasury and David G. Burnet as President; ten semi-annual interest coupons run along the foot. Issued by the young republic in the interval between San Jacinto and annexation, the note is an instrument of frontier sovereign credit, its specie-payable terms and receivability for public dues an effort to lend value to the debt of a cash-poor government. The engraved vignettes—a seated youth amid implements of labour, grazing cattle, and a steamboat—voice the era's faith in industry, husbandry, and river commerce.

title: Société des Grands Hôtels Indochinois Action au Porteur de 50 Piastres
description: This is a fully paid-up bearer share (action au porteur, entièrement libérée) of 50 piastres in the Société des Grands Hôtels Indochinois, a société anonyme established by deed received before Maître Mathieu, notary at Saïgon. Its capital of 600,000 piastres was divided into 12,000 such shares, and the certificate was printed in Saïgon by the firm A. Portail. The instrument belongs to the wave of French colonial ventures that, in the early twentieth century, raised capital to build hotels and travel infrastructure across French Indochina. Denomination in piastres rather than francs anchors the enterprise firmly in the colonial monetary economy of the peninsula. The green-and-orange engraved frame, with its dense foliate scrollwork and the interlaced company monogram, follows the conventional decorative idiom of French share certificates of the period, signalling permanence and commercial respectability to the bearer.

title: Great Atlantic & Pacific Tea Company stock certificate, Maryland, 1972
description: An engraved certificate for one hundred shares of the common stock of The Great Atlantic & Pacific Tea Company, Inc., each share carrying a par value of one dollar, issued under the laws of the State of Maryland and dated 24 January 1972. The transfer agent named is First National City Bank and the registrar Morgan Guaranty Trust Company of New York; a CUSIP identifier is printed beside the owner line, marking the post-1968 era of standardized securities. By this date A&P, long the dominant American grocery chain, was a publicly traded enterprise whose shares circulated as ordinary investment instruments rather than the closely held stock of its founding decades. The olive-toned border frames a vignette of two winged allegorical female figures attending a medallion bearing the company's monogram, a classicizing motif that lends mercantile commerce an emblem of dignified abundance.

title: Hungarian Fund One-Dollar Bond, New York, 1852
description: A one-dollar bearer bond of the Hungarian Fund, dated at New York on 2 February 1852 and bearing the engraved signature of Lajos Kossuth (L. Kossuth). The text promises that, on demand one year after the establishment in fact of an Independent Hungarian Government, the holder shall receive one dollar at the National Treasury or its agencies at London or New York, or may exchange sums of fifty dollars or over for certificates bearing four per cent interest, repayable in ten equal annual installments. Issued during Kossuth's American tour to fund a renewed bid against Habsburg rule after the failed 1848-49 revolution, the note set its face value low to invite contributions from ordinary sympathizers. Its motifs - a victorious figure standing over a vanquished foe and a helmeted Athena - cast the cause as righteous and embattled. The hoped-for republic never came, leaving the certificate a contingent claim on a state that never existed.
creator: Hungarian Fund (Kossuth exile government)
notes: Hungarian Fund $1 bearer bond. New York, February 2, 1852. Issued during Lajos Kossuth's American fundraising tour for Hungarian independence.

title: Imperial Russian Government 3% State Loan, London, 1859
description: This is an inscribed bearer bond for a capital of one hundred pounds sterling in the Imperial Russian Government's three-per-cent State Loan of 1859, issued by the State Commission for the Redemption of Debts (Государственная Коммиссія Погашенія Долговъ) and entered in the State Debt Book. The holder receives continuous income at three per hundred per year, paid half-yearly in London in two instalments of one pound ten shillings, on the dates 20 October / 1 November and 19 April / 1 May (Old and New Style), through the bankers J. Thomson and T. Bonar. Raised on the London market and denominated in sterling, the loan drew British capital into a Russia that, in the wake of the Crimean War and on the eve of emancipation, was reorganising its finances and funding railway and state expenditure. The engraved frame, crowned by the imperial double-headed eagle, asserts sovereign credit to a foreign creditor.
creator: Imperial Russian Government
notes: Imperial Russian Government 3% State Loan of 1859. £100 bearer bond. No. 31016.

# Original:

**БИЛЕТЫ**

**ГОСУДАРСТВЕННОГО ПОГАШЕНИЯ КОМИССИИ ДОЛГОВЪ.**

№31016                                                                                                №31016

РОССИЙСКАГО ТРЁХПРОЦЕНТНАГО ЗАЙМА 1859 г.
НА КАПИТАЛЪ СТО ФУНТ. СТЕРЛ.

записанный въ Государственную долговую книгу.

Книга          стр. 122.

Владѣлецъ сего билета получаетъ непрерывный доходъ по ТРИ шт СТО въ годъ, платежные по полугодіо ш Лондонъ, а именно, одинъ фунт стерл. десять шл. на сроки и одинъ фунт стерл.десять шл. на сроки [в АПРЕЛЯ] в банкиръ П. Томсонъ, Т. Бонарь и К°, щц., по желанию, въ Берлинъ, у банкира Ф. Март. Магнусъ, Прусcъ. курантъ. десять талеръ. три сер. гросъ. девять пфен. въ тѣ же самые сроки.

При семь билетъ находятся полугодовые купоны, для получения трёчентовъ, по выданы новые купоны, по предстанія приложенаго къ сему билету талона.

Выписка изъ Уставъ Комиссіи Погашения Долговъ (Сб. Зак. Т. XI издъ. 1857 г.)

Ст. 89. Безвозмездный непрерывный доходъ, равно какъ и напасяю сроочныхъ должниковъ, производятся и въ мѣрное и въ ночное время, безъ всякаго разраныя, къ дружественной ли или непрерывной казнѐ.

— 90. Въ случаѣ смерти иностранного вексельнаго бѣдт защищается, бережение какъ защищение содержатѣ его наслѣдникамъ, по порядку и правамъ наслѣдия согласно закону.

— 91. Капиталъ, въ бѣдосрочной домѣ номинальные, составляя неозгрушимую обѣстъанность вексельнаго, ни въ какомъ случаѣ не подлежатъ налогомъ.

Выписка изъ Высочайшаго Указа, данного 24 Марта 1859 г. на имя Министра Финансовъ.

На погашение сего займа, съ 1859 г., назначается особый, независимо другихъ займовъ, сумъ. Фондъ погашения создался ежегодно тотарѓ процента прежнего изъ расходалального капитала займа. Фондъ сей, который будутъ уважаемости ежегодно капитализируемыхъ по послѣдствия прогнозовъ, какія будутъ причисляны на погашающие билеты, имѣетъ быть обогащенъ на счетъ вынутёе и барчисанное их курсъ. Если его какъ обстоятельствъ облегчилъ, сп с предварительнымъ благоговѣйнымъ сознаніемъ на российскыхъ долго, то для сего развития идеально цѣна мѣсть этихъ сроскъ рублей за его фунтъ стеръ., и билетъ съ тѣмъ ясгіти далежить быть обращены. по цѣнѣ, опрѐдѣляемой ежегодно [illegible], и прочніеся по числу выеда будетъ выдѣляемые сроки.

Управляющій [signature]                    Бухгалтеръ [signature]

1859

# English Translation:

**BONDS**

**STATE REDEMPTION COMMISSION OF DEBTS**

№31016                                                                                                №31016

OF THE RUSSIAN THREE-PERCENT LOAN OF 1859
FOR THE CAPITAL OF ONE HUNDRED POUNDS STERLING

recorded in the State Debt Book.

Book          page 122.

The holder of this bond receives continuous income at THREE percent per annum, payable semi-annually in London, namely, one pound sterling and ten shillings on the deadline and one pound sterling and ten shillings on the deadline [IN APRIL] to banker P. Thomson, T. Bonarb and Co., etc., if desired, in Berlin, to banker F. Mart. Magnus, Prussia. current ten talers, three sixes, nine pfennigs at the same deadlines.

With this bond are attached semi-annual coupons for receiving interest; in case of loss of the coupons, new coupons are issued upon presentation of the receipt attached to this bond.

Extract from the Statute of the Commission for the Redemption of Debts (Compiled Laws, Vol. XI, 1857 edition)

Art. 89. Gratis continuous income, as well as payment of time-bound debtors, are executed at any time, both day and night, without any delay, to either the friendly or hostile treasury.

— 90. In the event of death of a foreign bill of exchange, protection as protection is provided to his heirs according to the procedure and rights of succession in accordance with law.

— 91. Capital, in time-limited houses nominally, constituting an indestructible obligation of the bill of exchange, in no case is subject to taxes.

Extract from the Supreme Decree issued on 24 March 1859 to the Minister of Finance.

For the redemption of this loan, from 1859, a special fund is designated, independent of other loans. The redemption fund shall be created annually from a portion of revenues allocated for this purpose. This fund, which shall be increased annually by capitalized earnings on the outstanding bonds which shall be extinguished on the redemption bills, shall be enriched at the expense of the increase in their value. If under certain circumstances this is facilitated, with preliminary devout acknowledgment for Russian debts, then for this purpose the determined price of these series of rubles for its pound sterling is [unclear], and the bond with this [illegible] must be exchanged at a price determined annually [illegible], and continued by the number of [illegible] will be allocated annual deadlines.

Director [signature]                    Accountant [signature]

1859

title: Romanian Public Debt Internal Consolidation Loan, Lei 5,000
description: A bearer bond of 5,000 lei issued by the Kingdom of Romania under its public debt ("Datoria Publică a României") as part of the Internal Consolidation Loan of 1935 ("Împrumutul Intern de Consolidare 3% 1935"). The instrument bears 3% annual interest — 150 lei a year, payable in two semi-annual installments on 15 May and 15 November — and was emitted by authority of the law published in the Monitorul Oficial No. 156 of 10 July 1934, a Council of Ministers decree of 21 June 1935, and article 19 of the budget law for the 1935/1936 fiscal year, signed by the Minister of Finance. Issued at Bucharest in 1935, it belongs to the wave of domestic debt consolidations by which European states refinanced obligations strained by the slump of the early 1930s. The Romanian eagle spreads above the title and the crowned royal arms anchor the foot, framing the loan in the heraldry of the monarchy that guaranteed it.
creator: Romania. Ministry of Finance
notes: Romanian Public Debt, Internal Consolidation Loan 3% 1935. Lei 5,000 bearer bond. No. 242914. With coupon sheet.

title: Nippon Kangyō Bank (Hypothec Bank of Japan) Premium Savings Bond, 50 yen, Tokyo, 1938
description: This printed premium savings bond (割増金附貯蓄債券, warimashikin-tsuki chochiku saiken) carries a face value of fifty yen (金五拾圓) and forms the 88th issue (第捌拾八回) of the Nippon Kangyō Ginkō (日本勸業銀行), the government-linked Hypothec Bank of Japan, dated Shōwa 13, November 1938 (昭和十三年十一月). It was sold to the public at a discounted price of ten yen (割引賣出價格金拾圓), the gap between issue price and redemption value standing in for interest, while an attached prize-drawing (割増金) offered lottery-style bonuses to holders. Issued as Japan's economy was being mobilised for war in China, the instrument exemplifies the state's drive to absorb household cash into public finance, dressing compulsory thrift in the appeal of a lottery. The dense red border of stylised scrollwork and the small vignette of figures at lower right lend the certificate an air of official solidity.
creator: Japan Industrial Bank (日本勧業銀行, Nippon Kangyo Ginko)

title: Nippon Kangyō Bank Premium Wartime Patriotic Bond, Japan, August 1942
description: A printed premium-bearing wartime patriotic bond (割増金附戦時報國債券, warimashikin-tsuki senji hōkoku saiken) with a face value of five yen (金五圓), issued in its fourth series (第四回) and dated Shōwa 17, August (昭和十七年八月), that is August 1942. The issuer named at left is the Nippon Kangyō Ginkō (株式會社日本勸業銀行), the semi-official mortgage bank through which the government placed small-denomination war loans rather than the central treasury directly. The 'premium' (割増金) terms printed at right attach a lottery to the instrument: a detachable coupon (附票) at the foot entered the bearer in periodic prize drawings, sweetening an otherwise modest return and widening the bond's appeal among wage-earners and small savers. The whole sheet militarizes saving: a long-range bomber crosses the upper field, a battleship and the rising-sun naval ensign flank the central oval, and a red seal reading 大東亞戰爭 ('Greater East Asia War') with Mount Fuji binds the purchase to imperial purpose. Palm fronds at left allude to the newly seized southern theatre. The object captures how the Pacific War financed itself by recasting thrift as patriotic duty and folding chance into civic obligation.

title: Japanese Wartime Patriotic Bond (Nippon Kangyō Bank), 10 Yen, 1942
description: A printed Japanese wartime patriotic bond (戦時報国債券, Senji Hōkoku Saiken) with a premium feature (割増金附), of ten yen face value (金拾圓), issued in the fourth series in the eighth month of Shōwa 17 (August 1942). Though the instrument funded the imperial treasury under the authority of the Ministry of Finance (大蔵省), as the legend on the right panel states, it was placed through the Nippon Kangyō Bank (日本勧業銀行), whose name and seal appear at left; the bond was repaid with a premium awarded by periodic lottery drawing, the detachable coupon (附票) below carrying the matching number for that purpose. Sold during the Greater East Asia War (大東亜戦争), named on the central seal, it formed part of a tiered savings campaign meant to absorb household income across all classes. The engraved imagery is martial and expansionist: a bomber crosses the upper field, a warship rides the sea below, while tropical palms and a rising sun over Mount Fuji evoke the southward conquests the loan was meant to sustain.

title: Kahetian Railway Company Bond
description: This printed bearer bond (облигація) of the Kahetian Railway Company (Общество Кахетинской желѣзной дороги) belongs to a 4½% loan of 1912, struck on a nominal capital of 13,230,000 rubles, or 1,400,000 pounds sterling. Each certificate carries a face value of 189 rubles, fixed at 20 pounds sterling, and runs in parallel Russian and English text. Issued at St. Petersburg in 1912 with the express sanction of the Imperial Russian Government, the loan financed a railway through the Kakheti wine country of eastern Georgia, joining Tiflis to its vineyards. The twin denomination and bilingual face reveal an instrument courting London capital while resting on the security of a sovereign endorsement. The sober typographic frame, the imperial double eagle, and the embossed red seal lend the paper the gravity of a state-backed obligation rather than a speculative venture.
creator: Kahetian Railway Company
notes: Kahetian Railway Company 4½% Bond. 189 rubles = £20 sterling. No. A14533. St. Petersburg, 1912.

title: Imperial Russian Government 4% State Loan Bond, 1902
description: This printed bearer bond (облигація) of 1,000 Imperial German marks, bearing 4 percent annual interest, belongs to the Imperial Russian Government's 4% State Loan of 1902. The face equates the marks to 463 rubles 50 kopecks, 590 Dutch guilders, and 49 pounds sterling, registering the holder in the State Debt Book and signalling a security pitched simultaneously at German, Dutch, and British savers. The loan, authorised by the Highest Ukase of 1 March 1902, was raised expressly to realise the indemnity owed to Russia by China following the events of 1900 to 1901, converting a diplomatic claim into tradable European paper. The nominal capital reached 181,950,000 rubles, or 393,000,000 marks. The double-headed Romanov eagle and dense guilloche borders assert imperial creditworthiness; the multilingual denominations reveal how heavily Tsarist finance leaned on foreign capital markets before 1914.
creator: Imperial Russian Government
notes: Imperial Russian Government 4% State Loan 1902. 1,000 German marks. No. 116485.
transcription:
# Imperial Russian Government 4% State Loan of 1902 — Bearer Bond for 1,000 Imperial German Marks — Transcription

## Original (Russian):

**ИМПЕРАТОРСКОЕ РОССІЙСКОЕ ПРАВИТЕЛЬСТВО.**

**РОССІЙСКІЙ**

**4% ГОСУДАРСТВЕННЫЙ ЗАЕМЪ 1902 г.**

выпущенный для реализаціи причитающагося Россіи отъ Китая вознагражденія, [слово скрыто краснымъ штампомъ / illegible under stamp]
**ВЫСОЧАЙШАГО Указа 1 Марта 1902 г.**

на нарицательный капиталъ въ **181.950.000 рублей**

= 393.000.000 имп. герм. марокъ = 231.870.000 голл. гульд. = 19.257.000 фунт. стерл.

(строки о количествѣ листовъ, частью подъ штампами:) въ количествѣ 22000 листовъ по 5000 имп. герм. мар. съ № 000001 по № 022000, 75000 листовъ по 2000 имп. герм. мар. съ № 022001 по № 097000, 110000 листовъ по 1000 имп. герм. мар. съ № 097001 по № 207000 и 46000 листовъ по 500 имп. герм. мар. съ № 207001 по № 253000.

**ОБЛИГАЦІЯ** № 116485

**ВЪ ТЫСЯЧУ ИМПЕРСКИХЪ ГЕРМАНСКИХЪ МАРОКЪ**

463 руб. = 590 голл. гульд. = 49 фунт. стерл.,

вписанная въ Государственную Долговую Книгу, кн. _____ стр. _____

**НА ПРЕДЪЯВИТЕЛЯ.**

(Основной текстъ:)

На основаніи ВЫСОЧАЙШАГО Указа 1 Марта 1902 года для реализаціи причитающагося Россіи вознагражденія въ возмѣщеніе убытковъ, понесенныхъ вслѣдствіе смутъ въ Китаѣ, выпущенъ настоящій 4% Государственный заемъ на нарицательный капиталъ въ 181.950.000 рублей, равныхъ 393.000.000 германскихъ марокъ, равныхъ 231.870.000 голландскихъ гульденовъ, равныхъ 19.257.000 фунтовъ стерлинговъ, ежегодные платежи по коему находились бы въ точномъ соотвѣтствіи съ причитающимися отъ Китайскаго Правительства ежегодными уплатами процентовъ и погашеній по слѣдуемой Россіи части вознагражденія, которое Китай обязался выплатить согласно эдикта Китайскаго Императора отъ 29 Мая новаго стиля 1901 года.

Заемъ сей внесенъ въ Государственную Долговую Книгу подъ наименованіемъ: «Россійскій 4% Государственный заемъ 1902 г.», выпущенный для реализаціи причитающагося Россіи отъ Китая вознагражденія. Облигаціи сего займа выпускаются именныя и на предъявителя, достоинствомъ въ 500, 1000, 2000 и 5000 имп. герм. марокъ, считая каждая 1000 имп. герм. марокъ = 463 рублямъ = 590 голл. гульд. = 49 фунт. стерл. Владѣлецъ сей облигаціи имѣетъ право на сумму въ ТЫСЯЧУ ИМПЕРСКИХЪ ГЕРМАНСКИХЪ МАРОКЪ = 463 руб., приносящую ЧЕТЫРЕ процента въ годъ дохода до погашенія оной тиражемъ или выкупомъ; теченіе процентовъ начинается съ 19 Декабря 1901 г./1 Января 1902 г.

Облигаціи сіи освобождены навсегда отъ всякихъ русскихъ налоговъ.

Проценты уплачиваются два раза въ годъ: 18 Іюня/1 Іюля и 19 Декабря/1 Января, по предъявленіи купоновъ, по желанію владѣльца:

(два столбца платежныхъ мѣстъ:)

въ РОССІИ — въ Конторахъ и Отдѣленіяхъ Государственнаго Банка — рублями;

въ ГЕРМАНІИ — имперскими германскими марками —
въ Берлинѣ: у гг. Мендельсонъ и К°,
„  „  у г. С. Блейхредеръ,
„  „  въ Дирекціи Учетнаго Общества,
„  „  въ Берлинскомъ Торговомъ Обществѣ,
„  „  у г. Роберта Варшауеръ и К°,
во Франкфуртѣ н/М., въ Дирекціи Учетн. Общества;

въ ГОЛЛАНДІИ — голландскими гульденами — у гг. Липпманъ, Розенталь и К° въ Амстердамѣ;

въ АНГЛІИ — фунтами стерлинговъ — въ Агентствѣ Русскаго для внѣшней торговли Банка въ Лондонѣ.

Облигаціи сего займа погашаются по нарицательной цѣнѣ въ теченіе 39 лѣтъ ежегодными тиражами по плану, на оборотѣ напечатанному. Тиражи будутъ производиться въ Сентябрѣ каждаго года, начиная съ 1902 г. Капиталъ по вышедшимъ въ тиражъ облигаціямъ уплачивается въ слѣдующій за тиражемъ срокъ уплаты процентовъ.

До 19 Декабря 1914/1 Января 1915 года отчисленіе на погашеніе займа не будетъ увеличиваемо сверхъ предусмотрѣннаго планомъ погашенія суммы, а равно до того же срока не будетъ приступлено ни къ выкупу, ни къ конверсіи настоящаго займа.

Уплата капитала по облигаціямъ производится въ тѣхъ же городахъ и въ тѣхъ же валютахъ, какъ и уплата купоновъ, по предъявленіи самой облигаціи съ талономъ и всѣми купонами, срокъ коихъ истечетъ послѣ срока уплаты капитала; сумма недостающихъ купоновъ будетъ удержана при выдачѣ капитала по выкупаемымъ облигаціямъ.

Облигаціи сего займа сохраняютъ платежную силу въ теченіе 30 лѣтъ отъ сроковъ, назначенныхъ для ихъ оплаты, а купоны сего займа — въ теченіе 10 лѣтъ отъ срока ихъ уплаты.

Каждая облигація сего займа снабжена купонами и талономъ для полученія новыхъ купоновъ по истеченіи старыхъ. Облигаціи сего займа выпускаются именныя и на предъявителя. Правила относительно именныхъ облигацій, ихъ обмѣна изъ одного вида въ другую, ихъ обмѣна на предъявительскія и обратно утверждаются Министромъ Финансовъ.

Облигаціи сего займа будутъ приниматься по цѣнамъ, устанавливаемымъ на каждое полугодіе Министромъ Финансовъ, въ публичныхъ торгахъ Правительственныхъ Палатъ, въ залогъ: а) по казеннымъ подрядамъ и поставкамъ, б) по разсрочиваемому акцизу и в) въ обезпеченіе таможенныхъ пошлинъ. Указанныя цѣны будутъ не ниже 90% нарицательной цѣны облигацій.

**Управляющій Государственною Коммиссіею Погашенія Долговъ** [подпись / signature]

**Бухгалтеръ** [подпись / signature]

(на документѣ имѣется нѣсколько красныхъ и фіолетовыхъ печатей; въ верхнихъ углахъ — двуглавый орелъ и знакъ; часть текста перекрыта штампами)

---

## English Translation:

**IMPERIAL RUSSIAN GOVERNMENT.**

**RUSSIAN**

**4% STATE LOAN OF 1902**

issued to realise the indemnity due to Russia from China, [word hidden under a red stamp / illegible]
**by the Supreme Ukase of 1 March 1902.**

on a nominal capital of **181,950,000 rubles**

= 393,000,000 Imperial German marks = 231,870,000 Dutch guilders = 19,257,000 pounds sterling

(lines on the number of sheets, partly under stamps:) in the quantity of 22,000 sheets of 5,000 Imperial German marks, Nos. 000001 to 022000; 75,000 sheets of 2,000 Imperial German marks, Nos. 022001 to 097000; 110,000 sheets of 1,000 Imperial German marks, Nos. 097001 to 207000; and 46,000 sheets of 500 Imperial German marks, Nos. 207001 to 253000.

**BOND** No. 116485

**FOR ONE THOUSAND IMPERIAL GERMAN MARKS**

463 rubles = 590 Dutch guilders = 49 pounds sterling,

entered in the State Debt Book, vol. _____ page _____

**TO THE BEARER.**

(Main text:)

By virtue of the Supreme Ukase of 1 March 1902, for the realisation of the indemnity due to Russia in compensation for the losses sustained on account of the disturbances in China, the present 4% State Loan is issued on a nominal capital of 181,950,000 rubles, equal to 393,000,000 German marks, equal to 231,870,000 Dutch guilders, equal to 19,257,000 pounds sterling, the annual payments upon which would correspond exactly to the annual payments of interest and amortisation due from the Chinese Government on the portion of the indemnity owed to Russia, which China undertook to pay in accordance with the edict of the Chinese Emperor of 29 May, New Style, 1901.

This loan is entered in the State Debt Book under the designation: "Russian 4% State Loan of 1902," issued for the realisation of the indemnity due to Russia from China. The bonds of this loan are issued as registered and to bearer, in denominations of 500, 1,000, 2,000, and 5,000 Imperial German marks, reckoning each 1,000 Imperial German marks = 463 rubles = 590 Dutch guilders = 49 pounds sterling. The holder of this bond is entitled to the sum of ONE THOUSAND IMPERIAL GERMAN MARKS = 463 rubles, yielding FOUR percent per annum in income until its redemption by drawing or repurchase; the running of interest begins on 19 December 1901 / 1 January 1902.

These bonds are exempt forever from all Russian taxes.

Interest is paid twice a year: 18 June / 1 July and 19 December / 1 January, on presentation of the coupons, at the holder's option:

(two columns of places of payment:)

in RUSSIA — at the Offices and Branches of the State Bank — in rubles;

in GERMANY — in Imperial German marks —
in Berlin: at Messrs. Mendelssohn & Co.,
"  "  at Mr. S. Bleichröder,
"  "  at the Direction of the Discount Company,
"  "  at the Berlin Trade Company,
"  "  at Mr. Robert Warschauer & Co.,
in Frankfurt am Main, at the Direction of the Discount Company;

in HOLLAND — in Dutch guilders — at Messrs. Lippmann, Rosenthal & Co. in Amsterdam;

in ENGLAND — in pounds sterling — at the Agency of the Russian Bank for Foreign Trade in London.

The bonds of this loan are redeemed at par over the course of 39 years by annual drawings according to the plan printed on the reverse. The drawings will be conducted in September of each year, beginning in 1902. The capital of bonds drawn is paid on the interest-payment date following the drawing.

Until 19 December 1914 / 1 January 1915 the amount set aside for the amortisation of the loan shall not be increased beyond the sum provided by the amortisation plan, and likewise until the same date no recourse shall be had either to repurchase or to conversion of the present loan.

Payment of the capital of the bonds is made in the same cities and in the same currencies as the payment of the coupons, upon presentation of the bond itself with its talon and all coupons whose maturity falls after the date of payment of the capital; the amount of any missing coupons will be deducted upon payment of the capital on the bonds being redeemed.

The bonds of this loan retain their payment force for 30 years from the dates fixed for their payment, and the coupons of this loan for 10 years from the date of their payment.

Each bond of this loan is furnished with coupons and a talon for obtaining new coupons upon the expiry of the old ones. The bonds of this loan are issued as registered and to bearer. The rules regarding registered bonds, their exchange from one type into another, their exchange for bearer bonds and back, are established by the Minister of Finance.

The bonds of this loan will be accepted, at prices fixed for each half-year by the Minister of Finance, at public tenders of the Government Chambers, as security: a) for treasury contracts and supplies, b) for excise payable by instalments, and c) as guarantee of customs duties. The said prices shall not be below 90% of the nominal value of the bonds.

**Manager of the State Commission for the Redemption of Debts** [signature]

**Accountant** [signature]

(the document bears several red and violet seals; the double-headed eagle and an emblem appear in the upper corners; part of the text is overlaid by stamps)

title: Imperial Russian Government 4½% State Loan Bond, 1905
description: This printed bearer bond (облигація) belongs to the Imperial Russian Government's 4½% Russian State Loan of 1905, authorised by the Imperial Ukase of 15 December 1904. Its face value of 500 imperial German marks is restated, in the manner typical of a loan placed simultaneously across several European exchanges, as 231 roubles 50 kopecks, 294 Dutch guilders, and 24 pounds 7 shillings 6 pence sterling; the whole issue totalled 231,500,000 roubles. Payable to bearer and entered in the State Debt Book, it was floated chiefly on German capital while the autocracy was financing the Russo-Japanese War and confronting the upheavals of 1905. The 4½% coupon, above the rate of earlier prewar issues, registers the heightened risk lenders attached to Russian credit. The imperial double-headed eagle and dense engine-turned guilloche borders project sovereign solidity and guard against forgery.
creator: Imperial Russian Government
notes: Imperial Russian Government 4½% State Loan 1905. 500 German marks. No. 319459.

**НА ПРЕДЪЯВИТЕЛЯ**

Владѣлецъ сей облигаціи имѣетъ право на сумму въ пятьсотъ имперскихъ германскихъ марокъ = 231 руб. 50 коп., приносящую четыре съ половиною процента въ годъ дохода до срока, съ котораго капиталъ облигаціи подлежитъ уплатѣ какъ вслѣдствіе розыгрыша ея по тиражу, такъ и вслѣдствіе досрочнаго выкупа займа Правительствомъ или заявленіи держателя о желаніи получить нарицательный капиталъ облигаціи.

Облигаціи сего займа освобождаются навсегда отъ всякихъ русскихъ налоговъ. Проценты уплачиваются два раза въ годъ, 18 Іюня/1 Іюля и 19 Декабря/1 Января, по предъявленіи купоновъ по желанію владѣльца:

въ РОССІИ — въ Конторахъ и Отдѣленіяхъ Государственнаго Банка — рублями;

„ ГЕРМАНІИ — германскими марками — въ Берлинѣ у Гг. Мендельсонъ и К°, „ „ у Г. С. Блейхредеръ, „ „ въ Дирекціи Учетнаго Общества, „ „ въ Берлинскомъ Торговомъ Обществѣ; во Франкфуртѣ н/М. въ Дирекціи Учетнаго Общества;

(dense fine-print legal paragraph; partially legible) Всѣ облигаціи настоящаго займа будутъ погашены не позже [illegible] г. ... въ Декабрѣ 1916 г./1 Января 1917 г. title: Kettingverklaring for a De Woning Maatschappij Batavia Share, Amsterdam, 1941
description: This printed Dutch form is a kettingverklaring (chain declaration) for the trading of foreign securities in regular exchange dealings, registering a 100-guilder (f 100,-) share in De Woning Maatschappij Batavia, a housing company in the Netherlands East Indies. It was issued at Amsterdam and dated 24 January 1941, certified by the Vereeniging voor den Effectenhandel (Association for Securities Trading) and requested by P. Boeken Jr., a member of that Amsterdam body; the alternative Rotterdam and 's-Gravenhage memberships are struck through. The form invokes the decree of the Secretary-General of the Department of Finance of 18 September 1940 (Verordening No. 155/1940) and article 10 of the Deviezenverordening 1940, the foreign-exchange ordinance imposed under German occupation. The document attests that the security was registered and so could pass between residents without separate declaration. Its bureaucratic restraint records how wartime currency controls bound even colonial shares into an unbroken, certified chain of ownership.
creator: De Woning Maatschappij Batavia (shareholder / Dutch notary)
notes: Dutch Kettingverklaring (chain declaration) No. 1499 for De Woning Maatschappij Batavia shares. January 24, 1941. Related to collection item No. 355.

Onder verkrijging in regelmatig beursverkeer wordt in dit verband verstaan verkrijging overeenkomstig de Beursvoorschriften 1940 en de reglementen van de Vereeniging voor den Effectenhandel te Amsterdam, van de Vereeniging van Effectenhandelaren te Rotterdam en van den Bond voor den Geld- en Effectenhandel in de Provincie te 's-Gravenhage, voorzover de verkrijging geschiedt door of door tusschenkomst van leden van de genoemde vereenigingen.

for trading of foreign securities in regular stock exchange traffic.

This security does not require notification as intended in art. 2 section 1 of the aforementioned decree, if the security is acquired between residents in regular stock exchange traffic.

Regular stock exchange traffic in this context is understood to mean acquisition in accordance with the Stock Exchange Regulations 1940 and the regulations of the Association for Securities Trading in Amsterdam, of the Association of Securities Dealers in Rotterdam and of the Bond for Securities Trading in the Province at 's-Gravenhage, insofar as the acquisition takes place through or by means of members of the said associations.

title: Royal Dutch Petroleum Company Share-Subscription Warrant, The Hague, 1937
description: This printed warrant (inschrijvingsrecht) is a transferable subscription right issued by the Naamlooze Vennootschap Koninklijke Nederlandsche Maatschappij tot Exploitatie van Petroleumbronnen in Nederlandsch-Indië — the Royal Dutch Petroleum Company — at 's-Gravenhage (The Hague), dated February 1937. Against surrender of the warrant, the bearer may purchase one new share of F. 1,000 nominal for F. 4,500 up to 31 March 1940, or for F. 5,000 from 1 April 1940 until the right expires on 31 March 1943. The escalating exercise price rewards early conversion and prices the option of waiting. Issued by a firm chartered in 1890 and by then the Dutch partner in the Royal Dutch/Shell combination, the instrument documents a rights offering through which the oil concern tapped existing holders for fresh capital. The green guilloché frame, the company seal, and the crowned Netherlands arms assert chartered respectability rather than any decorative programme.
creator: Koninklijke Nederlandsche Maatschappij tot Exploitatie van Petroleumbronnen in Nederlandsch-Indië (Royal Dutch Petroleum)
notes: Royal Dutch Petroleum Company (Koninklijke Nederlandsche Maatschappij) stock purchase warrant. No. 015281. The Hague, February 1937. Subscription price F. 4,500 (until March 31, 1940) or F. 5,000 (until March 31, 1943).

title: Austrian Third War Loan Bond
description: A printed bearer bond (Schuldverschreibung) for ten thousand kronen (Zehntausend Kronen) bearing 5½ percent annual tax-free interest, issued by the Imperial-Royal treasury at Vienna and dated 1 October 1915. It belongs to the Third Austrian War Loan (Dritte fünfeinhalbprozentige österreichische Kriegsanleihe), one of the successive loans floated by Austria-Hungary to fund its part in the European war. By this third subscription the conflict was proving costlier than foreseen, and the 10,000-krone face value placed the note among the largest denominations, aimed at banks and men of means rather than ordinary savers. Coupons fell due each 1 January and 1 July, with repayment promised from 1930. The double-headed Habsburg eagle crowns the sheet, an emblem of imperial credit, while a stylized allegorical female head set within ornamental framing at the foot reflects the decorative Viennese idiom of the period.
creator: Austro-Hungarian Government
notes: Austrian Third War Loan (Dritte Österreichische Kriegsanleihe), 5½% amortisable. 10,000 kronen, Serie E, No. 100356. Vienna, October 1, 1915.

# Austrian Third War Loan Bond - Transcription

**Original (German):**

10.000                                                                                                    10.000

SERIE E                                                                                        № 100.356

DRITTE FÜNFEINHALBROZENTIGE
ÖSTERREICHISCHE

KRIEGSANLEIHE
ZEHNTAUSEND KRONEN

Die k. k. Staatsschulden-Kasse in Wien zahlt an den Inhaber dieser auf Grund der kaiserlichen Verordnung vom 4. August 1914 R. G. Bl. Nr. 202 ausgegebenen Staatsschuldverschreibung am 1. Oktober 1930 den Betrag von Zehntausend Kronen. Diese Staatsschuldverschreibung wird mit jährlich fünfeinhalb Prozent verzinst; die Zahlung der Zinsen erfolgt ohne jeden sonstigen Gebühren- oder sonstigen Abzug am 1. Jänner und 1. Juli jeden Jahres nachmitten an den Inhaber der zu dieser Staatsschuldverschreibung gehörigen Coupons bei der k. k. Staatsschulden-Kasse in Wien. Die Staatsverwaltung behält sich das Recht vor, diese Staatsschuldverschreibung gegen [unclear] zu verlautbarten Kündigungsfrist auch vor dem 1. Oktober 1930 zurückzuzahlen. Im Falle unter die Verzinsung mit dem früheren Rückzahlungstermme. Der Anspruch aus dieser Staatsschuldverschreibung erlischt durch Verjährung in Ansehung des Kapitals binnen dreißig Jahren, in Ansehung der Zinsen binnen fünf Jahren von den Fälligkeitstermine. In diese Staatsschuldverschreibung ist mit 30 Coupons versehen, von denen der letzte am 1. Oktober 1930 fällig ist. Der letzte Coupon ist ein vierteljähriger, die ihrigen sind halbjährige; die am 1. Jänner 1916 fälligen Zinsen werden im Verzugswege beglichen.

Wien, am 1. Oktober 1915.                                Der k. k. Finanzminister:
[Signature - illegible]

Diese Schuldverschreibung ist in dem Hauptbuche der Staatsschuld eingetragen.

Für die Staatsschulden-Kontrollkommission des Reichsrates:
[Two signatures - illegible]

SERIES E                                                                                        No. 100,356

THIRD FIVE-AND-A-HALF PERCENT
AUSTRIAN

WAR LOAN
TEN THOUSAND KRONEN

The Imperial and Royal State Debt Office in Vienna pays to the bearer of this State Debt Certificate issued by virtue of the Imperial Ordinance of August 4, 1914, R. G. Bl. No. 202, on October 1, 1930, the sum of Ten Thousand Kronen. This State Debt Certificate bears annual interest of five and a half percent; the payment of interest is made without any deductions for fees or otherwise on January 1 and July 1 of each year to the bearer of the coupons belonging to this State Debt Certificate at the Imperial and Royal State Debt Office in Vienna. The State Administration reserves the right to repay this State Debt Certificate against [unclear] notice also before October 1, 1930. In the event of [unclear] the interest rate [unclear] with the earlier repayment deadline. The claim arising from this State Debt Certificate lapses through expiration of time in respect of the capital within thirty years, in respect of interest within five years from the due date. This State Debt Certificate is furnished with 30 coupons, of which the last falls due on October 1, 1930. The last coupon is quarterly; the others are semi-annual; the interest due on January 1, 1916, will be settled through the ordinary channels.

Vienna, October 1, 1915.                                The Imperial and Royal Finance Minister:
[Signature - illegible]

This debt certificate is entered in the principal ledger of the state debt.

For the State Debt Control Commission of the Imperial Council:
[Two signatures - illegible]

title: Russian-American Fur Company Share
description: This printed bearer share certificate (action de capital au porteur) represents one hundred francs of the capital of La Pelleterie Russo-Américaine, a société anonyme seated at 2 Place des Victoires in Paris and incorporated under French law before the notary Maître Dufour. The total capital of five million francs is divided into 4,000 actions de jouissance and 46,000 actions de capital, of which this is one. It is dated Paris, 31 July 1926. The firm, trading in furs drawn from Russia and North America, sat at the meeting point of the Soviet fur trade — which, under the New Economic Policy, exported pelts to earn hard currency — and the Western luxury market. The engraving is restrained and typographic: a rising-sun medallion bearing the company's seal anchors a finely guilloched border, the sober house style of inter-war Parisian share design rather than any pictorial flourish.
creator: La Pelleterie Russo-Américaine
notes: La Pelleterie Russo-Américaine, S.A. Action au porteur, 100 francs. Capital 5,000,000 francs. No. 036857. Paris, July 31, 1926.

title: La Platense Flotilla Company Share Warrant (Glasgow, 1886)
description: This printed share warrant to bearer certifies its holder as proprietor of one fully paid share of £10 in La Platense Flotilla Company, Limited, a concern capitalised at £1,000,000 divided into 100,000 such shares. The text runs in parallel English and French, and the warrant is given under the company's common seal at Glasgow, dated 20 March 1886, with a three-shilling impressed duty stamp cancelled by a Scottish revenue postmark of 25 March 1886. The firm was floated to work a steam flotilla on the Río de la Plata and its tributaries, the commercial artery draining the pastoral hinterlands of Argentina and Uruguay toward Buenos Aires, an enterprise typical of the British capital then financing River Plate shipping. The engraved cartouche of a scallop shell flanked by dolphins above a river landscape announces the maritime venture in restrained neoclassical ornament.
creator: La Platense Flotilla Company Limited
notes: La Platense Flotilla Company Limited. Share warrant to bearer, £10. No. 15821. One of 100,000 shares. River flotilla, Río de la Plata. Ca. early 1900s.

This is to Certify that the Bearer of this [warrant] is the Proprietor of ONE fully paid Certificat est Propriétaire dans Action at Borse numbered as above in the Capital of the Company thereof and is entitled to the Inheritance and all other Regulations of the Company for the time being.

La Platense Flotilla Company Limited.          La Platense Flotilla Company Limited.

Given under our hand at [location] this 3rd hand 1896

[Signatures]

The Bearer of the above Warrant will receive in exchange for this Talon a fresh supply when those below have all fallen due.

**JENNY** - Chairman

title: Little Miami Rail Road Company Share Certificate
description: This engraved certificate records ownership of ten shares, of fifty dollars each, in the capital stock of the Little Miami Rail Road Company, chartered under the State of Ohio and transferable only on the company's own books upon surrender of the instrument. It was issued at Cincinnati on June 17th, 1862, in the name of James G. King's Sons, the New York banking house here acting as Trustees. Incorporated in 1836 to link Cincinnati with the interior toward Springfield, the Little Miami was among Ohio's pioneering railroads, and by the early 1860s its stock had become a stable, dividend-bearing holding suited to fiduciary investors. The vignette of a passenger carriage drawn by a smoking locomotive announces the enterprise it funds, while the lower scene of a farmer ploughing behind horses ties the railroad to the agricultural country it was built to serve.
creator: Little Miami Rail Road Company
