title: Republic of China Lung-Tsing-U-Hai Railway Treasury Bill
description: This printed bearer treasury bill (schatkistbiljet aan toonder), face value F. 1,000 in Dutch currency at 8 percent interest, belongs to the 8% Lung-Tsing-U-Hai Railway (隴秦豫海鐵路) Treasury Bill loan of 1923 floated by the Government of the Chinese Republic. The total issue of F. 16,667,000 was divided into 16,667 bills of F. 1,000 each, the round figure betraying its origin as a fixed sterling-or-franc equivalent rendered into guilders for the Netherlands market. The line, conceived to run from Gansu in the northwest across Shaanxi and Henan to the Jiangsu coast, was financed piecemeal through separate European tranches; warlord rivalry and recurrent civil conflict left it only partly built and its service uncertain. A bridge spanning a mountain river crowns the certificate, while bilingual Dutch and Chinese text and vermilion seals mark the meeting of two fiscal worlds, the bond a token of European capital lent against China's still-unrealized railway future.
creator: Government of the Republic of China
notes: 8% Lung-Tsing-U-Hai Railway Treasury Bill Loan 1923. F. 1,000 Dutch guilders. No. 11638. Total issue F. 16,667,000. Issued by Republic of China.

title: Merchants Insurance Company Stock Transfer, Providence, 1858
description: This printed and hand-completed instrument records the transfer of twelve shares of the capital stock of the Merchants Insurance Company, executed "for value received" by the assigning shareholder in favor of James S. Ham and attested by a witness. The decorative left border fixes the par value at fifty dollars per share, placing the holding's nominal worth at six hundred dollars. Dated at Providence, April 1, 1858, it issues from one of the marine and fire insurers serving the maritime trade and textile mills of antebellum Rhode Island. The form's very existence speaks to an active secondary market in insurance shares, where such equities passed readily from hand to hand as a store and conduit of mercantile wealth. The restrained engraved heading and ornamental vertical panel reflect the plain, type-driven commercial printing typical of New England financial stationery of the period.
creator: Merchants Insurance Company
notes: Merchants Insurance Company (Providence, Rhode Island). Transfer of 12 shares to James S. Ham. No. 178. April 1, 1858.

title: The Mexican Telephone Company Share Certificate, New York City, 1905
description: This engraved certificate records ownership of twenty-five shares of capital stock in The Mexican Telephone Company, each share carrying a par value of ten dollars. The company was organized under the laws of the State of New York with an authorized capital of $2,000,000 divided into 200,000 shares. Issued at New York City and dated February 28, 1905, the certificate names Harry E. Jacobs of Cambridge as holder and required countersignature by the Boston Safe Deposit & Trust Company as transfer agent, binding the New York charter to Boston capital. The legend "Issued for Property Purchased" marks these shares as consideration paid in kind rather than cash, a common device for capitalizing assets contributed to a venture. The enterprise belonged to the wave of foreign-financed telephone concerns extending lines through Mexico during the long presidency of Porfirio Díaz, when overseas capital was courted to build the country's communications. The plain bank-note-style border and bold roman titling favor legibility and the assurance of a financial instrument over ornament.
creator: Mexican Telephone Company
notes: Mexican Telephone Company. 25 shares at $10 each. No. 16621. Issued to Harry E. Jacobs. Countersigned by Boston Safe Deposit & Trust Co., February 28, 1905. CANCELLED.

title: Minas Geraes Goldfields Limited Share Warrant, London, 1912
description: This printed share warrant to bearer certifies that its holder is the registered proprietor of twenty-five fully paid shares of £1 each in Minas Geraes Goldfields Limited, a company incorporated under the Companies Acts 1862 to 1900 with a capital of £150,000. It was given under the common seal of the company at London in July 1912, and it bears an impressed seven-shilling stamp duty surmounted by a crown. The warrant was issued to finance British exploitation of gold-mining ground in the Brazilian state of Minas Gerais (spelled "Minas Geraes" in the period), a region celebrated since its eighteenth-century gold rush for mineral wealth. As a bearer instrument, it allowed shares to pass by simple delivery, easing trade on the London market where Edwardian and Georgian investors funded numerous overseas mining ventures. The bold scarlet border, laurel sprays, and twinned heraldic shields lend the document a confident, prosperous air.

title: Mines d'Or de Nam Kok Share
description: This is a fully paid-up bearer share (action de cent francs au porteur, entièrement libérée) in the Société Anonyme des Mines d'Or de Nam Kok, a Paris-domiciled gold-mining company capitalised at 30,000,000 francs, its statutes deposited with Maître Ader, notary in Paris. The certificate is denominated at 100 francs and entitles the holder to a full sheet of dividend coupons printed below. The company's name points to gold workings in French colonial Asia, financed through the Paris bourse, where syndicates floated extractive ventures on distant terrain to draw small savers into speculative mining capital. The engraved frame, cloud-filled vignette and dense guilloche watermark spelling the company name are the conventional security devices of the period's French share certificates, lending a borrowed solidity to an inherently uncertain promise. A stamp-duty notice records the issue's authorisation in the Journal Officiel of 6 November 1929.
creator: Mines d'Or de Nam Kok
notes: Mines d'Or de Nam Kok. Action au porteur, 100 francs. Capital 30,000,000 francs. No. 059,820. Paris. Ca. early 1900s. Gold mining in Indochina or southern China.

title: Moscow-Kazan Railway Company Bond
description: This printed bearer bond (облигація) belongs to the 4% bond loan of 1901 of the Moscow-Kazan Railway Company (Общества Московско-Казанской Желѣзной Дороги), issued under the perpetual guarantee of the Imperial Russian Government. Its face value of 2,000 German imperial marks is restated in the principal markets of issue—925 rubles 80 kopecks (at 1 ruble = 2.16 marks), 1,180 Dutch guilders, and 97 pounds 18 shillings—against a total loan capital of 35,880,000 marks. Printed at Moscow in 1901 in parallel Russian, German, and Dutch columns, the document was placed simultaneously in the German and Netherlands capital markets, where French and German savers had become the chief financiers of Russian railway expansion. The sovereign guarantee, foregrounded in the text, converts a provincial trunk line to Kazan and the Volga into a near-government security; the multilingual frame and quiet engraved borders address a cautious rentier abroad rather than a domestic speculator.
creator: Moscow-Kazan Railway Company
notes: Moscow-Kazan Railway Company 4% Bond Loan of 1901. 2,000 German marks. No. 06181. Trilingual Russian/German/Dutch. Imperial Russian Government guarantee.
transcription:
# Moscow-Kazan Railway Company — 4% Bond of the 1901 Loan (2,000 German Marks, Bearer) — Transcription

## Original (Russian):

(Corner denomination numerals in all four corners: **2000**)

(Top-left stamp box:) Stempel der Kaiserlich Russischen Regierung
(Top-right stamp box:) Stempelabgabe bezahlt

**4% ОБЛИГАЦІОННЫЙ ЗАЕМЪ 1901 года**

**ОБЩЕСТВА МОСКОВСКО-КАЗАНСКОЙ ЖЕЛѢЗНОЙ ДОРОГИ**

на нарицательный капиталъ въ 35.880.000 марокъ германской имперской валюты =16.608.852 руб. (1 руб. = ¹⁄₁₅ империала) =21.169.200 голл. гульд. =1.756.326 фунт. стерл., съ безусловною гарантіею ИМПЕРАТОРСКАГО Россійскаго Правительства въ уплатѣ процентовъ и погашеніи.

**ОБЛИГАЦІЯ**

**№06181**

**ВЪ ДВѢ ТЫСЯЧИ МАРОКЪ ГЕРМАНСКОЙ ИМПЕРСКОЙ ВАЛЮТЫ**

=925 руб. 80 коп. (1 руб. = ¹⁄₁₅ империала) =1180 голл. гульд. =97 фунт. стерл. 18 шилл.

**НА ПРЕДЪЯВИТЕЛЯ.**

Владѣлецъ сей облигаціи участвуетъ ДВУМЯ ТЫСЯЧАМИ марокъ германской имперской валюты въ займѣ, заключенномъ въ размѣрѣ 35.880.000 марокъ германской имперской валюты на условіяхъ, на оборотѣ сего означенныхъ, и обезпечивается всѣмъ имуществомъ и всѣми доходами Общества и имѣющимъ преимущество предъ всѣми будущими займами.

Независимо отъ сего облигаціи сего займа даруется безусловная гарантія ИМПЕРАТОРСКАГО Россійскаго Правительства въ уплатѣ процентовъ и погашеніи со дня ихъ выпуска. Платежи процентовъ по купонамъ и погашеніе по облигаціямъ навсегда освобождаются отъ всякихъ русскихъ налоговъ.

Проценты изъ 4% годовыхъ выплачиваются по предъявленіи купоновъ ежегодно въ сроки 18 Іюня/1 Іюля и 19 Декабря/1 Января. Москва, 1901 г.

Правленіе Общества Московско-Казанской желѣзной дороги:

(three engraved signatures — [illegible])

Главный бухгалтеръ (signature — [illegible])

Уполномоченный отъ Правительства (signature — [illegible])

---

(Parallel German column — heading block:)

title: Morris Canal and Banking Company Stock Transfer Receipt
description: A single sheet bearing two engraved stock-transfer assignments of the Morris Canal and Banking Company, executed at its Agency at the Phenix Bank in the City of New York and dated June 28 and July 10, 1845. Each is a printed "For Value Received" form by which a holder assigns and transfers shares in the capital stock of the company's "OF 1844" issue, standing in the holder's name on the company's books, to be subscribed before a witness. The Morris Canal and Banking Company, chartered in New Jersey in 1824 to operate the Morris Canal, joined a public work to banking privileges in a manner characteristic of the era's mixed canal-and-banking corporations. Drawn during the company's troubled reorganization following its mid-1840s difficulties, the forms document a brisk secondary trade in its shares handled through New York's financial houses. The plain typographic frame, free of allegory, befits a routine instrument of transfer rather than a presentation certificate.
creator: Morris Canal and Banking Company (Agency at Phenix Bank, NYC)
notes: Morris Canal and Banking Company. Two stock transfer receipts: (1) June 28, 1845, to Hopkins & Berton; (2) July 10, 1845, to E.S. Map. Phenix Bank agency, New York City.

title: New York Central and Hudson River Railroad Company Debt Certificate
description: This engraved certificate is a debt certificate for one thousand dollars, bearing interest at four per cent per annum payable semi-annually, issued by The New York Central and Hudson River Railroad Company and dated December 1892. Its terms specify that principal shall not fall due until the first day of May 1902, and that both principal and interest are payable in gold coin of the United States "of the standard of weight and fineness" then prevailing, a gold clause that reflects the hard-money anxieties of the early 1890s, when fears over silver agitation and the security of the dollar pressed investors to demand specie guarantees. The instrument represents a form of medium-term borrowing the road used alongside its bonded debt and equity. The vignette, in the restrained engraver's classicism favored for American securities, sets two seated allegorical women, emblems of commerce and abundance, about a central globe, asserting the enterprise's reach and solidity.
creator: New York Central and Hudson River Railroad Company
notes: New York Central and Hudson River Railroad Company. Debt Certificate No. 1390. $1,000 at 4%. Due May 1, 1902. December 24, 1892.
transcription:

**FOR A VALUABLE CONSIDERATION RECEIVED BY EACH.**

**IT IS MUTUALLY AGREED BY:**

**The New York Central and Hudson River**
**Railroad Company**

**and the holder of the foregoing Debt Certificate No. 1390**
**of the New York Central Railroad Company for**

**ONE THOUSAND DOLLARS**

that the principal hereof shall not be due nor payable until the first day of May 1902, with interest at the rate of Four percent per annum, payable semi-annually on the first of twenty-four and shall both principal and interest shall be payable in gold coin of the United States of America of the standard of weight and fineness of Decem[ber] 24, 1892. Registered certificates of $1,000 or multiples thereof will be issued in exchange for certificates of the same, under such regulations as the said Company may prescribe. The assent to this agreement by the holder of said Debt Certificate is evidenced in its name, be for the purpose of having this agreement registered therewith, and by the acceptance of the accompanying coupons.

**Witness the seal of the Company**
**and [signature] at of December 1892**

[Signature: illegible/cursive]
**TREASURER**

title: New York, New Haven and Hartford Railroad Harlem River–Port Chester First Mortgage Gold Bond
description: This is a registered first mortgage gold bond of $10,000 bearing four per cent annual interest, issued by the New York, New Haven and Hartford Railroad Company under its Harlem River–Port Chester Branch First Mortgage. Its fifty-year term runs from the first day of November 1904 to maturity on the first day of May 1954, with principal and interest payable in United States gold coin at the company's New York office. Signed at New York in November 1904, the bond funded the New Haven's costly four-tracking and grade-separation of the corridor linking the Harlem River with Port Chester, the spine of its commuter traffic into Westchester and southern Connecticut. The gold-coin pledge and senior mortgage lien advertise the security's conservative grade to savings-bank investors. The engraved vignette of seated classical female figures flanking the company arms follows the allegorical convention of period bank-note engraving, lending an emblem of probity. Stamped SURRENDERED.
creator: New York, New Haven and Hartford Railroad Company
notes: New York, New Haven and Hartford Railroad. Harlem River–Port Chester First Mortgage 4% 50-Year Gold Bond. $10,000. No. 331. 1912. SURRENDERED.

title: Nuovo Monte Sussidio Vacabile della Città di Firenze share certificate, Florence, 1706
description: This handwritten and letterpress instrument is a share certificate (luogo) in the Nuovo Monte Sussidio Vacabile della Città di Firenze, the New Vacatable Subsidy Fund of the City of Florence, issued by its Protettori, Deputati and Amministratori under the Most Serene Grand Duke of Tuscany. It records the holder as creditor for one hundred ducati of Florentine money per luogo, yielding six per cent yearly ("ducati sei per cento l'Anno"). Dated at Florence on 11 August 1706, it rests on the fund's founding provision of 4 July 1692 and a decree of amplification of 8 August 1692. The Florentine monti were among Europe's earliest transferable public debts; in a monte vacabile the income reverted to the fund on the holder's death, blending bond and life annuity. The engraved Medici palle crowning the sheet bind sovereign credit to dynastic authority.
creator: Città di Firenze (City of Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany)
notes: Nuovo Monte Sussidio Vacabile della Città di Firenze. Public debt certificate at 6%. Florence, 1693. Grand Duke Cosimo III de' Medici. Among the earliest transferable public debt instruments in Europe.
transcription:
# Transcription

**Original (Italian):**

NOI Protettori, Deputari, & Amministratori del Nuovo Monte Sussidio Vacabile della Città di Firenze, ereto d'ordine del Serenifs. Gran Duca di Toscana nostro Signore, per Provvisione ottenuta nell' Ampliffimo Senato de' Quadrini del Serenifs. Gran Duca di Toscana nostro Signore, per Provvisione ottenuta nell' Ampliffimo Senato de' Quadrini del Serenifs.

A Tutti quelli, che vedranno la presente nostra faciamo piena, & indubirata fede, come

[handwritten text - partially illegible]

apparifce Creditore al Libro di detto Nuovo Monte Sussidio Segnaro E, a c. di valuta di ducati Cento di ire sette per ducaro moneta Fiorentina per ogni luogo, con li frutti di suo benefizio a ragione di ducati sei simili per cento l'Anno per ogni luogo dal di [handwritten - illegible] in avvenire,

come per partito nostro di questo giorno al Gior. di Permute, &c. segm. C, a c. [illegible]—Però ammettendo Noi detta Creditore di detto Monte di detti luoghi per luogo, come sopra pagabili di tre Mesi in tre Mesi per rata

[handwritten text - illegible]

e con li privileggi, prerogative, condizioni, e dichiarazioni di che in detta Provvisione, e nel nostro Decreto d'ampliatione, e dichiaraua. del di 8. Agofto 1692. al che tutto si abbia sempre relazzione: Ordinando in virtù di quella al nostro Camerlengo la presente, quale farà firmata da uno di Noi Protettori, e controfirmata dal nostro Cane.e figillata con il fogtlio figillo di d. Monte.

Data in Firenze nella nostra folita Residenza quell' [date - illegible]

[handwritten signature - illegible]

---

**English Translation:**

WE, the Protectors, Deputies, & Administrators of the New Monte Sussidio Vacabile of the City of Florence, established by order of the Most Serene Grand Duke of Tuscany our Lord, by Provision obtained in the Most Ample Senate of [the administrators]

To All those who shall see the present [document], we make clear and indubitable faith, as

[handwritten text - partially illegible]

appears [as] Creditor in the Book of said New Monte Sussidio, marked E, at [the value] of two hundred seventy ducats in Florentine money for every place, with the fruits of his benefit at the rate of six similar ducats per hundred per Year for every place from [date - illegible] going forward,

as by our decree of this day at [the office of] Permute, &c., signed C, at [location - illegible]—However, we, admitting the said Creditor of said Monte in said places for place, as above, payable in three-month intervals in three installments per payment

[handwritten text - illegible]

and with the privileges, prerogatives, conditions, and declarations which in said Provision, and in our Decree of amplification and declaration of the 8th of August 1692, to which all shall always refer: Ordering by virtue of that to our Chancellor the present [certificate], which shall be signed by one of Us Protectors, and countersigned by our Chancellor and sealed with the folio seal of said Monte.

Given in Florence in our usual Residence on [date - illegible]

title: Compañía del Ferro-Carril de Palencia á Ponferrada (Noroeste de España) Bond, Madrid 1862
description: A bilingual (Spanish/French) bearer obligation of the Compañía del Ferro-Carril de Palencia á Ponferrada, ó del Noroeste de España (Compagnie du Chemin de Fer de Palencia à Ponferrada ou du Nord-Ouest de l'Espagne), a joint-stock company sanctioned by royal decree of 18 May 1861 under a ninety-nine-year concession authorised by the laws of 21 April 1858 and 5 June 1859. The bond forms part of an emission of 68,420 obligations of 1,900 reales vellón (500 francs) each, bearing 57 reales (15 francs) of annual interest payable each 15 June and 15 December, redeemable at par by annual lottery drawings over ninety-two years from 1 July 1867; the headline total is 65,000,000 reales. Dated at Madrid, 15 June 1862, and signed by two administrators. The line spanning the Cantabrian range linked the Castilian grain plains to the coal of El Bierzo, and the dual-currency text registers the heavy dependence of Spanish railway promotion on French capital. The restrained engraved frame, with a small vignette of the works, subordinates ornament to the language of credit.
creator: Compañía del Ferro-Carril de Palencia a Ponferrada
notes: Compañía del Ferro-Carril de Palencia a Ponferrada (Nord-Ouest de l'Espagne). Bilingual Spanish/French bearer bond No. 32,105. 1,900 Reales = 500 Francs. 68,420 obligations in total. Madrid, ca. 1860s.

title: Norwich & Worcester Rail Road Company Bond
description: A printed coupon bond for $1,000 of the Norwich & Worcester Rail Road Company, chartered in the State of Connecticut, part of an issue whose whole authorized amount is $500,000. It bears six per cent interest, payable semi-annually on presentation of the attached coupons, the principal payable to the Treasurer of the State of Connecticut at the New England Trust Company in Boston. The form is dated at Hartford, its manuscript day and year left blank. That the bond runs to the State Treasurer marks it as security for Connecticut's aid to the line linking Norwich, on the Thames, with Worcester in Massachusetts. The engraved border crowds together emblems of the road's traffic and region: a locomotive at a station, a coasting vessel, a seated figure of Justice with sword and scales, mariners, harvesters with sheaves, and the state's arms, the route's commercial promise made ornament around the obligation it secures.
creator: Norwich & Worcester Rail Road Company
notes: Norwich & Worcester Rail Road Company (State of Connecticut). $1,000 bearer bond. No. 468. Total authorized issue $500,000. Printed by Evans, Hartford. Ca. 1840s–1850s.

title: Omnium Français du Film Share
description: This printed bearer share (action au porteur, Série A) of 100 francs belongs to the Omnium Français du Film, a société anonyme based in Paris with a capital of three million francs. That capital was divided into 30,000 shares of 100 francs each—26,000 of Série A and 4,000 of Série B—alongside 12,000 founder's shares (parts de fondation) carrying no nominal value. Dated 10 May 1928 and certified before Maître Baudrier, notary in Paris, the share was issued at the close of the French silent cinema's most prosperous decade, just as the arrival of synchronized sound threatened to upend production economics and demand fresh capital. The stylized geometric monogram "OFF" and the crisp sans-serif lettering of the corners reflect the Art Deco idiom then fashionable in French commercial design, lending a modern, industrial confidence to a speculative venture in a youthful entertainment trade.

title: Ostend Company Dividend Receipt
description: A printed and manuscript dividend receipt of the Generale Keyserlycke Indische Compagnie, the Imperial East India Company chartered at Ostend, signed by its cashier Joan Baptista Cogels and acknowledged by the shareholder C.J. De Witte at Antwerp on 11 August 1734. The form records payment of 360 guilders in wissel-gelt (bank money of exchange) as a repartition of six per cent, sixty guilders per share, on six shares held in the holder's name. The engraved vignette crowns the company's arms with the double-headed Habsburg eagle, asserting the imperial charter granted by Charles VI in 1722 that so provoked the maritime powers. Issued three years after Britain, the Dutch Republic, and France forced the company's suppression in 1731, the receipt belongs to its prolonged liquidation: the imperial insignia presides over a venture already being wound up, its capital returned rather than employed in trade.
creator: Generale Keyserlycke Indische Compagnie (Ostend Company)

title: Norbertine Fathers' Order 8% First Mortgage Bond, Gödöllő (Budapest), 1928
description: This printed bearer bond (obligatie luidend aan toonder) of 1,000 Dutch guilders, bearing 8 percent annual interest payable by half-yearly coupons, belongs to the First Mortgage Loan (1e Hypothecaire Leening) of the Order of Norbertine Fathers, the Praemonstratensian Canons of Jászó (Jassauer Praemonstratenser Koorheeren-Orde), seated at Gödöllő near Budapest. The whole loan amounts to 1,600,000 guilders, divided into 1,200 bonds of 1,000 guilders and 800 of 500, secured as a first mortgage on the order's properties and guaranteed by the Royal Hungarian Government. Dated Budapest, 1 August 1928, and administered by the Hollandsche Garantie & Trust Compagnie of Amsterdam, the instrument shows how a Catholic religious house in post-Trianon Hungary reached Dutch capital markets, the sovereign guarantee and guilder denomination together reassuring foreign lenders. The sober typographic frame, unadorned but for the order's seal, presents the loan as an institution of standing rather than speculation.
creator: Orde der Paters Norbertynen (Jassauer Praemonstratenser Koorheeren-Orde)
notes: Orde der Paters Norbertynen (Jászó Premonstratensian / Norbertine Fathers) 8% First Mortgage Bond. F. 1,000 Dutch guilders. No. 0.274. Budapest, August 1, 1928. Total issue F. 1,600,000. Royal Hungarian Government guarantee. Administered by Hollandsche Garantie & Trust Compagnie.
transcription:
# ORIGINAL (Dutch):

ORDE DER PATERS NORBERTYNEN
(JASSAUER PRAEMONSTRATENSER
KOORHEEREN-ORDE)
TE GÖDÖLLŐ BY BUDAPEST.

8% 1e HYPOTHECAIRE LEENING

GEGARANDEERD DOOR DE RON. HONGAARSCHE REGEERING

GROOT
F.1.600.000

VERDEELD IN 1.200 OBLIGATIES VAN F 1.000 EN 800 OBLIGATIES VAN F 500.

OBLIGATIE
LUIDEND AAN TOONDER

GROOT
F 1.000 NED. CT.

No. 0.274

De Jassauer Praemonstratenser Koorheeren-Orde te Gödöllő heeft krachtens een haar door de Kon. Hongaarsche Regeering verleende machtiging (Min. besluit 1872-1928 van 28 Juni 1926) het volgende leening te Gödöllő aangegaan ter grootte van F 1.600.000 (een miljoen zeshonderd duizend hollandsche guldens). Voor deze leening heeft de Jassauer Praemonstratenser Koorheeren-Orde te Gödöllő 1200 obligaties van F 1000 en 800 obligaties van F 500 Hollandsche guldens uitgegeven waarvan deze obligatie deel uitmaakt. De Obligatie verplicht niet tot betaling en terugbetaling op de volgende voorwaarden.

Voor de stipte betaling der rentecoupons en voor de terugbetaling dezer leening is de Jassauer Praemonstratenser Koorheeren-Orde met haar geheele roerend en onroerend vermogen aansprakelijk.

Als bijzondere zekerheid voor de rente-betaling en de terugbetaling dezer leening is een eerste hypotheek ingeschreven ten gunste der HOLLANDSCHE GARANTIE & TRUST COMPAGNIE te Amsterdam op de aan de Jassauer Praemonstratenser Koorheeren-Orde toebehoorende eigendommen te Gödöllő en verder op het grondbezeit a) Alsónovai, Felsonovai en Istaros, b) Vadaskesku, en Leopoldst.

Verder is de rente-dienst en de terugbetaling dezer leening verzekerd door den Kon. Hungarischen Staat, die volgens § V der tristacie van 19 Juli 1928, voor hoofdsom en rente volijdig borg blijft. Iedere obligatie draagt 8½% rente van 1 augustus 1928 tot aan den dag, dat zy terugbetaald wordt. De rente-betaling heeft telkens halfjaarliks plaats op 1 Februari en 1 Augustus en is betaalbaar te Amsterdam volgens de bepalingen der tristacie van 19 Juli 1928.

De obligatien worden terugbetaald volgens het plan en op de voorwaarden zoals die heiden gekeyde zijn afgedrukt.

De verschenen coupons vervallen in 5 jaren en de aflosbare gestelde obligaties na 30 jaar, beiden gereekend van af den dag, dat zy betaalbaar gesteld zyn.

Alle tristacie is gededoceerd by Notaris Franz Lázár te Budapest; een uittreksel is aan keerzyde afgedrukt.

Voor de Obligatiehouders:                    Budapest, 1 Augutus 1926.
Hollandsche Garantie & Trust Compagnie.    Jassauer Praemonstratenser Koorheeren-Orde.

[Signatures]                                 Probst-Praelat von Jassen.

Hierby een couponblad met 20 halfjaarlyskche coupons en een talon.

---

# ENGLISH TRANSLATION:

ORDER OF THE NORBERTINE FATHERS
(JÁSZÓ PREMONSTRATENSIAN
CANONS REGULAR ORDER)
AT GÖDÖLLŐ NEAR BUDAPEST.

8% FIRST MORTGAGE BOND

GUARANTEED BY THE ROYAL HUNGARIAN GOVERNMENT

TOTAL AMOUNT
F.1,600,000

DIVIDED INTO 1,200 BONDS OF F 1,000 AND 800 BONDS OF F 500.

BOND
PAYABLE TO BEARER

AMOUNT
F 1,000 DUTCH GUILDERS

No. 0.274

The Jászó Premonstratensian Canons Regular Order at Gödöllő has, pursuant to an authorization granted to it by the Royal Hungarian Government (Ministry decision 1872-1928 of 28 June 1926), contracted the following loan at Gödöllő in the amount of F 1,600,000 (one million six hundred thousand Dutch guilders). For this loan, the Jászó Premonstratensian Canons Regular Order at Gödöllő has issued 1,200 bonds of F 1,000 and 800 bonds of F 500 Dutch guilders, of which this bond is a part. The Bond obliges [the issuer] not to payment and repayment under the following conditions.

For the punctual payment of interest coupons and for the repayment of this loan, the Jászó Premonstratensian Canons Regular Order is liable with all its moveable and immoveable property.

As particular security for the payment of interest and the repayment of this loan, a first mortgage has been registered in favor of the HOLLANDSCHE GARANTIE & TRUST COMPANY in Amsterdam on the property belonging to the Jászó Premonstratensian Canons Regular Order at Gödöllő and furthermore on the real estate a) Alsónovai, Felsónovai and Istaros, b) Vadaskesku, and Leopoldst.

Furthermore, the payment of interest and the repayment of this loan is secured by the Royal Hungarian State, which according to § V of the deed of trust of 19 July 1928, remains fully liable for principal and interest. Each bond bears 8½% interest from 1 August 1928 until the day it is repaid. Interest payment occurs semi-annually on 1 February and 1 August and is payable in Amsterdam according to the provisions of the deed of trust of 19 July 1928.

The bonds are repaid according to the plan and under the conditions as printed on the reverse.

The matured coupons expire in 5 years and the scheduled redeemable bonds in 30 years, both calculated from the day they become payable.

All deed of trust is authenticated by Notary Franz Lázár at Budapest; an extract is printed on the reverse.

For the Bondholders:                        Budapest, 1 August 1926.
Hollandsche Garantie & Trust Company.       Jászó Premonstratensian Canons Regular Order.

[Signatures]                                 Abbot-Primate of Jászó.

Hereby a coupon sheet with 20 semi-annual coupons and a talon.

---

# NOTABLE MARKINGS:

- **Serial Number:** No. 0.274
- **Ornamental Border:** Decorative blue floral border framing entire document
- **Seal/Stamp:** Orange/tan colored official seal (Hungarian coat of arms) in upper right
- **Official Stamp:** Purple circular institutional seal at bottom center
- **Handwritten Signatures:** Two signatures present at bottom
- **Date:** Budapest, 1 August 1926 (printed)
- **Issue Date:** 28 June 1926 (referenced in Ministry decision)
- **Coupon Sheet:** Reference to attached coupon sheet with 20 semi-annual coupons and talon strip

title: Oregon and Transcontinental Company Share Certificate
description: This engraved certificate represents one hundred shares of one hundred dollars each in the capital stock of the Oregon and Transcontinental Company, whose authorized capital of fifty million dollars is proclaimed across the top of the sheet. Dated the twelfth day of September 1888 at New York and signed by Edward Ede as Assistant Secretary and by the President, it issued during the company's post-collapse years. The Oregon and Transcontinental Company was the holding vehicle Henry Villard assembled in 1881 to bind the Oregon Railway and Navigation Company, Pacific steamships, and a controlling interest in the Northern Pacific into a single Northwestern transportation system; Villard's overreach broke the structure in 1883, and certificates of this date belong to the chastened reorganization that followed. The central vignette, engraved by the American Bank Note Company, sets two native figures on a bluff above a river valley crossed by a railroad and a bridge, an allegory of the transcontinental line advancing through the Pacific Northwest.
creator: Oregon and Transcontinental Company
notes: Oregon and Transcontinental Company. 100 shares at $100 each. Capital $50,000,000. No. 10583. September 1888. President: Mayor Fiske. Secretary: Edward Ede.

title: Austrian Building Lottery Bond
description: This printed premium debenture (Prämienschuldverschreibung), the "Österreichisches Bau-Los, Em. 1926," carries a face value of zehn Schilling (10 Austrian Schilling). It forms one lot in a lottery loan authorised by the federal law of 27 July 1926 (BGBl. Nr. 199): a total of 6,000,000 Schilling divided into 600,000 such lots, guaranteed by the Bund and entered in the land register for housing purposes (für Wohnungszwecke). Issued at Vienna in August 1926 by the Bundes-Wohn- und Siedlungsfonds (Federal Housing and Settlement Fund), it answered the acute postwar housing shortage by pairing a modest fixed claim with the lure of periodic prize drawings, a savings device long familiar in Central Europe. The vignette of a steep-roofed settlement cottage above a terraced garden makes the purpose literal, the security itself depicting the dwelling its subscriptions would build.
creator: Bundeswohn- und Siedlungsfonds (Austrian Federal Housing and Settlement Fund)
notes: Österreichisches Bau-Los, Emission 1926. 10 Schilling. Serie 2,485, No. 049. Vienna, August 1926. Bundeswohn- und Siedlungsfonds (Federal Housing and Settlement Fund). Lottery bond for housing purposes.
transcription:
# Transcription of Österreichisches Bau-Los (Austrian Building Lottery Bond)

## Original Text (German):

**Header:**
Vom Bunde garantiertes und grundbücherlich einverleibtes Lotterieunternehmen von 1926 für Wohnungszwecke.

Serie 2,485.                                                                                          № 049.

**Main Title:**
Österreichisches Bau-Los
Gm. 1926

ZEHN SCHILLING

**Body Text (beneath the building photograph):**

Diese Prämienanleihe wurde laut Bundesgesetz vom 27. Juli 1926, B.G.Bl. Nr. 190, ausgegeben. Lotterieunternehmen vom Jahre 1926 des Bundeswohn- und Siedlungsfonds im Betrage von S 6.000.000.— eingestellt in 600.000 Lose à S 10.— Nominale, und wird nach Maßgabe des beiliegenden Tilgungs- und Prämienziehungs-Planes innerhalb 30 Jahren nach Verfügung zur Rückzahlung aufgerufen werden.

Die Gewinste des Prämienanlehens sind ohne jeden Abzug von Steuern und Gebühren auszuzahlen und in die Bemessungsgrundlage der Einkommensteuer nicht einzubeziehen.

Jeder Besitzer eines Loses erkennt mit seinem gesamten Vermögen und insbesondere mit den aus dem Ertragknis dieses Anlehens gewonnenen Hypothekardarlebehen, die als Kaufion für die Betriedigung der Ansprüche der Besitzer von Prämienschuldverschreibungen zu bestellen sind, Subsidiärhaft für diese Verbindlichkeiten des Fonds der Bund als Bürge.

Bundeszesetz dieses Prämienanlehens sind für Anlagen gemäß § 23 des Gesetzes vom 15. April 1921, B.G.Bl. Nr. 292, und zur freivilligen Anfügung von Kapitalien zu Depositalfonds, zu Pupillar-, Fideleikommi- und Depositionalfonds und zu Dienst- und Geschäftshaushältnissen verwendbar.

Wien, im August 1926.

**Footer:**
Bundes-Wohn- und Siedlungsfonds.

[Signature - appears to read] S Kettowsky

---

## English Translation:

**Header:**
Lottery enterprise from 1926 for housing purposes, guaranteed by the Federation and inscribed in the land register.

Series 2,485.                                                                                          No. 049.

**Main Title:**
Austrian Building Lottery
Emission 1926

TEN SCHILLING

**Body Text:**

This premium loan was issued pursuant to Federal Law of July 27, 1926, Federal Law Gazette No. 190. Lottery enterprise of 1926 of the Federal Housing and Settlement Fund in the amount of S 6,000,000.— divided into 600,000 lottery bonds of S 10.— nominal value, and shall be called for repayment within 30 years according to the attached redemption and prize drawing schedule.

The winnings of the premium loan are to be paid without any deduction of taxes and fees and are not to be included in the assessment basis for income tax.

Each holder of a lottery bond acknowledges with his entire property and in particular with the mortgage loans obtained from the proceeds of this loan, which are to be provided as security for meeting the claims of holders of premium bonds, subsidiary liability for these obligations of the Fund; the Federation acts as guarantor.

Federal regulations concerning this premium loan are for investments according to § 23 of the Law of April 15, 1921, Federal Law Gazette No. 292, and for the voluntary application of capital to deposit funds, pupillary, fideicommissary, and deposit funds and to service and business household [funds] applicable.

Vienna, in August 1926.

**Footer:**
Federal Housing and Settlement Fund.

---

- **Seal/Stamp:** Circular seal at bottom left reading "BUNDESWOHN- UND SIEDLUNGSFONDS IM BUNDES MINISTERIUM FÜR SOZIALE VERWALTUNG" (Federal Housing and Settlement Fund in the Federal Ministry for Social Administration)

title: Café de la Paix Béziers Founder's Share
description: A founder's part (part de fondateur) issued by the Café de la Paix, a société anonyme seated at 1, Allées Paul Riquet in Béziers, in the Hérault of southern France. The company's statutes, deposited with Maître Vidal, notary at Béziers, on 11 December 1920, fixed its capital at 1,200,000 francs, fully paid, divided into 2,400 shares of 500 francs each; the certificate is dated Béziers, 1 January 1921, and bears the signatures of two administrators. Unlike ordinary shares, a part de fondateur carried no nominal value and was granted to a venture's promoters as a claim on profits above a set dividend, capitalising the goodwill of those who launched the enterprise. The sheet is conceived in a confident Art Nouveau idiom: sinuous display lettering and an allegorical draped woman lifting a cup preside over a precise elevation of the grand café itself, fusing commercial portrait and decorative allegory.

title: Peruvian Republic Congressional War Loan Certificate
description: A handwritten subscription certificate of the nascent Republic of Peru, struck with the official seal, recording one share in the 100,000-peso emergency loan (empréstito) authorized by the Sovereign Congress (Soberano Congreso) at Lima in January 1823. The treasury promises to repay the holder—here Santiago Campos, for his part of a 1,000-peso action—within one month of date, with interest "al cinco por ciento mensual," five per cent monthly. The tally below sets principal at 66 pesos and interest at 3 pesos 2 reales 4, totalling 69 pesos 2 reales 4. The Congress raised the money "para subvenir á las necesidades públicas, y dar al ejército un impulso capaz de terminar la guerra"—to meet public needs and impel the army to end the war—and declared principal and interest irremissible national debt ranking before all other obligations. The crushing monthly rate measures the fiscal desperation of the independence war: Peru had proclaimed independence in 1821, yet royalist arms held out until Ayacucho in 1824.
creator: Peruvian Republic (Soberano Congreso)
notes: Peruvian Republic Congressional War Loan certificate. Lima, 1823. 100,000-peso loan authorized by the Soberano Congreso. 5% monthly interest. Irremissible national debt with priority claim. Independence war finance.

Lima [unclear] Enero de 1823. 4.° 2.°

title: Philadelphia & West Chester Turnpike Road Company Blank Stock Certificate
description: This is a blank, unissued stock certificate of the Philad'a & West Chester Turnpike Road Co. of Philadelphia, with a par value of 25 dollars per share, as stated in the engraved left margin. No shareholder name, number of shares, or date has been entered; the printed line reads only "Philadelphia, the ___ day of ___ 18__," leaving the year open. The form was printed by Evans, Fourth & Library Streets, Philadelphia. Chartered in 1803, the turnpike was among Pennsylvania's earliest toll roads, joining the city to the borough of West Chester and financed, like many such roads, by subscription shares sold to local investors. The central vignette presents a winged figure of Fame sounding a trumpet, reclining against a globe beside an eagle and shield; a separate corner vignette shows a covered Conestoga wagon, and a sheaf of wheat sits at the foot. The motifs bind commerce, agriculture, and national fortune to the road's promise of overland trade.
creator: Philadelphia & West Chester Turnpike Road Company
notes: Philadelphia & West Chester Turnpike Road Company. Unissued/blank stock certificate. $25 per share. Printed by Evans, Printer, Philadelphia. Ca. 1850s–1860s.

title: Société des Plantations d'Agaves de l'Annam Share (Saigon, 1924)
description: This printed bearer share (action au porteur, entièrement libérée) of 500 francs belongs to the Société des Plantations d'Agaves de l'Annam, a French colonial agricultural company seated at Saigon. The text records an original capital of 2,750,000 francs in 5,500 shares of 500 francs each, with statutes deposited before a Saigon notary on 27 March 1924; the certificate itself is dated Saigon, 31 December 1924. Later overstamps tell the company's financial history: a blue overprint raises the capital to 6,875,000 francs, while a red overprint reduces the face value to 100 francs by resolution of an extraordinary general meeting. The firm planted agave in central Annam for sisal fibre destined for rope and cordage, part of the interwar drive to extract commercial crops from Indochina. The red border, with key-fret meanders, confronted beast-heads, and a central I-Ching trigram, adopts a chinoiserie idiom signalling the venture's Far-Eastern setting.
creator: Société des Plantations d'Agaves de l'Annam

title: Polish-American Oil Company "Columbia" Share Certificate
description: The certificate represents fifty shares of 1,000 Polish marks each, a block totalling 50,000 marks, in the Polsko Amerykańskie Towarzystwo Naftowe "Columbia" (Polish-American Petroleum Company "Columbia"), a joint-stock company whose statute the sheet records as approved on 6 February 1922. Issued at Warsaw and signed by the President of the Council (Prezes Rady), the board (Zarząd), the bookkeeper (Księgowy) and the treasurer (Skarbnik), it belongs to the brief flowering of oil ventures in the recently reunified Polish state, whose Galician fields ranked among the oldest worked in Europe. A photographic vignette of wooden derricks at the "Columbia" field crowns the sheet, framed by engraved oil towers and foliate borders, a naturalistic iconography that trades older allegory for the company own working landscape. Its denomination in the depreciating Polish mark fixes the issue within the inflation that would soon consume such paper.

title: Compagnie Privilégiée des Ports de Cadix Mortgage Bond
description: This bearer mortgage bond (obligation hypothécaire au porteur) for 400 francs belongs to the Compagnie Privilégiée des Ports, Débarcadère Maritime et Terrains de Cadix, a French société anonyme constituted before the Paris notary Me Bourget, with its registered office at 15, Rue de la Chaussée d'Antin, Paris. Against a capital of ten million francs, the company raised a loan of twenty million, represented by 71,429 mortgage bonds issued at 280 francs and redeemable at 400 over twenty years — the gap between issue and redemption price serving as a built-in premium that lifted the effective yield above the nominal 24 francs annual interest, paid in quarterly six-franc coupons on 15 January, April, July and October. Dated Paris, 31 August 1869, it shows French Second Empire capital financing Spanish port infrastructure at Cádiz. The engraved vignette, framed in Renaissance-revival strapwork and a chain border evoking maritime mooring, sets an allegorical figure of commerce beside a putto with a globe, voicing the venture's trading ambitions.

title: Portuguese External Debt Interest Certificate
description: This printed Certificate of Interest, lettered "B," attaches to a £100 bond of the Three Per Cent Portuguese External Debt, issued under the Portuguese royal Decree of 18th December, 1852. The certificate, countersigned in London by the Financial Agent of the Portuguese Government (José Jorge Loureiro), records the holder's entitlement to an additional one per cent per annum, granted under Article 2 of the Law of 26th July, 1856, and confirmed by royal decree dated at the Palace of Cintra, 28th July, 1856; it derives from an arrangement entered into in London on 13th December, 1855. The instrument belongs to the protracted settlement of Portugal's foreign obligations after the Crown had defaulted on its London-held debt, the original 1852 conversion being sweetened here with a contingent interest top-up to coax bondholders into accepting the restructured terms. The text appears in English with a parallel Spanish translation, addressing the international market that held the paper.
creator: Portuguese Government (Financial Agent in London)
notes: Portuguese External Debt, Certificate of Interest on £100 Three Per Cent Bond (Letter B). Decree of December 18, 1852. Countersigned London, December 13, 1855. Financial Agent: José Jorge Loureiro.
transcription:
# Transcription: Portuguese External Debt Certificate of Interest

**LETTER B.**

**Portuguese External Debt D°.6944**

## CERTIFICATE OF INTEREST

ON THE SUM OF £100, CAPITAL OF THE THREE PER CENT. BOND NUMBER AS ABOVE.

---

By virtue of the authority granted to the undersigned by the Decree of His Majesty the King of Portugal, of which the following is a Translation:

**"DECREE.**

"Making use of the authority granted to my Government by Article 2nd of the law of 26th instant: I am pleased to grant to the holders of Three per Cent. Bonds of the External Debt issued under the Decree of 18th December, 1852 (the documents of the Deferred Debt not included) the eventual right to an increase of Capital: the right to an increase of such Bonds, to which they are to correspond—and to take place only under the hypothesis mentioned in said article. The Board of Public Credit will cause to be signed by the Financial Agency in London the Certificates alluded to in the present Decree, which are to be signed by the Councillor Guterres Candido of the War Department, and will have it thus method, and cause the same to be carried into effect. *Palace at Cintra on the 28th July, 1856.*" THE KING.

"Countersigned, JOSÉ JORGE LOUREIRO."

---

I, the Financial Agent of the Portuguese Government in London, do hereby certify that the holder of the Bond Letter B, No. 6944..........., of £100, issued under the Decree of 18th December, 1852, will be entitled to receive an additional annual interest at the rate of one per cent. per annum, or any smaller sum, upon the capital of said Bond, in terms of and in the case provided for in Article 2nd of the Law of 26th July, 1866, transcribed below, and of the agreement entered into in London on the 13th December, 1855.

---

We, Dom Pedro, by the grace of God, King of Portugal, and Algarves, make known to all our subjects that the Cortes have decreed, and we have sanctioned the following law:—

**ART. 1st.**

I. Decree [text regarding postponement of the Three per Cent. Bonds of the various Classes of Documents of the External Funded Debt, in addition to the terms of the Decree of the 18th December, 1852] [text regarding nominal Capital in Deferred Bonds with interest at the rate of three per cent. per annum from January 1850, for over £100 of nominal Capital of said.]

2nd. To the holders of Four per Cent. Bonds issued under the law of the 1st of April, 1854, £2 nominal Capital in Deferred Bonds, with interest at the rate of four per cent. per annum from the 1st of January, 1858, for over £100 of nominal Bonds of said.

3rd. To the holders of Debentures, £100 nominal Capital in Bonds of three per cent. per annum from the 1st of January of the present year, for every £100 nominal Capital of said.

**The Government is likewise authorized to grant to the holders of the External Three per Cent. Debt issued under the Decree of 18th December, 1852, in exchange for the**

[Additional articles continue with detailed provisions regarding interest payments, railways, concessionary arrangements, and revocation of prior laws]

**ART. 5th.**

The agreement entered into in London on the 13th December, 1855, between the Finance Minister and Mr. Richard Thornton, is hereby approved, with reference to the terms specified.

---

Signed with signature and seal:

**JOSÉ JORGE LOUREIRO.**
(Sealed with the Large Seal of the Royal Arms.)

---

**WHIFFING, LONDON.**

title: Royal Serbian Government Tobacco-Monopoly Prize Loan Bond, Belgrade, 1888
description: This printed prize-loan bond (Prämien-Obligation / Обвезница са догоцима / Obligation à prime) is a bearer obligation of the Royal Serbian Government for ten gold francs (dinars), one unit of a ten-million-franc gold loan divided into one million such bonds (ten thousand series of one hundred numbers each). Trilingual in German, Serbian and French, it was issued at Belgrade and dated 3/15 June 1888 under the law of 20 April (2 May) 1888, which created a state tobacco monopoly. The text pledges the monopoly's revenues to the loan's interest, amortization and prize draws over sixty-five years, payable at Belgrade and Vienna. Combining a fixed return with periodic lottery premiums, the instrument courted small savers while monetizing a new fiscal resource. Its dense Gründerzeit border of medallions and arabesques frames the obligation in the ornate register of late-nineteenth-century European state finance.
creator: Kingdom of Serbia, Finance Ministry

## Historical Context Notes:

This is a **Prize Loan Bond (Prämien-Obligation)** from the Kingdom of Serbia dated June 15, 1888, denominated in 10 gold francs/dinars. The trilingual format (German/Serbian/French) indicates it was marketed to investors across Central Europe, the Balkans, and France during the late Austro-Hungarian period. Prize bonds combined fixed interest with lottery drawings, making them attractive investment instruments for small savers in pre-World War I Europe.

title: Adelsverein Priority Bond
description: This printed Prioritäts-Obligation (priority bond) of 500 Gulden, reckoned in the 24½-Gulden standard and bearing interest at four per cent (zu vier vom Hundert), was issued by the Verein zum Schutze deutscher Einwanderer in Texas — the Adelsverein, or association of German nobles — at Wiesbaden on 1 July 1850. The bearer holds a 500-Gulden share of a 1,600,000-Gulden total, ranking ahead of the ordinary Stamm-Actien upon the Verein's assets, under statutes approved by the Herzoglich Nassau'sche Landes-Regierung (Biebrich, 23 July; statutes of 16 October 1847). Signed by Das Comite. The instrument is a salvage measure: the Adelsverein's costly project of settling thousands of German emigrants in Texas, founding New Braunfels and Fredericksburg, had left it heavily encumbered, and this senior, interest-bearing claim was meant to attract fresh capital while subordinating the founders' own shares. The sheet is wholly typographic, its authority resting on Fraktur display type rather than emblem or vignette.
creator: Verein zum Schutze deutscher Einwanderer in Texas (Adelsverein)
notes: Verein zum Schutze deutscher Einwanderer in Texas (Adelsverein). Priority Bond, 500 gulden at 4%. Wiesbaden, July 1, 1850. Total issue 1,600,000 gulden. Priority over common shares. Chartered by Nassau government. Founded New Braunfels and Fredericksburg, Texas.

hat nach Höhe des obigen Betrags der **Fünfhundert** Gulden des 24 1/2 Guldenfusses Antheil an den von Actionären des besagten Vereins über ihre Actien-Einlagen geleisteten Einzahlungen im Gesamtbetrage von **1,600,000** Gulden des 24 1/2 Guldenfusses, welchen mit Zinsen zu vier vom Hundert a/s Jahr vom 1. Juli 1850 an gemäss §. 26 der am 16. October 1847 von der Herzoglich Nassau'schen Landes-Regierung genehmigten Vereins-Statuten, d. d. Biebrich, 23. Juli 1847 die Priorität vor den Stamm-Actien an dem Vereinsvermögen nach Maassgabe des beigedruckten Reglementes zusteht.

Die Weitergebung dieser Obligation steht dem Inhaber frei, sie bedarf jedoch zu ihrer Gültigkeit, dass dem Comite des Vereins Nachricht hiervon gegeben würde, was von demselben auf der Rückseite der zu diesom Zwecke vorzulegenden Obligation bestätigt werden wird.

title: French Directory Promesse de mandat territorial, 25 francs, 1796
description: This printed note is a Promesse de mandat territorial — a promise of a territorial mandate — for twenty-five francs, issued by the Trésorerie Nationale of the French Republic and created, as its text declares, by the law of 28 Ventôse Year 4 (18 March 1796). Bearing the signatures of Bugarel and Chevallier and the notation "Vu au Contrôle," it circulated in 1796 under the Directory as a stopgap before the full mandats territoriaux were printed. The mandats were meant to supplant the collapsing assignats, the land-backed paper drawn on confiscated biens nationaux; like them they were nominally redeemable in national property, and like them they depreciated to near nothing within months. The note's restraint is telling: an engraved double frame and the small vignette of a seated figure with the legend "NATIONALE" lend the authority of the state to paper whose value the public already doubted, a faith in the printed promise that the Revolution's monetary experiments repeatedly tested.
creator: Trésorerie Nationale (French Republic)
notes: Trésorerie Nationale. Promesse de Mandat Territorial, 25 francs. No. 19891, Série 30. Law of 28 Ventôse An IV (March 18, 1796). Signed by Mugaret and Chevallier. French Revolutionary paper currency.

title: Reconstitution of French Royal 3% Rentes, Paris, 1763
description: A notarial deed of reconstitution of rentes, executed before the king's counsellors and notaries at the Châtelet de Paris and dated 20 July 1763. Printed on a partly engraved form and completed in manuscript, it re-establishes title to 3 percent perpetual and hereditary rentes (rentes effectives) created by the royal Edict of May 1760, registered in the Parlement on the 23rd of that month, which authorized 1,800,000 livres of such annuities. The income was secured on the Droit sur les Cuirs, the duty on tanned leather established by an earlier Edict of August 1759, and on the king's other revenues. Issued amid the ruinous costs of the Seven Years' War, it shows the crown borrowing against an earmarked excise, while the reconstitution form attests to a paper market in which lost titles could be juridically restored without extinguishing the claim. A small royal arms vignette heads the sheet; the engraving stays plain and administrative, subordinating ornament to legal force.
creator: France. Trésor Royal (Royal Treasury)
notes: Reconstitution de Rentes à 3 Pour Cent. Royal French annuity certificate. Paris, July 20, 1763. Secured on royal leather tax (Denier sur les Cuirs, Edit d'Avril 1759) and other royal revenues. Executed before notaries at the Châtelet de Paris. Seven Years' War era fiscal debt.
transcription:
# RECONSTITUTION DE RENTES À 3 POUR CENT

**Original:**

20; Juillet, 1763.

[Coat of Arms - Royal Seal]

RECONSTITUTION DE RENTES À 3 POUR CENT, créées par Édit du mois de Mai 1760, sur les deniers provenans du Droit sur les Cuirs, établi par autre Édit du mois d'Avril 1759, & sur les autres Revenus du Roi.

N° [number unclear] 4283

PARDEVANT les Confeilers du Roi, Notaires, Garde-notes & Gardes-scel de Sa Majesté au Châtelet de Paris, soulsignés, furent prêsens M. [several handwritten names - partially illegible: "Janneau? d'Orles Vendaux?" and others]

Tous Commisaires nommés par Sa Majesté, par ses Lettres patentes données à Versailles le pour l'exécution de l'Édit du mois de mai 1760, ci-après énoncé.

LESQUELS ont dit que le Roi, par son Édit du mois de mai 1760, enregistré au Parlement le 23 [handwritten: "Jambarde? pou"] du même mois, a créé & émis trois millions livres de rentes effectives & héréditaires à trois pour cent, [handwritten notation], à les avoir à prendre par privilège & préférence sur les deniers provenans du Droit sur Sa Majesté [handwritten] sur les Cuirs établi par l'Édit d'Avril 1759, enregistré au Parlement le 11.2 [handwritten: "payms sim Penfre"] septembre suivant, & qu'Elle s'pécialement & par privilège affectés, obligés & hypothéqués, ensembles [handwritten] tous les autres revenus, tant au payement des arrrérages desdites rentes qu'au remboursement des capitaux [handwritten notations] de la manière ci-après énoncée, après notamment fait la facilité dudit droit formé & delicences au payement des arrérages & au rembourcement des capitaux des liquidations des Offices supprimés par ledit Édit d'aoît, conformément à l'article 111 dudit Édit qui seroit executé de la forme & teneur.

Que lesdites rentes feroient vendues & aliénées par lesdits feigneurs Commisaires, sur les quitances [handwritten] du leur Garde du Trésor royal, & les contrats de constitution desdites rentes conséquence par les Notaires que les Acquéreurs voudroient choisir, lesquelles rentes feroient exemptes de la retenue du [handwritten: "trait dit pours"]; Deux tiers pour livre de Droitun, & de toutes autres impositions générallement quelconques, sous quelque dénomination que pu être.

Que chaque constitution particulière desdites rentes ne pourroit être moindre de mille livres de capital, pour faire levées livres de rente, dont les arrérages commenceroient à courir du jour dudit Acquereurs, du premier jour du quartier courant de la date desdites quitances de finance, & feroient payés de six en six mois sur les premiers jours des mois de Janvier & de Juillet de chaque année, par les simples quitances des Rentiers, à commencée du premier Janvier 1761.

Qu'il faudroit annuellement fait fondés dans l'État de la régie dudit Droit sur les Cuirs & des autres revenus de Sa Majesté, tant que lesdites rentes subsisteroient en tout ou en partie, de la somme de trois millions de livres, qui seroit annuellement remise par les Régisseurs ou Fermiers desdits droits, entre les mains des Gardes du Trésor royal, chacun en l'année de son exercice, pour etre employée au payement des arrérages & au remboursement des capitaux desdites rentes, sans que lesdits payts puissent être distraits ou entranchés au l'entendre reimbourse desdites rentes, sans précédent du prélèvement ci-dffus mentionné.

Que les capitaux desdites rentes feroient remboursés en derniers compans, à raison de la forme de douce cents mille livres pour la première année, de pareille forme pour les années suivantes, & par augmentation de la forme à laquelle se trouveroient monter les arrérages des capitaux qui auroient été remboursés durant l'année, en forte qu'il seroit employé tous les trois millions de fonds jusqu'à parfait reimbourrement desdites rentes, sans pouvoir difiaire aucune partie de ce fonds, sous quelque prétexte que ce fût.

Que ce remboursement seroit fait par le Garde du Trésor royal en exercice, aux premiers jours de Janvier & de Juillet de chaque année, par la voie du fort en forme de Loterie, à commencer du premier Janvier 1762, & ainsi de suite de six en six mois, à l'effet de quoi les contrats de constitution desdites rentes feroient numérotés, & les numéros mis publiquement, dans les derniers jours de Juin & de Décembre de chaque année, dans une roue en la grande salle de l'Hôtel de ville de Paris.

---

No. 4283

All Commissioners appointed by His Majesty, by his Letters Patent given at Versailles for the execution of the Edict of the month of May 1760, hereinafter stated.

WHICH [Commissioners] have declared that the King, by his Edict of the month of May 1760, registered in Parliament on the 23rd of the same month, has created & issued three million livres in effective & hereditary annuities at three per cent, to be drawn by privilege & preference on the monies derived from the Right on Hides established by the Edict of April 1759, registered in Parliament on the 12th of the following September, & that it [the Crown] particularly & by privilege affected, obliged & mortgaged, together with all other revenues, both for the payment of arrears of the said annuities & for the reimbursement of the capital, in the manner hereinafter stated, after having made provision for the facility of said right formed of delicts in the payment of arrears & the reimbursement of capitals of the liquidations of Offices suppressed by said Edict of August, in conformity with article 111 of said Edict which would be executed in the form & tenor.

That the said annuities would be sold & alienated by the said Commissioners, on the receipts of their Treasurer of the Royal Treasury, & the contracts of constitution of said annuities by consequence of the Notaries which the Purchasers would wish to choose, which annuities would be exempt from the retention of [amount]; Two thirds per pound of Rights, & from all other impositions generally whatsoever, under whatever denomination it might be.

That each particular constitution of said annuities could not be less than one thousand livres of capital, to produce five livres of income, of which the arrears would commence to run from the day of said Purchase, from the first day of the quarter current of the date of said quitances of finance, & would be paid every six months on the first days of the months of January & July of each year, by simple receipts of the Rentiers, to commence from the first of January 1761.

title: Reichsbank 1,000-Mark Reichsbanknote, Berlin, 1910
description: This is a one-thousand-mark Reichsbanknote ("Ein Tausend Mark"), a bearer note of the Reichsbank, the central bank of the German Empire. The text promises that the Reichsbank's principal cashier in Berlin will pay the bearer without verification of identity ("ohne Legitimationsprüfung dem Einlieferer dieser Banknote"), dated Berlin, 21 April 1910, and issued under the authority of the Reichsbankdirektorium, whose directors and deputies sign at the foot. As one of the largest denominations in ordinary circulation before 1914, the note belongs to the era of the classical gold standard, when the mark was convertible into gold and such paper functioned as a trusted claim rather than fiat. The design fills the field with dense Fraktur lettering and elaborately interlaced strapwork, flanked by red seals bearing the imperial eagle—an aesthetic of weighty, ornamental officialdom asserting the solidity of the imperial currency.
creator: Reichsbank (German Imperial Bank)
notes: Deutsches Reich Reichsbanknote. 1,000 Marks (Ein Tausend Mark). No. 6125588N. Berlin, April 21, 1910. Reichsbankdirektorium.

title: Reichsbank 1,000-Mark Reichsbanknote, Berlin, 1922
description: This printed banknote is a one-thousand mark (Tausend Mark) Reichsbanknote, issued by the Reichsbankdirektorium and payable by the Reichsbankhauptkasse in Berlin to the bearer. It is dated Berlin, 15 September 1922. A printed notice warns that from 1 January 1923 the note may be called in and exchanged for other legal tender, while a second clause threatens up to two years' imprisonment for counterfeiting or altering it. The issue belongs to the early stage of the German monetary collapse of 1921 to 1923: a thousand marks, once a substantial sum, was already being eroded as the Reichsbank financed government deficits by printing currency. The provisional redemption clause and the absence of figural allegory speak to wartime austerity and haste; the note relies instead on a dense green guilloche field, the bold numeral 1000, and two eagle-bearing seals, where engraved intricacy itself serves as the guarantee of value the design no longer needs to illustrate.
creator: Reichsbank (German Weimar Republic Bank)
notes: Weimar Republic Reichsbanknote. 1,000 Marks (Tausend Mark). Pa 688969, KH. Berlin, September 15, 1922. Hyperinflation era. Note callable from January 1, 1923.

title: Chinese Republic 5% Gold Bond, London, 1925
description: A bilingual French/English bearer bond (au porteur / to bearer) of the République Chinoise, headed "Bon 5% Or 1925 / 5% Gold Bond 1925," for fifty United States gold dollars at five per cent annual interest, dated London, 27 May 1925. Interest of $1.25 was payable half-yearly, the principal redeemable by annual drawings each December. Despite the gold-dollar denomination, the instrument is not a development loan but a settlement security: its text binds it to the Final Protocol of 7 September 1901 and the Chinese Indemnity, with service drawn from revenues of the Chinese Maritime Customs and channelled through the Société Française de Gérance de la Banque Industrielle de Chine, the body managing that bank's affairs after its 1921 failure. The engraved border, customs stamps, and Schröder counting-house imprint frame a sober claim on indemnity revenues rather than any promise of new enterprise.
creator: Government of the Republic of China
notes: République Chinoise 5% Gold Bond 1925. $50 U.S. gold dollars. No. 350,356. London, May 27, 1925.
transcription:
# Transcription of Republic of China 5% Gold Bond of 1925

## Header Text

**PAYMENT OF COUPONS AND OF DRAWN BONDS SHALL ALSO BE MET IN LONDON**
**AT THE COUNTING-HOUSE OF J. HENRY SCHRODER & CO.**

---

## Main Bond Text

**RÉPUBLIQUE CHINOISE**

**BON 5% OR 1925**
**5% GOLD BOND 1925**

**DE CINQUANTE DOLLARS OU FIFTY**
**FIFTY U.S.A GOLD DOLLARS**

**AU PORTEUR / TO BEARER**

No. 350,356

---

## French Text (Left Column)

**Original:**

Faisant partie d'un maximum de 877,878 Bons
Émis en vertu des Protocoles conclus:
1. Protocole final du 7 Septembre 1901
2. Arrangement du 1 Juillet 1902
3. Obligations de l'Indemnité Chinoise à 5% Or 1901,
signés respectivement par le Ministre des Affaires Étrangères et le Ministre des Finances de la République Chinoise, d'une part, et le Baron de Rothschild, Président de la Banque Industrielle de Chine, d'autre part.

4. Accord du 1 Février 1925
5. Lot Française du 8 Février 1925
6. Accord du 15 Avril 1925

Le présent Bon jouit d'un droit de priorité absolue sur les revenus affectés à l'Indemnité Chinoise, et il produit un intérêt de 5% l'an, payable semestriellement, les 15 Décembre de chaque année et le 15 Janvier suivant.

Le porteur du présent Bon aura droit:
1. Au remboursement de la valeur nominale à raison de Dollars U.S.A., lors de l'amortissement, chaque année, à partir du Janvier 1926 jusqu'à celui de Décembre 1953, ou au jour du tirage s'il est antérieur.

2. À la jouissance d'un intérêt semestriel de 5% l'an.

Le remboursement annuel lieu le 15 Janvier selon le premier tirage se fera le Décembre 1925.

Le présent Bon est transférable.

À New York, en Dollars Or des États-Unis d'Amérique, et à Londres, par l'intermédiaire de la Banque Industrielle de Chine (Établissement Franco-Chinois) et à Shanghai, Saïgon, aux guichets de la Société Générale (Établissement Franco-Chinois) et sur toutes autres places qui pourraient être désignées par cette Société ou du Gouvernement Chinois, les intérêts et le capital seront payés en or ou son équivalent.

La présentation au guichet payeur.

Conformément à l'accord à et suite à l'accord intervenu entre le Gouvernement Français et le Gouvernement Chinois, l'inspecteur Général de la Douane Chinoise, Le Ministre des Affaires Étrangères et le Directeur Général de la Banque Industrielle de Chine (Établissement Franco-Chinois) sont investis des pouvoirs pour la présentation du présent Bon.

Conformément à l'article 3 de l'Arrangement de 1902, le Gouvernement Français a accepté par le Ministre des Affaires Étrangères la responsabilité de la présentation de ce Bon et de la mise en œuvre des mesures nécessaires pour l'encaissement.

Au cas où les revenus affectés à l'Indemnité Chinoise seraient insuffisants pour le paiement des coupons, le Gouvernement Français s'engage à effectuer sur ses revenus affectés à l'Indemnité de 1901 un prélèvement supplémentaire de la difference.

**LONDON, MAY 27, 1925**

---

## English Text (Right Column)

**Translation:**

Being one of a total issue of 877,878 Bonds created in accordance with the following Protocols and Agreements:
1. Final Protocol of September 7th, 1901
2. Agreement of July 1st, 1902
3. Chinese Indemnity 5% Gold Bonds of 1901, signed respectively by the Minister for Foreign Affairs and the Minister of Finance of the Chinese Republic, on the one hand, and by Baron de Rothschild, President of the Banque Industrielle de Chine, on the other hand.

4. Agreement of February 1st, 1925
5. French Law of February 8th, 1925
6. Agreement of April 15th, 1925

The bearer of this Bond is entitled to receive interest at 5% per annum payable half-yearly on December 15th and January 15th of each year, the first coupon falling due on July 15th, 1925.

The bearer shall be entitled to:
1. Repayment of the face value in U.S.A. Gold Dollars at the time of redemption, which shall take place on December 15th of each year, commencing December 15th, 1925, and ending December 15th, 1953, or on the date of drawing should that date be earlier.

2. Receipt of interest at 5% per annum payable half-yearly.

The annual redemption shall commence on December 15th, 1925.

This Bond is transferable.

In New York, in U.S.A. Gold Dollars, at any financial institution to be named by the Banque Industrielle de Chine (Franco-Chinese Establishment).

In London, at the office of J. Henry Schroder & Co.

In Peking, Shanghai, Canton, or in Saigon, at the branches of the Société Générale (Franco-Chinese Establishment) or at such other agencies as may be designated by the Société Générale or by the Chinese Government.

Payment of interest and principal shall be made in gold or its equivalent.

In accordance with the Agreement concluded on April 15th, 1925, between the French Government and the Chinese Government, the Inspector General of the Chinese Maritime Customs, the Minister for Foreign Affairs, and the Director General of the Banque Industrielle de Chine (Franco-Chinese Establishment) are hereby vested with full power for presentation and encashment of this Bond.

In accordance with Article 3 of the Agreement of 1902, the French Government has undertaken through its Minister for Foreign Affairs to assume responsibility for presentation and for taking all necessary steps for collection and payment of interest and principal.

In the event that the revenues pledged for the Indemnity should prove insufficient for the payment of the interest and principal on this Bond, the French Government undertakes to make up the deficit from the revenues pledged for the 1901 Indemnity.

**LONDON, MAY 27, 1925**

2. **Red Stamp (top right):** Four shillings (British postal/tax marking)

title: Chinese Republic Lung-Tsing-U-Hai Railway Bond, Brussels, 1921
description: This printed bearer treasury bond (bon du trésor) bears a face value of 500 francs and an 8% annual yield, one of 100,000 such bonds making up a loan of 50,000,000 francs floated by the Government of the Chinese Republic to finance the Lung-Tsing-U-Hai Railway (隴秦豫海鐵路). Issued at Brussels and dated 1 July 1921, it was placed through the Belgian-domiciled Compagnie Générale de Chemins de Fer et de Tramways en Chine and bears the seals of the Chinese legation and a Chinese-character endorsement at lower right. The bond documents China's reliance on European money markets, here Belgian francs, to fund an ambitious east-west trunk line across Gansu, Shaanxi, Henan and Jiangsu. Its restrained green guilloche border and engraved classical lettering project the sober probity of European rentier finance rather than any Chinese ornamental vocabulary, signalling that the paper was made to reassure Continental investors.
creator: Government of the Republic of China
notes: République Chinoise 8% Lung-Tsing-U-Hai Railway (隴秦豫海鐵路) Treasury Bond 1921. 500 francs. No. 012918. Brussels, July 1, 1921. Total issue 50,000,000 francs / 100,000 bonds. Compagnie Générale de Chemins de Fer et Tramways en Chine.

title: Kingdom of Bulgaria State Gold Loan Bond
description: This is a bilingual (Bulgarian and French) bearer bond — Облигация / Obligation — for 500 gold francs of the amortizable 4½% Gold State Loan of the Kingdom of Bulgaria of 1909. The face value is stated as 500 gold francs, equal to 500 Bulgarian gold leva, and rendered in the leading gold-standard currencies of the day: 476 Austrian crowns, 405 Reichsmark, 19.163 pounds sterling, and 240 Dutch florins. The whole loan comprised 200,000 such bonds. Issued at Sofia in 1909, the loan came in the wake of Bulgaria's proclamation of full independence from the Ottoman Empire in 1908, which obliged the young kingdom to settle its inherited tribute and to raise fresh capital abroad. The vignette pairs two women in Bulgarian folk costume bearing roses and grain above a scene of factories and a railway, joining the agrarian wealth of rose-oil and wheat to the promise of industry the loan was meant to finance.
creator: Kingdom of Bulgaria, Finance Ministry
notes: Royaume de Bulgarie 4½% Or State Loan 1909. 500 gold francs = 500 leva zlatni. No. 055,199. 200,000 bonds total. Multiple-currency equivalences stated.
transcription:
# Kingdom of Bulgaria 4½% Amortizable Gold State Loan of 1909 — Bond for 500 Gold Francs — Transcription

## Original:

(top corners, large numerals) **500**  ...  **500**

**Български държавенъ 4½% погасяемъ заемъ въ злато отъ 1909 год.**

издаденъ въ основа на закона отъ 27 ноемврий 1909 година, утвърденъ съ царски указъ № 7 отъ 13 декемврий 1909 година и обнародванъ въ „Държавенъ Вѣстникъ", брой 279 отъ 19 декемврий 1909 година, който законъ потвърждава контракта, сключенъ на 3 декемврий 1909 година н. ст. между Българското Царско Правителство и Винеръ-Банкъ-Ферайнъ въ Виена.

**ПРЕДСТАВЛЯВАНЪ ОТЪ 200.000 ОБЛИГАЦИИ**

за една сума отъ номинални

**100.000.000 лева златни**

= 95.200.000 австрийски корони = 81.000.000 германски марки = 3.960.000 лири стерлинги = 48.000.000 холандски флорини

**ОБЛИГАЦИЯ**

на приносителя отъ

**ПЕТСТОТИНЪ ЛЕВА ЗЛАТНИ**

= 476 австрийски корони = 405 германски марки = 19,16,0 лири стерлинги = 240 холандски флорини.

Настоящия заемъ, който съставлява прѣко задължение за българската държава, е издаденъ:

въ 60.000 отрѣзки № 1— 60.000 по 1 облигация,
„ 10.000 „ № 60.001—110.000 „ 5 облигации,
„ 5.000 „ „ 110.001—160.000 „ 10 „
„ 2.000 „ „ 160.001—200.000 „ 20 „

Българското правителство се задължава до като трае заема да внася ежегодно въ бюджета на държавата потрѣбната сума за изплащане лихвитѣ и излѣзлитѣ въ тиражъ облигации.

Годишната лихва отъ четири и половина на сто (4½%) върху номиналния капиталъ ще се изплаща на двѣ равни части срѣщу шестмѣсечни купони съ падежъ 1 юний и 1 декемврий н. ст. всѣка година.

Падежътъ на първия купонъ е на 1 юний 1910 година н. ст.

(bottom left, large numeral) **500**

(right-hand / French column)

**ROYAUME DE BULGARIE.**

**Emprunt amortissable de l'État Bulgare 4½% Or 1909**

créé en vertu de la loi du 27 Novembre 1909 v. s., sanctionnée par l'Oukase Royal No. 7 du 13 Décembre 1909 v. s. et publiée dans le Journal Officiel No. 279 du 19 Décembre 1909 v. s., ratifiant le contrat intervenu à la date du 3 Décembre 1909 n. s. entre le Gouvernement Royal de Bulgarie et le Wiener Bank-Verein de Vienne

**REPRÉSENTÉ PAR 200.000 OBLIGATIONS**

portant sur un capital nominal de

**100.000.000 de Francs or**

= 95.200.000 Couronnes d'Autriche = 81.000.000 de Reichsmark = 3.960.000 Livres Sterling = 48.000.000 de Florins de Hollande

**OBLIGATION**

au porteur de

**CINQ CENTS FRANCS OR**

= 476 Couronnes d'Autriche = 405 Reichsmark = 19,16,0 Livres Sterling = 240 Florins de Hollande.

Le présent Emprunt constituant une dette directe de l'Etat Bulgare est émis:

en 60.000 coupures No. 1— 60.000 à 1 Obligation,
„ 10.000 „ „ 60.001—110.000 „ 5 Obligations,
„ 5.000 „ „ 110.001—160.000 „ 10 „
„ 2.000 „ „ 160.001—200.000 „ 20 „

Le Gouvernement Bulgare s'engage à inscrire tous les ans, et ce, pendant toute la durée de l'Emprunt, au budget de l'Etat la somme nécessaire pour le paiement des intérêts et le remboursement des obligations.

L'intérêt annuel, de quatre et demi pour cent (4½%) du montant nominal, sera payé en deux parties égales contre les coupons semestriels aux échéances des 1er Juin et 1er Décembre n. s. de chaque année.

L'échéance du premier coupon est fixée au 1er Juin 1910 n. s.

(printer's imprint, bottom center) Société anon. d'impr. et libr. anciens R. v. WALDHEIM, JOS EBERLE & Co., Vienne.

issued on the basis of the law of 27 November 1909, confirmed by Royal Decree (Ukase) No. 7 of 13 December 1909 and promulgated in the "State Gazette" (Darzhaven Vestnik), issue No. 279 of 19 December 1909, which law ratifies the contract concluded on 3 December 1909 (New Style) between the Bulgarian Royal Government and the Wiener Bank-Verein of Vienna.

for a total nominal sum of

in 60,000 coupures Nos. 1–60,000 of 1 bond each,
10,000 coupures Nos. 60,001–110,000 of 5 bonds,
5,000 coupures Nos. 110,001–160,000 of 10 bonds,
2,000 coupures Nos. 160,001–200,000 of 20 bonds.

The Bulgarian Government undertakes, for the whole duration of the loan, to enter each year in the State budget the sum necessary for the payment of the interest and the redemption of the drawn bonds.

The annual interest of four and one-half per cent (4½%) on the nominal capital shall be paid in two equal parts against semi-annual coupons falling due on 1 June and 1 December (New Style) each year.

The first coupon falls due on 1 June 1910 (New Style).

(Printer's imprint) Joint-stock printing and publishing company, formerly R. v. Waldheim, Jos. Eberle & Co., Vienna.

(Marginal) 4½%

Romanization of key names: Царство България = Tsarstvo Bulgaria (Kingdom of Bulgaria); Държавенъ Вѣстникъ = Darzhaven Vestnik (State Gazette); Винеръ-Банкъ-Ферайнъ = Wiener Bank-Verein; лева златни = leva zlatni (gold leva); петстотинъ = petstotin (five hundred).

Note: The per-bond sterling value is printed as "19,16,0" (i.e. £19 16s 0d in the £/s/d convention); the catalogue's "19.163 pounds" is a decimal approximation of this.

title: Russian General Oil Corporation Share Warrant to Bearer, London, 1912
description: A printed share warrant to bearer of The Russian General Oil Corporation (Société Générale Naphthifère Russe) Limited, entitling its holder to one fully paid share of £1 sterling. The text is set trilingually in English, French and Russian, and the capital is stated as £2,500,000 in 2,500,000 shares of £1 each. It was issued under the company's common seal in London and dated 1 October 1912. The corporation was registered in England to hold and work petroleum properties in the Russian Empire, one of many British vehicles channelling sterling capital into the Baku and Caucasus oilfields in the years before the First World War; the three languages address an investor base spanning London, Paris and Russia itself. The seated allegorical woman holding a caduceus, emblem of commerce, set against shipping and industry, frames the venture in the conventional grammar of prosperity. Later stamps record the company's voluntary liquidation (1949), a 1952 winding-up order, and a return of capital to warrant-holders.
creator: Russian General Oil Corporation (Société Générale Naphthifère Russe) Limited
