title: Royal Dutch Petroleum Stock Purchase Warrant, No. 015284
description: This is a stock-purchase warrant issued by the Koninklijke Nederlandsche Maatschappij tot Exploitatie van Petroleumbronnen in Nederlandsch-Indië (Royal Dutch Company for the Working of Petroleum Wells in the Dutch East Indies), a naamlooze vennootschap seated at 's-Gravenhage and founded by deed of 16 June 1890. Dated The Hague, February 1937, it grants the bearer an option on the company's equity: until 31 March 1940 one share of f.1,000 nominal may be bought for f.4,500, and from 1 April 1940 to 31 March 1943 for f.5,000, the instrument becoming void thereafter. The graduated exercise prices, set well above par, register the prized standing of Royal Dutch shares in the late 1930s and the firm's method of raising capital by selling forward claims on stock. Engraved in restrained classical letterforms with a finely guilloché green border, the document bears a 50-cent revenue stamp and the printer's mark of Lankhout, The Hague.
creator: Koninklijke Nederlandsche Maatschappij tot Exploitatie van Petroleumbronnen in Nederlandsch-Indië (Royal Dutch)
notes: Royal Dutch Petroleum Company warrant No. 015284, Series per 1943. Right to buy 1 share: until March 31, 1940 at f.1,000; from April 1, 1940 at f.500. Valid to March 31, 1943. 's-Gravenhage, February 1937. Royal Dutch/Shell predecessor. Green and orange.

ESTABLISHED BY DEED ON 16TH JUNE 1890 BEFORE NOTARY J.C.G. POLLONES IN AMSTERDAM EXECUTED.

INVALID AFTER 31 MARCH 1943.

THE HOLDER OF THIS WARRANT HAS, UPON SURRENDER OF THIS DOCUMENT, THE RIGHT UNTIL AND INCLUDING 31 MARCH 1940 FROM THE AFOREMENTIONED COMPANY TO PURCHASE ONE SHARE THEREIN OF NOMINAL VALUE F. 1,000.— AGAINST PAYMENT OF F. 4,500.— OR FROM 1 APRIL 1940 UNTIL AND INCLUDING 31 MARCH 1943 AGAINST PAYMENT OF F. 5,000.—

THE HAGUE, FEBRUARY 1937.

title: Compagnie des Indes Life Annuity (Rente Viagère), Paris
description: This is a notarial act drawn at the Châtelet de Paris, cast in the prévôtal form headed by Gabriel-Jérôme de Bullion, Prévôt of Paris, before whose notaries the parties appeared. By it the Syndic and Directeurs of the Compagnie des Indes (the French East India Company), acting in the Company's name, constitute a rente viagère — a life annuity — of 275 livres, payable yearly in coin from the Company's funds, on the joint lives of Charles-Claude Boizier, Écuyer, Conseiller-Secrétaire du Roi, and his wife, Dame Élizabeth Louise. Dated 13 January 1725 at Paris, the act invokes the royal Council edicts of February and 20 June 1724 that had reorganized the Company after the collapse of John Law's System. One sees here how the rechartered Company met its obligations not through tradable shares but through annuities secured on its own revenues — a quieter instrument of credit in the wake of speculative ruin. The engraved notarial cartouche, with its caduceus and bound emblems, asserts the public faith underwriting the deed.
creator: Compagnie des Indes (French East India Company)
notes: Compagnie des Indes legal document, January 13, 1745, Paris. Director: Gabriel-Jérôme de Bullion. Concerns share transfers and Loterie de Saint-Quentin proceeds. References King's Council edict February 3, 1724. Parties include Dame Juliane Elizabeth de Guise Holler.

# Transcription of Document

**Original (French):**

13 Janvier 1745.

[Top left seal: "GEN. DE PARIS"]
[Top right seal: ornamental design with text]

À Tous ceux qui ces presentes Lettres verront, Gabriel-Jérôme de Bullion, Chevalier Comte Defchilmont, Maistre de Camp du Regiment de Provence Infanterie, Conseiller du Roy en son Conseil, Prevôt de la Ville, Prevôté & Vicomté de Paris, Sçavoir faisons que pardevant M [illegible] Conseillers du Roy, Notaires, Gardes-notres & Gardes-[unclear] de Sa Majestè au Chastelet de Paris, soullignez, furent presens. [handwritten: unclear names]

Syndie de la Compagnie des Indes, Et [handwritten] à Moucharsard [unclear]

Directeurs de ladite Compagnie; lesquels en vertu de l'Arrêt du Conseil d'État du Roy du 15. Février 1724. par lequel Sa Majestè a accordè à ladite Compagnie le Privilege Exclusif de faire dans l'estendue du Royaume, differentes Loteries; Et en execution de la Declaration du 13. Decembre audit an, prise dans l'Assemblée generale d'Administration de ladite Compagnie, au sujet de la Loterie composée de Lots payables, les uns en Argent, & les autres en Rentes Viageres, qui doivent former un corps de fontine de Vingt-mille sept cens cinquante Numero, chacun d'eux, susceptible d'accroissement, à mesures des decès des Rentiers irreallez à ladite Loterie; favoir, pour leur à profit de ladite Compagnie. Et pour l'autre moitié à ceux des Rentiers survivans, que le sort preferera dans les Loteries qui doivent estre annuellement & successivement. Lesquelles Rentes Viageres ont le ront assignées sur le fonds de ladite Compagnie, lesdits Sieurs Syndic & Directeurs ont pouvoir de continuer en profit des Suites de Sa Majestè, que des Arrangers, auxquels le payement des Arrangess & des Accroissemens d'oit estre fait, quand même ils refideraient hors du Royaume, & même en tems de guerre, fans que lesdites Rentes puissent estre retranchées ou efisgées pour quelque cause que ce soit, pas même pour l'affaires de Sa Majestè, conformement à l'Arrêt du 26. Juin 1724. ains qu'il est plus au long porté par ladite Publication; par laquelle il est permis aux Acqueireurs desdites Rentes de passer les Contrats fous le nom de telles personnes qu'il voudront choisir, pour ou joüir, sans que par eux, que per ceux qu'ils nommeront fur les Quittances, pendant la vie desdites personnes fondées fur lyl desdites Constitutions auront cinq miles. Ont lesdits Sieurs Syndic & Directeurs comparu, en vertu desdites Declarations & Arrests du Conseil d'État & Fevrier & 26 Juin 1724. vendu, creé & constitué, afis & assigné par ces presentes; Et permis pour & au nom de ladite Compagnie des Indes, garantir de tous troubles & autres empechemens generalement quelconques, à [handwritten: unclear]

Royier [handwritten: unclear]
Seigneur &c.

Sieurs Quitances, qui ne pourront estre paisées que

title: Liverpool Corn Trade Association Perpetual Mortgage Debenture, Liverpool
description: This is a perpetual mortgage debenture, numbered 291, of the Liverpool Corn Trade Association, Limited, issued to George Grossman for £100 sterling and signed by Edward Briggs, Secretary. The printed conditions establish it as one of a series of "C." perpetual debentures securing principal not exceeding £100,000 in all, charged on the Association's property and bearing interest not above £5 per centum per annum. Issued at Liverpool, the great corn-importing port of late-Victorian Britain, the instrument let a self-governing trade association raise long-dated capital from its own members rather than a bank, the perpetual form fixing a permanent charge in place of a maturing loan. Handwritten endorsements trace its passage: on George Grossman's death his sons take it under letters of administration, Ernest Clifford Grossman of Great Crosby receives the transfer in June 1919, and Hannah Jones, spinster, of West Derby acquires it in 1933. The austere engraved frame and blackletter title eschew allegory, presenting the bond as a sober commercial covenant. (Reverse of the certificate, with conditions printed below.)
creator: Liverpool Corn Trade Association, Limited
notes: Reverse of C. Perpetual Mortgage Debenture No. 291, £100, Liverpool Corn Trade Association, Ltd. Shows printed conditions and transfer record: transferred May 25, 1910 to Ernest Clifford Grasman; registered June 1919; further transfer October 9, 1933.

title: Liverpool Corn Trade Association Debenture
description: This is a £100 "C" Perpetual Mortgage Debenture issued by the Liverpool Corn Trade Association, Limited, a company incorporated under the Companies Acts 1862 to 1893 with a capital of £60,000 in 400 shares of £150 each. It bears interest at £3½ per centum per annum, payable half-yearly on 1 February and 1 August, and forms part of an authorised issue of £100,000 charged on the whole of the Association's property and effects, present and future. Issued under Clause 54 of the Articles and a board resolution of 26 May 1897, it was given under the common seal at the registered office, 8 Brunswick Street, Liverpool, on 18 June 1897, and signed by the chairman, a director, and the secretary, Edward Graham. The perpetual, transferable instrument let a merchants' trade body raise long-term mortgage capital on its standing in Liverpool's grain market rather than on shareholders' calls. The restrained engraved border and roman lettering aim at probity, not display.
creator: Liverpool Corn Trade Association, Limited
notes: C. Perpetual Mortgage Debenture No. 291, £100 at 3½% p.a. Liverpool Corn Trade Association, Ltd. Issue of £100,000. Capital £60,000 in 400 shares of £150. 8 Brunswick Street, Liverpool. Common Seal, June 1897. Secretary: Edward Graham.
transcription:
# Transcription

THIS DEBENTURE IS ISSUED UNDER THE AUTHORITY OF CLAUSE 54 OF THE ARTICLES OF ASSOCIATION OF THE LIVERPOOL CORN TRADE ASSOCIATION, LIMITED, OF A RESOLUTION OF THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS DATED THE 26TH OF MAY, 1897.

THE

**Liverpool Corn Trade Association,**
LIMITED.

INCORPORATED UNDER THE COMPANIES' ACTS, 1862 TO 1893.

CAPITAL: £60,000, in 400 Shares of £150 Each.

Registered Office:
8, BRUNSWICK STREET, LIVERPOOL.

ISSUE of £100,000 C. PERPETUAL MORTGAGE DEBENTURES, carrying Interest at £3½ per Centum per Annum.

No. 291     C. PERPETUAL MORTGAGE DEBENTURE. £100.

The Liverpool Corn Trade Association, Limited, [acknowledges receipt of] One Hundred Pounds, being part of their Perpetual Mortgage Debenture Stock, and hereby agrees to pay to the bearer or registered holder for the time being the sum of One Hundred Pounds, or other the registered holder for the time being to be made on the 1st day of August.

The Association will, during the continuance of this security, pay to each registered holder interest thereon at the rate of £3½ per centum per annum. By equal half-yearly payments, on the 1st day of February and the 1st day of August, the 1st of such half-yearly payments to be made on the 1st day of August.

The Association doth hereby charge with the said principal money and interest all the present and future property of the Association, both real and personal, including uncalled capital, by way of security to the intent that the "C" Debenture and the other debentures forming part of the above-mentioned authorized issue of £100,000 shall rank, pari passu, as a charge upon the said property and future property of the Association, called only in the event of the Association being unable to pay the 1st and £10,000 in Debentures which are charged upon the said property in priority to the Debentures respectively named.

The Debenture is issued subject to and with the benefit of the conditions endorsed hereon, which are as to hereinafter set out.

**Given** under the Common Seal of the Association,

this 16th day of June, 1897

[Signatures present but largely illegible - appear to be three signatories]

- Official stamp/seal (orange/red colored) in top right corner

title: Liverpool Corn Trade Association Perpetual Mortgage Debenture, Liverpool, 1897
description: This is a C. Perpetual Mortgage Debenture, No. 291, for £100, issued by The Liverpool Corn Trade Association, Limited, and carrying interest at 3½ per cent per annum. Incorporated under the Companies' Acts 1862 to 1893, with capital of £60,000 in 400 shares of £150 each and a registered office at 8, Brunswick Street, Liverpool, the Association issued the debenture under the authority of Clause 54 of its Articles and a board resolution of 26 May 1897, as part of a £100,000 issue; it is given under the Common Seal in June 1897 and signed by the chairman, a director, and the secretary, Edward Graham. As a perpetual mortgage debenture, the instrument pledges the Association's whole property and effects, present and future, as security for borrowings that funded the trade body's premises and operations in Liverpool's great grain market. The engraved Gothic blackletter masthead and dense classical border evoke the mercantile gravitas of a Victorian commercial corporation, lending the printed obligation the look of institutional permanence its 'perpetual' terms promise.

title: Dutch Notarial Certificate for Bank of England Consolidated Annuities
description: A handwritten Dutch notarial certificate ("London Certificatie"), drawn by Victor Hendrik Hoogenbergh, a Dutch public notary admitted to practise in London. At the holder's request the notary certifies that the named party stands entered, in the party's own name, in the Grand Ledger of Transfers and Accounts at the Bank of England, credited with a sum of Twee Duizend Twee Hondert vijftig Ponden — £2,250 of stock in the Consolidated Capital of Three Per Cent Bank Annuities (Drie per Cent Bank Annuiteiten). It is dated London, the 16th of March, Anno Seventien Hondert Acht en negentig (1798), closed "In Testimonium Veritatis" beneath the notary's signature and a red wax seal. Such attestations served Dutch investors who held British consols, furnishing authenticated proof of title for probate, taxation, or litigation in the Republic. The sober chancery hand and the absence of decorative engraving befit a private legal instrument: its authority rests on the notary's seal and the Bank's ledger, not on ornament. The document records the deep Amsterdam appetite for British public debt, the consol being the era's benchmark perpetual annuity.

title: Continental Loan Office Bill of Exchange, Massachusetts Bay, 1778
description: This is a first bill of exchange drawn by the United States of America on 21 November 1778, ordering payment at thirty days' sight of Sixty Dollars, reckoned at five livres tournois to the dollar and thus Three Hundred Livres Tournois, to Jeremiah Green Esqr. or order, "for Interest due on Money borrowed by the United States." It is countersigned by Nath. Appleton, Commissioner of the Continental Loan-Office in the State of Massachusetts Bay, and signed by Francis Hopkinson, Treasurer of Loans, the bill being addressed to the Commissioners of the United States at Paris. Such drafts were the Continental Congress's device for servicing the interest on domestic Loan Office Certificates: rather than scarce specie, creditors received paper redeemable in France, a quiet admission that the young Confederation's wartime solvency rested on French credit. The plain typographic frame, free of allegory, suits an instrument meant to be negotiated, not contemplated.

title: Continental Loan Office Bill of Exchange, Massachusetts, 1778
description: This is a partly printed, partly manuscript bill of exchange issued by the United States of America and dated the 24th day of November 1778. Drawn on the Commissioners of the United States at Paris, it directs payment at thirty days' sight of the first bill (the second, third, and fourth remaining unpaid) to Timothy Newell, Esq., or order, of twenty-four Dollars in one hundred and twenty Livres Tournois—reckoned at five livres the dollar—for interest due on money borrowed by the United States. It is countersigned by Nathaniel Appleton, Commissioner of the Continental Loan-Office in the State of Massachusetts Bay, and signed by Francis Hopkinson, Treasurer of Loans. The instrument shows how the infant Congress, lacking specie, met the interest on its domestic loan-office certificates by drawing on French credit, settling a colonial obligation in the coin of its new ally. The plain typographic frame and engraved foliate border at left answer the document's purely fiscal purpose.

title: State of Massachusetts Bay Commodity-Indexed Treasury Note
description: A printed-and-manuscript interest-bearing treasury note of the State of Massachusetts-Bay, dated the First Day of January, A.D. 1780. In behalf of the State, the subscriber in the Office of Treasurer promises to pay Elisha Curtis, or his order, the sum of Two Hundred and Fifty-five Pounds with interest at six per cent per annum, on or before the First Day of March 1783. Distinctively, both principal and interest were payable not in a fixed sum but "in a greater or less Sum, according as" a fixed market basket — five bushels of corn, sixty-eight and four-sevenths pounds of beef, ten pounds of sheep's wool, and sixteen pounds of sole leather — should then cost in the current money of the State. The note records that this basket then cost about One Hundred and Thirty Pounds, thirty-two and a half times what the same goods commanded under the price schedule of the 1777 "Act to prevent Monopoly and Oppression" — a stark measure of wartime depreciation. Issued under a February 1780 act to supply the Treasury and meet the State's quota of the Continental Army, it stands among the earliest commodity-indexed public obligations, devised to shield holders from the collapse of Revolutionary paper. The engraved frame and copperplate flourishes follow the ornamental convention of the period's official paper. The Treasurer's signature is struck through, marking redemption.
creator: State of Massachusetts Bay (Treasurer; Committee)
notes: Mexican 5% Deferred Stock. Large format bearer bond with Mexican eagle vignette, ornate border, and full attached coupon sheet. Part of Mexican external debt restructuring, London market, mid-19th century. Extensive bond indenture text included.
transcription:
# Transcription

**No.** [number]

**STATE TREASURERS BOND**

*The First Day of JANUARY, A.D. 1780.*

IN behalf of the State of Massachusetts-Bay, I the Subscriber do hereby promise and oblige Myself and Successors in the Office of TREASURER of said STATE, to pay unto **Josias Curtis** —or to his Order, the SUM of **Two hundred fifty five pounds**

on or before the **FIRST DAY OF MARCH**, in the Year of our Lord One Thousand Seven Hundred and **eighty three** with Interest at **Six per Cent. psr Annum**; Both Principal and Interest to be paid in the then current Money of said STATE, in a greater or less SUM, According as Five Bushels of **CORN**, Sixty-eight Pounds and four-sevenths Parts of a Pound of **BEEF**, Ten Pounds of **SHEEPS WOOL**, and Sixteen Pounds of **SOLE LEATHER**, shall then cost, more or less than One Hundred and Thirty Pounds current Money, at the then current Prices of said Articles.—This Sum being Thirty-Times and an Half what the fame Quantities of the fame Articles would coft at the Prices affixed to them in a L[a]w of this STATE made in the Year of our Lord One Thousand Seven Hundred and Seventy-seven, intitled, "*An Act to prevent Monopoly and Oppression.*" The current Prices of said Articles, and the confequent Value of every Pound of the Sum herein promised, to be determined agreeable to a L[a]W of the STATE intitled, " **An ACT to provide for the Security and Payment of the Balances that may appear to be due by Virtue of a Resolution of the GENERAL ASSEMBLY of the Sixth of** *February* **One Thousand Seven Hundred and Seventy-nine, to this STATE's Quota of the CONTINENTAL ARMY**, agreeable to the Recommendation of **CONGRESS**, and for Supplying the TREASURERY with a Sum of Money for that Purpose."

Witness my Hand.

[Signature] *Treasurer*

**Middling Committee's**

title: Mexican Five Per Cent Deferred Stock Bond, London, 1837
description: This engraved bearer certificate is a Mexican Five Per Cent Deferred Stock bond for £500 sterling, bearing interest only from 1 October 1847 and convertible, at the holder's option, into a land warrant for 2,000 acres. It was issued in London on 30 September 1837 under the hand of Don Agustín de Iturbide, Chargé d'Affaires for the Republic of Mexico, and countersigned by the agents for the conversion of the Mexican foreign debt. The instrument records the rescheduling that followed Mexico's mid-1830s default: a presidential decree of 12 April 1837 created a National Consolidated Fund at five per cent to absorb the whole external debt, terms a London bondholders' meeting accepted on 5 September 1837. Surrendered deferred bonds, receipts and dividend warrants were exchanged here, half into this deferred stock and half into the consolidated fund. The Mexican eagle perched on a nopal anchors a dense engraved border, and a full coupon sheet remains attached. The deferred interest and alternative land grant both reveal a debtor short of specie offering frontier acreage in lieu of cash.
creator: Republic of Mexico
notes: Share subscription/forward contract, Generale Keijserlijke Indische Compagnie. Antwerp, April 30, 1608. Registered No. 36. Subscriber promises to deliver shares against received cash premium. Habsburg Imperial East India Company, Spanish Netherlands. Among the earliest known equity derivatives documents. Contemporary with Dutch VOC (founded 1602).

# Document Transcription

## Original (English):

**LEFT PAGE:**

MEXICAN
FIVE PER CENT. DEFERRED STOCK.

Letter D.

Bond for
£500 Sterling,

Bond Warrant
For 2000 Acres,

BEARING INTEREST FROM
OCTOBER, 1847.

Whereas The President of the Republic of Mexico did, under date of the 15th of April, 1837, with the concurrence of the Council of Government, issue a Decree for the creation of a National Consolidated Fund, with interest at the rate of Five Per Centum per annum, to be paid and distributed immediately through London Banking houses, to be paid half-yearly.

Whereas, at a General Meeting of the Mexican Bondholders held in London, pursued to public advertisement, on the 9th day of November, 1837, it was resolved, by the Bondholders then present, and the great bulk of the absent Bondholders, that a Committee should be appointed to arrange, adjust and settle the affairs of the Mexican Government at the Court of London; having in the same aid on behalf of and Government, by virtue of their said deed and authority vested in him, entered in full cancellation of said Deferred Stock, into the following Agreement for the purpose of conversion, Bond Deferred a copy in Spanish, and a translation in English, together with a copy of said Agreement, are hereto annexed:

And Whereas all other, they, it is the above named America De Verragas [illegible], all British and Mexicans, at the Court of London, acting under the authority aforesaid, do hereby annul this Obligation, superseding and having had been and other issue of a Bond for Five Hundred Pounds sterling, or a Warrant for Two Thousand Acres in the Republic of Mexico, issued under authority of the said Decree, and whereas the Government in London, are duly authorized to redeem convert for conversion of the proper amount, according to the terms of said Decree and Agreement as herein set forth of the Mexican Government, providing for the application of all monies to be received from the respective Governments of Mexico and England towards the payment of the principal or interest of the whole overdue thereof, as they shall deem fit, and whereas the Government of Mexico, at the Court of London, do hereby annul this Obligation, superseding and having had been and other issue of a Bond for Five Hundred Pounds sterling, or a Warrant for Two Thousand Acres in the Republic of Mexico, issued under authority of the said Decree:

And it is also further Bristler, That the holder of this Bond or Land Warrant shall be entitled in any time, until the first day of October, 1847, to receive in exchange for the same Two Thousand Acres of Land, comprised in the Republic of Mexico; and that, in case of the non-acceptance of such offer, the whole of the above Acres per Bond issued will be appointed in the amount of interest thereon, calculating from the first day of October, 1847, until the date of giving possession of said land, subject accordingly to the conditions and regulations herein contained.

And it is also further Bristler, That the holder of this Bond or Land Warrant shall be entitled in any time, until the first day of October, 1847, to receive in exchange for the same Two Thousand Acres of Land, comprised in the Republic of Mexico; and that, in case of the non-acceptance of such offer, the whole of the above Acres per Bond issued will be appointed in the amount of interest thereon, calculating from the first day of October, 1847, until the date of giving possession of said land, subject accordingly to the conditions and regulations herein contained.

And it, said America De Verragas, acting as aforesaid, do hereby guarantee and confirm to the holder of this Bond or Land Warrant, all and singular the Hypotecations, Securities, and Stipulations, originally granted to the Holders of the Deferred Stock issued under the authority of the said Decree, with all and every other and further advantages, powers, and privileges granted by the same, to the full benefit and effectual discharge of the obligations created by the Bond or Land Warrant, to grant and to pay at all times, and under all circumstances, as follows:

In Witness whereof, I, the said America De Verragas, have hereunto set my hand in London, this 20th day of September, 1837.

[Signature]

Safe Thereof Certifie, that the foregoing is the Signature of Don America De Verragas, the person named in the above Bond as the special Agent of Mexico appointed, by the Government of Mexico, for the purpose of conversion, Bonds of the Mexican Government proceeding from the Loans effected in London, or for internal Revenue, represented by Deferred Stock and Warrants, or Deferred Warrants otherwise, having the same, at the time of his Signature to this Instrument, full power and authority so to do as is by the Government of Mexico.

Consolidated Fund and Court, Paid of this date,

London, this 20th day of September, 1837.

[Signature]

[AGENTS FOR THE UNDERWRITERS OF THE MEXICAN GOVERNMENT]

The certificates appear to be a sheet from which individual bonds could be separated.]

title: Share Subscription Contract for the Ostend Company
description: A printed-and-manuscript forward contract in shares of the Generale Keijserlijke Indische Compagnie — the Ostend Company, chartered by Emperor Charles VI in 1722 to trade to the Indies from the Austrian Netherlands. Executed at Antwerp and dated, in the printed close, "den thienden novembris seventhien hondert ende negen en twintigh" (10 November 1729), the contract binds the undersigned subscriber, having received a cash premium (Contante premie), to deliver and transfer six shares (Actien) at 70 per cent on the first available transfer-day, the agreement standing as an option "tot prima augusti 1730." Issued on a stock form sold by the Antwerp printer Alexander Everaerts, it pledges the seller's person and goods under Antwerp law. An embossed armorial seal bearing the motto DUM SPIRO SPERO crowns the sheet. The instrument is a vivid record of speculative share trading in the Ostend Company, whose stock was avidly dealt at Antwerp and Amsterdam before imperial pressure suspended the venture.
creator: State of New York, Comptroller's Office
notes: New York State Per Cent Stock, $300. Walter Terry, State of Connecticut. Albany, October 10, 1815. Interest payable January 1 and July 1. Stamped "Funded" in red. Signed by John Ely, Dep. Comptroller.

title: New York State Per Cent Stock Certificate, Albany, 1815
description: This is a New York State "Per Cent Stock" certificate, a funded public-debt obligation issued by the Comptroller's Office at Albany and dated 10 October 1815. It acknowledges that the People of the State of New York owe Walter Perry of the State of Connecticut, or his assigns, the sum of three thousand dollars, bearing interest at seven per centum per annum and payable quarter-yearly on the first Mondays of January, April, July and October. The stock was created under an act of the Legislature, passed 7 April 1815, to raise a public fund and to levy additional taxes for the use of the State; it is recorded in the Comptroller's accounts and transferable in person or by attorney. Issued as the State refinanced its obligations after the War of 1812, the certificate documents how American states funded wartime expenditure through transferable interest-bearing stock. A red overstamp, "Per Cent redeemed," marks its later retirement. Signed by John Ely Jr., Deputy Comptroller, and Archibald McIntyre, Comptroller. The engraved heading and penmanship are plain and clerkly, the document's authority resting on signatures and the State's seal of office rather than on ornament.
creator: C. Freymerius en Zijn; Everard Cornelis Bordt (Notary)

title: Notarial Deed of the Demerara Plantation Negotiatie ("The Garden of Eden" and "The Kitty"), Amsterdam, 1817
description: Printed and manuscript notarial deed passed on 4 March 1817 before Everard Cornelis Bordt, public notary at Amsterdam, governing a negotiatie (planters' mortgage-loan fund) of three hundred thousand guilders divided into shares of one thousand guilders each, of which two hundred ninety-four remain outstanding. The loan is secured upon the plantations "The Garden of Eden" and "The Kitty" on the east coast of the river Demerary in the Colony of Demerara, held by Thomas Cuming, with Cornelis Frymersum and his son acting through the firm C. Frymersum en Zoon as directors, and brokers serving as commissioners. Such negotiatiën channelled Amsterdam capital into West Indian estates worked by enslaved labour, the soil and its bondsmen pledged as collateral. The plain, type-set frame is wholly administrative, an instrument of credit rather than display.
creator: Everard Cornelis Bordt (Notaris); C. Frymersum en Zoon (Directie)
notes: Optiebewijs No. 86786. Unilever N.V., Rotterdam. Eight certificates entitle holder to acquire 1 ordinary share (f.100 nominal) at f.130½; valid until June 30, 1942. Related to Van den Bergh's Fabrieken N.V. 3½% Obligatielening. Rotterdam, July 1937. Blue-printed. Same series as No. 97790 (goetzmann0469).
transcription:
# Document Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Op den vierden Maart des jaars achtien honderd zeventien; compareerden voor Mr. EVERARD CORNELIS BONDT, Openbaar Notaris te Amsterdam resideerednde, en voor de ondergenoemde getuigen.

De Heer CORNELIS FRYMERSUM, Koopman, wonende binnen deze Stad op de Keizersgracht tusschen de Leliegracht en Heerengracht, negotieernde onder de firma van C. FRYMERSUM en ZOON, en alzoo voor en in den naam van dezelvfe zijne firma van C. FRYMERSUM en ZOON, als Directeuren van de negotiatie groot dicemaal honderd duizend guldens, ten laste van den Heer THOMAS CUMING, onder verband van deszelfs Plantaadjen THE GARDEN OF EDEN EN THE KITTY; in de Kolonie Demerory geleegen; in den jaare seventien honderd twee-en-negentig, onder directie van den Heer SEBASTIAAN VAN NOOTEN JANES, allier opgerijgt.

En de Heeren DIRK WERNARD VAN VLOTEN ABRAHAMES; Makelaar, allier woonachtig op de Heerengracht bij de Vijzèlstraat, en DIRK JAN VOOMBERGH, Koopman, allier woonachtig in de Nieuwe Doelenstrat; beide in qualiteit als Commissarissen der voorschrevene Negotiatie.

Te kunnen gevende: Dat overeengkomdig de gemmaakte fehlkkingen niet den Heer THOMAS CUMING voornemoed, en de qualificatie der houders van de groote meerderheid der twee honderd vier-en-negentig overgeblevene Aandaclen, ieder van daizend guldens, in de voorschrevene Negotiatie, de hier voregenomelde Plantaadje genaamd THE GARDEN OF EDEN, geleegen op de Oost-kust van de rivier Demerory, met alle derzelver gebouwen, haven en verdere toebehooeren, op den achtlen Augustus des jaars achtien honderd vijftien, ten overlslans van Commissarissen van het Hof van crimineeele en civiele Justitie van de Kolonie Demerory en Essequebo, is goedeord, getransporteerd en in vollen vrijen eigendom overgegeven aan en te behoeve van de Heeren CORNELIS FRYMERSUM en CORNELIS FRYMERSUM Jr Jozor, negotieernde onder de firma van CORNELIS FRYMERSUM en ZOON, van Amsterdam, in derzelver qualiteit als Directeuren van zekere leening, gevestigd op de Plantaadjen KITTY EN GARDEN OF EDEN; zoo als blijkt uit het bij hun Heeren Comparanten ontvangene authentieke affidavit van het voormelde transport, waarop gefchreven staat:

"Geregistreerd te Amsterdam den zee en twintigten Febr. 1817, D. S. f. 179 verfo, vak 3 en 4. ontvangen met de Verhoog. negeri "en vijftig en 1 Cents (getteekend BONDAM."

En dat de Heeren Comparanten als nu, ter verdere voldoening aan den wil en het verlangen der voormelde houders van Aandaclen, met overleg van zooladige drie Heeren geinteressecrden in de voorschrevene negotiatie, welke voor het groothste getal Aandaclen tot de gezegde qualificatie hebben geoconcurreerd, hebben bepaald en vastgesteld, zoo als zij verklaren te bepalen en vaststelllen bij deren:

Dat de hier voregenomelde, als nu aan de houders der voorschrevene twee honderd vier-en-negentig Aandaclen in gemeenschaftspelijken eigendom toebehoorde Plantaadije THE GARDEN OF EDEN, met alle derzelver gebouwen, haven en verdere toebehooeren; en al wat werder tot het fonds der Associatio behoerende zijn mag, zal worden gereegerd en geadministreerd door de Heeren C. FRYMERSUM en ZOON, Kooplieden te Amsterdam, als Directeuren van het fonds der Plantaadije THE GARDEN OF EDEN, golegen

---

**English Translation:**

On the fourth day of March in the year eighteen hundred seventeen; appeared before Mr. EVERARD CORNELIS BONDT, Public Notary residing in Amsterdam, and before the hereinafter named witnesses.

Mr. CORNELIS FRYMERSUM, Merchant, residing within this City on the Keizersgracht between the Leliegracht and Heerengracht, negotiating under the firm name of C. FRYMERSUM and ZOON, and thus on behalf of and in the name of his said firm C. FRYMERSUM and ZOON, as Directors of the negotiation of a principal amount of one hundred thousand guilders, charged to Mr. THOMAS CUMING, under pledge of his Plantations THE GARDEN OF EDEN AND THE KITTY; in the Colony Demerory situated; in the year seventeen hundred ninety-two, established under the direction of Mr. SEBASTIAAN VAN NOOTEN JANES, broker appointed.

And the Gentlemen DIRK WERNARD VAN VLOTEN ABRAHAMES; Broker, residing on the Heerengracht near the Vijzèlstraat, and DIRK JAN VOOMBERGH, Merchant, residing in the Nieuwe Doelenstrat; both in the capacity of Commissioners of the aforementioned Negotiation.

To make known: That in accordance with the agreements made with Mr. THOMAS CUMING mentioned beforehand, and the qualification of the holders of the greater majority of the two hundred ninety-four remaining Shares, each of one thousand guilders, in the aforementioned Negotiation, the herein named Plantation called THE GARDEN OF EDEN, situated on the East coast of the river Demerory, with all its buildings, harbor and other belongings, on the eighth day of August in the year eighteen hundred fifteen, by transfer of Commissioners of the Court of criminal and civil Justice of the Colony Demerory and Essequebo, has been approved, transported and in full free ownership transferred to and for the benefit of the Gentlemen CORNELIS FRYMERSUM and CORNELIS FRYMERSUM Jr Jozor, negotiating under the firm name of CORNELIS FRYMERSUM and ZOON, of Amsterdam, in their capacity as Directors of a certain loan, established on the Plantations KITTY AND GARDEN OF EDEN; as appears from the authentic affidavit of the aforesaid transport received by the said Gentlemen Comparants, on which written appears:

"Registered in Amsterdam on the twenty-third of February 1817, D. S. f. 179 verso, section 3 and 4, received with the Higher Court, forty and fifty and 1 Cents (signed BONDAM."

And that the said Gentlemen Comparants now, for further satisfaction of the will and the desire of the aforementioned holders of Shares, with the advice of such three Gentlemen interested in the aforementioned negotiation, which for the greatest number of Shares have concurred in the said qualification, have determined and established, as they declare to determine and establish herein:

That the herein named, as now belonging to the holders of the aforementioned two hundred ninety-four Shares in common joint ownership Plantation THE GARDEN OF EDEN, with all its buildings, harbor and other belongings; and all that further belongs to the fund of the Association, shall be governed and administered by the Gentlemen C. FRYMERSUM and ZOON, Merchants in Amsterdam, as Directors of the fund of the Plantation THE GARDEN OF EDEN, situated...

title: Unilever N.V. Option Certificate, Rotterdam, 1937
description: An option certificate (optiebewijs), No. 86786, issued by Unilever N.V. of Rotterdam and declared void after 30 June 1942. It entitled the holder of eight such certificates, on their surrender, to one certificate of an ordinary share of Unilever N.V. with a nominal value of f. 1000 — at f. 1950 (195%) through 31 December 1938, and f. 2150 (215%) from 1 January 1939 to 30 June 1942 — under terms tied to the 3½% obligation loan of Van den Bergh's Fabrieken N.V. Dated Rotterdam, July 1937, and signed by the secretary and a member of the board of management (Raad van Bestuur). A printed note records the firm's renaming from Lever Brothers & Unilever N.V. by notarial act of 23 November 1937. Such warrants let the Anglo-Dutch combine reward bondholders and stagger equity issuance; the engraved guilloche border and royal-arms stamp project the solidity expected of a blue-chip security.
creator: Negotiatie Concordia Res Parvae Crescunt
notes: Negotiatie "Concordia Res Parvae Crescunt." Reçu No. 54. Transfer of f. 500 aandelen from [Martonokle?]. Per resolution Shareholders' Meeting, December 4, 1893. Amsterdam, January 1902. Amount: f. 430.553. Signed for Associatiekas.
transcription:
# Document Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

Bij notariele acte dd° 23 November 1937 is de naam der Unilever N.V. veranderd in: „Lever Brothers & Unilever N.V.". De naam der Van den Bergh's Fabrieken N.V. werd bij notariele acte dd° 2 September 1938 gewijzigd in: „Van den Bergh's en Jurgens' Fabrieken N.V.".

UNILEVER N.V.
GEVESTIGD TE ROTTERDAM.

OPTIEBEWIJS N°.86786
VAN ONWAARDE NA 30 JUNI 1942.

De houder van acht dezer optiebewwijzen heeft het recht, tegen inlevering dezer bewijzen van Unilever N.V. een certificaat van een gewoon aandeel in Unilever N.V., groot ƒ 1000.— nominaal, te verwerven tot de prijs van ƒ 1250.— (195 %) tot en met 31 December 1938 en ƒ 2150.— (215 %) vanaf 1 Januari 1939 tot en met 30 Juni 1942, op de voorwaarden vervat in de aan de taerzijde van dit bewijs vermelde bepalingen. Overeenkomstig art. 5 dezer bepalingen worden bij de storting 3½ %-obligatien, behorende tot de in 1937 ten laste van Van den Bergh's Fabrieken N.V.-obligatielening, ten hoogste groot ƒ 30.000.000.—, in betaling aangenomen tot haar volle nominale bedrag, vermeerderd met opgeslopen rente.

ROTTERDAM, Juli 1937.

[Signatures]
SECRETARIS.                    LID VAN DE RAAD VAN BESTUUR.

---

**English translation:**

By notarial deed dated 23 November 1937, the name of Unilever N.V. was changed to: "Lever Brothers & Unilever N.V.". The name of Van den Bergh's Fabrieken N.V. was changed by notarial deed dated 2 September 1938 to: "Van den Bergh's and Jurgens' Fabrieken N.V.".

UNILEVER N.V.
ESTABLISHED IN ROTTERDAM.

OPTION CERTIFICATE N°.86786
OF NO VALUE AFTER 30 JUNE 1942.

The holder of eight of these option certificates has the right, upon surrender of these certificates of Unilever N.V., to acquire a certificate of an ordinary share in Unilever N.V., nominal value ƒ 1000.—, at the price of ƒ 1250.— (195%) until and including 31 December 1938 and ƒ 2150.— (215%) from 1 January 1939 until and including 30 June 1942, on the conditions set forth in the provisions stated on the reverse of this certificate. In accordance with art. 5 of these provisions, upon payment, 3½% bonds, belonging to the Van den Bergh's Fabrieken N.V. bond loan of 1937, up to a maximum of ƒ 30,000,000.—, shall be accepted in payment at their full nominal value, increased by accrued interest.

ROTTERDAM, July 1937.

---

- Orange circular stamp/seal in upper right corner (50 denomination mark with Dutch coat of arms, dated 1938)
- Serial number: N°.86786
- Two signatures at bottom (Secretary and Board Member)
- Decorative blue lace-pattern border on all sides
- Printer mark at bottom: "JOH. ENSCHEDÉ EN ZONEN - HAARLEM"

title: Negotiatie "Concordia Res Parvae Crescunt" Share-Transfer Receipt, Amsterdam, 1894
description: This printed receipt (Reçu No. 54) records the transfer of a single share, nominally f. 500, in the Dutch investment pool (negotiatie) styled "Concordia Res Parvae Crescunt" — "through concord small things grow," the long-standing motto of the Dutch Republic. Acknowledging shares taken over (overgenomen) from a named gentleman [name illegible], the document is countersigned for the Associatie-Cassa and dated Amsterdam, 6 January 1894, executing a resolution of the shareholders' meeting (Vergadering van Aandeelhouders) of 4 December 1893. The settlement figure of f. 430.55 against a f. 500 face shows the share changing hands below par. Such negotiaties pooled subscribers' guilders into mortgages and fixed-income paper, and this slip captures the quiet administrative machinery — meeting, resolution, treasury, receipt — by which one holder's stake passed to another. The decorative oak-leaf border and blackletter title lend the everyday transaction a deliberate gravity.

title: Continental Loan Office Bill of Exchange, Pennsylvania, 1780
description: This is a bill of exchange — the first of a set of four ("First Bill, Second, Third and Fourth not paid") — drawn by the United States of America and dated the 14th day of October 1780. Payable at thirty days' sight to Jonathan D. Sergeant or order, it orders the payment of One Hundred and Twenty Dollars in Six Hundred Livres Tournois, reckoned at five livres tournois per dollar, "for Interest due on Money borrowed by the United States." It is addressed to the Commissioners of the United States at Paris, countersigned by the Commissioner of the Continental Loan Office in the State of Pennsylvania, and signed by Francis Hopkinson as Treasurer of Loans. Such interest-bearing drafts let the Continental Congress honour its Loan Office certificates by drawing on French credit abroad, a measure that reveals how heavily the young confederation leaned on its alliance with France to service domestic war debt. The plain engraved letterforms, free of allegory, befit a fiscal instrument valued for clarity rather than display.
creator: United States of America, Loan Office, Pennsylvania
notes: US Bill of Exchange No. 43, $120 / 600 Livres Tournois. October 1, 1780. Payable to Jonathan D. Sergeant. Interest on Loan Office Certificates. Pennsylvania Loan Office; signed by H. Atkinson, Treasurer of Loans. Continental Revolutionary War finance.
transcription:
# Transcription

Cash for 120 Dollars at five Livres Tournois Dollar Hunt. N°43

United States of AMERICA, 1st Day of October 1780

AT Thirty Days Sight of this First, Second, Third and Fourth and paid, pay to Jonathan D. Sergeant or Order, One Hundred and Twenty Dollars, in Six Hundred Livres Tournois, for Interest due on Money borrowed by the United States.

To the Commissioner or Commissioners of the
United States of America, at Paris.

H. Atkinson
(Treas. of Loans)

Countersigned Wm. Smith
Commissioner of the Loan-Office in the
State of Pennsylvania

---

# Notable markings:

- **Serial Number:** N° 43 (top right)
- **Signatures:** 
  - H. Atkinson (Treasurer of Loans) - handwritten, top right
  - Wm. Smith (Commissioner of the Loan-Office in Pennsylvania) - handwritten, lower left
- **Ornamental border:** Decorative vine/scroll design on left margin
- **Handwritten annotations:** Various notations and marks throughout document
- **Date:** October 1, 1780 (printed and handwritten)

title: Poyaisian Land Grant
description: A bilingual English/Spanish land-grant certificate (Class C) conveying 400 acres in "Poyais Proper," issued under the hand of Gregor MacGregor, styling himself "Cazique of Poyais," and countersigned at London by trustees appointed for the exchange and redemption of the outstanding Poyaisian securities. Dated London, 28 April 1834, with MacGregor's grant subscribed at Edinburgh, the sheet reproduces the purported original grant from the Mosquito Shore before promising the bearer a regular title to any unappropriated land, subject to an annual quit rent of one cent per acre payable each 24 December. Poyais was the imaginary Central American republic MacGregor invented to draw British capital after South American independence; the crest's motto, NESCIUS VINCI ("not knowing how to be conquered"), lends spurious sovereignty to one of the era's most notorious financial deceptions, here being wound up a decade on.
creator: Poyais Land Office (London)
notes: Potosian Land Grant, Class C, 400 Acres / Concesión de 400 Acres. London, April 1825. Bilingual English/Spanish. British speculative investment in Bolivian (Potosí) mining/land post-independence. Large format with ornate header and two-column bilingual text.
transcription:
# Transcription of Potosian Land Grant Certificate

## LEFT PAGE (English Version)

**Class C. No. 193 / Class C. No. 193**

**Grant of 400 Acres.**

[Header with coat of arms: "POTOSIAN LAND GRANT" / "STOCK UNIT" / "400 Acres"]

**Copy of the Original Grant from the King of the Mosquito Shore and Nation, to GEORGE FREDERICK II, D.G., King of the Mosquito Shore and Nation.**

By virtue of these Presents, We hereby grant and devise unto His Excellency Colonel the Right Honorable Lord Cochrane, and to his Heirs, Executors, Administrators and Assigns, all that certain parcel or tract of Land, situate, lying and being in that part of the Potosian Republic called Popayan Province, containing Four Hundred Acres of Land in the County of Popayan in a weight line, down the South, until it touches the 1st parallel of South Latitude.

To have and to hold the said parcel of Land, with all and singular the Houses, Buildings, Improvements, Hereditaments and Appurtenances thereunto belonging or in any wise appertaining, to him the said Lord Cochrane, and to his Heirs and Assigns, to the only proper use, benefit and behoof of him the said Lord Cochrane, and his Heirs and Assigns forever.

In Witness Whereof, We have caused our Great Seal to be hereunto affixed. Given under Our Hand, Seal and Signature, in the City of the Mosquito Shore, this [date] Day of [month], in the year of our Reign.

**By His Majesty's Command:—WILLIAM BOWES.**

[Additional official documentation regarding transfer and validation]

**Certified by:**
- WILLIAM SMITH & Co., Chancing Street, Whitechapel
- Deputy Clerk of Lieutenancy of the High Court of Chancery

[Extended legal text regarding the grant conditions and authority]

**Grant of 400 Acres.**

*Viz.* George Mac Gregor, Esquire, do hereby grant unto the Bearer hereof is entitled to Four Hundred Acres of Land in that part of the Popayan Republic called Popayan Province, in consideration of his having received the sum of Four Hundred Pounds Sterling as a proportioned part of the original subscription for the Copy of the Original Grants as in any other original Grants to the Potosian Republic, in manner as will not aid relief of the Potosian Republic, now bearing date, but to immemorial and exclusive possession of the said land in virtue hereof. And we hereby promise that the Bearer shall and will be entitled to the said Lands and Tenements, and deliver to the Bearer the Grant sealed with the seal aforesaid.

*Edinburgh, April 3rd., 1824.*

[Signatures]

---

## RIGHT PAGE (Spanish Version)

**Classe C. No. 193**

**Concession de 400 Acres.**

[Header: "POTOSIAN LAND GRANT" / "STOCK UNIT" / "400 Acres"]

**[TRADUCTION]**

Copie d'Acte de Concession ou ORIGINAL du Roi de la Cité de la Nation Métropolitaine, à GEORGE FREDERIC Deux, p.h. Cham de Dame, Roi de la Cité et de la Nation Métropolitaine.

[Extended French translation of the English grant document, maintaining parallel structure]

**Par ordre de Sa Majesté.—WILLIAM BOWES.**

[French legal documentation and certifications]

**Concession de 400 Acres.**

*Nous*, George Mac Gregor, Esquire du Popoli, certifions par les présentes que le Porteur de cette est autorisé à recevoir d'ordre Quatre Cent Acres de Terre en cette Partie du Popayan que l'on appelle Popayan Province... [continued French text]

*Edimbourg, le 3e Avril, 1824.*

[Signatures]

---

## Notable Markings:

- **Coat of Arms:** Heraldic seal at top center of document
- **Serial Numbers:** No. 193 (appears on both sides)
- **Signatures:** Multiple handwritten signatures on both pages
- **Date:** April 3rd/3e Avril, 1824 (Edinburgh/Edimbourg)
- **Official Stamps/Seals:** Various certification marks
- **Languages:** Bilingual (English/French with Spanish title) presentation
- **Notation:** "Entered Vol. / Pd. [?]" at bottom of left page

title: Amsterdam Prys-Courant der Effecten, 16 May 1822
description: This is a printed weekly price current, the Prys-Courant der Effecten, issued at Amsterdam on Thursday, 16 May 1822 (No. 39). The broadsheet tabulates quotations across two registers. The upper columns list securities: the funded debt of the Vereenigde Nederlanden alongside a long file of Dutch negotiaties (foreign-loan participations placed through Amsterdam houses) and the public obligations of Spain, France, Austria, Russia and other states, each set against par and the week's price. The lower section, Wissels en Specie Cours, reports rates for bills of exchange and specie on London, Paris, Hamburg, Frankfurt, Berlin, Vienna and further centres. Printed in restrained typographic columns without ornament, the sheet served the Amsterdam exchange as an authoritative weekly record. It attests to the city's enduring role, even after the Napoleonic upheavals, as a clearing point for European sovereign credit and international payments.
creator: Amsterdam Stock Exchange
notes: Amsterdam Prys-Courant der Effecten, No. 39, 1822. Lists prices for Dutch government bonds, negotiaties, Spanish, French, Austrian, Russian, and other foreign securities. Also: Wissels en Specie Cours (exchange rates and coin prices) for major European financial centers.

# ENGLISH TRANSLATION / SUMMARY:

**PRICE-CURRENT OF SECURITIES.** Year 1822. Amsterdam, Thursday, 16 May. No. 39.

A printed Amsterdam stock-exchange price list quoting bid-and-ask ranges for three trading days (14, 15 and 16 May); the 16th fell on a holiday (*Feestdag*), so only the 14th and 15th are priced. The sheet is arranged by issuer and by country.

**United Netherlands** — the active and deferred national debt, syndicate bonds, and Netherlands bank-notes; the loans of the Treasury of the City of Amsterdam (negotiations of 1791–1819, redeemable, some payable in bank-money or cash); and Middelburg obligations.

**Domestic private negotiations** — shares and certificates in Dutch funds and infrastructure through named banking houses (van Harpen & Pluym & Backer; Mercker & van Ophoven; Ketwich & Voombergh), including the Rhineland negotiation, the Muidersluis, the Amstelveen road, the Slooten causeway, the ceded East-Frisian domains, the Society for Beneficence (Vlaer & Kol, Utrecht), the Nieuwe Diep & Koegras and Groote Wegen works, the Linge–Steenenhoek canal, and shipping and lands in Overyssel.

**Foreign negotiations:**
- **America** — United States federal debt placed through the Amsterdam houses of Van Staphorst (3% and 6% funds, 1791 issues), Daniel Crommelin & Sons (deferred and "liquidated debts"), Texier, Angely & Massac, Croese & Co., and Stadnitski & van Heukelom (certificates of liquidated debts and obligations on American lands).
- **Russia** — Hope & Co. obligations, inscriptions in the Grand Book (in paper and in silver), and Hamburg administration certificates.
- **Denmark** — toll, crown, loan-and-exchange-bank, and Asiatic Company obligations, and the National Bank.
- **Austria** — Vienna Bank obligations and metalliques through Goll & Co., Hope & Co., and J. Osy & Son; Vienna Bank shares; the 1820 loan and the 37½-million loan.
- **Italy / Sicily** — Milan monte certificates (Saportas); Neapolitan consolidated debt (Lamaison & Bouwer).
- **Spain** — obligations via Echenique & Co. and Hope & Co.; the 1820 Paris loan; premium-bills; vales reales.
- **Great Britain** — India and Bank stock, South Sea annuities, and 3% and 4% Consols (settlement 12 Aug. 1822).
- **France** — inscriptions in the Grand Book deliverable at Paris, liquidation acknowledgements, and the City of Paris premium loan (bank shares via Buys & Kerkhoven).
- **Lordships of Count Bentinck**, the **Three English Princes**, the **Grand Duchy of Baden** (Goll & Sons, Frankfurt), and **Colombia** (London obligations).

**Exchange and specie rates of Amsterdam (15 May)** — bill rates on Madrid, Bilbao, Cadiz, Seville, Lisbon, Oporto, Naples, Venice, Livorno, Genoa, Paris, Bordeaux, London, Hamburg, Vienna, Augsburg, Frankfurt, Berlin and St. Petersburg, followed by quotations for gold coin (guineas, sovereigns, louis-d'or, bar gold) and silver coin (Mexican dollars, French and Brabant crowns, five-franc pieces, rixdollars).

---

# Notable markings:

- Single large broadsheet, letterpress, set in two main columns with ruled price tables of three dated columns (14/15/16 May 1822).
- Embossed/wax-seal impression at the upper right.
- Many figures are in tiny type and partly worn; the numeric quotations transcribed above are those legible with confidence, and a number of minor entries and digits remain indistinct.

title: Chinese Republic Lung-Tsing-U-Hai Railway Treasury Bill
description: This is an 8% bearer treasury bill (Schatkistbiljet aan Toonder) for f. 1,000 in Dutch currency (Nederlandsch Courant), issued in 1923 by the Government of the Chinese Republic (Regeering van de Chineesche Republiek) to fund the Lung-Tsing-U-Hai (Longhai) Railway. It forms part of a total issue of f. 16,667,000, divided into 16,667 bills of f. 1,000 each. Drawn in Dutch and authenticated by Chinese signatures and vermilion seals, the instrument records how a Chinese east-west trunk line reaching from the coast toward the interior was carried on Netherlands capital, a reminder that early Republican China financed its railways through a patchwork of foreign loans denominated in European money. The vignette of a steel viaduct spanning a river beneath wooded hills makes the borrowing's purpose literal, while the orange-and-gold Dutch arms at the center marks the source of the funds.
creator: Government of the Chinese Republic
notes: 8% Schatkistbiljet, Lung-Tsing-U-Hai Spoorweg (Longhai Railway), 1923. No. 11639, f. 1,000 Dutch guilders. Total issue f. 16,667,000 / 16,667 notes. Regeering van de Chineesche Republiek. Ornate multicolor certificate. Dutch-guilder denominated Chinese railway finance.

title: Middelburg Plantation Bond for Essequibo and Demerara
description: A handwritten plantation mortgage bond (No. 90) drawn on the Register of Middelburg in Zeeland and signed by Kornelis van den Helm Boddaert as Directeur for the geld-Opschieters (money-lenders) of various plantations in the colony of Essequibo and Demerary, issued under the printed "Plan" of hypotheken secured upon the colonial estates and authenticated with the Company seal. Dated at Middelburg the first of January 1760, it acknowledges a capital of six hundred guilders courant (Zes Hondered Guldens Courant) at five per cent yearly interest. The instrument belongs to the Dutch system of plantageleningen, by which West Indian mortgages were bundled and sold to investors at home, an early apparatus for turning distant, slave-worked estates into negotiable paper. The plain engrossed text and the small printed form, completed by hand and sealed, embody bureaucratic routine rather than display; later endorsements record its long secondary life.
creator: Kornelis van den Helm Boddaert; Middelburg Administration
notes: Middelburg plantation bond No. 99. Kornelis van den Helm Boddaert, Director/Secretary. Plantations in Essequibo and Demerara (Guyana). Capital ~500 Livres at 5% p.a. January 1760. Dutch plantageleningen system. Endorsements dated 1772, 1793, 1817.

title: French Life Annuity on Four Million Livres
description: This is a French royal life annuity (rente viagère au denier vingt-cinq), a partly printed and partly manuscript quittance issued by Antoine Pâris, Conseiller du Roy and Garde du Trésor Royal, acknowledging receipt at Paris of a principal of 1,175 livres in exchange for a yearly rente of forty-seven livres. The annuity belongs to the four millions of livres raised under the édit of July 1723, charged on the tailles and other impositions, and is constituted for Simonne Pinault, widow of Claude Rabaroux, upon the life of Simonne Agathe Rabaroux. Dated 1723, in the Regency-to-Louis-XV transition, the instrument shows how the crown, still recovering from the collapse of Law's System, leaned on life rents to draw in private capital. A marginal note records the rente's later reduction to 23 livres 10 sols under the édit of November 1726, evidence of the monarchy's serial repudiations.
creator: Jean Antoine Paris, Conseiller du Roy
notes: Rente Viagère au Denier Vingt-cinq, No. 21839. Quatre Millions de Livres, Édit de Juillet 1723, Tailles & autres Impositions. Nominee: Simonne Jenaille. Jean Antoine Paris, Conseiller du Roy. Paris, 1723. Annual payment ~23 livres 74 sous. French royal life annuity.
transcription:
# Document Transcription

**Original (French):**

Rentes Viageres au Denier Vingt-cinq, Sur les Quatre Millions de livres de l'Edit du mois de Juillet 1723. sur les Tailles & autres Impositions.
N.°21539.

JE ANTOINE PARIS, Conseiller du Roy en ses Conseils, Garde de son Tresor Royal, Confesse avoir reçû en cette Ville de Paris, de Simonne Jenaille, [handwritten:] d'une de Claude Rabazourt pour jouir par elle de la Rente cy apres. Sur la teste de Simonne agathe Rabazourt femme de Pierre Charles Luciles avocat en Parlement demeurant à Dreux

la Somme de [handwritten:] Onze cents soulante quinze livres
quarante sept livres

pour le Principal de Rentes Viageres au Denier Vingt-cinq, faisant partie des Quatre Millions de livres de Rentes Viageres créées par Edit du mois de Juillet 1723. Registré au besoin a été, à prendre sur les Tailles & autres Impositions, tant des Pays d'Election que des Pays d'Etats, pour jouir par [handwritten:] Jenaille
pour chacun an, de ladite Rente de [handwritten:] ... sur le pied du Denier Vingt-cinq, ainsi qu'il sera plus au long expliqué par le Contrat de Constitution qui sera expedié de ladite Rente par Messieurs les Commissaires du Conseil nommez à cet effet par Lettres Patentes du Roy du 9. Aout de la présente année; de laquelle somme de à moy ordonnée pour employer au fait de ma Charge, je quitte le [handwritten signature]. & tous autres. Fait à Paris le [handwritten:] vingtième jour de septembre mil sept cens vingt-trois.

Quittance du Garde du Trésor Royal, Année mil sept cens vingt-trois.
[Handwritten signature]

M. [handwritten:] Lacromont     N.re

## English Translation:

Life Annuities at Twenty-five Deniers, On the Four Millions of Livres from the Edict of the Month of July 1723, on the Tailles & other Impositions.
No. 21539.

I ANTOINE PARIS, Counselor of the King in his Councils, Keeper of his Royal Treasury, confess to have received in this City of Paris, from Simonne Jenaille, [handwritten: one of Claude Rabazourt to enjoy by her of the Annuity hereafter. On the life of Simonne agathe Rabazourt wife of Pierre Charles Luciles advocate in Parliament residing at Dreux

the Sum of [handwritten:] Eleven hundred seventy-five livres
forty-seven livres

for the Principal of Life Annuities at Twenty-five Deniers, forming part of the Four Millions of livres of Life Annuities created by Edict of the month of July 1723. Registered as needed has been, to be drawn from the Tailles & other Impositions, both of the Election Countries and of the States Countries, to enjoy by [handwritten:] Jenaille
for each year, of the said Annuity of [handwritten:] ... at the rate of Twenty-five Deniers, as will be more fully explained by the Constitution Contract which will be issued for said Annuity by Messieurs the Commissioners of Council appointed for this purpose by Letters Patent of the King of the 9th of August of the present year; of which sum ordered to me to be employed in the execution of my Office, I hereby release the [handwritten signature]. & all others. Done in Paris on the [handwritten:] twentieth day of September one thousand seven hundred twenty-three.

Receipt of the Keeper of the Royal Treasury, Year one thousand seven hundred twenty-three.
[Handwritten signature]

M. [handwritten:] Lacromont     N.re [Notary]

title: Royal Life Annuity Contract, Hôtel de Ville of Paris, 1759
description: A partly printed, partly manuscript contract for a French royal life annuity on one head (rente viagère sur une tête), passed before the notaries and gardes-scel of Sa Majesté at the Châtelet de Paris and the Prévôt des marchands et échevins of the city, Jean-Baptiste-Élie Camus de Pontcarré. It executes the edict of November 1758, registered by the Parlement on 12 December following, which authorised the sale of three million six hundred thousand livres of life annuities, charged by preference upon the royal treasury and payable through the garde du trésor royal. Annuities could be constituted on one or two heads, with returns graduated by the holder's age into six classes — from one hundred livres per thousand of capital in the first class to one hundred forty in the sixth (eighty per thousand on two heads). The terms reveal a crown borrowing during the fiscal strain of the Seven Years' War, recruiting even foreigners and members of religious orders with the inducement that the rents be exempt from the two vingtièmes, the deux sols pour livre, and the droits d'aubaine, bâtardise et confiscation. The manuscript docket names the holder as the Comtesse de Pons-Saint-Maurice and dates the act 26 July 1759.
creator: Notaires du Châtelet de Paris; Jean-Baptiste-Élie Canus de Pontcaillé
notes: Rente Viagère Sur une Tête, Édit de Novembre 1738, Première Classe. Nominee: Comtesse de Saint-Hermaurice. Issued July 26, 1739, Paris. Notaires du Châtelet. Marchands et Echevins financing (4,000 livres capital each). French royal life annuity under Louis XV.
transcription:
# TRANSCRIPTION

## Original (French):

M^de La Comtesse de Pont-Marcelle
26 Juillet 1739

RENTES VIAGÈRES SUR UNE TÊTE.
Édit de Novembre 1738.

Première Classe.

PAR-DEVANT les Conseillers du Roi, Notaires, Gardes-notes & Gardes-scel de Sa Majesté au Châtelet de Paris, soussignés, furent prêtés, M^de JEAN-BAPTISTE-ÉLIE CAMUS DE PONTCAILLÉ, seigneur de Vuarmet, Sirogu, Briey & autres lieux, Conseillers d'État, Prévôt [des Marchands & Echevins]

[Handwritten insertions - partially legible]:
Jeanne Antoinette Vallois, Veuves d'un Abbé de lille
Jean Baptiste Normant de St [?]
[unclear handwriting]
et [unclear] a l'enseignement au Colonnet et a l'enseignement de l'[unclear]

Tous Échevins de cette ville de Paris.

ESQUELS dit que Sa Majesté, par son édit du mois de novembre 1738, enregistrié au Parlement le 12 décembre luidit, auroit ordonné que par les Commissaires nommez à cet effet, il feroit vendu & alienié audités Sieurs Prévôt des Marchands & Échevins, trois millions fix sens mille livres actuelles & effectives de rentes viagères, à prendre fur tous les anciens provenant des droits d'aides, Chapelles & cinq grofses fermes, que Sa Majesté a affectées, obligées & hypothéquées par préférence à la partie du tréfor royal, au payement desquelles rentes dédites rentes qui pourroient être acquises, Soit fur une seule tête, à divers taux, suivant l'âge des acquéreurs, ou fut deus indigènes; à raison de huit pour cent par an.

Que les constitutions particulieres desdites rentes, qui ne pourroient être moindres fur une seule tête de cinquante livres, & sur deux têtes de quarante livres de jouissance annuelle, feroient faites par lesdits trois millions fix sens mille livres actuelles & effectives de rentes viagères, à prendre fur tous les anciens provenant des droits d'aides, Chapelles & cinq grofses fermes, que Sa Majesté a affectées, obligées & hypothéquées par préférence à la partie du tréfor royal, au payement desquelles rentes dédites rentes qui pourroient être acquises, Soit fur une seule tête, à divers taux, suivant l'âge des acquéreurs, ou fut deus indigènes; à raison de huit pour cent par an.

Que les rentes seront acquises fur une tête, feroient acquéreurs; la première, depuis la naissance jusqu'à cinquante ans accomplys; la deuxième, depuis cinquante-cinq ans jusqua' soixante; la troisième, depuis soixante jusqua' soixante-cinq; la quatrième, depuis soixante-cinq ans jusqua' soixante-dix; & la sixième, depuis soixante-dix ans jusqua' soixante-quinze ans & au deffus.

Que les rentes fur deux têtes feroient acquises fur une distinction d'âge ni de classe; & dans l'une desdites rentes, il feroit justifiée de l'âge de ceux fur la tête desquels ce qui a été ci-devant prescrit en pareil cas, & qu'annuellement il seroit payé les rentes, pendant leur vie ou de ceux fur la site desquels elles feroient confluées, favoir, pour chaque constitution de mille livres en capital dans la première classe, cent livres; dans la deuxième, cent livres; dans la troisième, cent dix livres; dans la quatrième, cent vingt livres; dans la cinquième, cent trente livres; & pour les rentes fur deux têtes quatre-vingt livres, auffi pour chaque confluence de mille livres en capital.

Que toutes fortes de personnes indiftinctement, de quelque âge, Sexe, qualité & condition qu'elles puiffent être, même les religieufes qui, fuivant leur Ordre, peuvoient avoir capacité, pourroient acquérir lesdites rentes; le leur vivant des pères & mères qui auroient acquis lesdites rentes, en quelque cas qui de leur compte, les enfans & autres qui enteroient en religion & feroient profeffion dans quelque Ordre que ce puiffe être, confidereroient par forme de dotation alimentaire, celles qui auroient été par eux acquises, ou qu'ayant été Ermites nous remarquant, lefquels n'ayant aucuns cas de leur comptes, les enfans & autres qui enteroient en religion & feroient profeffion dans quelque Ordre que ce puiffe être, confidereroient par forme de dotation alimentaire, celles qui auroient été par eux acquises, ou qu'ayant eu [unclear] lefquels feront demeures hors du Royaume, pays, terres & rentes, encores qu'il fuffent fujets de Princes & États avec lesquels Elle est ou pourroit estre en guerre; en confequence Sa Majesté à voulu que lesdites rentes & les arrérages qui en froient dûs au jour du deces de ces derniers, feroient exemptes de toutes lettres de marque & de represailles, droits d'aubaine, bâtardie, confifcation, ou autres qui pourroient lui appartenir, & auxquels pourroient estre renoncé, conformément à l'édit du mois de décembre 1674 & autres fubfequens.

Que lesdites rentes feroient exemptes de la revenne des deux Vingtièmes, des Deux fols pour livre

[Signature at bottom]: U. Gersault M^e

- **Seal/emblem**: Decorative royal coat of arms at top center
- **Classification marking**: "Première Classe" (First Class) noted on right side
- **Handwritten annotation**: At top left corner indicating recipient and date: "M^de La Comtesse de Pont-Marcelle / 26 Juillet 1739"
- **Signature**: "U. Gersault M^e" at bottom
- **Notarial marks**: Multiple handwritten insertions and annotations throughout, some partially illegible

title: Republic of New Granada Deferred Bond
description: A £100 sterling bearer bond styled "Deferred," issued in London on 1 June 1845 by the Chargé d'Affaires of the Republic of New Granada (forerunner of Colombia) under an Agreement signed at Bogotá on 15 January 1845 between the Republic's Secretary of Finance, Juan Climaco Ordóñez, and Powles, Illingworth, Willson & Co., agents of the bondholders of the foreign debt of Colombia. By its terms New Granada acknowledges half of the loans contracted by the old Republic of Colombia in 1822 and 1824, exchanging new bonds for one-half of each Colombian bond surrendered and so dissolving the joint liability inherited from Gran Colombia's dissolution. Interest is genuinely deferred: nothing falls due until 1 December 1861, and the rate climbs from one per cent, rising a quarter-point yearly toward six, payable half-yearly at Baring, Brothers & Co. The engraved condor and arms of New Granada crown a document that is less an investment than an admission of insolvency dressed as settlement, the word "Deferred" naming a young republic's chosen instrument for postponing the debts of its revolutionary birth.
creator: Republic of New Granada; Powles, Illingworth, Willson & Co.
notes: Republic of New Granada Deferred Bond, Letter A, No. 7376, £100 Sterling. London, January 1, 1845. Chargé d'Affaires: Juan Climaco Ordóñez. Agents: Powles, Illingworth, Willson & Co. 4% interest deferred; dividends at Runneys, Barings & Co. Signed Bogotá August 15, 1843. Bilingual English/Spanish.
transcription:
# REPUBLIC OF NEW GRANADA: DEFERRED BOND

**Letter A N° 7376**
**£100 Sterling**
**DEFERRED**

---

## ENGLISH TEXT (Left Column):

I, the undersigned Chargé d'Affaires of the Republic of New Granada at the Court of London, in pursuance of the Agreement between the Secretary of Finance of the said Republic and the Agents of the Bondholders of the Foreign Debt of Colombia, signed at Bogotá on the 15th January, 1845, of which a copy is subjoined, and in conformity with the Instructions received from my Government, as fiscal Commissioner in London, do hereby declare that the said Republic is indebted to the Bearer hereof in the Sum of One Hundred Pounds Sterling, and hereby, in the most solemn manner, bind the said Republic to pay the same, and the interest thereon, according to the tenor of the said Agreement. The interest of the said Sum of One Hundred Pounds is to be paid Half-yearly, on the first day of June and the first day of December in each year, the first Half-yearly Dividend falling due on 1st December, 1861, according to the rates specified in the Fourth Article of the said Agreement, at the Counting-house of Messrs. Barings, Bevan, Binneys and Co., in London, on delivery of the Warrants or Coupons attached hereto. The Government of New Granada engages to deliver additional Deferred Warrants to the Holder hereof, when those attached shall have been paid; and so from time to time until the Principal of the Bond shall have been redeemed, or payment thereof tendered by the said Government.

### TRANSLATION.

We the undersigned JUAN CLIMACO ORDÓÑEZ, Secretary of the Finance Department of the Government of NEW GRANADA, in the name of the said Government; and POWLES, ILLINGWORTH, WILLSON and CO., as Agents of the Bondholders of the Debt of Colombia, have agreed to the following proposals:—

1. New Granada acknowledges as her Debt Fifty per Cent. of the Loans contracted by the former Republic of Colombia in 1822 and 1824, which Debt had been repudiated by the Government of Venezuela as being consequently cancelled as respects on the half of the value, and New Granada assumes the other half upon condition which are set forth hereinafter.

2. The Bonds which New Granada may issue, in conformity with the following articles, will bear Interest at One per Cent per annum for the first four years, which Interest shall be loaned upon itself; and in each subsequent year, will it transfer at One per Cent designated for four years.

3. In payment of the Interest due and not paid, and of that which shall become payable until the Bond shall have been paid off, New Granada agrees to issue the rate of Four per Cent, of the Principal, as that the Bonds now issued in its name shall be developed for.

4. These last-mentioned Bonds shall not bear Interest during the first six years after their issue; but, in the seventh year, they shall bear One per Cent, which shall be increased by one eighth part in each of the following years, until it reaches Three per Cent, which is the maximum rate of Interest.

5. The Interest on the New Granada Bonds that may be issued in conformity with the stipulation of Article 3, shall be paid in London on the first and fifteenth of June, concurrently eight hundred and forty-five, and to this account may be paid the Government Deferred Bonds of former loans of Powles, Heritors, of London.

6. The Bonds of both Debts shall be redeemed, on the payment of one percent. of the amount of the nominal value of the said property at the proper period of the foreign debt.

7. The net produce of the Tobacco Revenue and the half of the produce of the Customs Revenue, are chargeable for the execution of this contract.

8. These Proposals shall bind the Government of New Granada after the ratification thereof by the Government of New Granada shall have been issued in the condition which have except that the First shall draw interest from the date hereof, and none of the Credits shall not affect thereof, this Agreement shall be null and void of express lich portion.

In faith whereof we sign these presents at Bogotá, on the fifteenth of January, one thousand eight hundred and forty-five.

JUAN CLIMACO ORDÓÑEZ,
POWLES, ILLINGWORTH, WILLSON, and CO.

[Seal A Copy]
The Secretary of Finance, J. C. ORDÓÑEZ.

**Dated in London, this First day of June, 1845**

---

## SPANISH TEXT (Right Column):

### ORIGINAL:

Los infrascriptos, á saber, JUAN CLIMACO ORDÓÑEZ, Secretario de Hacienda del Gobierno de NUEVA GRANADA, á nombre de dicho Gobierno; y POWLES, ILLINGWORTH, WILLSON i Cª, como Agentes de los Tenedores de valores de la Deuda de Colombia, hemos convenido en las siguientes proposiciones:

1º. La Nueva Granada reconoce como deuda suya cincuenta por ciento de los empréstitos que contrató la República de Colombia en 1822 y 1824; cuya Deuda habia sido repudiada por el Gobierno de Venezuela como consiguientemente cancelada respecto de la mitad de su valor, y toma la Nueva Granada la otra mitad bajo las condiciones que se expresan enseguida.

2º. Los obligaciones que emita la Nueva Granada en conformidad con el artículo siguiente, devengará interes al uno por ciento anual durante los primeros años; el cual interés se aumentará en un cuarto por ciento en cada uno de los años siguientes, hasta que llegue al tres por ciento, que es el máximum de interés.

3º. En pago de los intereses vencidos i no pagados, y de los que vayan devengándose subsecuentemente hasta que se redima esta Deuda, se obligation New Granada á emitir obligaciones al cuatro por ciento del Principal, en la forma que se expresará enseguida.

4º. Estas billetes no devengará interes alguno en los diez i seis primeros años de su emisión. Pero en el diez i seis año devengará el uno por ciento, que se aumentará en un octavo por ciento en cada uno de los años siguientes, hasta que llegue al tres por ciento, que es el máximum de interés en la Nueva Granada.

5º. El pago de los intereses de las obligaciones Granadinas que se emitan según lo estipulado en el artículo 3º se hará en Londres por semestres vencidos; verificándose el pago del primer semestre desde primero de Junio de mil ochocientos cuarenta i cinco, i en las oficinas que se designe en Londres para esto, tomando por base la inversión de los fondos que se destinen al pago de esta obligación.

6º. Los valores de ambas Deudas serán admitidos á la par en pago de los derechos de Aduana que se causen después de remitida esta Deuda, destinada por los tesoreros á Amortización de la misma.

7º. El producto neto de la Renta del Tabaco i la mitad del Producto de los Aduanas, quedan afectos al cumplimiento de este contrato.

8º. Estas proposiciones obligarán al Gobierno de la Nueva Granada desde que este fuere santificada por los interesados, al afecto d Gobierno Granadino dado inmediatamente sus ordenes para que se observe las disposiciones que las suscriben se cumpla en todas sus partes; i en caso de que alguno de los suscriptores falta al cumplimiento de lo que en este contrato se conviene, Sí los suscriptores se las suscriben, todo vida convenio será salo i ninguno sobre respecto de ambas partes.

En fé de lo cual firmamos el presente en Bogotá á quince de Enero de mil ochocientos cuarenta i cinco.

JUAN CLIMACO ORDÓÑEZ
POWLES, ILLINGWORTH, WILLSON i Cª

En Copia,
El Secretario de Hacienda, J. C. ORDÓÑEZ.

---

### ENGLISH TRANSLATION:

The undersigned, namely JUAN CLIMACO ORDÓÑEZ, Secretary of the Treasury of the Government of NEW GRANADA, in the name of said Government; and POWLES, ILLINGWORTH, WILLSON & Co., as Agents

title: Rjasan Uralsk Railway Company Certificate
description: This is a 4½% Certificaat A for f. 1,200 (Twaalf Honderd Gulden, twelve hundred Dutch guilders) of the Rjasan Uralsk Spoorweg Maatschappij (Ryazan-Ural Railway Company), the renamed successor of the Rjasan Koslow Spoorweg Maatschappij established at Moscow under the Imperial Ukase of 11 January 1892. The certificate was issued at Amsterdam on 14 July 1903 by the Algemeene Trust Maatschappij against surrender of an original share (Origineel Aandeel) No. 2722 of f. 100, under a deed passed before Notary J. C. G. Pollones on 23 June 1903, and carries twenty coupons and a talon. Such Dutch certificates repackaged Russian railway securities for Amsterdam investors, who financed the lines carrying Volga and Ural grain to export. The restrained engraved border, with its guilloche frame and plain serif lettering, reflects the sober commercial typography of the Dutch trust houses rather than any decorative program.
creator: Rjasan Uralsk Spoorweg Maatschappij; Algemeene Trust Maatschappij
notes: 4½% Certificaat A No. 1977, f. 1,200. Rjasan Uralsk Spoorweg Maatschappij (Ryazan-Ural Railway). Algemeene Trust Maatschappij, Amsterdam, July 14, 1903. Replaces 12× Certificaat A No. 2722 at f. 100 each. Imperial Ukase January 11, 1897.

title: The Rock Island Company Administration Certificate, Amsterdam, 1897
description: This is a Dutch administration certificate (Certificaat) for $1,000 of common stock in The Rock Island Company, issued through the Administratie-Kantoor van Amerikaansche Fondsen at Amsterdam founded by the firms Broes en Gosman, Ten Have en van Essen, and Jarman en Zoonen. The holder is entitled to ten common shares of $100 each (TIEN GEWONE AANDEELEN à $100) deposited with the trust office, whose terms are governed by a notice dated 1 October 1897; dividend coupons run to No. 20 with a renewal talon. The instrument embodies the late-nineteenth-century Dutch appetite for American railway securities: rather than holding the underlying shares abroad, Amsterdam investors received locally administered certificates against deposited stock, the kantoor collecting dividends and managing risk on their behalf. The engraving is sober and typographic, the firm name set in heavy roman capitals over a finely ruled rose ground, conveying probity rather than ornament.
creator: The Rock Island Company; Broes en Goosman; Ten Have en van Essen; Jarman en Zoonen; Plantenga (Amsterdam)
notes: Dutch Certificaat No. 52108, $1,000 Common Stock, The Rock Island Company (Chicago, Rock Island and Pacific Railway). Administered by Broes & Goosman, Ten Have & van Essen, Jarman & Zoonen, Plantenga, Amsterdam. Pink-printed certificate.
