Issue of Certificates of a total nominal value of 240,000 £ or 600,000 frs francs representing the purchase of 600,000 hectares of land of the same nominal value located in Lower California the said issue consisting of:

2550 certificates of 100 hectares each = £40 of 1000 fcs

# Transcription

## CONDITIONS.

1. Under an Agreement dated the 3rd day of May, 1885, "La Mexicana Compania Europea de Minas y Terrenos," the Vendor has agreed to sell to the Company the land herein set as therein provided in the General Agents of the said Company for the administration and direction (including the realty) of the said land, the subdivision or cancellation of any of the said proceeds of selling, said to 10 per cent. of the net proceeds of such thereof, and the respective holders of these proceeds shall be entitled to Certificates representing their amount in Francs, will belong to, and be distributed pro rata among the holders of the Certificates of the Company respectively.

Such illustration will be effected by means of the certificates issued in the form hereof as required.

2. The Company will furnish to the holder of the Company distributable as aforesaid amount of not less to the sum of £1,000, three times in succession with the approval of the Meetings: the first advertisement in the London and one Paris daily paper, to be repeated in each paper specifying the title and object of the Company, and the three price, the sharecopies payment will be made on presentation and delivery of the relevant coupon in the Bank in hand, and according to the terms and conditions provided by such order.

3. All Certificates will be fully paid without any preference of priority the one over the other. All Certificates and Coupons are transferable by delivery or endorsement. The Company will pay all sums due on the Certificates, issued to the bearer of this Certificate on or when it may become due.

4. Meetings of the Company will be held in London or Paris as determined upon by the Board of Certificates with all unpaid Coupons attached will be entitled to be present and vote at all such meetings, and will have equal voting powers as other holders of the Certificates produced by them respectively.

5. The seal of the Company will be kept at the registered office of the Company.

---

En conformité des termes d'un Contrat en date du 3me jour de Mai, 1885, "La Mexicana Compania Europea de Minas y Terrenos en Mexico"—vendeuses à la Californie (Mexico) Land Company, Limited, s'engage ainsi qu'il est spécifié en dit acte, à agir en qualité d'agents général de cette dernière pour l'administration et la direction (y compris la realty) de ces terres ; la subdivision ou l'annulation de quelconques des dites terres et la vente, au moyen des prospectus et prospectus du produit net, l'abolition est une répartition, les porteurs respectifs de ces procédés recevront l'annulation et la direction et Francs, appartenant à, et be distributed pro rata parmi les porteurs des Certificats de la Compagnie respectivement.

Tout porteur de certificat sera à la présent et compagnie aux partis, pourront assister à ses réunions et auront droits égaux, quant à la valeur nominale des certificats disposés par les représentants dudit de la valeur nominale d'livres.

Payment shall be made upon presentation and confirmation of the terms and conditions of said deed upon presentation of counter delivery of copy.

Every holder of certificate shall be in the present and company to the parties, may attend its meetings and shall have equal rights, as to the nominal value of the certificates disposed by the representatives thereof of the nominal value in pounds.

title: Chinese Account Ledger of Loans in Copper Cash, Reckoned at New Year, Tongzhi 13 (1874)
description: A handwritten Chinese account document of two sheets, densely brushed in cursive characters in vertical columns and overlaid with a lattice of red diamond tally marks. A red title slip at the upper right reads 同治十三年新正月合算大吉 — the reckoning of accounts in the first month of the new year, Tongzhi 13 (1874), closed with the auspicious phrase 'great fortune.' The text is a running ledger of loans and balances kept in copper cash, recurring terms being 原本 (principal), 合錢 (total cash), and sums in 千文 and 文 (strings and units of wén). Small red stamps and the red crossing-out grid mark entries as verified and settled. The document is Qing-dynasty Chinese, not the Japanese yen instrument of earlier cataloguing.

pages.description: Opening sheet, headed by the red slip 同治十三年新正月合算大吉. Black columns record principal and remaining balances — 原本錢…千文, 殘 (remainder), 合錢捌仟… — in copper cash, with a red diamond tally grid struck across the entries and two small red verification stamps near the foot. Page 1 of 2.

Continuation sheet in the same hand, without a title slip. Further account columns of individual debts and totals (合錢, 千文, 文), again crossed by the red reconciliation lattice; a small red stamp sits at the upper left. Names accompany several entries. Page 2 of 2.

creator: Chinese merchant or household (unidentified)
notes: Handwritten Japanese document; vertical cursive script; two red official seals; red label upper right; financial or legal instrument; Meiji era or late Edo
transcription:
# Chinese New-Year Account Reckoning of Values and Balances in Copper Cash, Tongzhi 13 (1874) — Transcription

## Original (Chinese):

*(Layout: a densely brush-written ledger of many vertical columns, read right-to-left. A vertical red paper title-slip is pasted at the upper-right corner. The entire body of black cursive text is overlaid with a lattice of red diamond-shaped tally marks used to cross off / verify entries, and small red seal-stamps appear along the lower edge. Most item labels and the recurring accounting terms are legible; the great majority of the individual numeric amounts are heavily cursive, obscured by the red grid, and cannot be read with confidence — these are marked [illegible].)*

**Red title-slip (upper right, vertical):**

同治十三年新正月合筭（算）大吉

**Recurring accounting terms used throughout the body (legible):**

原本 （principal）　價不…（"the price is now…"）　合殘（残）（combined balance）　…千文…文（strings and units of wén）　獲和（利?）錢（interest obtained）　以上共宗共合錢（"the above, all items combined, total cash"）　秋存（autumn balance?）

Numbers are written in the formal/大寫 forms: 壹　弍（貳）　叁　…　拾　佰　仟, together with 六　七 and a recurring cursive figure resembling 松 (uncertain — possibly 柒/陸).

**Right-hand opening columns (below the title-slip), representative, right-to-left:**

- （small annotation）寶眼收苓六月…[illegible]
- 誤外浮存錢弍仟…[illegible]…
- 十年獲和（利?）錢壹仟…[illegible]…
- 壬（土?）年獲和（利?）錢壹佰…[illegible]…
- 以上共宗共合錢…[illegible]…
- 秋存…錢…[illegible]

**Middle columns — item/produce labels with values, representative (each column: [label] 不 …千 … 合殘 …):**

- 高根（?）… 不 …[illegible]千 … 合殘 …[illegible]
- 蕎麥 … 不 …[illegible] … 合殘 …[illegible]
- 各麥（大麥?） … 不 …[illegible] … 合殘 …[illegible]
- 黑菜 紅蕪菁 豆 … 不 …[illegible] … 合殘 …[illegible]
- 架貨 不 壹千…[illegible] … 合殘 …[illegible]
- 紅豆 雜科（?）米 … 不 …[illegible] … 合殘 …[illegible]

**Left-hand closing columns, representative, right-to-left:**

- 地價 不 弍口弍拾弍…[illegible]千文　合殘 六拾六…[illegible]
- 樹價 不 …[illegible]加壹拾弍…千文　合殘 …[illegible]拾
- 崇古（?）地價 不 壹口松拾松…[illegible]千　合殘 弍拾松…[illegible]
- 宗（single-character item marker）
- 以上共宗共合錢 … 千文 … 合殘 …[illegible]

*(Further columns and the interior of every column follow this same form — an item or category name at the top, a value "…千文", and a running "合殘 …" balance — but the individual figures are largely illegible cursive obscured by the red tally grid and are not transcribed here rather than guessed.)*

**Lower edge:** several small red seal / verification stamps and the red diamond crossing-out grid marking entries as settled.

---

## English Translation:

**Red title-slip:** "Tongzhi 13, first month of the new year — accounts reckoned together. Great fortune." (Tongzhi 13 = 1874.)

The sheet is a running New-Year settlement of values and balances kept in copper cash (wén). Throughout it uses the recurring bookkeeping terms *yuánběn* (原本, principal), "the price now is…" (價不…), *hé cán* (合殘, combined remaining balance), amounts in strings and units of *wén* (…千文 / …文), "interest obtained" (獲利錢), "the above, all items together, combined total cash" (以上共宗共合錢), and an "autumn balance" (秋存).

The opening columns at the right record such items as a carried-over surplus ("mistaken/extra floating balance," 誤外浮存錢, some thousands of cash), interest obtained over ten years (十年獲利錢, over one thousand cash), interest obtained in the *rén/壬* year (over one hundred cash), and then a grand "all items combined total."

The middle columns list produce and commodity categories — *gāogēn* (高根?), buckwheat (蕎麥), barley/wheat (大麥), black greens (黑菜), red turnip (紅蕪菁), beans (豆), shelved/stocked goods (架貨), red beans (紅豆), rice (米) — each with a stated value in thousands of *wén* and a running remaining balance.

The closing columns at the left give land and tree valuations — land price (地價), tree price (樹價), an ancestral/old land price (崇古地價?) — each "now valued at…" so many thousands of *wén* with a "remaining balance" (e.g. sixty-six…, twenty-…), followed by a single "item" marker (宗) and the final "above, all items combined, total cash" line.

The whole body is overlaid with a red diamond tally grid crossing out entries as checked/settled, and small red seal-stamps along the bottom edge verify the account.

*(Note: this is a Qing-dynasty Chinese copper-cash account, consistent with the catalogue facts. Because the hand is very cursive and the red tally grid overlaps the digits, most individual amounts are illegible and have been marked rather than reconstructed, to avoid inventing figures.)*

title: Chinese Government 8% Sterling Treasury Note, Peking, 1919
description: This is a sterling treasury note for £100 issued by the Government of the Chinese Republic, bearing interest at 8 per cent and forming part of the 8 Per Cent Ten-Year Sterling Treasury Notes 1925-1926. The body dates the instrument at Peking on the first day of the tenth month of the eighth year of the Republic, being the first day of October 1919, with principal and interest payable at the office of Lloyds Bank in London. A long attached sheet of semi-annual interest coupons, each headed "The Chinese Republic," runs the full ten-year term. Issued as China sought foreign credit amid the financial strains of the warlord era, the note placed its security in the hands of a London bank and a sterling obligation, reflecting how the early Republic borrowed against its revenues through European money markets. An engraved vignette of harbour works and shipping crowns the green-printed face, an image of trade and infrastructure offered as warrant for the loan.
creator: Government of the Chinese Republic
notes: 8% Sterling Treasury Notes 1925-1926; No. 5654; £100; 10-year term; full coupon sheet attached with approximately 71 semi-annual coupons; green printed

title: Chinese Government Reorganisation Gold Loan Bond
description: This bearer obligation belongs to the Chinese Government's Five Per Cent. Reorganisation Gold Loan of 1913, one bond of a £25,000,000 sterling issue. Its face value is stated at once in the five markets that floated the loan—Frs. 505 = £20 = M. 409 = Roubles 189.40 = Yen 195.92—and printed throughout in English, French, German, Russian and Japanese. It carries the manuscript signature of Hu Weide (胡惟德), Chinese Minister in Berlin, the great red seal of the Republic of China (中華民國國璽), and the counter-signature of the Banque de l'Indo-Chine at Paris, this copy being issued there in 1913. Floated by the Six-Power consortium that financed Yuan Shikai's new republic and secured on the salt and customs revenues, its five parallel valuations testify to the syndicated funding of a state in transition. The engraved vignette—an allegorical figure amid sheaves, with steamship, cannon and industrial wheel—frames the borrowing as the harnessing of commerce, industry and arms.
currency: British pound sterling|French franc|German mark|Russian ruble|Japanese yen
creator: Chinese Government / Six-Power Consortium
notes: 5% Reorganisation Gold Loan 1913; £23,000,000 total; No. 489,731; Frs. 505 / £20; quintilingual; signed by Hu Weide; Six-Power Consortium; major Chinese republican bond

Text is dense and continues for multiple paragraphs covering financial terms and conditions.]

### GERMAN VERSION - "SCHULDVERSCHREIBUNG ÜBER M.409"

### FRENCH VERSION - "OBLIGATION DE FRS. 505"

### RUSSIAN VERSION - "ОБЛИГАЦIЯ ВЪ 193.40 РУБЛЕЙ"

**Original (Cyrillic):**
[Dense Russian text in Cyrillic script detailing the bond terms]

## BOTTOM SECTION

"Comptroller pour l'Identification de Monnaies à la
BANQUE DE L'INDO-CHINE À PARIS"

---

## NOTABLE MARKINGS

1. **Serial Number:** 489,731 (top center)
2. **Large Red Seal:** Chinese Republic official seal (center bottom) - features traditional Chinese characters in red with ornamental square border
3. **Vignettes:** 
   - Central pastoral scene with figures
   - Multiple small vignettes in corners showing industrial/commercial scenes

4. **Signatures:**

title: 5% Loan of the City of Baku (Reverse), Baku, 1910
description: This is the back of a bearer bond from the 5% Loan of the City of Baku, 1910 — issued by the Baku Municipal Duma and printed in Russian, English and French (Emprunt 5% de la Ville de Bakou). Each bond is denominated as Roubles 189 = Pounds sterling 20 = Francs 504, the triple reckoning that let the issue circulate on the London and Paris markets through Lloyds Bank and the London & South-Western Bank. The conditions, approved by the Assistant Minister of Finance on 7 May 1910, set the total nominal capital at 26,999,973 roubles (2,857,140 pounds; 71,999,928 francs), to fund a waterworks, the buyout of the horse-tramway company, electric street lighting, schools and market rows, and the discharge of municipal debts. The amortization table schedules redemption from 1911 to 1959, while the engraved coupon talons at right reflect the sober, ledger-like aesthetic of state-printer securities.
creator: City of Baku

title: Colombian National Railway Company First Mortgage Debenture, London, 1910
description: A £100 first mortgage debenture of The Colombian National Railway Company, Limited, a British-registered concern (incorporated under the Companies' Acts, 1862 to 1898) capitalised at £900,000 in 900,000 ordinary shares of £1 each. It forms part of an issue of first mortgage debentures totalling £200,000, created by a resolution of the board on 27 October 1899 and secured by a deed of trust; it carries interest at six per cent per annum and is payable on 1 July 1929, or earlier should the principal fall due. Sealed in London on the eighth day of August 1910, it is registered to Santiago Pérez Triana, the Colombian Minister in England, at the Legación de Colombia, 45 Avenue Road. The instrument exemplifies the early-twentieth-century pattern of financing Latin American railways through sterling debt floated in London: the issuer is British, the capital British, yet the enterprise and its risks lie in Colombia. Its restrained typographic frame, ornamented only by an engraved foliate border and a revenue stamp, asserts the sober creditworthiness such borrowing sought to project.
creator: Colombian National Railway Company Limited
notes: British-registered company; £200,000 total debenture issue; No. 2081; £100 at 6%; Colombian railway concession; repayable 1929

title: Compagnie de Colonisation Américaine Land Share, Paris, 1820
description: This engraved share certificate (action) of the Compagnie de Colonisation Américaine, of Rue de Choiseul No. 4 in Paris, makes its bearer—here named as Charles Guillaume Juste Jérome—proprietor of one hundred acres of land in the states of Virginia and Kentucky, against a subscription of 1,300 francs. The text binds the company to deliver the land and to admit the holder to a share of the venture's proceeds; the signature of the managing house, De Redern & Cie, is certified as genuine at the foot. Dated at Paris 23 June 1820—reckoned also as the forty-fourth year of United States independence—the document belongs to the wave of French land-colonisation schemes that followed the fall of the Empire, when promoters sold transatlantic acreage to investors at home. The sober, typographic sheet, framed only by detachable dividend coupons, presents itself as a serious title to property rather than a speculative lure, its restraint underscoring the claim to genuine landed value.
creator: Compagnie de Colonisation Américaine
notes: French land company; 100 acres in Virginia and Kentucky; Série B No. 4258; Frs. 1,300; dividend coupon strips attached at left; mid-19th century American land speculation

title: Baragua Sugar Company Mortgage Gold Bond
description: This is a First Mortgage, Fifteen-Year, Seven-and-One-Half Per Cent Sinking Fund Gold Bond for $1,000, issued by the Compañía Azucarera Baragua (Baragua Sugar Company) and dated the fifteenth day of July 1922. Principal and interest are payable in gold coin of the United States at the company's office in the Borough of Manhattan, City of New York, with interest at 7.5 per cent running from July 15, 1922 and falling due semi-annually each January and July. The bond was floated as Cuban cane sugar settled into the depressed prices that followed the speculative "Dance of the Millions" collapse of 1920–21, and its high coupon and first-mortgage security on all company property reflect the premium investors demanded of a single-crop Cuban venture financed through New York. The engraved vignette of an ox-drawn cart and laborers gathering cane in a tropical field anchors the instrument's value in the harvest itself; an ornate green guilloche border frames the text, and a pink Cuban fiscal revenue stamp is affixed at lower left.
creator: Compañia Azucarera Baragua
notes: Cuban sugar company; $1,000 bond No. M 728; 7.5% first mortgage 15-year sinking fund gold bond; sugar harvest vignette; green border; Cuban fiscal stamp; Baragua, Cuba

title: Suriname Plantation Fund Conditions
description: This printed sheet sets out the Conditiën van een Fonds of Negotiatie — the terms of a plantation loan fund — placed under the direction of the Amsterdam merchant house De Heeren Theodore Passalaigue en Zoon for the benefit of planters in the Colony of Suriname. Article I requires that each participating planter's estate be soundly mortgaged as security, free of prior claims, with an inventory submitted and the pledge confirmed by the colony's court (Confirmatie van Justitie der Colonie Surinamen). Article II binds the planter to mortgage all plantation produce and fixes interest at six per cent per annum, with ten per cent charged on further sums drawn down (geldopneeming) in later years. Such negotiaties channelled Dutch capital into Caribbean sugar and coffee estates, the produce consigned back to Amsterdam against the debt. An embossed armorial revenue stamp and a manuscript flourish authenticate the leaf, whose plain title typography keeps the document squarely legal rather than ornamental.
creator: Theodore Passalaigue en Zoon
notes: Suriname plantation negotiatie conditions; No. 306; Theodore Passalaigue & Zoon, Amsterdam; Article I-II of conditions; 10% interest; plantation mortgage; late 18th century

# Document Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

N°.306.

D

CONDITIËN
VAN EEN

FONDS OF NEGOTIATIE,

Onder Directie van

De Heeren THEODORE PASSALAIGUE en ZOON,

Kooplieden te Amsterdam, ten behoeven van daar in deelhebbende Planters in de Colonie Surinaamen, en die vervolgens daar in door gedachten Directeurs zullen werden geadmitteerd en aangenoomen.

A R T. I.

De Planters welke door de Heeren Directeuren geadmitteerd werden in dit Fonds deel te neemen, zullen na dat hunne Plantagiën behoorlyк door beëdigde Prijsgeers geïnventariseerd zijn geworden, dezelve Plantagiën in behoorlyke Forma verhypothecheeren en verbinden aan en ten behoeven van dit Fonds, by Acte ten overstaan van de Heeren Raaden in den Hove van Civile Justitie der Colonie Surinaamen te passeeren, welke met willige Condemnatie van welgemelde Raaden zal moeten werden geffeckt, en zal dezelve Acte beneevens de Inventaris en Prisatie behoorlyк geregistreerd zijnde, en een Declaratoir van Secretarissen waar by Consteert dat dezelve Verhypothecqueerde Effecten zijn vry en onbefraward, aan de Heeren Directeuren moeten toegezonden worden.

A R T. II.

De Planters zullen zich in 't zelve Hypotheecq moeten verplichten, om alle de Producten van hunnen Plantagien provenieerde, te Consigneeren en affezenden aan de Heeren Directeuren deezèr Negotiatie, van wien zy ook de benoodigheeden voor hunnen Plantagien zullen doen ontbieden, dat zy dezelve door de Heeren Directeuren alher zullen laaten verslueeren, en ten dien einde ter behoorlyke tyd kennis van 't afsenden der Producten, en de Jaarlyks van hunne verschulddigde Capitaelen zullen betaalen Interessen van Ses per Centybot de dadelyk voldoening toe, en dat de voldoeininge en aflossinge zal geschieden in tien Termynen, te weeten; na verloop van d'eerste tien Jaaren van de Geldopneeminge, tien per Cento van de verschulddigde Somma, en voorts van Jaar tot Jaar tien per Cento, zoo lang de geheele Schuld zal zijn gekweeten en affedaan, met overgifle, dat ingevalle zy aan het bovengomelde niet morgen voldoen, het by hun verschulddigde Capital met de Interesten en Kolten directelyк kan werden opgeëffychr en gevordert, en by Executie verhaald aan hunne Persoonen, Plantagien, roerande en onroerende Goederen.

A

Art.

---

**English Translation:**

N°.306.

D

CONDITIONS
OF A

FUND OR NEGOTIATION,

Under the Direction of

The Gentlemen THEODORE PASSALAIGUE and SON,

Merchants in Amsterdam, for the benefit of participating Planters in the Colony of Suriname, and those who shall subsequently be admitted and accepted therein by said Directors.

ART. I.

The Planters who are admitted by the Gentlemen Directors to participate in this Fund shall, after their Plantations have been duly inventoried by sworn appraisers, mortgage said Plantations in proper Form and bind them to and for the benefit of this Fund, by deed executed before the Gentlemen Counsel in the Court of Civil Justice of the Colony of Suriname, which shall be effected with the willing consent of said Counsel, and when said deed has been properly registered along with the inventory and appraisal, and a Declaration from the Secretaries certifying that said mortgaged effects are free and unencumbered, shall be sent to the Gentlemen Directors.

ART. II.

The Planters shall obligate themselves by said mortgage to consign and send to the Gentlemen Directors of this negotiation all the products from their plantations, from whom they shall also obtain the necessities for their plantations, which they shall cause to be delivered to them by the Gentlemen Directors, and to that end at the proper time give notice of the shipment of products, and the yearly [amount due], and they shall pay interest on their owed capitals at six per cent per annum, and that the payment and redemption shall occur in ten terms, namely: after the expiration of the first ten years from the borrowing of money, ten per cent of the owed sum, and thereafter from year to year ten per cent, so long as the entire debt shall be paid and discharged, with the proviso that should they fail to meet the aforesaid obligation, their owed capital with interest and costs may be directly enforced and collected, and by execution recovered from their persons, plantations, moveable and immoveable property.

A

Art.

- Letter "D" stamped at top center
- Handwritten notation in upper right corner (appears to be a library or archive mark)
- Footer indicates continuation with "A" and "Art." suggesting this is page 1 of a multi-page document

title: Confederate States of America $1,000 Cotton Loan Bond, Richmond, 1863
description: A $1,000 coupon bond of the Confederate States of America, bearing interest at six per cent per annum and issued under the loan authorized by the Act of Congress approved April 30, 1863. Payable to bearer at the Seat of Government, it matures on the first day of June 1883, twenty years after its date, and was executed by the Register of the Treasury at Richmond on the first day of June 1863. Its defining clause makes the interest payable, at the holder's option, in sterling or in cotton of the quality of New Orleans Middling valued at six pence per pound, deliverable at New Orleans, Mobile, Savannah, Charleston, Wilmington, or Richmond. The instrument thus binds the Confederacy's credit to its one exportable staple, a measure of how a blockaded government, starved of specie and foreign confidence, sought to borrow against the cotton in its fields. The engraved frame, ornamental script, and a central bearded portrait lend the paper the gravity of established public finance the new government otherwise lacked.
creator: Confederate States of America, Treasury Department
notes: Confederate States of America; $1,000 bond; Act of Congress April 30, 1863; American Civil War finance; portrait of Confederate official; Register of the Treasury signature

title: Connecticut Treasury Certificate
description: An interest-bearing treasury certificate of the State of Connecticut, issued from the Treasury-Office at Hartford on 1 June 1782 in the sum of £12.17.02 Connecticut currency. The instrument acknowledges that the state owes a named soldier of the Continental Army one fourth part of the balance found on his account, the principal to be paid in gold or silver on 1 June 1786, with lawful interest payable annually each first of June until then. It is issued pursuant to an Act of the General Assembly held at Hartford the second Thursday of May preceding, and signed by the Treasurer. The certificate documents how a state, starved of specie during the Revolutionary War, settled the arrears of its soldiers not in cash but in dated promises secured on future revenue. The engraved border, naming "the payment of" the "Connecticut Line," frames the obligation as a debt of honour to its own troops.
creator: State of Connecticut, Treasury-Office
notes: Connecticut state debt certificate; £12.17.02; No. 11593; Continental Army payee; printed by Hudson and Goodwin, Hartford; one-fourth payment in gold or silver
transcription:

D. One Thousand Seven Hundred and Eighty-Two, with annual Interest thereof; which Interest shall be paid unto him or his Order annually, at the same Place, on the first Day of June, if demanded, pursuant to an Act of the General Assembly, held at Hartford, the second Thursday of May last.

[Signature]
Treasurer.

title: Dutch Life-Annuity Tontine Conditions (No. 21), 1657
description: This sheet is a printed copy (COPIA) of the conditions and terms ("Conditien en Voorwaarden"), numbered No. 21 ("N°. Een en Twintig"), of a Dutch life-annuity tontine. The subscribing parties agree jointly to furnish a capital of ten thousand guilders, constituted upon the respective lives of twenty persons each above thirty years of age; the sum is payable within eight days into the hands of Pieter de Lange, who with his wife Sara de la Forterie (or the longer-living of the two) is named director, empowered to co-opt a successor resident in the city. The first reckoning falls on the first of July 1657, the capital to be laid out in obligations charged to the public offices, the annuities falling due yearly at the first of August. The terms embody the tontine's defining mechanism: as the named lives expire, their shares accrue to the surviving contractants, so payment narrows toward the last survivors, letting a projector raise durable capital while spreading mortality risk across a pool of staked lives.
creator: Pieter de Lange (director)
notes: Life annuity tontine No. 21; f.500 capital on 20 lives over 30 years; August 1688 first interest payment; 30-year term; early Dutch tontine document
transcription:
# Document Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

8
COPIA.                                    No. EEN EN TWINTIG.

Conditien en Voorwaarden, daar op de
ondergetekende Persoonen met den anderen geac-
cordeert, en over-een gekomen zijn, nopende
het fournissement van een somme van tien duy-
sent guldens Capitaal, te constituëren op de
respective Lyven van twintig Persoonen, boven
de dertig Jaaren oudt.

I.

Dat elk der Contractanten, tot de gefeyde Somme fal moeten fourneren en opbrengen, voor
Jaaren, posten, een Somma van vyf honderd Caroli guldens, hunnen deel vyt van acht dagen
naar 't pasferen defes, op Affigatie van de Gecommitteerdens, in handelen van Pieter de Lange,
als fpecialyk by de Geinterefleerdens daar toe gauthorifoert mitsdien, verklaerende yeder
zyn bellofte de Somme te constituëren ten lyve van loodiange Persoonen, als vyder by Lyve on-
dertekeninge fal nomineren, fyndse gebooren voor den eerften Iuly des Jaars lestienhoridert tevyn fyftg.

Dat het voorfz. Capitaal van tien duysfent guldens alloo gefourneerdt fynde, da naar te noemen Gecommitteer-
dens 't'telve met de aiderecriften doenlyek, fullen aanleggen en beftedien in Obligation ten laften van 'rgemeene Landt,
of noch vyt de Stadt, en andere publyque Comptoren binnen de felve, naar fy Gecommit-
teerdens raadtdaam en beft doordcelén fullen.

III.

Dat de voorfz. Obligatie, voor de voornoemde Capitale Somma als boven komende te provenieren, op
Intereffen fullen moeten loopen, foo lange dit Collegïe of Contract fal komen te duuren; en by aldien de felve
Obligatien eerder mochten werden afgesloft, fullen de Gecommitteerdens als dan geholden welen, met den
aldercerijten of wel binnen den tytsk van vier weken, de penningen daar van procedaende, wedeom aan te leg-
gen, als vooren Articul 2 geifrt ?, oock mede beyorens de afloffinge, alle mogeyicke devooire moeten aan
wenden, omme de felve Obligation, vas 't doenlyek, noch loopende te houden.

IV.

Dat de Gecommitteerdens de Intereffen der vooren geroerde Obligation, r'elkens ten vervaldage, of bin-
nen korten tyd daar na fullen latén ontfangen, en alle Jaren laten maken eene rekeninge, gereekent
naar 't getal der Persoonen, die op de vervaldinge van het—vooraande Jaren in het leven geweeft zyn, en ingevol-
ge van dien aan de Participanten, wegcnds felve Persoonen, de voorfz. oultangen Intereft, yder pro rata
fyn ingelegt Capitaal uytgeleyt, en doen genieten, tot welcke uytreckeninghe de voornoemde Pieter de Lange,
en Sara de la Forterie 't'Huysvrouwe, of de langft levende van hen beyde gecommitteerd werden mitsdeden
en werden de Gecommitteerdens geathorisiert om naar 't'overlvden van de langtlivende, een ander in fyn
plaatfe te cligen en aan te ftellen, mits dat de felve woonachtig zy binnen defte Stad.

V.

Dat de vervaltyd van deie Lyfsfenten Jaarlyck fal zyn den cerften Auguiti, aanvang nemende zoo ras de
kloeke des nachts naar den laafste Iuly, 12 iuren zal hebben gejlugen, en zulcks het eerfte Iaar den eerften
Auguiti 1688.

VI.

Dat alle de Intereffen of Renten, die jaarlycks door 't overlvden van eenige der gemelte Lyven of Persoonen,
waar op ymandt van de Contractanten fyne Actie fal hebben conftitveertt fullen komen te vervallen, weder-
om fullen komen te accresieeren by egale repartitie, naar het getal van de noch vordere in 't leven blyvenide
Persoonen, en een iegde rante de felve, of der felver recht hebbende, en niet te conftitveren tot de twee laat-
fie toe, als in 't volgende Articul nader wordt uitgedruckt; des nochtans dat de Erfgenamen, of het recht
hebbende van deriglftorvcne, fullen genieten de repartitie van het Sterf-Jaar, daar mne de felve overleden fal
zyn; 's doelantg als of ihev geleche Sterf-jaer hadt uitgeleeft, mits het contract overleverde. Kommentceren
de de Contractanten in cas van overlvden van nul af aan, voor haar en hare Erigenamen, van alle foudange.
recht en achtie, als fy refpectivelcyk door haar fomfillement, tot de gemeete Capitaal en Renten hebben ge-
hadt, en behooste van alle vordere Intereffen, aan welcke het voorfz. Capitaal, en de volgende Intereffen
fucceffivelyck invoegen als vooren, fullen komen te aceresleeren.

VII.

Dat alle de gemeelte Lyven of Persoonen, tot op twee na overlrden fynde, alle de voorfz. Obligatien als dan
volkomen eigen fullen zyn, aan de genen de welcke op de voorfz. twee laaffte Persoonen hebben getekent, of
hun recht hebbende, en onder de felve in gelyckc Portien verdeelt moeten werden, niet tegenftaande den
eenen van beter of lleelicbter Confituutie al'andere foude mogen welen, s foo dat als dan yeder gerechte hclft
als in geen goedti, naar fich zal vermogen nemen, en daar mede doen en handelen maar ijn blleuen als ihy
met syn ander vry eysen goedi kan en vermacht te doen, en dat daar toe mede gerechtigt fullen zyn d'Erfgena-
men of het recht hebbende van die gene, die uyt de gemelte twee laatfte Persoonen voor de uytdeelinge overle-
den foude mogén welen, werdende mitsdelen de Geinterefleercénts ten lyve van de felve twee laaffte Persoonen
van geleyde Collegïe, oock foo veel noodt zy, door hun overige Contractanten, nu voor als dan, de
voorfz.

---

## English Translation:

**8
COPY.                                    No. TWENTY-ONE.**

**Conditions and Terms which the undersigned Persons have mutually agreed upon, concerning the provision of a sum of ten thousand guilders Capital, to be constituted on the respective Lives of twenty Persons, over thirty years of age.**

**I.**

That each of the Contractors shall furnish and provide, for the aforesaid Sum, for Years, amounts, a Sum of five hundred Caroli guilders, their portion out of eight days after the passing thereof, on Affigation of the Commissioners, in the handling of Pieter de Lange, as specifically authorized by the Interested parties herein, declaring each his promise to constitute the Sum for the life of lodging Persons, as further by Lives under-signed shall name, being born before the first July of the Year one thousand six hundred eighty-eight.

That the aforesaid Capital of ten thousand guilders, having been thus furnished

title: Suriname Plantation Negotiatie Conditions
description: Headed "Copia," this printed transcript sets out the conditions of a plantation negotiatie organised at Amsterdam and dated the first of May 1765. Above the conditions stands the binding pledge of Nicolaas Brant, the Amsterdam merchant directing the fund, who undertakes to honour its terms and to issue further obligations as the negotiatie expands. The fund was to advance between 100,000 and 150,000 Hollands guilders to planters in the Colony of Suriname at six percent yearly interest. The numbered articles that follow govern the security: each planter pledges his estate as a first mortgage, the document specifying plantations, buildings, enslaved people ("Slaaven"), horses, cattle and crops, reinforced by voluntary submission to the Court of Justice there. The sober, text-only sheet embodies the Dutch financing structure that bound Amsterdam capital to Caribbean coerced labour at the height of the colony's plantation boom.
creator: Nicolaas Brant
notes: Suriname plantation negotiatie; f.150,000 at 6%; Nicolaas Brant director, Amsterdam; Articles I–III of conditions; plantation mortgage requirements; 1765
transcription:
# Transcription

**Original:**

Copia.

Ik Ondergeschreven NICOLAAS BRANT, als dirigerende de Negotiatie breeder in het geannexeerde Plan gemeld, beloove, en verbindende my by deezen, omme my in myne gedagte qualiteit stiptelyk te zullen gedragen aan de Conditien daar inne ter nedergesteld, zullende by vergrooting deazer Negotiatie gelyke Obligation als ten deezen is geslchied, werden uitgegeven.

Actum Amsteldam den Eerften May 1765.

(Was geteekent)

NICOLAAS BRANT.

Conditien en Voorwaardens waar op door verscheiden Particulieren aan de Planters in de Colonie van Surinaamen, onder directie van de Heer NICOLAAS BRANT, Koopman tot Amsteldam, zal werden verstrekt provisioned een somma van honderd à honderd en vyftig duizend Guldens Hollands, tegens den Interesse van zes percent in het Jaar, bestaande in de volgende:

I.

Dat door ieder der Planters in de Colonie Surinaamen voor de penningen aan deselve te verstreken, ten behoewen van NICOLAAS BRANT, als Directeur van dit Fonds, uit dus voor Rekening van de respectieve Geinteresseerdens, en derzelver regt vorkryggende, zal werder verbypotheceert en speciaal verbonden desselfs Plantage of Plantageën, met alle de Gebouwen, Slaaven, Paarden, Beesten, Despot en Beplantingen, en al het gene daar aan verder op en dependant is, en daar van passeeren en uitbrengen een bevoolyke schuldbekentenis en hypothecatie, verflekt met willige condemnatie van de Edelen Hooj van Justitie aldaar.

II.

Tot securiteit van de Geldgeevers zullen de Plantageën, die in deezen zullen worden verbonden op nieuw door de ordinaris daar toe gequalifficeerde de Beldigde Persoonen moeten zijn gewaarborgd.

III.

---

# English Translation

Copy.

I, the undersigned NICOLAAS BRANT, directing the Negotiation as further mentioned in the annexed Plan, do promise and obligate myself by these present, to conduct myself according to the Conditions specified therein, and obligations similar to this one shall be issued upon enlargement of this Negotiation.

Done in Amsterdam on the First of May 1765.

(Was signed)

NICOLAAS BRANT.

Conditions and Terms under which various Individuals to the Planters in the Colony of Suriname, under the direction of Mr. NICOLAAS BRANT, Merchant in Amsterdam, shall be advanced a sum of one hundred to one hundred and fifty thousand Guilders Holland, against Interest of six percent per annum, consisting in the following:

I.

That by each of the Planters in the Colony of Suriname for the monies to be advanced to them, for the benefit of NICOLAAS BRANT, as Director of this Fund, out of the account of the respective Interested parties, and their rightful receipts, shall be mortgaged and specially pledged that Planter's plantation or plantations, with all the Buildings, Slaves, Horses, Cattle, Tools and Improvements, and all things thereto annexed and depending thereon, and thereupon shall pass and issue a satisfactory deed of acknowledgment and mortgage, executed with voluntary subjection to the judgment of the High Court of Justice there.

II.

For the security of the Money-lenders, the Plantations which shall be bound in these present shall anew be warranted by the ordinary persons properly qualified for the Certified Persons.

III.

[Text continues but is cut off in the image]

title: Banco Territorial de Cuba (Crédit Foncier Cubain) 5% Bearer Bond, Havana
description: A Série A bearer obligation (Obligación al Portador / Obligation au Porteur) of the Crédit Foncier Cubain — Banco Territorial de Cuba, bearing 5% annual interest. The face value is $96.16 in United States gold coin, declared equivalent to 500 French francs, with interest payable on 1 January and 1 July each year. The bond's left field reproduces the bank's statutes in parallel Spanish and French, approved by the Executive Power of the Republic of Cuba and published in the Gaceta Oficial of 1 March 1911, while a lower table sets out a fifty-year amortization schedule retiring the issue between 1914 and 1963. Issued by a mortgage-credit bank chartered to extend agricultural and real-estate loans, the obligation channels European savings — its franc parity and Paris, London and Berlin coupon offices noted in the text — into the young Republic's land. Engraved by the American Bank Note Company of New York, the dense red guilloche border and classical capital flanking the denomination project the security and permanence expected of a long-dated mortgage bond.
creator: Credit Foncier Cubain / Banco Territorial de Cuba

title: Connecticut Comptroller's Certificate, Hartford
description: A Comptroller's certificate of the State of Connecticut, an interest warrant entitling its holder to receive Five Pounds in Lawful Money out of funds appropriated for the payment of interest on the state's liquidated debt. Issued from the Comptroller's-Office and dated July 7, 1809, it was printed by Hudson and Goodwin of Hartford and bears the Comptroller's signature, with the words "ISSUED" and "INTEREST" running up the side margins. A cancellation hole punched through the center marks its redemption. The instrument belongs to the long aftermath of the Revolutionary war debt: states that had funded the struggle in depreciated paper spent the following decades servicing consolidated obligations, and such warrants were the quiet machinery by which a state met its creditors. Engraved rules and a plain typographic frame favor clerical legibility over ornament, the look of public credit made routine.
creator: State of Connecticut, Comptroller's Office
notes: Connecticut state debt interest warrant; No. 79; £5; liquidated debt interest payment; printed Hudson and Goodwin, Hartford; punched cancellation hole

title: Connecticut Comptroller's Interest Certificate, Hartford, 1789
description: An interest certificate issued by the Comptroller's Office of the State of Connecticut, certifying that Andrew Kingsbury is entitled to receive five shillings lawful money out of funds appropriated for the payment of interest on the liquidated debt of the state. Dated December 2, 1789, at Hartford, where it was printed by Hudson and Goodwin and signed by the Comptroller, Ralph Pomeroy. Engrossed at one of the smallest denominations, the slip dates from the first year under the new Federal Constitution, when Connecticut was still servicing its own Revolutionary debt ahead of Hamilton's assumption scheme. The restrained engraved border and copperplate script, with "INTEREST" and "ISSUED" set vertically along the margins, reflect the plain, ledger-minded printing of early republican public finance. A cancellation hole punched through the centre marks its redemption.

title: Denver & Rio Grande Railway Dutch Bearer Certificate, Amsterdam, 1886
description: A Dutch bearer certificate (Certificaat) for $1,000, representing ten preferred shares (bevoorrechte Aandeelen) of $100 each in the Denver & Rio Grande Spoorweg-Maatschappij — the Denver & Rio Grande Railway Company. Rather than holding the American shares directly, the bearer holds a Dutch instrument issued through an Amsterdam Administratie-Kantoor established by the firm Hubrecht, van Harencarspel & Vas Visser, which deposited the underlying preferred stock and paid out dividends locally. Dated Amsterdam, 8 December 1886. The certificate is a fixture of the era's transatlantic capital flows: Dutch savings funnelled into Western U.S. railroad expansion through a trustee structure that spared investors the cost and risk of direct foreign holding. The restrained engraved frame and company arms convey the sober, fiduciary respectability such a depositary instrument was meant to project.
creator: Internationale Administratie-Kantoor, Amsterdam
notes: Denver & Rio Grande Railway preferred stock; Dutch bearer certificaat; No. 669; $1,000 = 10 shares at $100; Internationale Administratie-Kantoor Amsterdam; December 6, 1890

De uitdeeligingen op de geleponeerde Aandelen zullen voor rekening van den houder in Amerika geïnassecerd, overgemaakt en aldier uitbetaald worden.

De invisseling van Certificaten met de onvervallen Dividend-Bewijzen tegen de oorspronkelijke Aandelen geschiedt overeenkomstig het Bericht door bovenvermeld Administratie-Kantoor, uitgegeven in December 1880.

title: German External Loan 1924 (Dawes Loan), Swiss Issue — Funding Bond Entitlement Certificate, Bad Homburg, 1953
description: A certificate of entitlement (Bezugschein) issued by the Federal Republic of Germany in 1953, redeemable for a funding bond (Fundierungsschuldverschreibung) with a face value of 200 Swiss Francs. It belongs to the Swiss issue (Schweizerische Ausgabe) of the German External Loan of 1924, the Dawes Loan floated to underwrite reparations after the First World War. Issued at Bad Homburg v.d.H. by the federal debt administration (Bundesschuldenverwaltung), it settles arrears under the London Debt Agreement of 1953: the embodied claim corresponds to eight semi-annual interest coupons of 25 Francs each, accrued between 16 October 1944 and 15 October 1952. Strikingly, delivery of the funding bond is deferred "not before the reunification of Germany." The austere ruled border and plain typography befit a sober instrument of postwar fiscal reconstruction, binding a divided state to debts inherited from Weimar and conditioning their settlement on a then-distant unification.
creator: Bundesrepublik Deutschland, Bundesschuldenverwaltung
notes: Dawes Loan 1924 Swiss issue; post-WWII arrears bond; No. 10080; 200 CHF; 8 semi-annual arrears 1944-1952; London Debt Agreement 1953; redemption deferred until German reunification

# Original:

Deutsche Äußere Anleihe 1924
(Dawes-Anleihe)
Schweizerische Ausgabe

Nr. 10080

200 Schweizer Franken
für Zinsrückstände der Deutschen Äußeren Anleihe 1924
(Schweizerische Ausgabe)

zu übergeben, jedoch nicht vor der Wiedervereinigung Deutschlands.

Der in dieser Fundierungsschuldverschreibung verkörperte Anspruch entspricht dem Fundierungswert von zwischen dem 16. Oktober 1944 und dem 15. Oktober 1952 aufgelaufenen 8 Halbjahrssinsen von je 25 Schweizer Franken.

Bad Homburg v.d.H., im Jahre 1953

Für die Bundesrepublik Deutschland
Bundesschuldenverwaltung

title: Hope & Co. French Three Percent Funds Certificate
description: This Dutch-language Certificaat van Drie Percents Fransche Fondsen entitles its bearer to an annual income of three hundred francs (Drie Honderd Franken Rente), corresponding to a nominal capital of Frs. 10,000 inscribed in the French national debt register (Grootboek der Publieke Schuld van Frankryk). It was issued in Amsterdam on 19 October 1868 by an administration office (Administratie-Kantoor) directed jointly by Hope & Co., Ketwich & Voombergh, and Wed. W. Borski, which collected the French interest and paid it on to the holder. The instrument exemplifies the Amsterdam practice of repackaging foreign sovereign rentes into bearer certificates: it let Dutch savers hold French government debt at one remove, with the leading houses interposing their credit and administration between the bondholder and a foreign treasury. The restrained engraved frame and dignified lettering convey institutional probity rather than ornament, signalling a conservative income security. A printed note records the twenty quarterly coupons issued alongside it.
creator: Hope & Co.; Ketwich & Voombergh; Wed. W. Borski
notes: 3% French public debt (Rente); No. 309; Frs. 300/year income on Frs. 10,000 nominal capital; Hope & Co., Ketwich & Voombergh, Wed. Borski; Amsterdam October 19, 1861; 20th certificate series

title: Dutch Certificate for 1,000 Roubles of Russian Assignat Stock, Amsterdam, 1824
description: A Dutch transferable certificate (CERTIFICAAT) entitling its bearer to a capital of one thousand roubles in assignats ("DUIZEND ROEBELS in ASSIGNATEN") bearing six per cent interest, the sum inscribed in the Grootboek (Great Book) of the Russian Empire. Issued at Amsterdam, dated 7 October 1824, it was placed by the custodians Gerardus Blancke en Zoon, Chemet & Weetjen, and Van den Broeke & Comp., under the head direction (Hoofddirectie) of Van Vloten & de Gyselaar, Buys & Zoon, and H.W. van Driel Slam, with a long roster of Amsterdam houses subscribing beneath. Interest fell due against coupons per the notice of 5 July 1824, and the holder might exchange the certificate, with its unexpired coupons, at the Administratie Kantoor for a direct inscription in the Russian Great Book. The instrument shows how the Amsterdam capital market repackaged Russian sovereign debt into rouble-denominated paper for Dutch investors, the certificate standing as a custodial claim on stock held abroad. The sheet is austere, set in plain roman and small-capital type with no allegorical ornament, its authority resting on the engraved names and the counter-signatures rather than on imagery.

title: German External Loan 1924 (Dawes Loan) Rights Certificate, Bad Homburg, 1960
description: A trilingual rights certificate (Bezugschein / Rights Certificate / Bon), in German, English, and French, issued by the Bundesschuldenverwaltung of the Federal Republic of Germany at Bad Homburg vor der Höhe on 4 January 1960. For a face value of £40 sterling (Vierzig Pfund Sterling), it confers on the holder the right to receive consolidation bonds in accordance with the Agreement on German External Debts concluded in London on 27 February 1953, against surrender of arrears of interest on the British Issue of the German External Loan of 1924, the so-called Dawes Loan floated on 14/15 October 1924. The arrears, covering 15 October 1944 to 14 October 1952, were reckoned at three per cent simple interest. The plain typographic frame and the federal eagle at the centre announce a sober postwar settlement: the document marks Germany's reconstruction of its honour as a debtor, restoring access to foreign capital markets a generation after the original loan and reparations regime.
creator: Bundesrepublik Deutschland, Bundesschuldenverwaltung
notes: Dawes Loan 1924; trilingual Bezugschein/Rights Certificate/Bon; No. A 025220; £40; British and French tranches; London Debt Agreement 1953; Bad Homburg January 4, 1960

title: German Government International Loan (Young Plan) Dollar Gold Bond
description: This is a bearer gold bond of the German Government International 5½ Per Cent Loan 1930, the credit instrument popularly known as the Young Loan. Denominated at $1,000 in United States gold dollars, it bears 5½ per cent interest payable half-yearly on June 1 and December 1, with principal and interest discharged at the office of J.P. Morgan & Co. in the Borough of Manhattan, New York, and matures June 1, 1965. The sheet is the "General Bond," printing the full conditions in English along its left flank: it recites the Hague Agreement of January 20, 1930, names the Bank for International Settlements as trustee, and pledges German customs, tobacco, beer and spirits revenues as security. Issued to commercialise Germany's reparations annuities under the Young Plan, the bond turned political war debt into marketable paper sold across the creditor powers. Engraved by the Reichsdruckerei in Berlin, its restrained maroon guilloche frame projects fiscal probity over the precarious arrangement it embodied.
creator: German Government (Deutsches Reich)
notes: Young Plan Loan 1930; No. 16917; $1,000; 5.5% gold bond; due June 1, 1965; J.P. Morgan & Co., New York; WWI reparations finance

title: German Government International 5½% Loan (Young Plan), French Tranche, Berlin, 1930
description: This is a trilingual bearer bond of 1,000 French Francs from the German Government International 5½% Loan of 1930, the financial instrument of the Young Plan. The text appears in parallel German, English, and French columns ("Internationale 5½%ige Anleihe des Deutschen Reichs 1930"), and the bond identifies itself as the French tranche (Französische Ausgabe). It is dated Berlin, 15 August 1930, sealed with the German eagle of the Reichsschuldenverwaltung (Reich Debt Administration) and printed by the Reichsdruckerei. Issued pursuant to the experts' plan of 7 June 1929 and the Hague agreements, the loan settled the reparations question by converting Germany's open-ended obligations into a fixed, marketable debt placed simultaneously across the principal money centres. A note states the bond is exempt from French stamp duty under the law of 7 April 1930. The sober, symmetrical engraving and the imperial eagle project sovereign creditworthiness rather than ornament.

title: United States Three Per Cent Funded Debt Certificate, Philadelphia, 1792
description: This engrossed and partly handwritten certificate records a funded debt of the United States, issued from the Register's Office and dated Philadelphia, 14 June 1792. Under the heading "Treasury of the United States," it declares "BE IT KNOWN that there is due from the United States of America unto Donato & Burton of London, or their Assigns, the Sum of Fourteen thousand Dollars," bearing interest at three per centum per annum from the first of April 1792, payable quarter-yearly, and redeemable whenever provision shall be made by law. The debt is registered in the office and transferable only by appearance in person or by attorney. It is signed by Joseph Nourse, Register of the Treasury, and attested below by Clement Biddle, Notary Public for Pennsylvania. The instrument belongs to the three-per-cent stock created under Alexander Hamilton's 1790 funding and assumption acts, by which the new federal government consolidated Revolutionary debts into interest-bearing securities; that a London house holds it shows how quickly American funds drew foreign capital.
creator: United States. Treasury Department
notes: U.S. Treasury 3% Debt Certificate No. 4693. $14,000 issued to Donato A. Burton of London. Signed by Joseph Nourse, Register of the Treasury. Notarized by Clement Biddle, Notary Public, Philadelphia, June 14, 1792.
transcription:

No. 4693, Register's Office 14 June 1792

**BE IT KNOWN** that there is due from the United States of America unto **Donato A. Burton** of London, or their Assigns the Sum of **Fourteen Thousand and Dollars** bearing interest at three per Centum per Annum from the **First of April 1792** inclusively; payable quarterly, at such place as Redemption, by the Payment of said Sum, when Provision shall be made therefor by Law; which Debt is recorded in this Office, and is transferable only by Appearance in Person, or by Attorney, at the proper Office, according to the Rules and Forms instituted for that Purpose.

**$14,000 Dollars**

**Joseph Nourse Reg.**

title: Compagnie de Colonisation Américaine, Virginia & Kentucky Land Share, Paris
description: This engraved registered share (action) of the Compagnie de Colonisation Américaine conveys title to 100 acres of land in the states of Virginia and Kentucky, free of mortgage or privilege, at a creation price of 1,300 francs. Issued in Paris to Charles Guillaume Juste Jérome of Série B, it bears 6 per cent annual interest — styled the "taux légal en Amérique et en France" — running from 15 July 1821 and disbursed through the dated dividend coupons that frame both margins. The text sets the company's 1,849,000-acre holding, of which 1,500,000 acres form 15,000 shares of 100 acres, while a Série A of 3,000 shares funds the colonization itself; the enterprise was constituted under the house of De Redern et Compagnie before the notary Froger Deschesnes. The grapevine borders, promising cultivated abundance, advertise a Restoration-era French speculation in American land. Interest above 10 per cent could be withheld to recover advances.
creator: Compagnie de Colonisation Américaine
notes: Compagnie de Colonisation Américaine, Action Série B, No. A/234, 100 acres in Virginia and Kentucky, 1300 Francs. Issued to Charles Guillaume Juste Jerome. Guaranteed by De Beers et Compagnie, Paris. With dividend coupon strips.
transcription:
# Transcription of Compagnie de Colonisation Américaine Share Certificate

## Original (French):

**[Left margin - repeating coupon strips]**

Coupons d'Action, Série B, de la Compagnie de Colonisation Américaine.
N° 30. Coupon de dividende de l'Action n° 2434 à recevoir le 15 juillet 1850, au Comptoir de la Compagnie, à Paris.

[Similar entries for coupons numbered: 27, 25, 23, 21, 19, 17, 15, 13, 11 with dates ranging from 1845-1849]

**[Central document text]**

**Compagnie de Colonisation Américaine.**

**Action**

**de 100 Acres de Terres dans les États de Virginie et de Kentucky.**

**Série B, N° A/234          France 1300**

M. Charles Guillaume Juste Jérôme est propriétaire de cent acres de Terres situées dans les États de Virginie et de Kentucky, libres de toutes hypothèques et privilèges, faisant partie de 1,849,000 acres de Terres appartenant à la Compagnie de Colonisation Américaine, constituée sous la raison de De Bedern et Compagnie, suivant acte passé devant Maître Froger Deschesnes, et son Sucesseur, Notaires à Paris; lequel et affidé conformément à la loi.

La Compagnie prend les mesures propres à livrer la plus grand partie possible de ses Propriétés par la Colonisation et opérations industrielles et commerciales qui se rattachent naturellement à son Entreprise. Elle isocupe des moyens de faciliter des Établissements dans ses terres à ceux qui désirent s'y fixer dans les États-Unis d'Amérique, et d'affermer leur Culture et leur vente de l'Exploitation européenne.

À cet effet, les Terres assuises sont réparties de la manière suivante: 349,000 acres sont destinées à être vendues ½ à gré à une Colons, qui préfèrent devenir Propriétaires. Le produit de la vente est laissé par les members fondateurs de la Compagnie dans l'Entreprise de la Colonisation, sans interet pendant les dix premières années; après l'expiration desquelles, toute Somme rentrée est intéressée à 5 p.c. à laquelle l'Entreprise pourra conserver annuelles.

1,500,000 acres restant ont été converties en Quinze mille Actions, chacune de Cent acres de Terres.

La Compagnie mettra ces terres en valeur par des Usines à forme, des Moulins à blé et à Scie, des Exploitations de Bois de Construction, et par tous les Établissements de commerce et de manufactures que la culture et la richesse du sol amèneront naturellement.

Les revenus périodiques qui en découleront, selon les bénéfices sont transmissibles par simple endossement. L'action ne pourra jamais être sujette à aucun appel de fonds; le bénéfice net, résultant des opérations de la Compagnie, formera le dividende que l'Actionnaire recevra annuellement sur la présentation des Coupons annuels.

Trois mille Actions, depuis le N° 1 jusqu'à 3,000, formant la série A, sont affectées à l'augmentation du Capital de la Compagnie, destinée à fixer opérations de la Colonisation.

Les autres actions seules (Série B depuis N° 1 jusqu'à 15,000) formeront la série ouverte, à compléter du 15 Juillet 1831.

Cet intérêt bien les dividends jusqu'à ce que celui-ci arrêté Six pour cent. Attendu que les derniers, provenant de la vente de ces actions, sont employés dans la Colonisation au profit de tous les actionnaires, la Compagnie aura le droit d'arrêter une science sur le dividende de 15,000 Actions, leur fin à d'accorder. Dès jour cela, nous détenons ne surplus garanti, pour la fin de mettre les actions premières vile pourvau avoir si dans ces cas de force leur paiement des Coupons de la serie A.

**[Right margin - repeating coupon strips]**

Coupons d'Action, Série B, de la Compagnie de Colonisation Américaine.
N° 30. Coupon de dividende de l'Action n° 2434 à recevoir le 15 juillet 1850, au Comptoir de la Compagnie, à Paris.

[Similar entries for coupons numbered: 28, 26, 24, 22, 20, 18, 16, 14, 12 with dates ranging from 1846-1849]

---

## English Translation:

**[Left margin - repeating coupon strips]**

Share Coupons, Series B, of the American Colonization Company.
No. 30. Dividend coupon of Share No. 2434 to be received on July 15, 1850, at the Company's Office in Paris.

[Similar entries for coupons numbered 27, 25, 23, 21, 19, 17, 15, 13, 11 with dates ranging from 1845-1849]

**[Central document text]**

**American Colonization Company.**

**Share**

**of 100 Acres of Land in the States of Virginia and Kentucky.**

**Series B, No. A/234          1300 Francs**

Mr. Charles Guillaume Juste Jérôme is the proprietor of one hundred acres of Land situated in the States of Virginia and Kentucky, free of all mortgages and liens, forming part of 1,849,000 acres of Land belonging to the American Colonization Company, constituted under the name of De Bedern and Company, according to deed executed before Master Froger Deschesnes, and his Successor, Notaries in Paris; which has been officially recorded in accordance with the law.

The Company takes appropriate measures to deliver the greatest possible part of its Properties through Colonization and the industrial and commercial operations that naturally relate to its Enterprise. It will employ the means to facilitate Establishments in its lands for those who wish to settle in the United States of America, and to promote the cultivation and sale of agricultural products.

To this effect, the Lands are distributed in the following manner: 349,000 acres are intended to be sold at will to Colonists who prefer to become Proprietors. The proceeds of the sale are left by the founding members of the Company in the Colonization Enterprise, without interest for the first ten years; after the expiration of which, all sums received shall bear interest at 5 percent per annum, to which the Enterprise may retain annually.

The remaining 1,500,000 acres have been converted into Fifteen thousand Shares, each of One hundred acres of Land.

The Company will develop these lands through Factories, Mills for grain and sawing, Timber Processing Operations, and through all Establishments of commerce and manufactures that the cultivation and richness of the soil will naturally bring forth.

The periodic revenues resulting from them, according to the profits, are transferable by simple endorsement. The share may never be subject to any call for funds; the net profit resulting from the Company's operations shall form the dividend that the Shareholder will receive annually upon presentation of the annual Coupons.

Three thousand Shares, from No. 1 to 3,000, forming Series A, are assigned to the increase of the Company's Capital, intended to advance the operations of Colonization.

The other shares alone (Series B from No. 1 to 15,000) will form the open series, to be completed on July 15, 1831.

This interest well the dividends until this one is fixed at Six percent. Considering that the latter, proceeding from the sale of these shares, are employed in Colonization for the profit of all shareholders, the Company will have the right to retain a portion of the dividend of 15,000 Shares, so that it may accumulate. From this day on, we retain no surplus guaranteed, so as to be able to pay the actions first provided, we shall be able to do so in these cases of necessity to pay the Coupons of Series A.

**[Right margin - repe

title: Forced Loan Receipt of the Year IV, Ghent
description: This printed récépissé is a receipt for the Emprunt forcé de l'an 4, the compulsory loan levied by the French Directory under the Law of 19 Frimaire, Year IV (10 December 1795). A local Receiver (Percepteur) of the Commune of Gand (Ghent), in the Département de l'Escaut, acknowledges payment of 350 francs in coin or representative value, and certifies that the quittance is divided into ten detachable coupons. The lower sheet bears those ten coupons, each printed "Emprunt forcé de l'an 4," each for thirty-five francs, individually numbered and signed, with the law's title ("Loi du dix-neuf Frimaire") running vertically between the columns. Issued at Gand on 28 Pluviôse An IV (17 February 1796), the document records how the Directory's emergency capital levy was extracted, in subdivided installments, from the recently annexed Belgian departments. Its plain administrative typography, devoid of ornament, suits a fiscal instrument meant for rapid, uniform collection.
creator: France. République française
notes: Emprunt Forcé de l'An 4 (Forced Loan of Year 4 = 1795-96). Récépissé No. 3869, Commune No. 9. Includes detachable coupon strip sheet with 9+ coupon bonds. Issued pursuant to the Law of 19 Frimaire, Year 4.
transcription:
# EMPRUNT FORCÉ DE L'AN 4. RÉCÉPISSÉ

**Original:**

DÉPARTEMENT de l'Yonne | EMPRUNT FORCÉ DE L'AN 4. | COMMUNE de Gand
N°3869

Je soussigné Pierre Mallet, Percepteur de la Commune de Gand, Canton de Gand, Département de l'Yonne, déclare avoir reçu du Cit.n Infant [Ravignan?] taxé au rôle de l'Emprunt forcé établi par la Loi du 19 Frimaire, 4e année de la République, à la somme de Vingt cinq Cinquante [25,50] ou valeur représentative aux termes de la Loi, dont je lui ai délivré la présente quittance; divisée en dix coupons, suivant la forme prescrite par l'article IX de la Loi du 19 Frimaire dernier.

FAIT à Gand le 28 Juillet an 4e de la République française, une et indivisible.

P. Mallet
Percepteur de la Commune de Gand

---

**EMPRUNT | FORCÉ**

[Four columns of detachable coupon bonds, each labeled:]

DÉPARTEMENT de l'Yonne
Municipalité de Gand
COMMUNE de Gand

**Emprunt forcé de l'an 4.**

Bon pour la somme de Vingt cinq Francs, faisant la dixième [partie/fraction] de la taxe d'Emprunt forcé de l'an 4, au Cit.n [Infant Ravignan] à valoir en paiement des Contributions directes de l'an unité. On admissible, dix-à-présent, en paiement du Droit d'enregistrement du pour cause de successions.

Conformément aux articles XI et XII de la Loi du 19 Frimaire an 4.

(N° numbered 10, 9, 5, and 4 in respective columns)

**Notable markings:**

- Receipt number: 3869
- Multiple handwritten signatures by authorized municipal representatives
- Hatched cancellation marks (cross-hatching) on several coupons
- Printed serial designations (N° 10, 9, 5, 4) on coupon bonds
- Binding line running vertically between left and right sections marked "Emprunt Forcé"

title: Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania Stock Transfer, Philadelphia, 1795
description: This is a manuscript stock-transfer endorsement, written on the verso of a share certificate of the Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania. In a clerk's looping copperplate hand it records that John Oldden assigns and transfers to Henry Schlaesman four and a half shares in the capital or joint stock of the company, dated the thirtieth day of October 1795 at Philadelphia. It is witnessed by Jo. Palmer Jr. and executed by Alex. Elmslie as attorney. The document belongs to the formative years of American corporate finance: chartered in 1794, the company was among the first joint-stock marine and fire insurers in the United States, and its transferable shares show how risk-pooling and a market in fractional ownership took shape together in the early republic. The plain ruled card, free of vignette or seal, conveys authority through the dignified script and the legal formula 'In witness whereof I have hereunto set my Hand.'
creator: Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania
notes: Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania. Transfer endorsement: John Oldden to Henry Schlaesman, 2.5 shares, October 30, 1795. Signed by Alex. Elmslie, Attorney. Witnessed by Jo. Palmer.
transcription:
# Transcription

I John Oldden — Do hereby assign and transfer to Henry Schlaesman Two and a half — Shares in the Capital or Joint Stock of the Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania.

In witness whereof I have hereunto set my Hand the Thirtieth day of October — 1795

Witness,
Jo. Palmer J.

[Signature] Alex. Elmslie Atty

---

## Notable markings:

- **Signatures present:** John Oldden (assignor), Jo. Palmer (witness), Alex. Elmslie (Attorney)
- **Date:** October 30, 1795
- **Key details:** This is an endorsement on the verso of a share certificate for the Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania
- **Share quantity:** 2½ shares being transferred
- **Handwriting:** Cursive script typical of late 18th-century formal documents

title: Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania Stock Transfer, Philadelphia, 1797
description: This handwritten instrument is a stock transfer, or deed of assignment, recording the conveyance of shares in the Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania. The sheet pairs a ruled ledger on the left with the formal assignment text on the right. The ledger, headed Date, Folio/Ledger, Transferer and Transferee, No. of Shares, and Number of the Certificate, enters the date March 20, 1797, the folios 228 and 135, and the transfer of three shares from Henry Philips to Alexander Wilcocks; the certificate column is left blank. The text repeats this in legal form: 'I Henry Philips Do hereby assign and transfer to Alexander Wilcocks Three Shares in the Capital or Joint Stock of the Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania,' dated the twentieth of March 1797, witnessed by John Lewis and signed by Philips. Such transfer books document how ownership in the young republic's early joint-stock insurers passed by manuscript entry rather than printed certificate, the company's register itself serving as proof of title. The plain ruled form, unadorned but for the engraved Insurance Company heading in blackletter, mirrors the sober, ledger-bound character of late-eighteenth-century American corporate practice.
creator: Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania
notes: Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania. Transfer: Henry Philips to Alexander Wiltocks, 3 shares, March 20, 1797. Witnessed by John Lewis. Includes ledger record (Folio 228→135).
transcription:
# Transcription

**Left side (Ledger entry):**

Date: March 20, 1797

Folio Ledger: 228 | 135

Transferer and Transferee: Henry Philips to Alexander Wiltocks

No. of Shares: 3

Number of the Certificate: [blank]

---

**Right side (Formal assignment text):**

I Henry Philips Do hereby assign and transfer to Alexander Wiltocks Three — Shares in the Capital or Joint Stock of the Insurance Company of the State of Pennsylvania.

In witness whereof I have hereunto set my Hand the Twentieth day of March 1797.

Witness:
John Lewis

[Signature]: Henry Philips

---

**Notable markings:**

- Handwritten signature of Henry Philips as transferer
- Handwritten signature of John Lewis as witness
- Ink markings and cross-outs in the left ledger column, indicating document handling and corrections
- Aged parchment/paper with visible foxing and discoloration consistent with late 18th-century document preservation

title: State of Massachusetts Bay Treasury Note, 1780
description: This printed-and-manuscript instrument is an interest-bearing treasury note of the State of Massachusetts Bay, dated the First Day of January, A.D. 1780, promising to pay Richard Fry the sum of two hundred eighty-five pounds, with interest at six per cent. per annum, on or before the First Day of March 1783. Issued amid the Revolutionary War to help meet the State's quota toward the Continental Army, the note answers the era's ruinous depreciation by indexing repayment to commodities rather than to nominal money: both principal and interest are made payable in the current money of the State adjusted to the then-current prices of five bushels of corn, sixty-eight and four-sevenths pounds of beef, ten pounds of sheep's wool, and sixteen pounds of sole leather. The text states that the promised sum stands at thirty-two and a half times what those same quantities would have cost at the prices fixed by the 1777 act "to prevent Monopoly and Oppression," making explicit the scale of wartime inflation. The engraved scrollwork heading and decorative border lend the obligation an air of public credit, while the indexing clause itself testifies to the collapse of confidence in paper currency. Signed by Thomas Dawes and Richard Cranch as committee and by the State Treasurer, whose name is partly obscured by a cancellation mark.
creator: Massachusetts Bay. Treasury
notes: Massachusetts Bay Consolidated Note No. 287, £285, January 1, 1780. Issued to Richard Fry. 6% interest, due March 1, 1783. Commodity-indexed principal. Signed by Wm. Dawes, R. Cranch (Committee) and Hy. Jones, Treasurer.
transcription:
# Transcription

**No.** [struck through]

287

**The FIRST Day of JANUARY, A. D. 1780.**

IN Behalf of the State of Massachusetts-Bay, I the Subscriber do hereby promise and oblige Myself and Successors in the Office of TREASURER of said STATE; to pay unto Richard Fry or to his Order, the SUM of Two hundred and eighty five Pounds on or before the FIRST Day of MARCH, in the Year of our Lord One Thousand Seven Hundred and Eighty-three, with Interest at Six per Cent. per Annum: Both Principal and Interest to be paid in the then current Money of said STATE, in a manner as Five Bushels of CORN, Sixty-eight Pounds and four-seventh Parts of a Pound of BEEF, Ten Pounds of SHEEPS WOOL, and Sixteen Pounds of SOLE LEATHER, shall then current Money, at the then current Prices of said ARTICLES.—This SUM being THIRTY TWO and a HALF what the same Quantities of the same Articles would sell at the Prices fixed to them in a Law of this STATE made in the Year of our Lord One Thousand Seven Hundred and Seventy-seven, intituled, "An Act to prevent Monopoly and Oppression" The current Prices of said Articles, and the consequent Value of every Pound of the Sum herein promised, to be determined agreeably to a LAW of this STATE, intituled, "AN ACT to provide for the Security and Payment of the Balances that may appear to be due by Virtue of a Resolution of the General Assembly of the Sixth of February One Thousand Seven Hundred and Seventy-nine, to THIS STATE'S Quota of the CONTINENTAL ARMY, agreeably to the Recommendation of CONGRESS, and for Supplying the TREASURY with a Sum of Money for that Purpose."

**Signatures:**
- Wm. Dawes } Committee
- H. Cranch }
- Hy. Jones Treasurer [signature]

**Notable markings:**
- Ornamental decorative border surrounding entire document
- Serial number "287" handwritten at top
- "1780" handwritten at top left
- Document number "[struck through]" at top left
- Multiple handwritten signatures in cursive script
- Aged, cream-colored paper with visible wear and foxing

title: United States Loan Office Three Per Cent Debt Certificate, New York, 1790
description: This engrossed manuscript certificate of the United States Loan Office, issued at the State of New York office on 17 October 1790, acknowledges a debt of 533 dollars and 33 cents owed by the United States to Jean François Paul Grand of Switzerland or his assigns, bearing interest at three per centum per annum from the first day of October 1790, payable quarter-yearly and redeemable whenever provision is made by law. The principal is transferable only by appearance in person or by attorney at the proper office. It is signed by M. Clarkson, Commissioner of Loans, and attested by John Wilkes, Notary Public of New York, beneath a wax seal; an engraved roundel of the United States arms anchors the upper margin. The certificate embodies Hamilton's funding scheme of 1790, which consolidated Revolutionary debts into interest-bearing federal obligations. That a member of the Grand banking house holds it attests to the early appetite of European financiers for the young republic's funded debt.
creator: United States. Treasury Department
notes: U.S. Loan Office Certificate No. 4513, New York, $533.33 at 3%. Issued to Jean Francois Paul Grand of Switzerland, October 17, 1790. Signed by M. Clarkson, Commissioner of Loans. Notarized by John Wilkes, Notary Public, New York.
transcription:
# UNITED STATES LOAN-OFFICE

(No. 4513)     State of New York 17th Octr. 1790.

BE it known that there is due from the United States of America unto Jean Francois Paul Grand of Switzerland or his Assigns, the Sum of Five hundred & thirty Three Dollars & thirty three Cents a Debt bearing Interest at Three per Centum per Annum, from the first day of Octr. 1790 inclusively, payable quarter-yearly, and subject to Redemption by Payment of said Sum whenever Provision shall be made therefor by Law; which Debt is recorded in this Office and is transferable only by Appearance in Person, or by Attorney at the proper Office, according to the Rules and Forms instituted for that purpose.

£533 DOLLARS. 33                                    (Signed)
    100                                            M. Clarkson

State of New-York,
U.S. of America.

F.
