title: French Royal Treasury Receipt
description: A handwritten quittance on a printed form, this receipt records a transaction in French royal credit. Jean de Turmenyes, Conseiller du Roi and Garde du Trésor Royal, acknowledges receiving 8,000 livres in gold louis, silver, and other coin as the principal for 400 livres of annual and perpetual rente, computed at the denier vingt (one-twentieth, or five percent). The rente is to be constituted by the Prévôt des Marchands et Échevins of Paris upon the one million livres of perpetual rente newly alienated by the King under the Édit of March 1698, secured on the revenues of the Aides et Gabelles. Dated at Paris, 20 March 1698. The instrument shows how the crown, pressed by the costs of the Nine Years' War, borrowed through the municipality of Paris, whose civic credit was lent to the throne. The austere engraved form, all text and almost no ornament, befits a fiscal mechanism that ran on signatures and ledgers rather than display.
creator: France. Trésor Royal
notes: Quittance du Garde du Trésor Royal, No. 391, April 10, 1698. 8,000 livres principal for 400 livres of annual rente on the Hôtel de Ville de Paris (Aides et Gabelles). Issued to Jean Eloy Udtin. Signed by Jean de Turmenyes, Garde du Trésor Royal. Pursuant to Edit of March 1698.
transcription:
# Document Transcription

**Original (French):**

N° 391                                    DENIER 20. Mars 1698

JE JEAN DE TURMENYES, Conseiller du Roy en fes Confeils, Garde de fon Trefor Royal, Confeile avoir receu comptant en cette Ville de Paris, de Jean Eloy Udtin [argentier de Monsieur l'Archevêque de Rouen]

[several lines with handwritten corrections and annotations]

la Somme de huil mille livres en Louis d'or, d'argent, & autre Monnoye pour le principal de quatre cens livres de Rente qu'il ly feront vendus & constituez par Messieurs; les Prevost des Marchands & Efchevins de ladite Ville de Paris fur le Million de livres auquels & effectifs de rente annuelle & perpetuelle à eux nouvellemnt alienez par Sa Majefté en confequence de fon Edit du mois de Mars 1698. regiftré où befoin a été, à prendre fur les droits d'Aydes & Gabelles de France, pour jouïr par ledit Udtin à raifon de Denier Vingt, ainfi qu'il fera plus au long expliqué par le Contrat de confti tution qui luy fera expedié de ladite Rente par lesdits Sieurs Prevost des Marchands & Efchevins, en execution dudit Edit. De laquelle Somme de huit mille livres

à moy ordonnée pour employer au fait de ma Charge, je quitte lesdits Sieurs & tous autres. A Paris le [date]

jour d'[illegible] mil fix cent quatre-vingt-dix-huit.

Quittance du Garde du Trésor Royal, Année mil fix cent quatre-vingt-dix-huit.

[Signature: de Turmenyes]

M. Sougis                N.e

---

**English Translation:**

I, JEAN DE TURMENYES, Counselor to the King in his Councils, Keeper of his Royal Treasury, certify that I have received in cash in this City of Paris, from Jean Eloy Udtin [financial agent of the Archbishop of Rouen]

the Sum of eight thousand livres in gold louis, silver, and other coin for the principal of four hundred livres of annual perpetual rente which will be sold and constituted to him by the Messieurs Provost of Merchants and Aldermen of the said City of Paris on the Million livres of annual and perpetual rente newly alienated to them by His Majesty in consequence of his Edit of the month of March 1698, registered where necessary has been, to be drawn from the rights of Aides and Gabelles of France, for the enjoyment by the said Udtin at the rate of Denier Vingt, as will be more fully explained by the Contract of constitution which will be issued to him for said Rente by the said Messieurs Provost of Merchants and Aldermen, in execution of said Edit. From which Sum of eight thousand livres

ordered to me for employment in the discharge of my office, I acquit the said Messieurs and all others. At Paris on [date]

day of [illegible] thousand six hundred ninety-eight.

Receipt of the Keeper of the Royal Treasury, Year thousand six hundred ninety-eight.

---

**Notable markings:**

- Document number: N° 391 (upper left)
- Date notation: "DENIER 20. Mars 1698" (upper right)
- Signature: "de Turmenyes" (cursive, center-right)
- Multiple handwritten corrections and annotations in the margins and between lines
- Names at bottom: "M. Sougis" and "N.e"
- Several passages marked with strikethrough or revision marks

title: Piscataqua Bridge Corporation Share Certificate
description: This part-printed, part-manuscript certificate records a single transferable share in the Piscataqua Bridge, issued by its proprietary corporation and held here by the Town of Portsmouth in the County of Rockingham, New Hampshire. Dated the seventh day of December 1793, it is authenticated under the corporation's seal and signed by James Sheafe as President, a Treasurer, and Nathaniel Adams as Proprietors' Clerk, with the share declared transferable by assignment recorded on the Proprietors' Records. The document belongs to the first wave of American chartered toll-bridge companies, which spanned the Piscataqua River between Portsmouth and Kittery and raised capital from towns and private subscribers through joint-stock shares. Plain and text-dominated, without ornament, the certificate's authority rests on the affixed seal and officers' hands rather than engraved imagery, reflecting the early republic's reliance on transferable proprietary shares to fund public works.
creator: Piscataqua Bridge Corporation
notes: Piscataqua Bridge Corporation, Share No. 262, Town of Portsmouth, Rockingham County, New Hampshire, December 7, 1793. President: James Sheafe. Proprietors' Clerk: Nathaniel Adams. Early American toll bridge infrastructure share.
transcription:
# Transcription

**No.262**

**Town of Portsmouth**

---

BE IT KNOWN, That the Town of Portsmouth in the County of Rockingham in the State of New Hampshire is the Proprietor of Share Number Two hundred sixty two in the Piscataqua Bridge, which Share is transferable by making an assignment on the back of this Certificate, and causing the fame to be recorded on the Proprietors' Records.

IN testimony whereof, the Seal of the Corporation is hereto affixed, the seventh day of December One thousand seven hundred and ninety three

James Sheafe President.

[illegible] Treasurer.

Attest,

Nathl. Adams Proprietors' Clerk.

---

## Notable markings:

- Document number: **No. 262** (handwritten, upper left)
- Date: **December 7, 1793** (handwritten in cursive)
- Three signatures in cursive handwriting: James Sheafe (President), a Treasurer (signature illegible), and Nathaniel Adams (Proprietors' Clerk)
- Large blank rectangular area (approximately 3" x 4") appears to be space for a seal or official stamp (seal not visible/impressed in this image)
- Document shows age-related discoloration and creasing consistent with an 18th-century paper document

title: Caisse d'Épargnes Lafarge Tontine Share, Paris, 1793
description: This is an action (share) of ninety livres issued by the Caisse d'Épargnes et de Bienfaisance Lafarge, Seconde Société, a Parisian savings-and-benevolence institution operating tontine-style life annuities. For the sum of ninety livres the subscribers, Paul Beautier and his wife Catherine Ferraudin, acquire a share whose rente and accroissements (the survivors' accruing gains) are established on the life of Catherine Ferraudin, up to a ceiling of three thousand livres of rente per annum, payable on production of the certificat de vie. It is dated at Paris, the thirtieth of September 1793, and signed by the Directeur Général and Inspecteur Général. Issued amid the Revolution's fiscal turmoil and assignat inflation, the share illustrates how private mutual schemes courted small savers with longevity-linked income. The engraved seal — a coffer with attendant figures beneath the legend "L'Économie est la Seconde Providence du Genre Humain" — moralises thrift as a providential virtue.

title: Chilian 5 Per Cent Loan Coupon Sheet (Rothschild, London)
description: A sheet of attached interest coupons from the Chilian 5 Per Cent Loan of 1896, a sterling external loan of the Republic of Chile. Each coupon promises £2.10.0 — six months' interest on a £100 bond at five per cent — payable at the office of Messrs. N.M. Rothschild & Sons, London, or at the exchange of the day in Santiago, Paris, Berlin, Hamburg, Amsterdam and Brussels, the network of houses through which Chile placed its debt in the European market. The surviving coupons run semi-annually from January 1938 to January 1946, the loan's later maturities. That a South American republic could borrow in London at five per cent, serviced through the Rothschilds and quoted across the continent's principal bourses, attests to Chile's standing as a reliable sovereign creditor in the age before the First World War. The reverse bears finely engraved guilloche rosettes and interlaced medallions, the security ornament of the period.
creator: Republic of Chile

title: Russian Provisional Government Liberty Loan Bond, Petrograd, 1917
description: This is a 5% bearer bond (облигація) of fifty rubles nominal, issued under the "Заемъ Свободы" — the Liberty Loan — of the Russian Provisional Government, marked IV series and dated Petrograd, 27 March 1917. Floated weeks after the February Revolution toppled the monarchy, the loan sought to fund a state in fiscal crisis and to bind the citizenry to the new order through patriotic subscription rather than coercion. The engraved text makes the appeal explicit: it addresses the "citizens of great free Russia," warns that a strong enemy threatens to break and return the country to its dead past, and frames lending as the fulfillment of duty owed to a homeland rebuilt on equality and truth. The central vignette of the domed State Duma (Государственная Дума) supplies the visual argument, casting the parliamentary chamber as the emblem of the freedom the loan was meant to defend. The minister-president and assembled ministers sign below.
creator: Russian Provisional Government
notes: Russian Freedom Loan (Заем Свободы), 5% bond, 50 rubles nominal, IV Series, No. 059857. Petrograd, March 27, 1917. Provisional Government. Same issue as goetzmann0677; lower denomination. Handwritten "½ of 50R" annotation at top.

title: Republic of Texas Consolidated Fund Stock Certificate
description: This is a $500 certificate of stock in the Ten per Cent Consolidated Fund of the Republic of Texas, created by Act of February 5th, 1840, and issued to Edwards Hall. The principal bears interest at ten per cent per annum, payable semi-annually upon presentation of the certificate and delivery of the several interest warrants at the Treasury Department in gold or silver; the principal is redeemable at the pleasure of the government after the 30th day of June 1845, and the stock is transferable by simple endorsement. It was issued at Austin on 15 June 1840 over the signature of the Comptroller, with ten interest coupons attached below. The instrument records the young Republic's effort to fund its chronic deficits by consolidating debt into an interest-bearing fund, a recurring expedient of a treasury starved of specie. Neoclassical engraved vignettes carry the message: a maritime scene of a youth and a winged-capped figure of commerce gazing toward ships, and at left Hebe offering a cup to an eagle, allegories of trade and aspiration befitting a fledgling state seeking credit.
creator: Republic of Texas / Treasury Department
notes: Republic of Texas, Certificate of Stock in the Ten Per Cent Consolidated Fund, $500, issued to Edward Hall. Created by Act of Feb. 5, 1840. Austin, June 18, 1840. Signed by Comptroller. Ten interest coupons attached below.

title: Mississippi State Bonds Deposit Certificate
description: This is a deposit certificate, not a bond itself, issued by the Corporation of Foreign Bondholders at 17 Moorgate, London, E.C.2, and dated 21 December 1925. It acknowledges that Mississippi State Bonds to the face value of ten thousand dollars ($10,000) — part of the 5 per cent issue made by the State of Mississippi to the Union Bank in 1838 — have been lodged with the Corporation on the conditions endorsed upon it; a note warns that on any redistribution securities of the same class, but not necessarily the very bonds deposited, will be returned. Such a paper, issued in London nearly nine decades after the underlying loan, attests to the long shadow of Mississippi's repudiation of its Union Bank debt: British holders of the defaulted bonds banded together under the Corporation, the standard body for pressing claims on delinquent foreign borrowers, in the lingering hope of recovery. The ornate guilloche border and engraved lettering, executed by Bradbury, Wilkinson & Co., lend the instrument the gravity of a genuine security.
creator: Corporation of Foreign Bondholders, London
notes: Deposit Certificate of 5% Bonds of the State of Mississippi U.S.A. (issued to the Union Bank in 1838). No. A139. $10,000. Deposited with the Corporation of Foreign Bondholders. London, December 21, 1925. Printed by Bradbury, Wilkinson & Co.

title: Insull Utility Investments Gold Debenture
description: A ten-year six per cent gold debenture, Series B, in the denomination of one thousand dollars, issued by Insull Utility Investments, Inc., of the State of Illinois, dated the first day of January 1930 and due January 1, 1940; interest and principal are made payable in gold coin of the then-current standard. As an unsecured corporate obligation rather than a mortgage bond, the instrument rested on the credit of Samuel Insull's holding company at the moment his Midwestern utility pyramid reached its zenith. The document notes that two common-stock purchase warrants were originally annexed (voided after December 31, 1933 and December 31, 1934), packaging fixed income with an equity sweetener to court speculative buyers. The engraved vignette of an allegorical female figure flanked by laborers and industrial works renders electric power as civic progress, an idealized self-portrait of an empire that collapsed in 1932.
creator: Insull Utility Investments, Inc.
notes: Insull Utility Investments, Inc., 7% Gold Debenture, Series A, Due January 1, 1949. No. 47837. $1,000. Full coupon sheet attached at right. Chicago utility empire of Samuel Insull, which collapsed in 1932.

title: Middle West Utilities Company Common Stock Certificate, Chicago, 1930
description: A common stock certificate of the Middle West Utilities Company, a holding corporation organized under the laws of Delaware, representing one share without par value and issued at New York or Chicago to Edward Matousek. Dated September 12, 1930, it is countersigned by the president, Martin J. Insull, brother of the financier Samuel Insull. The engraved vignette presents a winged allegorical female figure rising in triumph above a panorama of office towers and street life, attended by two kneeling male nudes — a Beaux-Arts allegory of electric power animating the modern city. Middle West Utilities sat at the apex of Samuel Insull's vast pyramid of interlocking utility holding companies; issued on the eve of the structure's unraveling, the certificate captures the speculative confidence of the late 1920s utility boom shortly before the empire's 1932 collapse.

title: West Shore Railroad Company First Mortgage Guaranteed Bond
description: A registered first-mortgage guaranteed bond of the West Shore Railroad Company, in the denomination of one thousand dollars, bearing interest at four per centum per annum payable semi-annually on the first days of January and July at the City of New York. Drawn to The Farmers Indemnity Company or assigns, it forms part of an issue limited to fifty million dollars, equally secured by a mortgage or deed of trust dated 1885 to the Union Trust Company of New York as trustee, covering railroad, equipment, and property in New York and New Jersey. Striking is the principal due date of January 1, A.D. 2361 — a maturity centuries distant that mirrors the company's long lease to the New York Central, rendering the obligation effectively perpetual. The engraved vignette of a Hudson River reach, with sail and steam vessels beneath wooded heights, fixes the road's identity as the line along the river's west shore.

title: Kingdom of Yugoslavia International Stabilization Gold Loan Bond
description: This is a bearer bond (Titre d'une Obligation au Porteur / Jedna Obveznica na donosioca) of 1,000 French francs, one of 1,025,000 obligations comprising the Kingdom of Yugoslavia's International Stabilization Gold Loan 7% of 1931, a total of 1,025,000,000 French francs. Issued at Belgrade and dated 1 June 1931 under the law of 14 May 1931, the text runs bilingually in French and Serbian, with the face value restated in a gold clause across many monetary standards — dollars, sterling, Swiss francs, Dutch florins, Swedish crowns, Czechoslovak crowns, Belgas, and stabilized dinars — so that the obligation should hold its value in gold whatever a single currency might do. The loan belongs to the wave of interwar stabilization credits arranged through international financial bodies to anchor a fragile currency; the seven-percent coupon measures the price a small Balkan kingdom paid for foreign capital on the eve of the Depression's deepening. The royal arms of the Karađorđević dynasty crown the sheet, framed by dense interlaced Byzantine-revival ornament asserting dynastic and national legitimacy.

title: Kreuger & Toll Participating Debenture
description: A participating debenture for twenty Swedish kronor (Kr. 20) issued by Aktiebolaget Kreuger & Toll, the Stockholm holding company of the financier Ivar Kreuger, the so-called Match King. It forms part of an issue of Kronor 65,000,000 in denominations of 20, 40, 100 and 500, authorised by a shareholders' resolution of 15 May 1928, and bears interest at five (5) per cent per annum from 1 January 1928 until redemption. Beyond fixed interest, the holder shares proportionally in the company's profits alongside the ordinary shares, into which the Kr. 65,000,000 share capital is divided as 650,000 shares of Kr. 100 each. The hybrid debt-equity terms typify Kreuger's intricate financing, which funnelled international capital into match monopolies and sovereign loans before his empire's 1932 collapse. The drawn-out engraved title and guilloche borders project the solidity such instruments were meant to convey. Printed in Sweden by Centraltryckeriet, Stockholm.
creator: Aktiebolaget Kreuger & Toll
notes: Aktiebolaget Kreuger & Toll (Swedish match empire), Participating Debenture, Kr. 20, No. 1163457. 5% interest per annum from January 1, 1928. With coupons and talon. Ivar Kreuger's match monopoly empire collapsed in 1932.
transcription:
# Transcription

**Original (Swedish/English Mixed):**

KR. 20 KR.                                                                                                No. 1163457

PARTICIPATING DEBENTURE

Aktiebolaget
KREUGER & TOLL

Issue of Honour Kr.20,000 participating debentures in denominations of Honour 20, 40, 100 and 500 pursuant to resolution of the shareholders of the Company passed on 15th May 1928.

The undersigned, Aktiebolaget Kreuger & Toll, a limited liability Company of Stockholm, (hereinafter called "the Company") registered under the Swedish Companies' Act and having a share capital of Kronor 63,000,000 divided into 636,000 shares of Kronor 100 each (which shares together with all other shares which may be issued ranking equally therewith as regards participation in profits are hereinafter called "Ordinary shares"), hereby promises to pay to the HOLDER of this Debenture on notice given at the time and in the manner hereinafter mentioned, the sum of

TWENTY KRONOR

with interest thereon at the rate of five (5) per cent per annum from 1st January, 1928, until redemption:

1. On 1st July, 2003, and on any interest date thereafter the Holder hereof has the right, on giving six months' previous notice in writing to the Company and upon surrender of this Debenture, to call for payment of this Debenture at par and accrued interest at the rate of five (5) per cent per annum.

2. Should the dividend paid or declared on the Ordinary shares in any year to the Holders of this Debenture shall be entitled in respect of such fiscal year to additional interest of one (1) per cent so paid or declared on the Ordinary shares in excess of five (5) per cent the Holder of this Debenture shall be given on the Ordinary shares in excess of five (5) per cent per annum, such sum shall be paid or declared for the year ending on the preceding 31st December, will be paid annually on 1st July, 1929, and will represent interest at five (5) per cent, plus additional interest, if any, in respect of the year ending on 31st December 1928.

3. Attached to this Debenture are 30 coupons and a talon for further coupons. Interest on this Debenture, including additional interest, if any, calculated on the dividend paid or declared for the year ending on the preceding 31st December, will be paid annually on 1st July against surrender of the appropriate coupon. The first interest payment will be made on 1st July, 1929, and will represent interest at five (5) per cent, plus additional interest, if any, in respect of the year ending on 31st December 1928.

[Right column text continues with detailed terms regarding capital increases, new debentures, ranking rights, and conditions - text becomes increasingly difficult to read but discusses:]

- If the increase in capital should be in the form of shares not ranking equally as regards participation in profits with the Ordinary shares, the new Debentures to be issued shall carry similar rights as regards participation in profits to the shares then being issued but shall in all other respects rank equally with and be subject to the same conditions as the present Debentures.

- All issues of new Debentures under this clause shall be on such terms that the position of the holders of the existing Debentures [continues but becomes less legible]

title: Chinese Imperial Government Coupon Sheet (Lower Row)
description: A sheet of detachable interest coupons belonging to a bearer obligation of the Imperial Chinese Government 4½% Gold Loan of 1898 ("Kaiserlich Chinesische Staatsanleihe in Gold von 1898"), denominated in sterling and serviced through German banking channels. Each coupon promises semi-annual interest of £2.5s. — two pounds five shillings, the half-year's yield on a £100 bond at 4½% — payable on fixed dates by draft on London ("Wechsel auf London, zahlbar zum Tagescours") at the Deutsch-Asiatische Bank in Berlin and its houses in London, Hamburg, Frankfurt, Cologne and Shanghai. The surviving coupons run to maturities as late as 1936 and 1943, all cancelled by hole-punch. Issued amid the post-1895 scramble for Chinese loans, the sheet records a Qing borrowing floated on European markets and underwritten by German finance, its sober engraved guilloche borders projecting the creditworthiness such instruments needed to sell abroad.
creator: Chinese Imperial Government / Deutsch-Asiatische Bank
notes: Chinese Imperial Government, 4½% Gold Loan of 1898. Interest coupon sheet (lower portion), 13 cancelled coupons. Continuation of goetzmann0699.

title: Monte del Sale Share Transfer, Florence, 1693
description: A transfer certificate of shares in the Monte non vacabile del Sale della Città di Firenze, the non-redeemable salt fund of the Florentine public debt, erected by provision published in the Senate of the Forty-Eight on 12 September 1645. Dated 10 September 1693, it records the conveyance of 44½ luoghi (places) from Pier Carlo Giraldini to Caterina Gianfigliazzi, widow Giraldini, each luogo carrying a capital value of 100 scudi and an annual rendita of 4 scudi paid quarter by quarter — a fixed 4 percent return on a perpetual, inheritable claim on salt-tax revenues. Issued under the name of the Grand Duke of Tuscany and authenticated by the Protettori del Monte, their chancellor, and an affixed wax seal, the printed form with manuscript insertions shows how a Medicean state monetised an essential commodity, turning the salt gabelle into transferable annuities held and bequeathed within Florentine patrician families. Three engraved devices, including the Medici palle, crown the sheet.
creator: Protettori del Monte del Sale, Firenze
notes: Monte non vacabile del Sale della Città di Firenze (Non-vacuous Salt Fund of Florence). Transfer of 44.5 luoghi to Caterina del fu Giov. Battista Gianfigliazzi (née Giraldini). Signed by Protettori del Monte. Wax seal at lower left. Florence, September 10, 1693. Grand Duchy of Tuscany.
transcription:
# Transcription

**Original (Italian):**

Noi Protettori del Monte non vacabile del Sale della Città di Firenze, ereetto d'ordine del Serenissimo Gran Duca di Toscana nostro Signore, per la Prouisione pubblicata nell' Amplissimo Senato de' Quarant' otto il dì di 12. Settembre 1645. A tutti quelli, che vedranno la presente nostra facciamo piena, & indubitata fede, come [handwritten: Cav.r Pierino delgi Albzi Balt. Gioan Batl Bina Gianfigliazzi Vedovante Giraldini]

di Luoghi [handwritten: Giuarantaquadro Omezzo] Apparisic — credit. al Libro di detto Monte segn. [handwritten: Giuarantaquadro —] di Luoghi [handwritten: Giuarantaquadro Omezzo] —

in furuuro rilegatialda conto di [handwritten: Giuarantaquadro —] come per Partito nostro di questo giorno al libro delle Permute à. [handwritten: Giraldini] al quale si abbia relatione. Però annullando ogni altra Patente fatta per giorno al libro delle Permute e, et per l'avvenire facciamo, e dichiaiaamo detta [handwritten: Marierina Gianfigliazzi —] di detto Monte di detti luoghi [handwritten: Giuarantaquadro] assegniamo con credit di detto Monte di detti luoghi Giuarantaquadro à Mesi in iste Mesi per rata, e con li Priuilegi, condi zioni, & dichiarazioni di che in detta Prouisione; et in virtù della presente ordinamio al nostro Camarlengo presente, & che per li tempi farà, che li paghi detti frutti di tre Mesi in tre Mesi: Et in fede ib— aiamo fatta la presente, quale farà sottocritta da vno di Noi Protettori, e dal nostro Cancelliere, e figliata con il nostro solito figillo

Dat. in Firenze nell' Vfizio del nostro Monte del Sale il dì [handwritten: 12 Settembrẽ 1693.]

[Handwritten signature: Attilii Vettor Uno de'Protettori]

[Handwritten: Ottaui Langliane]

---

**English Translation:**

We, Protectors of the Non-Redeemable Salt Fund of the City of Florence, established by order of the Most Serene Grand Duke of Tuscany, our Lord, pursuant to the Provision published in the Most Ample Senate of the Forty-Eight on the 12th of September 1645. To all those who shall see the present [document], we make full and undoubted faith, as [names inserted in manuscript] 

of Places [44.5 inserted in manuscript]—

appearing—credit to the Book of said Monte [signature, properties listed in manuscript]—of Places [44.5 inserted in manuscript]—

in future [assigned] to the account of [names in manuscript]—as per Our Decree of this day in the Book of Exchanges [to Giraldini] to which reference shall be made. However, annulling every other Patent made for the day in the Book of Exchanges, and henceforth we make and declare said [Caterina Giamfigliazzi inserted in manuscript] of said Monte of said places [44.5 inserted in manuscript] assigned with credit of said Monte of said places 44.5 at 4 scudi in these months per rate, and with the Privileges, conditions, & declarations which are in said Provision; and in virtue of the present we ordain to our Chamberlain present, & that for all times shall make, that he pay said fruits of three months in three months: And in faith we have made the present, which shall be subscribed by one of Us Protectors, and by our Chancellor, and sealed with our customary seal.

Given in Florence in the Office of our Monte del Sale on the day [12 September 1693 inserted in manuscript.]

[Signature: Attilio Vettor, One of the Protectors]

[Signature: Ottavio Langliane]

---

**Notable markings:**

- **Wax seal:** Large affixed wax seal in lower left corner (tan/beige colored, partially visible)
- **Coat of arms:** Three heraldic emblems at top of document (left, center, right) depicting crowned shields and ornamental flourishes
- **Signatures:** Two manuscript signatures at bottom (Attilio Vettor and Ottavio Langliane)
- **Date:** 12 September 1693 (handwritten insertion)
- **Printed form:** Document is a printed template with extensive manuscript insertions throughout
- **Document class:** Printed official form with wax seal affixation and notarial signatures

title: Caisse Autonome des Monopoles Stabilisation Loan Bond, Kingdom of Romania, 1929
description: This is a bearer bond of the Caisse Autonome des Monopoles du Royaume de Roumanie (Casa Autonomă a Monopolurilor Regatului României), a gold-denominated 7% external obligation drawn on the Kingdom of Romania's Stabilisation and Development Loan of 1929 (Emprunt de Stabilisation et de Développement). Its face value is fixed dually at Frs.F. 2.352,90 or US $100, amortisable and guaranteed by the State, falling due 1 February 1959, with semi-annual coupons paying Frs.F. 89,35 or US $3.50 each February and August. Issued at Bucharest in 1929, the bond belongs to the wave of monopoly-secured stabilisation loans by which post-war Romania pledged the revenues of its state monopolies to attract foreign gold capital and anchor the leu. The royal arms, the guarantee of the Crown, and the spread-winged eagle assert sovereign backing for an instrument that effectively mortgaged the nation's monopoly income to outside lenders.
creator: Caisse Autonome des Monopoles du Royaume de Roumanie
notes: Caisse Autonome des Monopoles du Royaume de Roumanie, Obligation Extérieure 7% de l'Emprunt de Stabilisation et de Développement de 1929. No. 161,517. Frs.F 2352.90 / US $100. Coupon sheet (coupons 31–60) attached at right. Issued February 1, 1930. Signed by Le Gouverneur and Mihail Popovici.

title: Romanian Monopoles Administration Stabilisation Loan Coupon Sheet
description: Two paired documents associated with the Caisse Autonome des Monopoles du Royaume de Roumanie 7% External Stabilisation and Development Loan of 1929, a foreign-currency obligation through which the Romanian monopolies administration funded budgetary stabilisation. The first sheet carries the loan's bearer coupons, denominated jointly in French francs and United States dollars and payable each February and August, reflecting the dual-market placement of interwar sovereign credit. Alongside sits an unissued certificate for the same series, its blue guilloche frame and vignette of a fortified hilltop town printed but never completed with holder details. Together the pieces document the mechanics of interest service and the physical apparatus of a stabilisation loan raised as Romania sought monetary order after the post-war inflation.

pages.description: A printed coupon sheet of the 7% loan fills most of the leaf: rows of small bilingual coupons in French and Romanian, each headed with a payment date (e.g. "Prima zi din Februarie 1949") and the sums due, Fr. 89.30 or US$3.50. At right, an unissued bearer certificate shows the blue frame, the title "Royaume de Roumanie / Caisse Autonome des Monopoles," the vignette of a walled town, and the denomination Fr. 2532.90 / US$100. Page 1 of 2.

An unrelated quadrilingual bond face, green guilloche border with arched ornamental panels. Headings in French, English, German and Turkish read "Bons Représentatifs des Annuités Arriérées de la Dette Publique Ottomane / Bonds Representing Annuities in Arrear of the Ottoman Public Debt, Série A 1928." Below, "Titre de UN Bon au Porteur" of 7 fr. 50 c. (£0.6.0). A block of attached coupons survives at right. Page 2 of 2.

creator: Caisse Autonome des Monopoles du Royaume de Roumanie

title: Ottoman Public Debt Administration Bond (Reverse)
description: The reverse of a bond issued by the Ottoman Public Debt Administration (Conseil d'Administration de la Dette Publique Ottomane), the international body that administered the Ottoman state's defaulted obligations on behalf of its European creditors. This face carries the binding terms: a dense, four-column printing of extracts from the governing contract in French, English, German, and Ottoman Turkish, setting out the security, service of interest, and redemption arrangements. Down the left margin runs the attached coupon sheet, each coupon overstruck with the Administration's red circular seal, the instrument through which a bearer claimed successive interest payments. The document reflects the regime of foreign financial control imposed after the empire's insolvency, by which revenues were pledged and managed to satisfy holders abroad.

pages.description: Reverse of the Ottoman bond. Four parallel columns print extracts from the contract under the headings Extraits du Contrat / Extracts from the Contract / Auszuge aus dem Vertrage / Mukavelenameden Mustahrec Ahkam (French, English, German, Ottoman Turkish). A coupon sheet runs down the left edge, each coupon stamped with the red circular Administration seal; a small green adhesive stamp appears mid-margin. Page 1 of 2.

[MISMATCH] This image does not show the Ottoman bond. It is the face of a separate Bulgarian bond: Principality of Bulgaria (KNYAZHESTVO BULGARIYA), 4 1/2% Gold Loan of 1907, a Bond to Bearer for Five Hundred Francs from the 145,000,000-franc issue. Multilingual text (Bulgarian, French, English, German), signatures, an embossed seal, and the date Sofia, 31 May / September 1907. Page 2 of 2.

notes: Ottoman Public Debt, Série A 1928, No. 175,406. Verso: quadrilingual conditions (Extraits du Contrat / Extracts from the Contract / Auszüge aus dem Vertrage / Mukavelenameden Müktasas Ahkam). Large coupon sheet with red Ottoman stamps at left. 2-page document; see goetzmann0727 for recto.
transcription:
# Ottoman Public Debt Administration Bond - Contract Terms and Coupon Sheet

## Document Structure

This is a multi-language document with four parallel columns containing the same contract terms in:
1. **French** (EXTRAITS DU CONTRAT)
2. **English** (EXTRACTS FROM THE CONTRACT)
3. **German** (AUSZÜGE AUS DEM VERTRÄGE)
4. **Turkish** (MUKAVELENİN MÜSTAHSıL ANKAİM)

---

## FRENCH TEXT (Left Column)

**EXTRAITS DU CONTRAT**

Renvoyant à autorisation gouvernementale des Articles:

Art. I
La présente Obligation représente une part de la Dette Consolidée de l'Empire Ottoman créée en vertu du Décret en date du 28 février 1903 à 5 p.c. par an, en Livres Turques, de capital nominal, laquelle Dette a été augmentée par Décrets ultérieurs, notamment par celui du 10 novembre 1908 et celui du 19 octobre 1913. title: Principality of Bulgaria Gold Loan Bond (Reverse)
description: This is the reverse of a bond of the Principality of Bulgaria 4½ per cent Gold Loan of 1907, presenting the loan's legal conditions in four parallel columns — Bulgarian (Условия на заема), French, German, and English — beneath a full amortisation schedule (Тарифа за погашение / Tableau d'Amortissement / Tilgungs-Plan / Table of Amortisation). The table sets out the half-yearly drawings, dated 19 January/February and 19 July/August, that retire the loan from 1908 onward, with coupons and redeemed bonds payable in gold at Sofia, Paris, St. Petersburg, Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Berlin, Zürich and Basel. Issued by a young, nominally Ottoman-tributary principality, the gold clause and the spread of European paying-agents reflect Bulgaria's reliance on Western capital markets — and on Russian and French financial patronage — to fund its state and military ambitions on the eve of full independence. The layout is sober and typographic, the document's authority resting on legal text and figures rather than ornament.

title: Ottoman Empire Unified Converted Debt Bond
description: A bearer bond of the 4% Unified Converted Debt of the Ottoman Empire, issued at Constantinople in 1903 under the administration of the Ottoman Public Debt Council. Each obligation carries a face value of 500 francs, equivalent to £20, drawn from a consolidated loan that reorganised the empire's defaulted external borrowings into a single uniform instrument. The certificate speaks in four tongues at once, setting French and English columns to either side of a central Ottoman Turkish and Arabic text crowned by the Sultan's tughra, an arrangement that registers both the international creditors and the issuing sovereign. Dense interlace borders and the repeated 4% roundels frame a security designed to circulate freely across European markets while affirming Ottoman authority over the obligation.

pages.description: Front of the bond, printed in red within dense ornamental interlace. Corner roundels read 4%; trilingual headings flank a central tughra: French at left, Ottoman Turkish and Arabic at center, English at right. Denomination given as Fr. 500 / £20. Signatures and a seal appear below; a coupon sheet is attached at right. Page 1 of 2.

Reverse, in green and red. Columns of decree extracts run in French, Ottoman, and English across the top. A central TABLEAU D'AMORTISSEMENT lists redemption draws. Three large vignettes below repeat the Fr. 500 / £20 denomination around a tughra; numbered coupons line the left margin. Page 2 of 2.

Annual interest: 20 francs / £0.16.0. Constantinople, September 1/14, 1903. Bilingual French/Arabic. Talon and coupon sheet attached at right.

title: Hagenow Municipal Benzol-Value Bond
description: Inflation-era commodity-value bond (Benzolwert-Anleihe) issued by the Staedtisches Gas- und Elektrizitaetswerk (Municipal Gas and Electricity Works) of Hagenow in Mecklenburg, series Lit. C, bearing 5% interest. Rather than a fixed sum of money, the Schuldverschreibung is denominated in the monetary value of 50 (fuenfzig) kilograms of benzol (benzene), pegging the obligation to a real commodity instead of the collapsing paper Mark. Dated Hagenow, 30 July 1923, at the height of the German hyperinflation. Signed for Der Rat (the town council) and by the Stadtverordnetenvorsteher (chairman of the city assembly). The face carries a red diagonal cancellation overprint; the serial is Lit. C, No. 173 (read beneath the red cancellation overprint).

Page 3 of 4 - Mechlenburg-Vorpommern City bonds (Stadtanleihen Mecklenburg-Vorpommern) for the city of Rostock dated November 12, 1927, bond redemption with draw slip, bond over 50 RM.
Page 4 of 4 - Letter describing bonds

# Transcription

## Original:

**5 Prozent                                                                    5 Prozent**

**Benzolwert-Anleihe**

**des**

**Städtischen Gas- und Elektrizitätswerks**
**Hagenow i.M.**

**Lit. E                                                                      No. 173**

**Schuldverschreibung**

**über den Geldwert von 50 kg Benzol.**

Das Städtische Gas- und Elektrizitätswerk Hagenow i.M. schuldet dem Inhaber dieser Schuldverschreibung den Geldwert von

**fill (fünfzig) Kilogramm Benzol**

Die Schuld wird vom 1. Juli 1925 ab mit 5 (fünf) vom Hundert auf das Jahr in der Weise verzinst, daß der Geldwert von 2½ kg Benzol in halbjährlichen, am 2. Januar und 1. Juli jeden Jahres fälligen Treidträgen nachträglich ausgeäahlt wird. Die Schuld-verschreibung ist tilgbar des Inhabers Untuntigung.

Für die Anteile haffen die Bestimmungen der Stadt Hagenow und das Städtische Gas- und Elektrizitätswerk.

Im übrigen gelten die umseitig abgedruckten Bestimmungen.

Hagenow, den 20. Juli 1925.

**Der Rat**

**D. Stump**                                                    [Signature]

**Der Stadtverodrfnetenvorsteher**

**[Signature - illegible]**

---

## English translation:

**5 Percent                                                                    5 Percent**

**Benzol Value Bond**

**of the**

**Municipal Gas and Electricity Works**
**Hagenow i.M.**

**Lit. E                                                                      No. 173**

**Bond Certificate**

**for the monetary value of 50 kg benzol.**

The Municipal Gas and Electricity Works Hagenow i.M. owes to the holder of this bond certificate the monetary value of

**fifty (50) kilograms of benzol**

The debt accrues interest from July 1, 1925, at 5 (five) percent per annum, in such a way that the monetary value of 2½ kg benzol in semi-annual installments due on January 2 and July 1 of each year is paid retroactively. The bond certificate is redeemable at the holder's request.

For the shares, the provisions of the City of Hagenow and the Municipal Gas and Electricity Works apply.

For all other matters, the provisions printed on the reverse side apply.

Hagenow, July 20, 1925.

**The Council**

**D. Stump**                                                    [Signature]

**The City Council Chairman**

# Transcription

**Original:**

Buchstabe E. Reichsmark 200.—

Ablösungsanleihe der Stadt Rostock.

Buchstabe E.                                Nr. 08939

**Schuldverschreibung**

über

ZWEIHUNDERT REICHSMARK

Ausfertigig: auf Grund der durch das Meckl.-Schweinische Ministerium des Innern in Schwerin erteilten Genehmigung vom 6. November 1927 Nr. B 49514.

Die Stadt Rostock bekennt sich durch diese, für jeden Inhaber gültige Schuldverschreibung zu einer sieben des Gläubigers unkündbar Darlehnsschuld im Betrage von

ZWEIHUNDERT REICHSMARK.

Eine Verzinsung der Ablösungsanleihe kann bis zum Erlaßchen der Reparationsverpflichtungen nicht gefordert werden. Der Beginn und die Höhe der Verzinsung werden durch die Landesregierung bestimmt werden. Für den Fall der Verzinsung bleibt vorbehalten, dem Inhaber dieser Schuldverschreibung gegen deren Rückgabe an die gleich geltenden Nennwerts mit Zinszinsen und Ersparnisschleinen auszuzählungen. Die Tilgung der Ablösungsanleihe ist auf besonders gewährte, selbstständig veräußerliche Auslosungsanrechte beschränkt und wird durch Ziehung der Gültigung dieser Rechte vollzogen. — Über diese Rechte werden besondere Auslosungsscheine ausgegebem werden. — Im übrigen kann eine Tilgung der Ablösungsanleihe nur nach Bestimmung der Landesregierung verlangen werden (§ 44 des Reichsgesetzes über die Ablösung öffentlicher Anleihen). Für die Sicherheit der Kapitalien und der Zinsen haftet die Stadt Rostock mit ihrem gesamten gegenwärtigen und zukünftigen Vermögen und mit ihrer Steuerkraft.

Rostock, den 12. November 1927.

**Der Rat der Stadt Rostock.**

[Signature] Dr. Fleßheim Hedtka.

Der Stadtverobnetevorsteher

[Signature] Dr. jun. Friedrich Mowske

Der Kontrollbeamte: [signature - illegible]

# English Translation

**Redemption Loan of the City of Rostock**

Letter E.                                 No. 08939

**Debt Certificate**

for

TWO HUNDRED REICHSMARK

Issued: on the basis of the authorization granted by the Mecklenburg-Schwerin Ministry of the Interior in Schwerin on November 6, 1927 No. B 49514.

The City of Rostock hereby confirms through this document, valid for every bearer, a debt obligation of the creditor that cannot be terminated in the amount of

TWO HUNDRED REICHSMARK.

The redemption loan cannot accrue interest until the reparations obligations are extinguished. The beginning and rate of interest shall be determined by the State Government. In the event interest accrues, it remains reserved that the bearer of this debt certificate may exchange it for its equivalent nominal value plus interest and savings payments upon return. The redemption of the redemption loan is limited to specially granted, independently transferable redemption rights and is carried out through the drawing of the validity of these rights. — Separate redemption certificates shall be issued for these rights. — Moreover, redemption of the redemption loan can only take place in accordance with the provisions of the State Government (§ 44 of the Reich Law on the Redemption of Public Loans). The City of Rostock is liable for the security of capital and interest with all its present and future assets and with its tax power.

Rostock, November 12, 1927.

**The Council of the City of Rostock**

# Document Transcription

**Original:**

Ausschreibung.

5%ige Benzolwert-Anleihe
des
Städtischen Gas- und Elektrizitätswerkes
Hagenow i. M.

Das Städtische Gas- und Elektrizitätswerk Hagenow i. M. nimmt mit Genehmigung des Rates der Stadt Hagenow und des Landesverrwaltungsrates eine Anleihe in Höhe des Wertes von 14 000 Kilogramm Benzol auf. Die einzelnen Anleihestücke lauten auf den jeweiligen Wert der befindlichen Benzolmenge. Es werden Stücke in 5, 10 und 50 Kilogramm Benzol ausgereicht. Die Stücke sind auf den Inhaber ausgestellt. Zu jedem Anleihestück gehören 14 halbj[ährliche] Zinsscheine. Die Zinsscheine lauten auf den jeweiligen Wert der Hälfte von 5%, der auf dem zugehörigen Anleihestück gehörigen Benzolmenge. Es wird also bei einer auf 10 Kilogramm Benzol lautenden Anleiheistück ein halbj[ährlicher] Zins von 1/4 Kilogramm Benzol gegeben. Der Zins wird halbj[ährlich] nachträglich am 2. Januar brw. 1. Juli gegen Einsendung der Zinsscheine in Gold entrichtet. Der zur Ausschreibung kommende Gebrauch wird noch dem letzten amtlichen Benzolpreis vom November brw. Mai berechnet und wird in Hagenower Kreisblatt und in der Amtlichen Beilage des Mecklenburg-Schwerin[schen] Regierungsblattes bekannt gegeben.

Die Pflügung der Anleihe erfolgt vom 1. Juli 1924 ab mit jährlich mindestens 2000 Kilogramm Benzol durch freihändigen Rückkauf oder durch Auslosung. Die Ausszahlung der ausgelosten Stücke erfolgt am 2. Januar und 1. Juli jedesJahres und zwar zuerst am 1. Juli 1924. Im Jahre 1930 erfolgt die Ausszahlung der gelösten bis dahin noch nicht ausgelosten Anleiheistücke. Ausgelählt wird der, in derselben Weise wie bei der Feststellung des jeweiligen Zinsbetrags, ermittelte Preis für Benzol. Die Bekanntmachung der ausgelosten Stücke erfolgt im Hagenower Kreisblatt und in der Amtlichen Beilage des Mecklenburg-Schwerin[schen] Regierungsblattes.

Für die Anleihe haften die Bestiftungen der Stadt Hagenow und das Städtische Gas- und Elektrizitätswerk.

Der Ertrag der Anleihe ist bestimmt zur Errichtung einer Benzolgewinnungs- und Abhitzeverwertungsanlage im Städt. Gaswerk Hagenow.

---

# English Translation:

**Announcement**

5% Benzene-Value Loan
of the
Municipal Gas and Electricity Works
Hagenow i. M.

The Municipal Gas and Electricity Works Hagenow i. M. is raising, with the approval of the City Council of Hagenow and the State Administration Council, a loan in the amount of 14,000 kilograms of benzene. The individual loan documents are denominated in the respective value of the benzene quantity contained therein. Pieces of 5, 10, and 50 kilograms of benzene will be issued. The pieces are made out to bearer. Each loan document is accompanied by 14 semi-annual interest coupons. The interest coupons are denominated in the respective value of half of 5% of the benzene quantity belonging to the corresponding loan document. Thus, for a loan document denominated at 10 kilograms of benzene, a semi-annual interest of 1/4 kilogram of benzene is given. The interest is paid semi-annually in arrears on January 2 and July 1 respectively upon submission of the interest coupons in gold. The loan to be announced will be calculated according to the last official benzene price from November and May respectively and will be published in the Hagenow District Gazette and in the Official Bulletin of the Mecklenburg-Schwerin Government Gazette.

The redemption of the loan begins from July 1, 1924 onwards with at least 2,000 kilograms of benzene annually through free purchase or by drawing. Payment of the drawn pieces occurs on January 2 and July 1 of each year, first on July 1, 1924. In 1930, payment is made for the drawn pieces not yet drawn by then. The price for benzene is determined in the same manner as for determining the respective interest amount. Notification of the drawn pieces is made in the Hagenow District Gazette and in the Official Bulletin of the Mecklenburg-Schwerin Government Gazette.

The city of Hagenow and the Municipal Gas and Electricity Works are liable for the loan.

The proceeds of the loan are designated for the establishment of a benzene recovery and waste heat utilization system at the City Gas Works Hagenow.

title: Silesian Loan of Holy Roman Emperor Karl VI
description: AMSTERDAM. 5% Obligatie Fl.1000. black. No 1254. The Habsburg emperors were always short of money. A large refinancing operation was required by 1734, and Karl VI raised a loan with the Amsterdam banking-house of Willem Gideon Deutz. In guarantee, he offered the Silesia tribute fund, into which payments were made by the princes and nobles of Silesia (thus, the Silesian loan). As interest rates declined, a conversion operation was carried out in 1736. The new bonds, paying 5% interest, were for a total of 700.000 gulden. The new loan was very successful, and within two months had attracted all the capital needed. At first, interest was paid regularly from the contributions by the Silesian nobility. However, after 1742, things changed, when, after the war between Maria Theresia, daughter and successor of Karl VI, and Frederick the Great of Prussia led to Prussian rule over the Silesian territories. There were no further interest payments ; the investors lost everything they had lent. The founder of the important house of Deutz, Willem Gideon Deutz, has signed this bond in person. He was burgomaster of Amsterdam at this time.

# Transcription

**Original (Dutch):**

(I)

WY KAREL de Zesde, door Godsgénaden verkooren Roams Keyzer, ten allen tyde Vermeerderaar des Kyks, Koning in Germanien en Spanjen, Hongarien, Bohemen, Arrds-Hertzog van Oostenryk, Hertzog van Burgiindien, Stirien, Carinthien, Crain, en Wurtenberg, in Opper-en Neder-Silselen, Markgraaf tot Mähren, in Opper en Neder-Lausitz, Graaf tot Habsburg, Tyrol en Goritzy &c. bekennen, voor Ons, onze Erven en Nakomellingen, in kragt dezes Schulthbrief openlyk, en doen daar nevens kond aan een igelyk, dat op onze genadiafte bewilfiginge en goedkeurige, door middel en directie van Willem Gideon Deutz, tot Amsterdam, van versckeydene Persoonen en Particulieren, ten decle ook op andere plaatsen voorgesteld, een declen ook op andere plaatsen voorgesteld, dienst van onseu van het gemeneete weezen genegotieerd, en by forme van beleeninge opgebragt, gelyk meede in handen van onze Keizerlyke Schatkamer in contant geld, tegens de da vour volle krijtige beteonibg valg gelyk vol zekere somme contant geld, bedragende zevenmal hondert duytzend guldens Hollands Courant, en wy derhalven de gemelde somme van zevenmal hondert duytzend Guldens Hollands Courant, aantmen hier by derhalven de gemelde somme van zevenmal hondert duytzend Guldens Hollands Courant, aantmen hier by Willem Gideon Deutz, en verdiere mede Geinteresseerdenis, dewelkee boventialade leeningen te zamen hebben opgestoroten, verthioldigt zyn geworden, en aan entregens voor Ons, ondat Wy en Nakomellingen, voorwerens en verplicht agte tot voorgaande onderhandeling daar over met zyne daar toe benoernde Gevolmagtigidens gehouden, toegezegd, betaald, en en wy voor het gemeelde ter lenen opgenomene Kapitaal s jaarlrks aan Intereellen, na rato des tyds betalen zullen vyf per cent, dat wy ook de voornoelmde opgenomene, en vertrekte somsme van zevenmal hondert duytzend guldens Hollands Courant, wyftien Jaaren binnen Amsterdam, in gelyck Hollandtze Courant Munt weder zullen lettelderen Obligation dertelonen, ondat namntlyk in de tien eerft naalkoomende Jaaren, synde Anno een duytzend zeventhondert zeven en derttig, tot Anno een duytzend zeventhondert zes en veertig ingeslotene, die invoeging der voornoemde kapitaals, zal in de tyd pertiode elk van vijf pertiode elk van vijf jaaren, als meede ten naastvolf der jaaren, dertelonen, tot bilyk een volkomeh gemongen van hem Willem Gideon Deutz, en der overige mede Geinteresseerdenrs; zonder enig manjagement afgeloid, en onder volkdan ze boramen worden, wyl wy nu het zelve Obligatoire Instrument door onze Voorten en Standen in Silselen deswegns opgemaakt, dat namntyk de afflositinge zo wel van het ter lenen veniltaande wyze, en in de vailgeldeelde ternynen, zonder ceedige exception oft uyttel gelchieiden zal ; alleins voor aangenaem gehouven, en daer over onze genadigte toe teminyge en goedkeurige verleend hebben.

WY

## English Translation:

WE CHARLES the Sixth, by the Grace of God elected Roman Emperor, at all times increaser of the Empire, King in Germany and Spain, Hungary, Bohemia, Archduke of Austria, Duke of Burgundy, Styria, Carinthia, Crain, and Württemberg, in Upper and Lower Silesia, Marquis of Moravia, in Upper and Lower Lusatia, Count of Habsburg, Tyrol and Gorizia etc. acknowledge, for Us, our Heirs and Successors, by virtue of this deed of bond publicly and hereby declare to all that at our gracious will and good pleasure, through the mediation and direction of Willem Gideon Deutz, of Amsterdam, from various Persons and Individuals, partly also at other places, a sum required for the service of our State has been negotiated and through means of borrowing raised, in like manner deposited in our Imperial Treasury in ready money, against the said for full discharge thereof. And we therefore acknowledge the mentioned sum of seven hundred thousand guilders, Hollands Courant, and we do hereby, through Willem Gideon Deutz, and further interested parties, who have together contributed these loans, confirm and obligate ourselves to the same.

And we declare that we owe and are bound for the named loan, that we shall to the mentioned gathered borrowed Capital annually pay interest, at the rate of five percent, and that we will also the aforesaid borrowed and received sum of seven hundred thousand guilders, Hollands Courant, fifteen years within Amsterdam, in like Hollandse Courant money repay to the Obligation herein mentioned, namely in the ten first coming years, being in the year one thousand seven hundred and thirty-seven, until the year one thousand seven hundred and forty-six, with the consequence that in each five-year period, as also at the following of the years, shall be paid, without full and complete discharge of him Willem Gideon Deutz, and the other interested parties; without any management excluded, and under full faith and credit, we have caused this Obligatoire instrument through our authorities and estates in Silesia to be made, namely that the payment, as well of the principal and interest to be loaned, in the agreed and appointed terms, without any exception or failure shall happen; solely for acceptance esteemed, and thereover our gracious approval and good pleasure granted.

WE

# English Translation:

We the Princes and Estates of Upper and Lower Silesia, hereby acknowledge, declare, and make known to all those to whom it may concern, in what manner His Most Serene and Royal Majesty, our Most Gracious Emperor, King, and Lord, has most graciously permitted us, that His Imperial Majesty was pleased, on account of His Most Pressing Expenditures, to raise a Capital of seven hundred thousand guilders of the Holland Currency in Amsterdam, to be negotiated and taken up at the rate of Five percent per annum, which shall be repaid within the period of Fifteen Years, namely, in the Years 1739, 1740, 1742, 1743, 1744, 1745, and 1746, from the aforesaid Capital of seven hundred thousand guilders Holland Currency, according to all required stipulations, whereby in the earlier Years the Interest for a quarter of a Year shall be paid, but in the latter five Years, namely, in the Years 1747, 1748, 1749, 1750, and 1751, one fifth of the Capital itself, for a period of twenty percent per annum, from six months to six months, and the Interests calculated pro rata for the time and periods, shall be paid every three months from the aforesaid Silesian levies for the Revenues, collected and paid promptly; His Imperial and Royal Majesty has further desired that we shall bind ourselves for the aforesaid Sum by the Name of the Public to pay the same with and together with His Most Serene Imperial Majesty.

Given that we, from the most subordinate consideration, with which we are bound to His Most Exalted and Most Gracious Imperial Majesty and the Most Honored House of Austria, consider ourselves obliged and willing to comply with such most gracious request, we bind ourselves herewith, through this present Instrument, for Us and our Successors, in the most solemn manner, with full knowledge and deliberate intent, to pay the aforesaid sum of seven hundred thousand guilders Holland Currency, and the Interest promised thereupon at the rate of Five percent per annum to those to whom His Most Serene Imperial and Catholic Majesty, our Most Gracious Lord, shall lend the same, or to those who may come into possession thereof by inheritance, without the least deduction, from the annual Silesian levies for the Revenues, namely, in the first ten Years only the Interest, payable quarterly, and in the following five Years the Capital itself, with the aforesaid payment of twenty percent, or one hundred and twenty thousand guilders Holland Currency, (in which case we shall repay and return this Bond without deduction), and in such manner that all of this

title: Commerce d'Asie & d'Afrique (Trieste Company)
description: ANVERS. Action de 1000 Florins. black. No 194. - Date: October 1, 1781 -  In 1775 Guilleaume Bolts obtained permission from Empress Maria Theresa to establish a company in the Austrian free port of Trieste, for trading with the East Indies. In 1781 the first shares were issued, with great success. The director was the famous Antwerp businessman Charles de Proli, son of the founder of the Keyserlijke Indische Compagnie, of Ostend; he was also linked with the formation of the Assurance d'Anvers. The company could not compete with the French and English competition, and after an unfortunate expedition to China, the company was unable to meet its liabilities. The Trieste Company is the last of the great trading companies of the Habsburgs. The share is printed on hand-made paper, and is signed by Guillaume Bolts, Count Charles de Proli, and J.C.J. Borrekens.
keywords: Commerce D'Asie & D'Afrique, Soc. De Trieste Company

title: The Caracas Company (Real Compañía Guipuzcoana de Caracas)
description: MADRID. 1 June 1752. Accion de 500 escudos. black. #2000. No 2530. The 'Caracas Company', the Real Compania Guipuzcoana de Caracas, was formed by a group of merchants in the Guipuzcoa province, around San Sebastian, in the Basque region of Spain, to trade with the Caracas province of Venezuela, with the further aims of (a) developing its agriculture, especially cocoa, and (b) keeping watch on the coast, from the mouth of the river Orinoco to the river Hacha (later extended to cover Maracaibo). This latter aim was to prevent illegal (in Spanish eyes) trading, particularly by the Dutch from Curaçao. The leader of this group was the Count of Penaflorida. This was the first of the great Spanish overseas trading companies, and the first Spanish trading company known to have issued shares to the general public. The company had the right to send two ships per year from San Sebastian and nearby Pasajes to Venezuela, carrying all types of Spanish goods. For the return voyage the ships brought precious metals, cocoa, sugar, tobacco and leathers, back to Sevilla and, later, Cadiz. In 1730 the company set about establishing a chain of trading-posts along the coast of what is now Venezuela and the offshore islands of Margarita and Trinidad, and in 1732 obtained a monopoly of trade between Caracas and Spain; in 1742 this was extended for an indefinite period. In that same year the company's Head Office was moved from San Sebastian to Madrid. From the outset the company was very profitable, paying large dividends to its shareholders. King Felipe V and the Queen took 100 shares each, for a combined investment of 100,000 escudos. The initial capital was 1,000,000 escudos (2,000 shares of 500 escudos of 15 reales each), but shares were a new idea to the Spanish public, and they did not sell well at first; only 500,000 escudos were sold (including those to the royal house, as above). The capital was later increased, in 1752, and again in 1766. The company's activities expanded rapidly, especially after 1734, when restrictions on the number of sailings were removed. The Caracas production, especially of cocoa, rose sharply. By mid-century the company owned 12 large ocean-going ships and 19 coastal ships, with some 2,500 crew, and carrying 2,500 or more tons of freight annually in each direction. However, the company mis-used its monopoly powers, by charging excessive prices for imported goods, and cutting steadily the already low prices paid to Caracas growers for their produce; this, together with interference in the government of Caracas, led in the end to a rebellion by the the colonists. Not until 1752 was this finally subdued, and these events led the Spanish government to reduce the company's privileges and introduce fixed prices for buying cocoa and other produce. Profits fell sharply, and the company's fortunes went into steady decline. Its investments in Spain were unsuccessful; wars against England in 1761-3 and 1779-83 cost it dearly; the free-trading policies of King Carlos III were the final blow. In 1781 the company lost the monopoly with Venezuela, and in 1785 it was absorbed by the Real Compania de Filipinas. The Caracas shares were exchanged for those of the Filipinas company. The share is very attractive, printed on vellum, with a fine copper-engraving. At the top are the royal arms of Guipuzcoa; on each side, and down the sides is a fine design of leaves, fruit,weapons, and an Indian man, with bow-and-arrow, and woman. Boxes of company freight are all around. There is a fine paper seal, also with the royal arms of Guipuzcoa. This is a share from the fist capital increase.

ACCION DE LA COMPAÑIA.

title: Swedish West-India Company
description: STOCKHOLM. 15 December 1787. Share of 100 Reichstaler. black. No 259. Folds. As early as 1745 the trading house Abr. Jocab Arfwedson & Co. was given the privilege of creating a West India company, but due to a sharp protest from the Spanish envoy in Stockholm against this undertaking, the firm renounced its privilege. After the Swedish acquisition in 1784 of Saint Barthélemy in the West Indies, town secretary Johan Liljencrants put a proposition to the Chamber of Commerce for the formation of a share-based company to conduct trade in the West Indies. In 1786, the company received a 15-year privilege for commerce and shipping with the Swedish island of Saint Barthélemy and other islands of the West Indies as well as with North America. Liljencrants and the Stockholm wholesale dealers Arfwedson, Schinkel and Pauli were to supervise the subscription. The business did not develop as hoped for, and the first expedition was lost as a consequence of the war with Russia in 1788. Later on, the business was paralysed due to prohibitions of importation of certain goods, especially coffee. High costs caused by captures of ships brought bad commercial results and the company was dissolved in 1805. The island became French, as part of Guadeloupe, in 1878. A 4-page document, ink-cancelled with a large 'X'. Printed, with manuscript additions of payment

title: Strand Bridge Company Share
description: An engraved share certificate of the Strand Bridge Company, the London undertaking incorporated by Act of Parliament to build the Thames crossing later renamed Waterloo Bridge. Flourished copperplate lettering spells out the company name across the head, and the text certifies the named holder as a proprietor of one share, entered in the company's Registry Book and held under the Common Seal, here a gilt oval wafer affixed at the left. Issued at London on 30 December 1809, the document represents a private subscription company raising capital for public infrastructure; a footnote requires every sale or transfer of the share to be entered by the company's clerk, tying ownership to a controlled register.

pages.description: Engraved certificate with an ornamental "STRAND BRIDGE" header and number at upper left. The body certifies that the holder is a proprietor entitled to one share, entered in the Registry Book and incorporated by Act of Parliament, given under the Common Seal at London, 30 December 1809. A gilt oval wafer seal sits at left; a footnote requires transfers to be entered by the clerk. Page 1 of 2.

Verso, headed "1810," bearing a handwritten ledger of calls received on the share. Successive entries note dates (including January, March, May and July) against money columns, recording the instalment payments made by the proprietor toward the subscribed capital. The lower sheet is blank. Page 2 of 2.

creator: Strand Bridge Company
notes: Strand Bridge Company later became Waterloo Bridge; reverse (goetzmann0913) shows call payment records 1810-1811

title: Derby Canal Company Share, Derby, 1793
description: This printed share certificate of the Derby Canal Company certifies the holder's title to one share in the undertaking, entered by name in the company's book and authenticated under the common seal. It records the enabling Act passed in the last session of Parliament for making and maintaining a navigable canal from the River Trent near Swarkston Bridge through the borough of Derby to Little Eaton, with a cut joining the Erewash Canal near Sandiacre and railways serving collieries at Denby, Horsley, and Smalley. Issued at the company's second General Assembly, held the seventh day of October 1793 at Derby, it belongs to the canal-mania of the early 1790s, when joint-stock subscriptions financed inland navigation to move coal and goods. A note warns that all transfers require duplicate deeds lodged with the clerk before any purchaser may claim profit or vote as a proprietor. An engraved red seal anchors the lower margin.
creator: Derby Canal Company
notes: Derby Canal Company share certificate, Share No. 320; certified to Matthew [illegible] of Derby; established under Act of Parliament for canal from River Trent through Borough of Derby to Little Eaton; issued at second General Assembly, October 7, 1793
transcription:
# DERBY CANAL

THIS Ticket, or Instrument, is to certify, that Matthew How of Derby in the County of Derby (Hatter)

is entitled to the share No. 320 in this undertaking; and that her name and proper addition, with the number of the said share, are fairly and distinctly entered in the book kept by "The Derby Canal Company" for that purpose, agreeable to the directions of the act passed in the last session of Parliament, intituled, "An Act for making and maintaining a Navigable Canal from the River Trent at or near Swarklton Bridge, to and through the Borough of Derby, to Little Eaton, with a Cut out of the said Canal in or near the said Borough, to join the Erewash Canal near Sandiacre; and for making Railways from such Canal to several Collieries in the parishes or liberties of Denby, Horfley, and Smalley, all in the county of Derby." Given under the Common Seal of the said Derby Canal Company, at their second General Assembly, held the seventh day of October, in the year One Thousand Seven Hundred and Ninety Three.

N.B. There must be Duplicates of all Transfers of Shares, each of them executed both by the Seller and the Purchaser, one part whereof must be delivered to the Clerk to the *Derby Canal Company*, and entered by him before the purchaser can have any share of the profits of this undertaking, or be entitled to vote as a proprietor.

**Notable markings:**
- Circular wax seal (red/ochre colored) affixed to lower right of document
- Handwritten annotation at top left corner (partially visible)
- Serial number: 320
- Date: 7 October 1793

title: Société Toulousaine du Bazacle Share
description: This is a bearer founder's share (part de fondateur au porteur) of the Société Toulousaine du Bazacle, a société anonyme with its registered seat at 10, Quai Saint-Pierre in Toulouse. The statutes, deposited before Maître Moyne, notary at Paris, were modified by the extraordinary assemblies of 18 March 1927 and 26 April 1928; the company's capital then stood at 13,150,000 francs, divided into 131,500 shares of 100 francs each. Created by the extraordinary general assembly of 26 April 1928, this instrument stands apart from that capital, conferring instead a 1/20,000th claim on the founders' advantages reserved by the statutes. Such parts de fondateur rewarded promoters without diluting subscribed capital, monetising goodwill rather than equity. The Bazacle company, raised upon Toulouse's ancient Garonne mill works, had by this date become an electricity concern. A restrained border of stylised foliage frames a document whose worth lay not in machinery but in a contractual share of surplus.
creator: Societe Toulousaine du Bazacle
notes: Societe Toulousaine du Bazacle traces its origins to a medieval water mill cooperative; one of the oldest joint-stock companies in history

title: Hotel Waldorf-Astoria Corporation Specimen Share
description: A specimen share certificate of the Hotel Waldorf-Astoria Corporation, incorporated under the laws of the State of New York. Printed in green with an ornate engraved border, it certifies ownership of full-paid, non-assessable shares of common stock without par value, drawn from an authorized capital of 300,000 shares. At its head an allegorical vignette shows reclining female figures attended by a winged genius, an emblem of prosperity and hospitality befitting a grand hotel enterprise. The certificate is transferable in New York or Boston, with The First National Bank of Boston as transfer agent. Marked SPECIMEN in red and numbered 000, it is an unissued printer's proof rather than a circulated share, documenting the financing of one of America's most celebrated hotels.

pages.description: The certificate face: an engraved green border frames an allegorical vignette of reclining women and a winged genius above the firm's name in ornate script. Text certifies non-assessable, no-par common stock, transferable in New York or Boston. Numbered 000, twice stamped SPECIMEN in red, with blank date and facsimile officers' signatures. Page 1 of 2.

The reverse, printed in black, quotes clauses of the certificate of incorporation governing share issuance, director self-dealing, and the absence of preference among common shares. Below, a blank assignment and power-of-attorney form for transferring the stock, with punched binding holes at the margin. Page 2 of 2.

creator: Hotel Waldorf-Astoria Corporation

transcription:
# Hotel Waldorf-Astoria Corporation Stock Certificate - Transcription

## Main Text:

**INCORPORATED UNDER THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF NEW YORK**

**Hotel Waldorf-Astoria Corporation**

THIS CERTIFICATE IS TRANSFERABLE IN NEW YORK, N.Y. OR IN BOSTON, MASS.
AUTHORIZED CAPITAL STOCK–300,000 SHARES WITHOUT PAR VALUE

**This is to Certify that** [blank] **is the owner of** [blank] **fully paid and non assessable shares of Limited Stock without par value of Hotel Waldorf-Astoria Corporation transferable only on the books of the Corporation by the holder hereof in person or by duly authorized attorney upon surrender of this Certificate properly endorsed. The shares hereof shall be held subject to all the terms and conditions of the Certificate of Incorporation on file in the office of the Secretary of State and to the By-laws of the Corporation heretofore adopted and now in effect, a copy of which is on file herewith, and the holder is entitled to all the rights and privileges incident to the ownership of such shares.

**Witness the facsimile seals of the Corporation and the facsimile signatures of duly authorized officers.**

**Dated** [blank]

**Treasurer** [signature - marked SPECIMEN]

**President** [signature - marked SPECIMEN]

## Notable Markings:

- Serial number at top: **110537**
- Red text stamps: **SPECIMEN** (appears twice - below Treasurer and President signature lines)
- Large corporate seal in center bottom with text around perimeter (partially legible): **[SEAL OF THE ASTORIA CORPORATION]**
- "THE FIRST NATIONAL BANK OF BOSTON" printed vertically on left margin
- "THE COMMONWEALTH BANK OF BOSTON" printed vertically on right margin
- Ornate green border with decorative floral and geometric patterns throughout
- Empty green cartouches/boxes on either side of central engraving (for denomination or identifying numbers)
- Central allegorical engraving showing two figures seated on barrel or vessel

---

# Transcription

The Certificate of Incorporation, as amended, provides, among other things, as follows:

The Corporation may issue and sell its authorized shares without par value for such consideration as from time to time may be fixed by the Board of Directors.

"No contract or other transaction between the corporation and any other corporation shall be affected or invalidated by the fact that any one or more of the directors of this corporation is or are interested in, or is a director or officer or are directors or officers of, such other corporation, and any director or directors, individually or jointly, may be a party or parties to, or may be interested in, any contract or transaction of this corporation or in which this corporation is interested; and no contract, act or transaction of this corporation with any person or persons, firm or corporation, shall be affected or invalidated by the fact that any director or directors of this corporation is a party, or are parties to or interested in such contract, act or transaction, or in

any way connected with such person or persons, firm or association, and each and every person who may become a director of this corporation is hereby relieved from any liability that might otherwise exist from contracting with the corporation for the benefit of itself or of any firm, association or corporation in which it may be in anywise interested."

"No holder of the capital stock of this corporation shall be entitled as such as a matter of right, to subscribe for or purchase any part of any new or additional issue of stock of any class whatsoever whether now or hereafter authorized, or of securities convertible into stock of any class whatsoever, whether now or hereafter authorized, or whether issued for cash, property or services or by way of dividend, and all such rights are waived by each holder of the capital stock of this corporation."

---

Forwardly received, hereby sell, assign, and transfer unto

_____________________________________________________________________________

Shares of the Stock represented by the within Certificate, and do hereby irrevocably constitute and appoint _____________ Attorney, to transfer the said Stock, on the books of the within-named Corporation, with full power of Substitution in the premises.

Dated _________________ 19 _____

In Presence of:

_____________________________________________________________________________

_____________________________________________________________________________

_____________________________________________________________________________

THIS SPACE MUST NOT BE COVERED IN ANY WAY

---

**Notable markings:**
- Three black circular punch holes on the right side of the document
- Stamps/text visible on the right margin (partially illegible due to document condition)
- Signature lines in cursive script
- Date field marked "19____" indicating vintage document

title: United States Radium Corporation Common Stock
description: A common stock certificate of the United States Radium Corporation, a firm incorporated under Delaware law in 1917 and known for producing luminous radium paint. Engraved in brown with an ornate floral border, the certificate represents forty shares at a par value of one dollar each. It was issued in 1968 to two holders as joint tenants in Kenilworth, Illinois, and authenticated by the facsimile signatures of the treasurer and president together with the embossed corporate seal. The reverse carries the standard assignment form, here completed and guaranteed by a brokerage. The document records the company late in its corporate life, decades after the radium-dial industry that gave it notoriety.

pages.description: Engraved face of the certificate: brown ornamental border framing the title "United States Radium Corporation," Common Stock, par value $1.00 each, forty shares. Names two joint-tenant holders in Kenilworth, Illinois, dated May 6, 1968, with the embossed Delaware corporate seal and facsimile signatures of treasurer and president. Page 1 of 2.

Reverse of the certificate: the printed legend of ownership abbreviations (TEN COM, JT TEN, UNIF GIFT MIN ACT) above the assignment-and-transfer form. The form is completed and stamped with attorney instructions and a Dean Witter & Co. signature guarantee, dated August 1968. Page 2 of 2.

title: Silk-Reeling Partnership Share Certificate
description: A share certificate of a Japanese silk-reeling partnership (seishi gōmei kaisha), issued in Meiji 33 (April 1900). Printed in classical vertical script on tinted paper within an elaborate floral border, it certifies one share of 50 Yen held in the firm. The face declares the denomination, the share number, and the holder's name, then names the company's officers — president, vice-president, and directors — each authenticating the document with a red seal impression. An orange government revenue stamp is affixed at the upper right. The reverse carries the printed rules governing the share and a ruled ledger for recording future transfers, reflecting the registered-name regime by which ownership in such partnerships changed hands.

pages.description: Front of the certificate within a dense floral border with corner medallions. Large central text gives the denomination, "one sum, 50 Yen," beside the company name and share number (No. 44). An orange revenue stamp sits at upper right; the holder's name and the date, Meiji 33, fourth month, run down the side. President, vice-president, and directors are listed below, each over a red seal. A transfer-registration grid occupies the right. Page 1 of 2.

Reverse of the certificate. The right half prints the regulations governing the share in vertical columns — dividend entitlement, name registration, and the transfer procedure requiring the company seal. The left half is a blank ruled ledger headed by date columns (year/month/day) for recording successive ownership changes, with printed officer seal-lines beneath. Page 2 of 2.

creator: Nobino Seigai Gomei Kaisha (Nobino Seigai Partnership)
notes: Reverse (goetzmann0921) contains conditions and blank dividend recording table
